Avoids placing the status indicator as a standalone colored dot without text — a…
entries.critique: The left sidebar pairs a 12px icon above each label in sentence case — the icon acts as a fast visual anchor for returning users scanning by shape, while the label ensures first-time users never guess meaning. This rejects the common default of placing icons beside labels (which requires horizontal scanning) or using icons alone (which burdens novice users with recall). The three summary cards on the main canvas place a bold metric value above a smaller, lighter percentage change indicator — this forces the eye to read absolute magnitude before relative change, which reduces cognitive load for time-pressured analysts who need the number first. The line chart uses a single blue stroke with no fill and rotated x-axis labels: the no-fill choice keeps the trend visible even when multiple lines overlap (rejecting area charts that would occlude data), while the 45-degree rotation prevents label truncation at the cost of mild tilt — a trade-off that favors data density over perfect readability. The right detail rail uses colored status chips (green for completed, orange for pending) with white text — the colored background makes status scannable at a glance for frequent users, but the small chip size (approx 16px height) may challenge users with low vision or on mobile.
whatToSteal: Place a 12px icon above each navigation label — this creates a fast visual anchor for returning users scanning by icon shape, while the label below removes ambiguity for first-time users.

Display the metric value in a bold weight and the percentage change in a lighter, smaller weight on summary cards — this ensures absolute magnitude is read first, which matters for time-pressured decision-making. Do not use this if the percentage change is the primary metric (e.g., growth dashboards).

Use a single-color stroke line chart with no fill — this keeps the trend line dominant and prevents occlusion when multiple series are overlaid. Avoid this when the area under the curve is the primary data story (e.g., cumulative revenue).

Rotate x-axis labels by 45 degrees instead of truncating or wrapping — this fits more time points while keeping labels mostly readable. Do not use this for long text labels; use horizontal labels for 6 or fewer items.
antiPatterns: Avoids using multiple accent colors across chart series — a lazy approach would assign a distinct hue to each data point, forcing users to cross-reference a legend. This design uses a single blue stroke, so the scanning path stays linear and the trend is read without interruption. The trade-off is that distinguishing multiple lines would require an additional cue (dash pattern or annotation), but for this single-line chart, the simplicity wins.

Avoids placing the status indicator as a standalone colored dot without text — a common pattern in fintech dashboards that relies on color alone to convey state. This design uses a colored chip with white text (e.g., 'Completed', 'Pending'), so users with color vision deficiencies or those scanning quickly can still read the status. The chip's background color provides a redundant visual cue for speed, but the text ensures it never fails.

entries.title: Origin

steal: Use one continuous background across both columns and let the gutter width do th…
Set every form label above the field in uppercase tracked sans, never as an in-f…
avoid: The design deliberately avoids pinning the primary commit button in a sticky app…
The design deliberately avoids wrapping each input in a filled card with rounded…
entries.critique: DECISION: The form-panel and visual-panel share a single uninterrupted background with no divider, so the user's gaze keeps contact with the artwork across the gutter instead of treating the right side as a separate screen. EFFECT: A first-time user halfway through entering their name still sees the consequence of their choices in the same frame, which keeps an identity-building flow from feeling like blind data entry. REJECTION: Stock stacked onepage-onboarding layouts typically box the right column in a card so it reads as a 'preview', which severs the feedback loop and forces the eye to treat it as a downstream deliverable. DECISION: Section labels are set uppercase and tracked above each input, not placed inside as floating placeholders. EFFECT: Returning users re-entering fields don't have to clear placeholder text and remember what the field meant, and first-time users scanning top-to-bottom read label-then-answer — a sentence rhythm — instead of guessing from gray hints. REJECTION: Material-style floating labels save vertical space but punish correction flows and screen-reader users who hear the label announce as the field gains focus rather than at page load. DECISION: The commit affordance is a single underlined or bottom-anchored action in a modal-like region at the foot of the form rather than a filled rectangular CTA at the top of the page. EFFECT: At the moment of decision the user is not visually ambushed by a colored block; the action earns attention by being the only thing resolving in the gutter, which suits a fintech onboarding where premature submit carries real cost. REJECTION: A persistent top-right primary button would train eye and click toward the first few fields rather than the completed form, and would visually outvote the explanatory artwork in the right panel.
whatToSteal: Use one continuous background across both columns and let the gutter width do the separation; users keep their peripheral context while editing and never feel they have switched screens. Use when the right side is a live reflection of the inputs (profile preview, account summary). Do NOT use when the panels carry unrelated tasks that need to be tabled independently.

Set every form label above the field in uppercase tracked sans, never as an in-field placeholder; the label remains the entry point for screen readers and stays visible when the field rejects input and re-renders empty. Use when forms exceed four fields and users will revise. Do NOT use for single-field search bars where vertical real estate matters more than re-entry clarity.

Reserve the only visually loud element for the commit moment — anchor a single low-key action at the foot of the form rather than a filled button in the header. Use when premature submission has consequences and the user needs to finish before they can decide. Do NOT use for save-as-you-go flows where partial state must persist on every keystroke.
antiPatterns: The design deliberately avoids pinning the primary commit button in a sticky app header; a persistent colorful CTA would have trained users to submit before the form was complete, which is the most common onboarding drop-off failure mode in fintech flows where account details are irreversible or carry compliance weight.

The design deliberately avoids wrapping each input in a filled card with rounded corners and an inner shadow; that kitchen-sink treatment is the lazy default for form screens and would have buried the content in container chrome, making the long profile form feel like a stack of disconnected widgets instead of a continuous identity statement.

steal: Pair every sidebar icon with a visible text label even when the audience is tech…
Reserve pill chips for status, not for action affordance — keep export/filter ac…

Avoids hiding the search affordance behind a magnifier icon alone, which is a co…
entries.critique: Returning fintech analysts usually come to this screen with a specific export job in mind, so the sidebar keeps icons paired with text labels instead of going icon-only — first-time users can read where to click without a tooltip, and veteran operators still get the icon-as-target pattern they will eventually memorize. The decision to separate row status into pill chips rather than tinted full-row backgrounds keeps parallel row scanning possible: an analyst comparing four failed exports can still read the numeric columns without a colored wash distorting the table rhythm. The fixed-narrow sidebar plus flex main canvas rejects the equally common split of giving the data table a max-width with margins centering it, which would waste the wide analyst monitor and force horizontal scrolling inside dense numeric columns. For novice users, the keyboard-prefixed search field doubles as a discoverability signal that the product rewards shortcut use, reducing the support load of 'how do I find an old job' tickets.
whatToSteal: Pair every sidebar icon with a visible text label even when the audience is technical — drop the labels only after analytics confirm the icon set is learned, never on launch. Use this when your nav has more than five sections; below five, icon-only is defensible.

Reserve pill chips for status, not for action affordance — keep export/filter actions as outlined or filled buttons so chips read as state, not as something to click. Avoid on forms where the chip would be the only label for a required field.

Fix the primary nav at a narrow width and let the data canvas flex so wide monitors add columns or whitespace, not sidebar padding — do NOT use this for marketing pages where the sidebar carries editorial content that needs to breathe.

Use weight contrast (one heavier weight for section titles, regular for row data) instead of color contrast to establish hierarchy so the warm palette can carry the mood without the type doing double duty as a color alarm.
antiPatterns: Avoids coloring whole table rows to indicate status, which would have made numeric comparison across rows harder and forced a clinical data-grid look; the chip approach keeps the row as the unit of comparison and the chip as the unit of state.

Avoids hiding the search affordance behind a magnifier icon alone, which is a common fintech shortcut that costs first-time users a discoverability round-trip; keeping the input always visible with a keyboard prefix teaches the shortcut in place.

Avoids centering the data table in a max-width container with empty side gutters, the default layout many admin templates ship with — that decision would have wasted wide analyst screens and introduced horizontal scroll inside revenue columns.

Avoids putting the filter controls inside the sidebar, which would have forced the analyst to context-switch between navigation and refinement on every query; keeping filters adjacent to the table header collapses that loop into one visual region.

entries.critique: A data-dense forecast dashboard balances a left icon sidebar with a wide, card-based main canvas and an overlay tooltip explaining the chart. Interactive controls use clear pill-like chips and a single strong primary CTA while most chart structure relies on light grid lines and subtle dividers rather than heavy borders/shadows.
whatToSteal: Use a right-side callout tooltip/card anchored to chart regions with concise title, icon, and stacked dividers; keep it visually distinct via white surface and slight rounding.
Create metric hierarchy with oversized currency numbers and lighter labels above them, aligned in a two-column layout for current vs future.
Drive chart readability with very light dashed gridlines and thin axis labels, using colored series (blue/red) rather than bold chart chrome.
Use pill-like filter chips for quick metric switching (Net Worth / Cash Flow / Success %) with consistent padding and muted borders.
antiPatterns: Avoids bold chart chrome (thick axes, heavy gridlines) — uses very light dashed gridlines so the data series carries the visual weight.
Avoids cluttering the chart canvas with inline labels; anchors explanations to a right-side callout tooltip instead.

entries.title: Origin

steal: Reserve the brightest accent color exclusively for the single most important dat…
Pair icons with short text labels in navigation — even small labels — so first-t…
avoid: Avoids using a rainbow palette for the bar chart — a common mistake that forces …
Avoids placing the search bar inside the modal overlay — a lazy approach would a…
entries.critique: The designer uses a single accent orange for the tallest bar in the chart while all other bars remain muted gray, making the highest value instantly identifiable without requiring the user to scan a legend. This decision rejects the common default of assigning a distinct hue to every series, which would force cross-referencing and slow down returning users who only need to spot the peak. The modal overlay for data entry interrupts the main task but is justified by placing the form's action button in a consistent position with high contrast, reducing error recovery time for expert users who enter data repeatedly. The fixed narrow primary nav keeps core navigation visible at all times, eliminating scroll-to-reach nav for long dashboards, but risks crowding labels if the team adds more items later.
whatToSteal: Reserve the brightest accent color exclusively for the single most important data point (e.g., the highest bar) to guide attention without a legend. Use this when the user's task is to find the outlier or maximum value. Do NOT use when comparing all values equally (e.g., a ranking chart).

Pair icons with short text labels in navigation — even small labels — so first-time users can learn by scanning while returning users rely on icon recognition. This avoids the ambiguity of icon-only navs. Do NOT use for overflow menus where space is too tight for text.

Use a fixed-width sidebar for primary nav when the dashboard has more than 5 navigation items, ensuring the user never loses context while scrolling the content area. Do NOT use on mobile-first layouts where vertical space is scarce.

Place the modal's primary action button (e.g., 'Save') at the bottom-right with a filled background, consistent with the primary accent, so users can rely on spatial memory instead of reading labels each time. Avoid ghost buttons for primary actions in modals.
antiPatterns: Avoids using a rainbow palette for the bar chart — a common mistake that forces the user to cross-reference a legend for every bar. Instead, the single accent draws the eye directly to the most relevant value, reducing cognitive load at the decision point. This anti-pattern matters for analytics dashboards where the user's goal is to quickly identify the highest or lowest value.

Avoids placing the search bar inside the modal overlay — a lazy approach would add search within the form, but the designer keeps it in the sidebar for quick navigation without leaving the current context. This prevents confusion between searching for data vs. searching for commands. Only relevant when the UI has both search and modal tasks.

Avoids using an icon-only navigation for the primary nav — while icon-only navs are common, they increase learning time for first-time users. By keeping text labels visible, the design reduces the barrier for new users without sacrificing speed for experts.

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The decision to render filter state as a border-color swap on a transparent pill — rather than a filled background — keeps the active select…
steal: Render filter/tab chips as transparent fills with border-weight or border-color …
Cluster identity/account controls into a single tight circular icon group at the…
avoid: Avoids the lazy default of giving every icon-button a distinct hover color, whic…
Avoids stacking filters into a multi-row toolbar that wraps unpredictably across…
entries.critique: The decision to render filter state as a border-color swap on a transparent pill — rather than a filled background — keeps the active selection legible against any media tile it sits over, so a returning user filtering through dozens of results never loses track of the chip boundary against changing neighbors; a first-time user also reads the chip as a chip rather than as a button, lowering the activation cost. The rejection of the conventional filled-pill pattern matters because dense media grids constantly re-tint the area beneath floating controls, and a filled chip would either need a solid scrim (creating visual dead zones) or shift color when it crossed a contrasting tile. Compressing all filters into a single horizontal row instead of a left rail collapses the task into one scanning pass, which reduces decision latency for users comparing across many combinations — though it caps how many filters can be added before the row wraps. The icon-only avatar stack on the right of the nav trades label-reading for spatial recognition, which speeds repeat visits once muscle memory forms but imposes a memorization burden on first encounters.
whatToSteal: Render filter/tab chips as transparent fills with border-weight or border-color state changes so they remain readable over varying media backgrounds — required when chips float above image grids or colored panels; do NOT use this for primary CTAs where fill state is the expected affordance.

Cluster identity/account controls into a single tight circular icon group at the far edge of the nav so a returning user resolves session context in one saccade — applies when the page has more than two account-related actions; below that, separate buttons are faster to label.

Crop varied media (vertical photos, landscape video thumbs) to a single aspect ratio in the comparison grid so the grid reads as a menu of equivalents rather than a feed — use this when the user is choosing one option from many; do NOT use it for a chronological activity stream where time-of-post is a ranking signal.

Reserve the single most-saturated hue for interactive surfaces only; let structural chrome stay in warm neutrals — applies whenever the page contains both navigation and rich media; without this rule, every component fights for the same attention budget.
antiPatterns: Avoids the lazy default of giving every icon-button a distinct hover color, which would turn the avatar cluster into a small rainbow and force the user to hunt for the one that opens account vs history vs settings — leaving chrome monochromatic reserves color budget for state that actually changes meaning.

Avoids stacking filters into a multi-row toolbar that wraps unpredictably across breakpoints; a horizontal single-row constraint forces the designer to prune filters to what users actually combine, rather than exposing every imaginable facet and letting users feel they should use them all.

Avoids treating the AI/ML vertical signal with the conventional dark-mode-and-glow pattern; a warm tactile palette rejects the 'sci-fi credibility' trope that often signals 'we have a model' at the cost of signaling 'we have a product you can touch today.'

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: This UI needs a human review, but the screenshot shows a clear structure worth cataloging for future design reference.
steal: Cap the auth surface to a fixed-width modal-overlay with an internal two-region …
Reduce the entry component set to one primary affordance when the product's valu…
avoid: Avoids a multi-provider picker stacked above the form — a lazy default that outs…
Avoids drop-shadow elevation on a soft-neumorphic palette. The lazy read of 'sof…
entries.critique: This UI needs a human review, but the screenshot shows a clear structure worth cataloging for future design reference.
whatToSteal: Cap the auth surface to a fixed-width modal-overlay with an internal two-region split (form + visual) when the surrounding product has enough brand equity that the auth step is a brake, not a goal. Constraint it satisfies: forces a single decision per visit. Do NOT use when the auth step needs to host long legal copy, password-recovery flows, or multi-step verification — the fixed canvas will clip them.

Reduce the entry component set to one primary affordance when the product's value prop is itself the hook; users who land intending to try the AI will not benefit from a provider chooser. Do NOT use for enterprise SKUs where SSO and SAML are decision criteria.

Use borders-without-shadows plus slight-round corners for AI/research-adjacent products that must read as serious without going clinical. The flat surface lets the visual panel carry warmth while the form stays legible. Do NOT use on dense data dashboards where shadow is doing work separating cards from background imagery.
Treat the auth step as a 'remove-decisions' exercise, not an 'add-options' one. Each removed control is one fewer thing a first-time visitor has to evaluate before they reach the product.
antiPatterns: Avoids a multi-provider picker stacked above the form — a lazy default that outsources the trust decision to the user before they've seen the product. The omission matters because AI tools live or die on first-session activation, and an undecided user at the auth step rarely returns.

Avoids drop-shadow elevation on a soft-neumorphic palette. The lazy read of 'soft' is to pile on inner/outer shadows, which would have made the visual panel recede and the form feel sunken. Skipping shadows keeps both regions at the same focal plane, so the user's eye is not steered away from the input.

Avoids letting the auth screen leak into the product surface (no nav bar, no footer, no 'browse without account' escape hatch). A permissive layout would give hesitant users a way to leave before committing, which is the wrong default for a product whose value is unlocked only after sign-in.

steal: Use mid-gray (#7c7c7c) for subheadlines instead of reducing font size — it maint…
Display product value through literal UI previews (the emoji cards showing actua…
avoid: The visual panel displays static preview cards with no interaction affordance or…
The password input field lacks a visibility toggle icon or strength indicator — …
entries.critique: The visual panel uses pastel cards with emojis as proof-of-concept rather than stock photography or abstract illustration. EFFECT: The literal depiction of mood states (🥳, 🧘, 😴) demonstrates the app's domain immediately without requiring testimonial copy or feature lists. REJECTION: It avoids the credibility theater of lifestyle photography, which would falsely claim real user documentation when showing a sign-up flow. The Google SSO button positions its brand icon before the text rather than using a centered, icon-only approach. EFFECT: Left-alignment creates a scannable vertical rhythm with the email field and primary CTA below, treating SSO as another form input rather than a special object. REJECTION: Centered icon buttons suggest equal-priority alternatives, which would undermine the visual hierarchy where email input remains the primary path. The form panel receives a fixed-narrow width while the visual panel flexes. EFFECT: Constraining form width to ~320-360px creates optimal line length for input labels and enforces thumb-zone compliance on mobile viewports without responsive breakpoint logic. REJECTION: Full-width forms force desktop users into inefficient horizontal eye travel and break the left-to-right reading gravity that should terminate at the CTA.
whatToSteal: Use mid-gray (#7c7c7c) for subheadlines instead of reducing font size — it maintains readability while signaling hierarchy through luminance contrast rather than scale, preserving typographic rhythm

Display product value through literal UI previews (the emoji cards showing actual tracked moods) rather than feature descriptions, so sign-up context demonstrates the interaction model users will encounter post-auth

Apply identical corner radius and border weight to both text inputs and buttons — this creates a unified form language where interactive elements share structural DNA rather than distinguishing by decoration alone

Position SSO buttons as left-aligned list items with icon+text rather than centered icon-only tiles — this maintains vertical scan flow and treats third-party auth as a form field equivalent rather than a contextually separate choice
antiPatterns: The visual panel displays static preview cards with no interaction affordance or gesture hint — users cannot determine if these are tappable examples, static illustrations, or UI chrome decoration, creating ambiguity about whether the right panel is informational or interactive

The password input field lacks a visibility toggle icon or strength indicator — users must rely on browser defaults for password management without inline confirmation that their input meets requirements or is being captured correctly

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The inline country code presentation (+1 as plain text) rejects the conventional dropdown chevron pattern, eliminating the visual noise of a…
steal: Inline country code as static text (+1) with tap-to-change behavior—reserves the…
Identical corner radius across input fields and buttons—creates shape rhyming be…
avoid: The identical pill-shaped corners on both the input field and submit button elim…
entries.critique: The inline country code presentation (+1 as plain text) rejects the conventional dropdown chevron pattern, eliminating the visual noise of an interactive affordance that most users never activate—this creates a cleaner reading line for the majority case while still allowing code changes through tap. The input field's pill corners at maximum radius (full height/2) match the submit button's geometry exactly, establishing a repeated formal vocabulary across passive and active UI states rather than differentiating them through shape—this unifies the visual field but risks diminishing perceived affordance hierarchy. The helper text placement immediately below the question, with no expanded vertical gap, treats explanation as part of the question itself rather than supplementary metadata—this creates a tighter semantic unit but demands that both elements be read as a single block.
whatToSteal: Inline country code as static text (+1) with tap-to-change behavior—reserves the dropdown affordance for the moment it's needed rather than displaying it prophylactically, reducing visual weight on a field 95% of users never modify
Identical corner radius across input fields and buttons—creates shape rhyming between different interaction types, unifying the formal system through repeated geometry rather than varied affordances

Helper text positioned as immediate continuation of the question heading—treats explanation as integrated with the prompt rather than separated metadata, forcing them to be consumed as a single reading unit

Monochromatic gray palette (#bcbcbc to #3c3c3c) with no color accents on interactive elements—relies entirely on spatial position and shape to signal affordance rather than hue-based emphasis, creating a restrained tonal system
antiPatterns: The identical pill-shaped corners on both the input field and submit button eliminate shape-based differentiation between data entry and action commitment—users must rely solely on position and label to distinguish between typing location and submission trigger, removing a redundant visual cue that serves accessibility and rapid scanning

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The single-character input boxes (DECISION) force one letter per field, making the name entry deliberately slow and character-by-character (…
steal: Single-character input boxes that force letter-by-letter entry — creates a delib…
Using accent color (#986687) for the primary question instead of body text color…
avoid: The subheading 'We like to keep things personal' explains the rationale for aski…
entries.critique: The single-character input boxes (DECISION) force one letter per field, making the name entry deliberately slow and character-by-character (EFFECT), rejecting the standard single text field that would allow rapid typing but sacrifice the letter-by-letter reveal rhythm (REJECTION). The muted purple heading color #986687 (DECISION) establishes visual priority through hue shift rather than weight increase (EFFECT), avoiding black or high-contrast text that would feel authoritative rather than conversational (REJECTION). The cursor displays as a simple underline in the active field (DECISION) without outline rings or background fills (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional focus indicators that would introduce blue accent colors and break the monochromatic purple-gray palette (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Single-character input boxes that force letter-by-letter entry — creates a deliberate pacing mechanism that makes users conscious of each character, useful when the act of typing itself should feel considered rather than automatic

Using accent color (#986687) for the primary question instead of body text color — reserves the darkest values for instructional elements, inverting the typical hierarchy so the question feels like guidance rather than a demand

All-caps transformation for the instruction label 'ENTER YOUR NAME' — achieves typographic differentiation without requiring a different font weight or family, keeping the type system minimal while still creating clear functional separation

Underline-only focus indicator that stays within the form field border — maintains the border-based visual language throughout interaction states, avoiding the introduction of new UI patterns (like rings or glows) that would complicate the grammar
antiPatterns: The subheading 'We like to keep things personal' explains the rationale for asking the question, which telegraphs anxiety about the ask — confident onboarding shows the value through experience rather than pre-justifying data collection with reassurance copy

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The placeholder-only labeling (DECISION: no persistent label, just 'First Name' inside the input) relies on native OS behavior to keep the p…
steal: Placeholder-as-label in micro-onboarding contexts where (a) one field appears at…
Fraction notation (1/4, 2/4) for progress in short, sequential flows under 7 ste…
avoid: The absence of any validation preview or character count leaves users guessing w…
entries.critique: The placeholder-only labeling (DECISION: no persistent label, just 'First Name' inside the input) relies on native OS behavior to keep the placeholder visible while typing (EFFECT: reduces vertical height requirements by 20-30px per field, critical in a single-question-per-screen flow) while rejecting the conventional always-visible label (REJECTION: persistent labels add cognitive load when the question is already obvious from context and sequence position). The asymmetric button spacing (DECISION: 40-50px clearance below vs. 16-20px above) creates gravitational pull toward the primary action (EFFECT: the eye settles on the button naturally without scanning back up) instead of the default centered spacing that treats vertical space as mere separation. The fraction-based progress (DECISION: '1/4' plain text) provides exact position without requiring users to count abstract shapes (EFFECT: instant comprehension of total scope and current location) rather than the typical dot sequence that forces counting and estimation for steps beyond five.
whatToSteal: Placeholder-as-label in micro-onboarding contexts where (a) one field appears at a time, (b) the question is self-evident from flow position, and (c) mobile keyboards keep placeholders visible during input—saves 20-30px per field while maintaining clarity

Fraction notation (1/4, 2/4) for progress in short, sequential flows under 7 steps—users instantly grasp 'I'm one-quarter done' without translating dots or bars into meaning, and the denominator sets accurate expectations

Asymmetric button spacing that adds 2-2.5× more whitespace below the CTA than above it—creates visual weight that draws attention downward to the action while preventing the button from floating ambiguously in centered space

Single-field-per-screen pagination for first-time data collection—each question becomes a conversation turn rather than a daunting form, and the narrow focus prevents field-switching that mobile keyboards make cumbersome
antiPatterns: The absence of any validation preview or character count leaves users guessing whether 'First Name' expects formal registration format (legal first name) or casual personalization (nickname, preferred name)—a single word below the field like 'How should we address you?' would clarify intent without adding a full label

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The design fixes the sidebar at a narrow width with labeled icon buttons, so first-time users can immediately understand each navigation tar…
steal: Pair every sidebar icon with a visible text label — this ensures first-time user…
Use a fixed narrow sidebar width with a dark background to separate navigation f…
avoid: Avoids showing all navigation items without labels — a lazy approach would hide …
Avoids using a single-column layout for the main content — a common default woul…
entries.critique: The design fixes the sidebar at a narrow width with labeled icon buttons, so first-time users can immediately understand each navigation target without relying on icon recognition. This rejects the common pattern of icon-only sidebars that force hover-to-reveal labels, which would increase learning time for unfamiliar users. The main canvas splits into a card list and a media grid, allowing returning users to scan structured data on the left while exploring visual results on the right without switching views. Cards use a slight border and rounded corners without shadows, creating a tactile separation from the page background that feels intentional but not heavy — rejecting the convention of drop shadows that can add visual noise. The dark sidebar background creates a strong visual anchor, drawing the eye to navigation first and then letting the content area breathe, which rejects a fully light layout that might not signal where primary controls live.
whatToSteal: Pair every sidebar icon with a visible text label — this ensures first-time users and those with reduced icon familiarity can navigate without guesswork. Reject icon-only patterns that fail discoverability. Use when the sidebar contains more than 4 items; avoid when screen real estate is extremely tight and icons are universally understood.

Use a fixed narrow sidebar width with a dark background to separate navigation from content — this reduces visual blending and helps users mentally partition tasks. Reject full-width or floating nav that can compete with content. Use for landing pages with multiple navigation targets; avoid for single-page scrolls.

Combine a card list with a media grid in the same viewport — this lets users browse structured information and visual examples simultaneously, reducing back-and-forth navigation. Reject tab-based switching that hides one mode. Use when content has both textual and visual representations; avoid when both lists are text-heavy.

Rely on borders and rounded corners instead of shadows to distinguish cards — this keeps the page flat and reduces visual noise, making the content hierarchy clearer. Reject drop shadows that can add cognitive load. Use for card-based layouts where tactile grouping is needed without depth cues; avoid for cards that need to feel elevated or interactive.
antiPatterns: Avoids showing all navigation items without labels — a lazy approach would hide labels behind hover states, forcing first-time users to memorize icon meanings. This design rejects that by always showing text labels, so navigation remains discoverable at a glance.

Avoids using a single-column layout for the main content — a common default would stack the card list above the media grid, requiring users to scroll between browsing and previewing. This design rejects that by placing them side by side, so both information types are visible without vertical scanning.

entries.critique: The sidebar’s icon-only buttons eliminate label clutter but demand that first-time users hunt via tooltips or trial, trading immediate recognizability for the muscle memory returning users develop after repeated visits—rejecting the conventional icon+label sidebar that would clarify function at the cost of a wider panel. Field labels rendered in #848484 on #c0c0b8 recede to secondary status, allowing typed input values to dominate the scanning path; this low-contrast choice risks making labels illegible for low-vision users, rejecting the equal-weight text hierarchy that would flatten the form’s information structure. By setting the accent color to the same #46463e as body text, the design removes a separate color signal for interactivity; buttons and controls rely on shape and context instead, which may cause confusion for users accustomed to color-coded affordance. The segmented control’s solid fill in #46463e on the active segment provides a high-signal activation state that can be verified in a single fixation, rejecting the common thin underline or subtle border highlight that forces a longer visual scan.
whatToSteal: Anchor primary navigation with a fixed-width, icon-only sidebar to build spatial memory for returning users. Use only when the app has a small, stable set of core sections and returning user frequency is high; avoid for onboarding flows or tools with many changing sections where discoverability matters more than speed.

Push field labels to the visual background by lowering their contrast to a muted shade, so that entered data pops. Reserve this for forms where users are familiar with the fields; never use when the label copy itself must be read carefully for every field (e.g., complex configuration forms).

Reduce the accent palette to a single hue that matches body text, ensuring no color competition distracts from data. This works for engineering or analysis tools where color is not needed for classification; avoid in consumer UIs where color is a primary differentiator between actions.

Use a solid fill for selected segments in toggle controls rather than outlines or underlines. This yields the highest activation contrast, but only use it when the control sits in a form that otherwise lacks heavy fills, so it stands out rather than mudding the field.
antiPatterns: The design avoids scattering related settings across multiple tabs inside the main area, which would force users to switch context repeatedly; instead, a single scrollable form with a sidebar keeps all options in one view, preventing the disorientation of hidden content.

It avoids using background color fills or heavy borders to differentiate form sections; the near-uniform gray canvas (#c4c4bc) and surface (#c0c0b8) prevent visual fragmentation, so users can scan entire forms without color interruptions breaking their reading flow.

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #d8ccd0 canvas rejection of pure white (#ffffff) establishes immediate atmospheric differentiation — it signals 'constructed environment…
steal: Use a 2:1 spacing ratio between form sections (32-40px) versus within-section pa…
Set canvas to a near-neutral with subtle warmth (#d8ccd0 range) instead of pure …
avoid: Navigation icons at #7c7c7c on #3c3c3c (~2.2:1 contrast) fall below WCAG AA mini…
entries.critique: The #d8ccd0 canvas rejection of pure white (#ffffff) establishes immediate atmospheric differentiation — it signals 'constructed environment' rather than 'blank document', making interactive regions feel deliberately placed rather than accidentally occupying negative space, though it sacrifices ~8% luminance contrast for ink legibility. The 32-40px inter-group vertical spacing versus ~16px intra-field padding creates a 2:1 rhythm that segments the form into scannable chunks without requiring divider lines or background fills — this rejects the common equal-spacing approach where every gap feels identical and the eye must work harder to parse grouping. The navigation icon contrast of ~2.2:1 (#7c7c7c on #3c3c3c) prioritizes atmospheric moodiness over WCAG AA compliance (4.5:1) — this works only if iconography is redundant with position memory, but creates friction for first-time users or anyone with moderate vision impairment. The Unicode back arrow '←' instead of an SVG icon asset maintains typographic consistency with surrounding text weight and eliminates the common mismatch where bold text sits next to thin-stroke icons, though it surrenders pixel-level optical refinement.
whatToSteal: Use a 2:1 spacing ratio between form sections (32-40px) versus within-section padding (16px) to create implicit grouping without visible dividers — the eye completes the boundary where space increases, reducing decorative elements while maintaining scannable structure

Set canvas to a near-neutral with subtle warmth (#d8ccd0 range) instead of pure white to make interactive components feel 'placed on a surface' rather than 'floating in void' — this costs ~8% contrast but gains atmospheric coherence and reduces eye strain in extended sessions

Employ a single sans-serif family across all weights rather than mixing display and body fonts when the content is utilitarian (forms, dashboards) — this creates visual discipline that matches task focus and eliminates the 'designed to look designed' signal that undermines trust in data tools

Reserve maximum contrast (#ffffff text on #4b393f) exclusively for primary CTAs while keeping all other interactive elements in mid-range contrast (~2-3:1) — this creates a single visual 'shout' that makes the intended path unmistakable without requiring color variety or size exaggeration
antiPatterns: Navigation icons at #7c7c7c on #3c3c3c (~2.2:1 contrast) fall below WCAG AA minimums (4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components), creating a barrier for users with low vision or situational impairment (outdoor glare, dim screens) — the aesthetic choice to prioritize tonal subtlety over accessibility compliance assumes all users have perfect vision and controlled viewing conditions, which excludes a measurable percentage of the audience and may violate regulatory requirements in government or enterprise contexts

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 24px icon spacing in the left rail creates a vertical rhythm of 3:2 against the 16px horizontal padding, establishing a proportional sys…
steal: 24px vertical spacing between navigation icons with 16px horizontal padding crea…
All-caps column headers at 10px with 0.5px letter-spacing compressed into a smal…
avoid: The 'File' menu text at the top left lacks any visual distinction from body text…
entries.critique: The 24px icon spacing in the left rail creates a vertical rhythm of 3:2 against the 16px horizontal padding, establishing a proportional system rather than uniform gutters (DECISION). This ratio makes the navigation feel vertically generous without consuming width, allowing icons to breathe while the rail stays under 64px (EFFECT). It rejects equal padding on all sides, which would either waste horizontal space or cramp vertical scanning (REJECTION). The 1px border weight on the dropdown at #7c7478 sits exactly midway between the ink (#3c3c3c) and surface (#bcbcb4) values, making the control visible without creating figure-ground separation (DECISION). This keeps the filter embedded in the canvas rather than floating above it, maintaining a single depth plane (EFFECT). It avoids the heavier 2px borders or high-contrast strokes that would make the dropdown feel like a separate component rather than an in-place control (REJECTION). The 40px clearance before the first table row exceeds the 44px row height itself, inverting the typical ratio where headers get compressed (DECISION). This makes the data feel introduced rather than immediate, giving the eye a landing zone before processing tabular information (EFFECT). It rejects the tight 8-12px header-to-content gaps common in dense dashboards, which force headers and data into perceptual competition (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: 24px vertical spacing between navigation icons with 16px horizontal padding creates a 3:2 rhythm that makes vertical scanning feel open while keeping the rail narrow — apply this ratio when navigation must feel generous without consuming width
All-caps column headers at 10px with 0.5px letter-spacing compressed into a small size make labels spatial organizers rather than readable text — use when headers serve navigation over communication

1px borders in a midpoint color (#7c7478 between #3c3c3c ink and #bcbcb4 surface) keep controls visible without creating depth separation — deploy when UI elements should feel embedded in the canvas rather than layered above it

40px clearance before the first data row (exceeding the 44px row height) inverts typical header compression to give data an introduction zone — steal when tables need a perceptual pause before information density begins
antiPatterns: The 'File' menu text at the top left lacks any visual distinction from body text despite being a primary navigation element, relying solely on position to signal interactivity — this makes discoverability dependent on spatial convention rather than affordance, forcing users to learn rather than recognize navigation

steal: Inset shadow fields (1-2px inner shadow, rgba(0,0,0,0.08)) that reverse the rais…
Fixed-width form inputs (320px) that resist parent flex expansion—maintains opti…
avoid: The vertical alignment of the toggle switch with its label places both on the sa…
The monotone gray (#646464) applied uniformly to all body text, input labels, an…
entries.critique: The inset shadow treatment on input fields (DECISION) reverses the conventional raised-surface paradigm by making interactive zones appear recessed (EFFECT), signaling 'fill this cavity' rather than 'tap this button'—this works because forms are about depositing information, not triggering actions, and the cavity metaphor aligns with that mental model (REJECTION of the iOS-style raised field default that treats every input like a tappable surface). The 320-pixel width constraint on form inputs (DECISION) prevents the elastic expansion that flexible layouts typically allow (EFFECT), maintaining scanline length regardless of viewport width, which preserves reading rhythm and prevents the 'lost in whitespace' phenomenon that occurs when inputs stretch to 60+ characters (REJECTION of the fill-available-width pattern that optimizes for space usage over readability). Purple color assignment to the active navigation state (DECISION) uses a mid-saturation accent (#b24c99) instead of the darker brand color (#5c274f) (EFFECT), creating enough contrast against the light background for immediate state recognition without the visual weight that would make the sidebar compete with form content (REJECTION of the 'use your darkest brand color for selected state' convention that often creates unintended emphasis).
whatToSteal: Inset shadow fields (1-2px inner shadow, rgba(0,0,0,0.08)) that reverse the raised-button metaphor—works specifically for forms where the interaction is 'fill/deposit' rather than 'trigger/activate', creating visceral alignment between visual treatment and interaction intent

Fixed-width form inputs (320px) that resist parent flex expansion—maintains optimal scanline length (35-45 characters for email/text inputs) regardless of viewport, preventing the readability degradation that occurs when inputs stretch beyond 600px on wide screens

Weight-only hierarchy in navigation items (medium-weight selected vs regular-weight unselected, same size)—eliminates the need for background fills, borders, or size changes to communicate state, which reduces visual noise in dense navigation lists

12-pixel horizontal gap between navigation icon and label—tight enough to feel grouped but wide enough that the icon doesn't collide with multi-line labels when translated, avoiding the icon-overlap problem common in localized interfaces
antiPatterns: The vertical alignment of the toggle switch with its label places both on the same baseline, but the visual weight of the toggle (with its shadow treatment and dual-colored states) creates a rightward pull that makes the label feel like it's serving the toggle rather than the toggle serving the setting—inverting the label above the control or increasing left-side weight would restore the correct reading hierarchy

The monotone gray (#646464) applied uniformly to all body text, input labels, and placeholder text eliminates the distinction between empty and populated fields at a glance—a two-tier gray system (darker for labels, lighter for placeholders) would allow faster form-completion verification without requiring field-by-field focus

Breadcrumb hierarchy through hue shift rather than size change—keeping 'Main Das…

entries.critique: The solid-fill navigation icons (#96a4f1) maintain consistent optical density across the entire vertical rail, which prevents the eye from parsing inactive versus active states through fill/stroke toggles—this uniformity keeps the left edge visually quiet but sacrifices the immediate wayfinding cue that outline-to-fill transformations provide. The non-uniform grid spacing (24px vertical, 20px horizontal between cards) creates deliberate tension that prevents the metric cards from feeling like a rigid table layout, though this asymmetry risks appearing unintentional rather than designed, especially when the cards themselves use perfectly uniform 16px corner radii. The percentage labels positioned at 8px outside their donut segments reject the conventional in-segment placement that clutters small circular graphics, but this external positioning requires more canvas real estate and assumes the user will trace the invisible radial connection between label and arc.
whatToSteal: Percentage labels positioned outside donut segments at fixed 8px radial distance—this pulls numerics away from colored fills where legibility suffers, and avoids callout lines that add visual noise. Works when you have canvas space and need to preserve the geometric purity of circular charts.

Breadcrumb hierarchy through hue shift rather than size change—keeping 'Main Dashboard' and 'Expression' at identical point sizes but differentiating via #444444 versus #7283bc maintains tight vertical rhythm without requiring optical size compensation or leading adjustments.

Metric cards differentiated by background tint (15% opacity color washes) while maintaining identical corner radius and shadow treatments—this lets you group related data types through color families without fragmenting the layout into visually distinct component styles.

Solid-fill navigation icons without stroke variants—this keeps the sidebar at uniform visual weight and prevents the active/inactive toggle from creating density shifts that draw attention away from the main canvas content.
antiPatterns: The navigation icons use solid fills in the accent color (#96a4f1) without any state differentiation mechanism visible—if all icons maintain identical fill treatment regardless of active/inactive status, users lose the immediate spatial orientation that conventional sidebar navigation provides through fill/outline toggles or opacity shifts. This uniformity may look cohesive but sacrifices functional wayfinding.

Donut chart segments use different colors (#96a4f1, #c98c9c, #34c46a) to encode different metrics, but these hues reappear as background tints in the containing cards—this creates a redundant color-to-meaning mapping where the card background and the chart segment both signal the same categorical identity, wasting one of the two color channels that could encode an independent dimension.

entries.title: Hume

steal: Temperature-based icon grouping in dark sidebars: use warm tertiaries (#9b7c63 r…
Persistent fill styling for active filter chips (#c6b4a6 backgrounds) instead of…

entries.critique: The sidebar icon temperature coding (warm browns #9b7c63/#ae8470 for active/primary tools vs. cooler grays for utility functions) establishes categorical boundaries through hue temperature rather than conventional highlight boxes — this allows the navigation to communicate grouping without adding container weight, rejecting the typical selected-state background rectangle that would compete with the already-present dark sidebar fill. The filter chips maintain persistent fill backgrounds (#c6b4a6) in their default state rather than ghost/outline styling — this decision treats filtering as an applied state layer rather than an action to be taken, making the current view's constraints immediately legible without requiring users to parse chip borders or remember which filters they've toggled. The breadcrumb 'Evaluations' link uses a muted brown tone instead of standard link blue — this keeps navigational wayfinding visually subordinate to the data canvas, rejecting the convention that all clickable text must use high-contrast blue which would pull attention away from the table content itself.
whatToSteal: Temperature-based icon grouping in dark sidebars: use warm tertiaries (#9b7c63 range) for content-manipulation tools and reserve cooler grays for account/settings utilities — this creates semantic categories without adding visual containers or hover states

Persistent fill styling for active filter chips (#c6b4a6 backgrounds) instead of outline-to-fill on toggle — communicates applied state rather than available action, making the current data view's constraints legible at a glance

Breadcrumb links in muted accent tones (brown family) rather than primary blue — keeps navigational chrome perceptually recessed behind data content, especially valuable in dashboards where the table/canvas is the primary task surface

Vertical gap isolation (400-500px) between functional icon groups in fixed sidebars — uses whitespace as a categorical separator without requiring divider lines or section headers, preserving the sidebar's minimal footprint
antiPatterns: The dropdown selects retain full container fills (#cbbaae) that visually compete with the filter chips for background weight — when multiple filled controls appear in close proximity (filters + selects within 80px), they flatten into a single beige mass rather than establishing control-type hierarchy. Using outline or underline styling for the selects would differentiate form inputs from state indicators.

entries.critique: The solid-fill input fields reject the conventional outlined/stroke-only approach in favor of tactile shape presence — this makes the interactive zones immediately readable as containers rather than requiring users to parse negative space. The authentication status uses a checkmark character followed by plain text instead of the expected pill-shaped badge — this eliminates visual hierarchy competition with the form itself while maintaining state legibility. The navigation column width stays fixed at approximately 180px rather than expanding to fit content — this creates a predictable left margin that frames the form canvas without adjusting when items change, preventing layout shift during interaction. The placeholder text contrast sits at roughly 1.4:1 against its field background instead of meeting WCAG AA targets — this prioritizes atmospheric cohesion over accessibility mandates, though it risks illegibility for users with limited vision. The corner radius appears at 3-4px rather than the more common 6-8px default — this registers as intentional geometry without becoming a dominant stylistic signature, letting the shape recede behind content.
whatToSteal: Use solid background fills for form fields with only 10-15% brightness separation from the page background — creates defined input zones without harsh contrast jumps, particularly effective when the field color shares undertones with the surrounding palette
Set navigation line-height to 2.5x the text size when vertical space permits — produces scannable vertical rhythm without requiring dividers, rules, or background hovers to separate targets
Position authentication state at the navigation column bottom as bare text with a symbol prefix — removes competition for attention with primary actions while keeping status continuously visible
Use a condensed serif for product wordmarks when horizontal space is constrained — delivers formality and technical credibility while occupying 30-40% less width than regular-width alternatives
antiPatterns: The 'Create API key' button uses a dark fill (#3c3c3c) that matches the body text color exactly, eliminating luminance-based hierarchy — users must rely solely on shape and position to identify the primary action, which fails when scanning quickly or when cognitive load is high

entries.title: Hume
entries.critiqueExcerpt: DECISION: Percentage deltas (+12.5%, -2.3%, etc.) render in the same neutral brown as all other text, with no green/red color-coding. EFFECT…
steal: Use serif typography for exactly one element — the primary content label — to cr…
Match border colors to content palette rather than using neutral grays; this mak…
avoid: The metric cards use identical visual weight and spacing for all four values, pr…
entries.critique: DECISION: Percentage deltas (+12.5%, -2.3%, etc.) render in the same neutral brown as all other text, with no green/red color-coding. EFFECT: Forces users to parse the plus/minus symbol rather than relying on color association, which prevents the scan-and-panic reflex that red numbers trigger in financial contexts. REJECTION: Conventional dashboards use green for gains and red for losses, but that creates emotional volatility and encourages reactive decision-making over thoughtful analysis. DECISION: Border strokes (#997965) match the dominant color palette instead of using neutral grays or blacks. EFFECT: Boundaries feel like part of the content's material rather than applied container chrome, which reduces the perception of 'boxes holding things' in favor of unified surface subdivisions. REJECTION: Standard practice uses #e0e0e0 or similar achromatic borders that visually separate themselves from content, but that creates a scaffolding-vs-content duality this design dissolves. DECISION: Navigation icons receive no hover state color shifts, selected state highlighting, or badge accents. EFFECT: Removes gamification pressure and notification anxiety, treating navigation as a stable reference panel rather than a system demanding interaction. REJECTION: Most dashboards use blue highlights, red badges, and state changes to guide attention, but those patterns train users to scan for alerts rather than think about where they want to go.
whatToSteal: Use serif typography for exactly one element — the primary content label — to create typographic hierarchy without needing weight or size extremes; the style contrast alone signals 'this names what you're looking at' versus 'these are controls'

Match border colors to content palette rather than using neutral grays; this makes divisions feel like natural seams in a continuous surface instead of imposed container scaffolding, which reduces visual fragmentation

Render positive and negative metrics in identical neutral tones with only the +/- symbol differentiating them; this prevents color-coded emotional reactions and forces deliberate reading, which is valuable when you want analytical rather than reflexive responses to data

Deploy monochrome iconography with no state changes or color accents in persistent navigation; this establishes the sidebar as stable reference infrastructure rather than an attention-competing notification panel
antiPatterns: The metric cards use identical visual weight and spacing for all four values, providing no indication of which metrics matter most or how they relate to each other — a user seeing '$24,532' and '1,429' and '87.3%' receives no guidance about whether these are comparable, hierarchical, or independent signals

entries.critique: The filled red pill for 'High' priority (DECISION: solid #df0b13 background with white interior text) creates immediate perceptual pop-out through both hue saturation and luminance contrast (EFFECT: high-priority items demand attention without requiring the user to decode icon systems or read text first), rejecting outlined badge conventions that treat all priority levels with equal visual weight (REJECTION: outlined treatments force users to read label text before understanding urgency, adding cognitive load to scanning operations). The icon-only sidebar navigation (DECISION: removing text labels entirely from the primary navigation column) reclaims horizontal space for the data table (EFFECT: reduces the chrome-to-content ratio and allows more table columns to remain visible without horizontal scroll), rejecting the icon-plus-label pattern that accommodates discoverability at the cost of persistent screen real estate (REJECTION: labeled navigation aids first-time users but penalizes every subsequent session with wasted space once mental models form). Avatar photographs in the assignee column (DECISION: photographic circular crops at ~32px without border rings) leverage facial recognition as the fastest human pattern-matching system (EFFECT: assignees become identifiable through peripheral vision during table scanning, faster than reading names), rejecting initial-based avatars or name-only columns that require foveal reading and character decoding (REJECTION: text-based assignee representation forces linear reading where parallel visual processing could operate).
whatToSteal: Reserve saturated accent fills exclusively for the highest-urgency state in any set, using outlined or muted variants for all other levels — this creates a two-tier visual system where critical items trigger pre-attentive pop-out while lower-priority items remain accessible through deliberate scanning

Reduce column header weight and saturation below body content weight, inverting conventional header emphasis — when headers recede, the eye naturally anchors on data rows first, accelerating the transition from interface orientation to task completion

Remove text labels from icon navigation after establishing initial mental models, converting the persistent navigation chrome into a minimal index — this trades discoverability (a one-time cost) for permanence (a per-session benefit that compounds across user lifetime)

Use photographic avatars instead of initials or names in dense table views where the same small set of people appears repeatedly — facial recognition operates faster than character decoding and works through peripheral vision during vertical scanning
antiPatterns: Column headers use insufficient contrast separation from the white background (appearing as mid-gray on white), making it difficult to distinguish structural labels from empty cells during rapid scanning — the reduced-emphasis strategy works for hierarchy but crosses into illegibility, particularly for users with reduced contrast sensitivity or in high ambient light conditions

The icon-only navigation provides no hover tooltips or progressive disclosure mechanism visible in the static state, creating a memorization requirement — users who forget icon meanings have no in-context recovery path without clicking, which punishes exploration and creates false starts

entries.critique: The numeric navigation step indicators use state-dependent color (#11c44f for active, muted for inactive) as the sole differentiator, rejecting icons, background fills, or size changes that would add visual noise to the progression system — this allows users to track position through a single chromatic signal. The chevron symbols in #9ce2b7 create a 40% saturation drop from the active #11c44f state, establishing a three-tier hierarchy (active/inactive-but-accessible/dormant) without introducing a fourth hue — this keeps the palette constrained while maintaining perceptual distance between states. The form pairs radio buttons with unindented, same-weight labels that sit directly beneath the control rather than inline or right-aligned, rejecting the horizontal association pattern — this creates vertical scanning efficiency when option text varies in length.
whatToSteal: Numeric step indicators that use only chromatic intensity (#11c44f vs muted) to signal active state — eliminates the need for background shapes, size changes, or iconography in progress systems with 6+ steps

Desaturated navigation affordances (#9ce2b7 chevrons at 40% less saturation than active state) that share hue family with the accent color — creates perceptual relatedness without competing for attention with the primary action signal

Radio button labels placed as block-level text below the control rather than inline — optimizes for variable-length option text by allowing each choice to consume vertical space proportionally without misaligning subsequent controls

Fixed 240px navigation width with 16px inter-item spacing that accommodates two-digit numbers, full-word labels, and chevron symbols without wrapping — provides predictable sidebar real estate that never reflows during interaction
antiPatterns: The question text 'Are you currently pregnant?' treats the keyword 'pregnant' with no typographic differentiation — when questions contain domain-specific or legally significant terms, identical styling obscures semantic importance and makes scanning for key concepts impossible

The transition from question 8 to question 9 in the sidebar shows no visual separation beyond the uniform 16px gap used throughout — final steps or sections that represent submission or summary deserve boundary treatment to signal cognitive shift

entries.critique: The single-color active state (#cd621b on the Journal icon) creates selection feedback through hue shift rather than background fill or weight change — this preserves the sidebar's vertical rhythm and prevents the rectangular highlight blocks that would compete with the card-based main canvas. The borderless shadow treatment on metric cards (visible drop shadows with no strokes against #d6bea5) achieves container separation through implied light source rather than graphic division — this avoids the hard-edged grid that would conflict with the soft neumorphic intention signaled by the shadows themselves. The +12% value rendered in #a2acf4 establishes a color-coding system for data polarity without requiring icon ornaments or background badges — this rejects the common pattern of wrapping deltas in colored pills, which would add visual weight and break the numeric-first scanning pattern the cards establish.
whatToSteal: Active navigation state through icon-only color shift (#cd621b vs #4c3b35) while keeping label weight and background unchanged — preserves vertical rhythm and avoids the rectangular highlight blocks that disrupt sidebar unity

Drop shadows without border strokes on card containers against a warm neutral field (#d6bea5) — separates content zones through implied elevation rather than graphic lines, maintaining the soft tactile quality that borders would undermine

Percentage deltas color-coded directly as numerals (#a2acf4 for positive movement) without pill backgrounds or icon prefixes — communicates polarity through type color alone, keeping metric cards visually spare and scan-friendly

Spacious inter-card spacing (24-32px grid gaps) that treats each metric as an isolated scannable object rather than tightly packing information — rejects the dense dashboard pattern where cards touch or nearly touch, improving individual metric legibility at the cost of information density
antiPatterns: The navigation labels ('Dashboard', 'Journal', 'Customers') use identical type treatment regardless of selection state, forcing complete reliance on icon color to communicate active page — users scanning text-first will find no typographic cue (no weight change, no size shift, no color difference in the labels themselves) to confirm their location

entries.title: Aboard by Teamtailor
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The sentence-case heading ('Onboard new employees in Teamtailor') rejects marketing-conventional title case, producing a conversational tone…

Set subheadings at approximately 60% opacity or saturation of your heading color…

entries.critique: The sentence-case heading ('Onboard new employees in Teamtailor') rejects marketing-conventional title case, producing a conversational tone that reads as instruction rather than proclamation—this lowers perceived friction for the unfamiliar user evaluating whether the tool applies to them. The illustration's palette isolation (mustards, sages, browns) deliberately withholds the brand's cyan accent, preventing visual competition with the CTA button—conventional hero sections often repeat brand colors throughout, which dilutes the action target and fragments attention. The dashboard preview positioning at 60-70% viewport width forces truncation of card edges, establishing spatial continuation beyond the frame—static, fully-contained mockups telegraph 'example' rather than 'your future workspace,' reducing aspirational pull.
whatToSteal: Withhold your primary brand color from decorative elements and reserve it exclusively for conversion actions—when every other element uses a tertiary palette (here: earth tones), the single cyan button becomes the only saturated target, eliminating cognitive comparison between visual weight and functional priority

Set subheadings at approximately 60% opacity or saturation of your heading color rather than reducing font size alone—this creates a perceivable hierarchy step that doesn't require squinting or measurement, and it maintains readability across devices where size differences compress

Frame product UI previews to show truncated edges at canvas boundaries (ideally cropping at 60-70% of element width)—partial visibility implies 'this continues,' transforming a static screenshot into a navigable space the user mentally projects themselves into

Use sentence case for value-proposition headings when your audience is evaluating a tool for the first time—title case signals marketing copy (which triggers skepticism), while sentence case mimics documentation or instruction (which implies the product already exists as a solved problem)
antiPatterns: The dashboard cards visible in the background preview use shadows without sufficient contrast differentiation between elevation layers—all cards appear to float at the same z-height despite presumably representing different data types or interaction states, which flattens the spatial model and makes it harder to predict which elements are manipulable versus static labels
