entries.critique: The cyan background (#80d8fb) saturates the entire canvas at full intensity (DECISION), forcing the interface chrome and camera preview to compete for attention through elevation and shadow rather than color contrast (EFFECT), rejecting the standard neutral canvas that would allow UI elements to claim visual priority through hue alone (REJECTION). The greeting copy deliberately uses a muted brown-gray (#79756b) for its text (DECISION), creating approximately 3:1 contrast against cyan—just below WCAG AA thresholds—which forces users to read through slight visual resistance (EFFECT), avoiding the safe black-on-white legibility that would contradict the brand's playful irreverence (REJECTION). The toolbar icons render as unfilled strokes in dark gray (DECISION), establishing a weight hierarchy where the active camera preview carries all the visual mass (EFFECT), rather than using filled icon states that would fragment attention across multiple simultaneous focal points (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use background color as the primary brand carrier instead of reserving it for accents—saturating the main canvas (#80d8fb) makes the product identity inescapable while forcing all other elements to earn attention through depth and contrast
Set greeting copy contrast just below accessibility thresholds (3:1 vs 4.5:1) when the message is non-critical and the slight reading friction reinforces a casual, low-stakes interaction tone
Keep toolbar icons as outline-only strokes with no fills when you want them to recede until interaction, preserving visual weight for the content preview window that floats above them
Center navigation wordmarks with symmetrical icon spacing when your product name needs to function as an always-visible anchor point rather than a left-aligned utility label
antiPatterns: The greeting text sits unanchored in a vast cyan void with no vertical rhythm established by preceding or following elements, leaving users with no spatial cues about where content creation will occur or how the blank canvas will populate

entries.title: Aboard 13
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The icon sidebar deploys #29b3f2 as a fill state rather than a stroke or background highlight (DECISION), which makes the selected tool imme…
steal: Use saturated accent color as icon fill for selected states rather than backgrou…
Lock navigation icon columns to fixed pixel widths (40-50px range) while making …

entries.critique: The icon sidebar deploys #29b3f2 as a fill state rather than a stroke or background highlight (DECISION), which makes the selected tool immediately parsable through figure-ground reversal—the icon becomes a negative space cutout (EFFECT), rejecting the common tab-style underline or background tint that requires users to first locate a secondary indicator before confirming selection (REJECTION). The header row assigns #f2da95 as background (DECISION), establishing category permanence through warmth while data rows remain white, so users parse the structure as 'fixed taxonomy above, mutable records below' (EFFECT), avoiding the all-white or alternating-row pattern that treats headers as just another data type (REJECTION). The leftmost icon column locks to a 40-48px width (DECISION), guaranteeing icon optical centering regardless of table reflow while letting text columns breathe proportionally (EFFECT), discarding the elastic icon-column approach that causes icons to drift off-center when content wraps or resizes (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use saturated accent color as icon fill for selected states rather than background highlight—it creates instant figure-ground reversal that's legible even at small sizes and doesn't require hunting for secondary indicators like underlines
Lock navigation icon columns to fixed pixel widths (40-50px range) while making text columns flex—guarantees optical centering for icons across all viewport sizes without manual rebalancing

Apply warm accent (#f2da95 range) to table headers and leave data rows pure white—the temperature contrast builds a mental model of 'permanent structure vs. variable content' without needing divider lines

Differentiate editable fields from labels using alignment instead of font weight—left-align the editable, center-align the categorical—saves a font-weight variant and creates two distinct voices in one typographic system
antiPatterns: The 'Untitled' text sits in a table cell that lacks any visual affordance (no cursor change icon, no background tint on hover state visible, no edit icon adjacent)—users must discover editability through trial-and-error clicking rather than seeing an invitation to interact

entries.title: Aboard 14
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The yellow (#fcec7c) background functions as a glowing substrate that makes soft shadows readable through luminance proximity — instead of w…
steal: Use a saturated warm background (#fcec7c) as the primary substrate instead of ne…
Pair desaturated action colors (#a96a90 purple) with highly saturated background…

entries.critique: The yellow (#fcec7c) background functions as a glowing substrate that makes soft shadows readable through luminance proximity — instead of white or gray neutrals that would render neumorphic depth invisible, this warm base lets every inset well register as a tactile depression. The muted purple (#a96a90) call-to-action rejects high-contrast button conventions (bright accent on neutral ground) because the yellow canvas already monopolizes attention; a saturated accent would fight the background, while this desaturated complement recedes into gentle availability. Navigation icons in dark teal (#4e6976) with no visible active state abandon the standard selected-indicator pattern (background fill, underline, or color shift) — this works because the two-column layout already isolates navigation from content, making positional context sufficient for wayfinding without redundant visual highlighting.
whatToSteal: Use a saturated warm background (#fcec7c) as the primary substrate instead of neutral white/gray when employing soft shadows — the luminance proximity between substrate and shadow makes embossed depth legible where neutrals would flatten it into imperceptibility

Pair desaturated action colors (#a96a90 purple) with highly saturated backgrounds to avoid chromatic competition — when the canvas already commands attention through hue intensity, interactive elements gain clarity by stepping back in saturation while maintaining sufficient contrast

Build single-family hierarchy using one hue (#69551b olive) at full and reduced opacity for headers and metadata rather than introducing secondary typefaces or weights — this creates consistency through repetition while opacity variance alone carries the hierarchical signal

Place the sole high-contrast element (golden ring #d2b151 around avatar) in a fixed-width detail rail to create a predictable focal anchor — instead of scattering contrast across the canvas, concentrating it in one stable location makes scanning patterns reliable
antiPatterns: Dark teal (#4e6976) navigation icons lack any active or hover state distinction, forcing users to rely entirely on spatial memory and layout position to understand their current location — this rejection of state feedback works only in rigid two-column layouts where position is unambiguous, but becomes a liability in responsive or single-column breakpoints where spatial anchors collapse

entries.title: Aboard 15

entries.critique: The yellow (#f7e292) navigation background creates perceptual separation through hue rather than value contrast, allowing the sidebar to register as a discrete zone without requiring a dark surface that would increase overall page weight or demand white text that limits density. The cyan accent (#0fbefa) appears exclusively on action verbs ('Export', 'Share', 'Manage workspace'), functioning as a behavioral cue rather than decoration—rejecting the common pattern of scattering accent color across static labels, icons, and borders where it dilutes functional meaning. The right panel's drop shadow isolates it as an overlay layer while the flush left sidebar remains spatially integrated with the canvas, establishing two different spatial models (embedded vs. floating) that communicate permanence vs. transience without animation or state indicators.
whatToSteal: Apply accent color only to interactive text labels, never to container borders or icon fills—limits the accent to exactly where user action is possible, making interactivity instantly scannable without hunting for hover states

Use hue-based sidebar backgrounds (saturated but light-value colors like #f7e292) instead of dark or neutral grays when sidebar content density is high—maintains text contrast while preventing the heavy visual 'weight' of dark columns that anchor the eye before users reach primary content

Set unassigned/null state values in the same typographic weight and color as populated fields ('Unassigned' in regular dark text, not gray italic)—treats absence as data rather than apology, reducing visual noise from differentiation that implies incompleteness

Reserve drop shadows for temporary overlay UI (detail panels, modals) while keeping permanent navigation flush—uses shadow as a semantic indicator of dismissibility rather than a generic depth treatment applied everywhere borders appear
antiPatterns: The table lacks zebra striping or any row background differentiation, forcing users to manually track horizontal alignment across six columns of dense text—particularly problematic when names vary in length and metadata wraps unpredictably, creating visual 'floating' where row boundaries become ambiguous during vertical scanning

entries.title: Aboard
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The cyan input field background (#8cf4ec) inverts the standard neutral-container assumption by making the interactive element itself the bri…
steal: Use saturated accent fills for input backgrounds instead of neutral containers—i…
Fragment conversational copy with grammatical incompleteness (trailing commas, u…
avoid: The left panel provides no character count, format hints, or validation feedback…
entries.critique: The cyan input field background (#8cf4ec) inverts the standard neutral-container assumption by making the interactive element itself the brightest color anchor, creating immediate focal magnetism without relying on border weight or size inflation—this rejects the invisible-input pattern where fields recede until activated. The greeting fragment 'Nice to meet you,' uses grammatical suspension (trailing comma with no completion) to simulate conversational pacing and imply the user's input will finish the sentence, rather than presenting form instructions as detached imperatives. The profile photo button reduces affordance signaling to icon-plus-label only, eliminating the rectangular container that typically screams 'clickable,' which works here because spatial isolation and the camera icon already communicate interactivity without needing a visual crutch. The yellow card's shadow-based separation creates spatial depth through simulated elevation rather than stroke containment, allowing the preview element to feel like it's hovering in front of the interface rather than being embedded within a flat grid.
whatToSteal: Use saturated accent fills for input backgrounds instead of neutral containers—it inverts the typical hierarchy where inputs are passive vessels, making data entry feel like an active, colorful moment rather than administrative drudgery

Fragment conversational copy with grammatical incompleteness (trailing commas, unfinished clauses) to create temporal pacing that positions user input as the natural completion of the interface's sentence
Reduce button chrome on tertiary actions to icon-and-label only when spatial context already provides sufficient clickability signaling—saves visual weight for moments that genuinely need emphasis
Deploy drop shadows for preview cards or output elements rather than input containers, establishing a spatial metaphor where user creations 'rise' above the interface plane as they take shape
antiPatterns: The left panel provides no character count, format hints, or validation feedback near the name input field—users entering lengthy names or special characters have no preemptive guidance about constraints until they attempt to proceed, forcing error discovery through trial rather than prevention

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The full-width 'Sign up' button with underlined text 'Log in' below it (DECISION) creates a primary/secondary action hierarchy through size …

Accent color (#f2c34d) threaded identically through button fills, checkbox borde…

entries.critique: The full-width 'Sign up' button with underlined text 'Log in' below it (DECISION) creates a primary/secondary action hierarchy through size and styling contrast rather than competing button weights (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional dual-button pattern where both CTAs receive equal button treatment and force users to parse semantic differences through labels alone (REJECTION). The custom checkbox with yellow accent border matching #f2c34d exactly (DECISION) maintains brand consistency across all interactive states (EFFECT), avoiding the visual disconnect of system-default form controls that introduce foreign colors and styling into an otherwise controlled palette (REJECTION). The grayscale illustration figure holding a yellow phone that precisely matches the UI accent (DECISION) creates visual cohesion between decorative and functional elements (EFFECT), rejecting the common practice of treating illustrations as separate branded content that competes with interface colors rather than reinforcing them (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Full-width primary action with text-link secondary below it: dedicates the button treatment exclusively to the encouraged path, making the alternative available without visual competition — eliminates the 'which button matters more' parsing cost of dual-button layouts

Accent color (#f2c34d) threaded identically through button fills, checkbox borders, and illustration props: creates a unified color story where interactive elements and decorative elements speak the same language — prevents the palette fragmentation that occurs when illustrations import their own color schemes

Consistent drop shadow application across all interactive surfaces (inputs and buttons): establishes elevation as a property of interactivity itself rather than button importance — makes every touch target announce its affordance through depth

Scale-only hierarchy between headline and subhead paired with muted gray (#7c7c7c) for secondary text: achieves clear information layering without introducing weight variation — keeps the type system simple while maintaining strong visual priority
antiPatterns: The 'Remember me' checkbox and label alignment places text baseline-adjacent to the checkbox rather than optically centered, creating a vertical misalignment that requires users to parse which text belongs to which control — particularly problematic when the forgotten password link appears at the same vertical zone

entries.critique: The cyan sidebar (#8cf2eb) fill creates a full-bleed chromatic anchor that owns the navigation zone independently of content state. EFFECT: The saturated field separates chrome from canvas without relying on stroke or shadow divisions, making the boundary perceptual rather than drawn. REJECTION: Avoids the typical neutral-gray sidebar that requires stroke borders to establish territory. The brown CTA button (#a4593c) sits in complement territory to cyan rather than repeating the accent. EFFECT: The warm/cool opposition signals a different action class—external navigation vs. in-app—and prevents color from becoming decorative repetition. REJECTION: Rejects the pattern of painting primary actions in the accent color regardless of semantic role. The empty state illustration uses three-color spot fills (#d2a9b0, #59ae89, #efcb48) that map to non-accent palette colors. EFFECT: The illustration feels authored for this specific system rather than dropped from a stock library, and the yellow speech bubble bridges conceptually to the 'add to Slack' prompt. REJECTION: Avoids monochrome or single-accent illustrations that feel generic across applications.
whatToSteal: Use all-caps with letter-spacing on persistent chrome labels (like branding lockups) to establish hierarchy through spatial rhythm rather than weight or size increases—works when the text won't reflow

Paint primary CTAs in a complementary hue to the dominant accent rather than repeating it, especially when the action crosses a boundary (external link, modal trigger)—signals functional difference through color temperature shift

Commission empty-state illustrations using 3-4 non-accent colors from your established palette to create visual consistency without literalness—the character holding a speech bubble semantically bridges to the Slack integration context

Apply full-bleed color fields to navigation zones instead of using borders or shadows to establish territory—works when the chromatic contrast is strong enough (cyan vs. near-white) to define the edge perceptually
antiPatterns: The 'MOBBIN.COM/SLACK' branding lockup uses all-caps on a URL string, which violates the typographic convention that URLs should preserve case-sensitivity for technical accuracy—even though Slack handles case-insensitively, the visual suggests the literal URL when it's actually a namespace label

entries.title: RUN

Restrict your accent color to interactive elements and progress indicators exclu…

entries.critique: The icon-only left rail (24px monochrome glyphs at fixed vertical rhythm) DECISION creates instant pattern recognition through position memory rather than label scanning EFFECT, rejecting the tab-bar-with-labels pattern that demands cognitive reading overhead REJECTION. The full-bleed photographic card backgrounds DECISION eliminate the need for artificial container shapes, letting actual imagery define boundaries EFFECT, avoiding the default rounded-rectangle-grid that treats every content type as equivalent abstract data REJECTION. The accent color (#1a94e4) appears only in progress visualization and interactive controls DECISION, making every blue element inherently actionable EFFECT, rejecting the decorative-accent approach where brand color appears in static headings or backgrounds and dilutes interaction signaling REJECTION. The serif display type at 48px paired with 12px sans-caps labels DECISION uses style contrast (not just size) to mark editorial voice versus system chrome EFFECT, avoiding the single-typeface-family convention that forces weight and size alone to carry all hierarchical meaning REJECTION.
whatToSteal: Use monochrome icon navigation with exactly zero labels when vertical position becomes the mnemonic device — the Y-axis location is more memorable than reading a word, especially for <6 destinations

Restrict your accent color to interactive elements and progress indicators exclusively; if something is blue, it must respond to input or show dynamic state — this trains users that color means 'this will do something'

Pair all-caps 12px utility labels directly above (not beside) 48px+ serif headlines to create a two-tier voice system: the whisper that categorizes versus the voice that declares, using style contrast to make the size jump feel intentional rather than erratic
Let photographic content define card boundaries through full-bleed placement instead of drawing boxes around images — the subject matter becomes the container, eliminating redundant geometric framing
antiPatterns: The bottom navigation bar uses text-only labels without icons, creating ambiguity about their tap targets and failing to provide the dual-encoding (word + symbol) that accelerates recognition — especially problematic when the left rail demonstrates that icons alone suffice for navigation, making the inconsistency feel arbitrary rather than context-driven

entries.title: SLMobbin

Chromatic reservation for data: assign your accent color exclusively to visualiz…
avoid: Typographically identical filter states: the '24h' and '7d' options show no visu…
entries.critique: The 1:2.5 icon-to-gap ratio in the left rail (16px icons with 40px spacing) creates breathing room that prevents pattern-matching fatigue during quick scanning—rejecting the typical 1:1 or 1:1.5 ratios that force users to parse icon shapes rather than letting vertical rhythm guide movement. The data cards use zero padding between metric and label, stacking them into a single visual unit where the number's mass anchors the meaning—avoiding the conventional 8-12px gap that treats metric and label as separate components requiring conscious association. The graph line's exclusive use of #0484dc reserves chromatic attention for data signals, never diluting it with interactive element highlighting—rejecting the common pattern of repeating accent colors across buttons, links, and selections, which trains users to ignore color as semantically meaningful.
whatToSteal: Zero-padding metric-label stacks: place the contextual label immediately beneath the number with no vertical gap, letting typeface size difference (3× ratio minimum) create the hierarchy instead of whitespace—eliminates the association delay caused by spatial separation

Chromatic reservation for data: assign your accent color exclusively to visualization elements, never to UI chrome or controls—preserves the perceptual link between 'colored thing' and 'thing representing information', making graphs decode faster

Asymmetric icon-gap ratios: use vertical spacing 2-2.5× larger than icon dimensions in navigation rails—the exaggerated rhythm lets peripheral vision track position without requiring foveal focus on each glyph

Background-foreground luminance jumps: use 5:1+ contrast ratios between canvas and content cards to create containment through luminance rather than borders—the optical 'pop' makes cards feel layered without requiring stroke rendering or shadow blur
antiPatterns: Typographically identical filter states: the '24h' and '7d' options show no visual distinction between active and inactive states—users must rely on memory or trial-and-error clicking rather than scanning to determine current selection, increasing cognitive load on every return visit to the interface

entries.title: SLMobbin

steal: Tan/warm neutral backgrounds for modal overlays that need to feel like physical …
Tone-on-tone input borders (darker value of the same hue family) when you want f…

entries.critique: The tan modal background (#9c8267) creates warmth against typical dashboard grays, producing a tactile quality that separates the overlay from workspace chrome—this rejects sterile white modals that blend into application backgrounds, making the form feel like a distinct object users handle rather than a system dialog. The borderless close 'X' with no hit-state container forces precision but eliminates visual noise, trading safety margins for spatial economy—conventional circled close buttons add 24-32px of visual weight this design reclaims. Full-width button geometry converts the entire bottom edge into activation area, establishing a definitive endpoint that pulls the eye downward through the form—this abandons centered or right-aligned CTAs that create ambiguous negative space users must interpret.
whatToSteal: Tan/warm neutral backgrounds for modal overlays that need to feel like physical cards lifted from the interface rather than system dialogs—works when the base application uses cool grays or whites

Tone-on-tone input borders (darker value of the same hue family) when you want field containers to exist without shouting—requires careful contrast management but removes the 'form grid' aesthetic of hard borders
Full-width primary actions in constrained vertical layouts to create a bottom anchor that resolves visual tension—eliminates the need for users to hunt for the commit button
Uncontained close icons (just the glyph, no background shape) in layouts where every pixel of chrome competes with content—only works if hit targets remain above 40px despite visual minimalism
antiPatterns: Light gray labels (#bcc4d4) on tan backgrounds fail WCAG contrast requirements, forcing users with any visual impairment to strain—this appears to prioritize aesthetic restraint over legibility, a choice that excludes users rather than serving them

entries.title: BU
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The search field's cyan monochrome treatment (#8cf4ec for both border and placeholder text) creates a visual anchor that doubles as wayfindi…
steal: Accent-color search placeholder (#8cf4ec text matching border) — makes the input…
Hue-family state toggling (#46311c to #b18e59 for nav selection) — maintains pal…
avoid: The label-free icon navigation on the left relies entirely on symbol recognition…
Using color value alone (#b18e59 vs #46311c) to signal navigation state creates …
entries.critique: The search field's cyan monochrome treatment (#8cf4ec for both border and placeholder text) creates a visual anchor that doubles as wayfinding — the DECISION is using the accent color as the sole chromatic element in the top bar, the EFFECT is that search becomes the primary suggested action without requiring larger size or button styling, and this REJECTS the standard gray placeholder convention which would hide the field until moused over. The navigation state signaling through #46311c versus #b18e59 — the DECISION is a 50-unit lightness shift in the brown family for active/inactive states, the EFFECT is maintaining color temperature consistency while differentiating selection, and this REJECTS the typical blue highlight or background fill which would introduce a new hue family and break the earth-tone palette. The white-bordered avatar containers in #88bdb8 — the DECISION is layering a stroke over a desaturated teal fill, the EFFECT is creating depth through edge definition rather than shadow or scale, and this REJECTS the flat circle or gradient-ring approaches that rely on dimension tricks rather than clean geometry.
whatToSteal: Accent-color search placeholder (#8cf4ec text matching border) — makes the input field discoverable without enlarging it or adding icons, useful when search isn't the primary action but needs visual priority over surrounding chrome

Hue-family state toggling (#46311c to #b18e59 for nav selection) — maintains palette cohesion while signaling interaction, applicable when your design already commits to a restricted color set and can't afford blue highlights

Stroke-defined avatar containers (white border on #88bdb8 fill) — creates containment through edge contrast instead of shadow depth, works when your background is already light and drop shadows would create visual noise

Icon-only left rail navigation with zero labels — commits fully to recognition over recall, only viable when your icon set is industry-standard (home, settings, users) and your user base skews expert rather than occasional
antiPatterns: The label-free icon navigation on the left relies entirely on symbol recognition without text fallbacks — this fails for any non-standard functions or users unfamiliar with the specific icon language, creating a memorization tax that compounds with each new feature added to the sidebar

Using color value alone (#b18e59 vs #46311c) to signal navigation state creates an accessibility failure for colorblind users and low-contrast displays — there's no underline, background fill, or weight change to provide redundant encoding of the selected state

entries.title: Aboard
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The DECISION to place #456bee blue on the message bubble background rather than interactive elements (the 'Get started' link uses underlined…
steal: Reserve the primary brand color for content provenance rather than interaction—a…
Push icon contrast to 14:1 or higher in persistent navigation rails—WCAG minimum…

entries.critique: The DECISION to place #456bee blue on the message bubble background rather than interactive elements (the 'Get started' link uses underlined black text) creates an EFFECT where brand color signals information source rather than clickability, training users to associate blue with system-authored content. This REJECTS the convention of reserving primary brand colors for CTAs, which would compete visually with the conversational flow and make every blue element feel like a demand for action. The DECISION to set icons at ~14:1 contrast (#302e2d on #fcecd4) in the rail navigation produces an EFFECT of immediate recognizability without requiring hover states or labels, making the six-icon set function as a persistent orientation layer. This REJECTS the 4.5:1 minimum WCAG standard as insufficient for glanceable navigation, acknowledging that icons already carry higher cognitive load than text. The DECISION to stroke the message input field while leaving card containers stroke-free creates an EFFECT of invitation, where the single bordered element on screen becomes the natural focus target without requiring placeholder text or explicit instruction. This REJECTS the approach of uniform border treatment across containers, which would flatten the functional hierarchy between passive content and active input zones.
whatToSteal: Reserve the primary brand color for content provenance rather than interaction—apply it to message bubbles, info cards, or system-generated content so users learn to associate the color with 'this came from us' rather than 'click this now', reducing false affordances in content-heavy interfaces

Push icon contrast to 14:1 or higher in persistent navigation rails—WCAG minimums assume text legibility, but icons require instant pattern recognition from peripheral vision, and the additional contrast eliminates the need for labels or hover states

Apply borders to exactly one container type—the input field or form area—while leaving all passive content borderless, so the stroke itself becomes the affordance that says 'you can type here' without placeholder text or icons

Position sender avatars 8-10px outside the message bubble's left edge rather than aligning them, creating a hanging indent that visually anchors the chat thread while keeping avatars grouped with their messages rather than floating in a separate column
antiPatterns: The message input field sits at the canvas bottom with no visible character count, send button, or formatting controls, creating ambiguity about whether this is a single-line quick reply or a full composition area—users accustomed to expanded composers may hesitate to start typing without seeing the full affordance set first

entries.critiqueExcerpt: The beige canvas (#d9d0b8) creates spatial separation without using drop shadows or borders—DECISION: using a mid-value neutral as the prima…

Fill the entire navigation item bounds with highlight color when active (here #f…

No visual differentiation exists between the navigation items except for the act…
entries.critique: The beige canvas (#d9d0b8) creates spatial separation without using drop shadows or borders—DECISION: using a mid-value neutral as the primary canvas instead of white; EFFECT: content surfaces appear raised through color contrast alone, allowing the white cards to feel dimensional without requiring shadow rendering; REJECTION: avoids the standard white-on-light-gray pattern that demands subtle shadows to distinguish layers. The yellow navigation highlight (#fcec7c) occupies the full item bounds including vertical padding—DECISION: extending background color to fill the entire clickable area rather than underlining or adding a left border; EFFECT: transforms the entire row into an obvious interaction target while maintaining the lateral rhythm; REJECTION: sidesteps accent bars or underlines that fragment the vertical scanning pattern. The 'Add a person' control uses the same ink color (#64534b) as surrounding text—DECISION: rendering the addition affordance in body text color rather than the accent color; EFFECT: treats adding members as a natural continuation of the list rather than a promoted action; REJECTION: refuses the bright-button pattern that would visually separate creation from reading.
whatToSteal: Use a warm neutral canvas color (here #d9d0b8) as the application background—surfaces in pure white then gain depth through hue contrast rather than requiring shadow or border effects, reducing visual noise while maintaining clear layering

Fill the entire navigation item bounds with highlight color when active (here #fcec7c behind 'My Team')—the user perceives the full row as the interaction zone, making the target area legible without needing additional indicators like icons or borders

Embed micro-visualizations (the stacked bar charts) directly in list items without labels or axes—when repeated across multiple cards, the pattern becomes readable through repetition, allowing data density without instructional overhead

Treat primary actions ('+Add a person') with the same visual weight as content—when creation is rendered as just another list item rather than a promoted button, it suggests adding is as common and casual as viewing
antiPatterns: The miniature bar charts in team member cards lack any labeling, axis values, or legend—users must infer meaning entirely from context and relative bar heights, which succeeds only if the data pattern is immediately obvious or users have been trained elsewhere in the application

No visual differentiation exists between the navigation items except for the active state—hovering or focusing on inactive items provides no preview of interactivity, leaving users to discover clickability through experimentation rather than affordance

entries.critique: The #d2f493 accent as a dot-only indicator (DECISION) creates navigation state through minimal surface area, making active items recognizable without chromatic dominance (EFFECT), avoiding full-height sidebar highlighting that would create color blocks competing with the canvas (REJECTION). The 4-column grid for team member avatars with fixed 16px gaps (DECISION) produces a scannable roster that fills the 320px detail rail width exactly, preventing orphaned partial columns (EFFECT), instead of a single-column list that would require excessive scrolling or variable-width flex wrapping that breaks rhythm (REJECTION). Username color matching the muted navigation state (#8ea4c4) (DECISION) subordinates the handle without hiding it, maintaining visual continuity with inactive UI elements (EFFECT), rejecting black text that would give the username equal prominence to the proper name or gray so light it implies disabled state (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use accent color exclusively for state indicators (dots, badges) rather than backgrounds — when #d2f493 appears only beside 'Searches' and 'Screens', it signals selection through position and contrast without needing to fill navigation items or create chromatic weight

Match secondary text color to inactive UI element states — setting '@imarysmith' in #8ea4c4 (the same blue as unselected nav icons) creates a visual language where muted = non-primary, making hierarchy readable through color roles rather than inventing new shades

Grid avatar dimensions to detail rail width with exact-division columns — 4 columns of 48px avatars with 16px gaps fills 320px precisely (48×4 + 16×3 = 240px of visible content), preventing layout collapse or awkward remainders when the pattern repeats

Separate brand identity capitalization from UI text conventions — 'SLMobbin' uses mixed case as a proper noun while all other labels follow sentence case, making the logo distinctive without forcing unusual capitalization patterns onto functional copy
antiPatterns: The all-white canvas and surface colors create zero depth distinction between the navigation column, main content area, and detail rail — users must rely entirely on content boundaries and whitespace to understand panel separation, increasing cognitive load when multiple panels show similar density of information

Navigation icon spacing (32px center-to-center) paired with small hit targets (approximately 24px icons) creates dead zones between items that feel neither tight enough for visual grouping nor generous enough to imply deliberate separation — the ratio suggests no intentional rhythm

entries.title: People
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The status badge uses DECISION: saturated orange fill (#ef8144) with white text positioned outside the table grid as a floating label. EFFEC…
steal: Position critical state indicators (Active/Inactive/Suspended) as floating badge…
Set table headers in a recessive color-weight combination (#a67f74 at light weig…
avoid: The photograph uses a circular crop positioned prominently in the header, consum…
entries.critique: The status badge uses DECISION: saturated orange fill (#ef8144) with white text positioned outside the table grid as a floating label. EFFECT: creates an immediate triage signal that bypasses the need to scan table content, functioning as a visual alarm for account state. REJECTION: avoids inline status columns that bury critical state information within uniform grid cells where it competes equally with less urgent data like email addresses. The column header weight uses DECISION: #a67f74 muted brown at lighter weight versus #4a453e black bold for data cells. EFFECT: makes headers functionally invisible during horizontal scanning, allowing the eye to track across 'John Doe → Senior Designer → john@' without interruption by structural labels. REJECTION: refuses equal-weight headers that force readers to cognitively filter label-versus-content on every row, slowing pattern recognition across records. The search button uses DECISION: external placement as a filled orange circle rather than embedded icon. EFFECT: creates a discrete hit target that doesn't shrink the input's active area, while the filled state signals executability rather than mere decoration. REJECTION: dismisses the inline-icon convention that makes users guess whether clicking the icon or pressing Enter triggers search, eliminating interaction ambiguity.
whatToSteal: Position critical state indicators (Active/Inactive/Suspended) as floating badges outside the table grid rather than as columns—lets users triage before reading, and prevents burying urgent signals in uniform cell spacing

Set table headers in a recessive color-weight combination (#a67f74 at light weight) so they become invisible during horizontal eye tracking, eliminating the cognitive cost of filtering labels from data on every row scan

Use 48px fixed row heights in data tables to create scannable rhythm—taller than minimal text requirements, preventing visual congestion while establishing a predictable vertical grid for rapid navigation

Make search execute buttons external filled shapes rather than inline icons, clarifying that they're clickable triggers rather than decorative field indicators and preserving maximum input width for text entry
antiPatterns: The photograph uses a circular crop positioned prominently in the header, consuming significant vertical real estate for decorative identification—creates emotional connection at the cost of information density, forcing critical account data (the entire table) below the fold on smaller viewports where a compact rectangular thumbnail would preserve more functional space

entries.title: Akrivia HCM
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The empty-state card floats above the canvas via a soft neumorphic shadow, guiding first-time users to the onboarding content while maintain…
steal: Limit the accent color to exactly one element on empty-state screens—the primary…
Tint the page canvas with a subtle non-white hue (here #98d8ef) behind the empty…
avoid: This design deliberately avoids hiding the tab navigation or replacing it with a…
It rejects the temptation to use the accent blue for decorative elements like th…
entries.critique: The empty-state card floats above the canvas via a soft neumorphic shadow, guiding first-time users to the onboarding content while maintaining visual quiet. By restricting the accent color #119bef to only the primary CTA button, the design gives returning users a single, instinctive focal point that accelerates task initiation after even a long absence. The tab navigation persists with its active state despite the emptiness beneath, allowing the user to build a mental map of the interface layout before any data appears; this orientation work pays off the moment content loads. An intentionally desaturated illustration (#a1c2d2) steps back perceptually so instructions land first, avoiding the common misstep where decorative graphics steal the reading order. Finally, the side nav’s stable width and presence from the first screen creates a reliable spatial frame—muscle memory for navigation begins in the empty state itself, not after data fills the canvas.
whatToSteal: Limit the accent color to exactly one element on empty-state screens—the primary action—so the next step is unmistakable. Use this when a single task must dominate; avoid it when multiple equally critical actions spawn confusion.

Tint the page canvas with a subtle non-white hue (here #98d8ef) behind the empty-state card to soften the psychological weight of an empty area, making the absence of data feel less stark for first-time users. This works best for enterprise tools where a pure white canvas can feel sterile or intimidating.

Keep navigation bars and tabs fully styled and visible during empty states, matching their eventual filled-state appearance, to support spatial learning from the very first visit. Do this for any interface where returning users need to navigate quickly; skip it only if the navigation will change drastically when content appears.

Mute illustration colors to a secondary or tertiary palette color so the graphic supports rather than competes with instructional text. This is especially useful for onboarding flows where reading comprehension is the priority.
antiPatterns: This design deliberately avoids hiding the tab navigation or replacing it with a full-page splash when content is absent. A lazy alternative would collapse the nav into a single 'get started' button, forcing users to re-learn the interface structure later—this design preserves spatial orientation from moment one.

It rejects the temptation to use the accent blue for decorative elements like the illustration or summary card borders. Diluting the accent across multiple areas would create a flat hierarchy with no clear focus element, slowing task completion for both new and returning users.

Avoids a pure white (#ffffff) canvas behind the empty state, which in many enterprise products creates a cold, alert-like feel that can increase anxiety around missing data. The light blue tint here keeps the tone supportive.

entries.title: SLMobbin!
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The italic chevron (›) in the cyan navigation block (#8df3ea background) signals expandable hierarchy through typographic convention instead…
steal: Numeral-first headline lockup: render step numbers at 3–4× the body text size as…
Single-item accent in summary lists: apply accent color to only the active subse…
avoid: The 'Dashboard' label appears identically in two different navigation contexts (…
entries.critique: The italic chevron (›) in the cyan navigation block (#8df3ea background) signals expandable hierarchy through typographic convention instead of iconography — the slant creates directional momentum at zero pixel cost, avoiding the visual noise of a rotated caret or plus glyph which would compete with the already saturated background. The right rail's selective blue highlighting (#17acf7 on 'About You' only) broadcasts current subsection within the larger step, rejecting the common pattern of highlighting the entire parent step which would leave users guessing their exact position within a multi-part form. The 1px bottom-border-only input treatment with #bdc7c7 (matching canvas) eliminates perimeter strokes, letting the label-to-value vertical stack breathe without rectangular containers that would create competing grid lines in a dense 20-field form.
whatToSteal: Numeral-first headline lockup: render step numbers at 3–4× the body text size as a prefix rather than inline or superscript — creates instant positional awareness in multi-step flows without requiring progress bars or additional UI chrome

Single-item accent in summary lists: apply accent color to only the active subsection name in a multi-item preview rail, leaving sibling items in default ink — eliminates ambiguity about where the user currently sits within nested progress structures

Italic chevron for hierarchy signaling: use the › character in italic weight instead of icon fonts or SVG carets when indicating expandable/nested navigation — the slant provides directional affordance using only OpenType features, maintaining visual weight consistency across zoom levels

Bottom-border-only inputs with canvas-colored strokes: replace full perimeter input borders with a single 1px bottom rule in the page background color rather than a contrasting stroke — reduces rectangular clutter in forms with 15+ consecutive fields while preserving tap-target definition
antiPatterns: The 'Dashboard' label appears identically in two different navigation contexts (sidebar list item + standalone button below 'Submit') with zero visual differentiation in size, weight, or color — this violates the principle of one meaning per signifier, forcing users to infer context purely from vertical position which fails when scrolling or at narrow viewports

entries.title: Aboard 16
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #10aaf8 plus icon breaks neutrality in a field of #b8b8b8 navigation controls (DECISION), drawing focal attention to the creation action…
steal: Color a single action control (#10aaf8 plus icon) while keeping peer controls ne…
Use a continuous 1px vertical rule to separate navigation from canvas rather tha…

entries.critique: The #10aaf8 plus icon breaks neutrality in a field of #b8b8b8 navigation controls (DECISION), drawing focal attention to the creation action before any context exists (EFFECT), rejecting the convention of muted CTAs in empty states where exploration precedes creation (REJECTION — premature emphasis on output when users need input first). The 21-item vertical icon list uses identical folder glyphs with no size, color, or spacing variation (DECISION), transforming navigation into a visual texture rather than a scannable hierarchy (EFFECT), abandoning the pattern of progressive disclosure or grouping that helps users locate position within deep structures (REJECTION — forcing full cognitive load upfront). The pale yellow geometric illustration occupies prime canvas real estate (DECISION), establishing decorative presence as the primary content (EFFECT), inverting the typical empty-state approach where instructional copy or sample data demonstrate the interface's purpose (REJECTION — ornament replaces orientation).
whatToSteal: Color a single action control (#10aaf8 plus icon) while keeping peer controls neutral (#b8b8b8) to create priority without adding UI weight — useful when one path needs behavioral priming but the chrome should stay recessive

Use a continuous 1px vertical rule to separate navigation from canvas rather than background color changes — preserves the white-on-white aesthetic while maintaining clear zone boundaries through linear definition

Deploy a large geometric shape in a muted accent color (#f6e18a) as the empty-state placeholder — communicates 'this space will hold content' without prescriptive imagery that might mislead about content types
antiPatterns: Repeating the identical folder icon 21 times without visual grouping, labels, or hierarchy markers turns navigation into wallpaper — users cannot distinguish between items or understand organizational structure, forcing them to read every label sequentially instead of using visual cues to jump to sections

entries.title: Asana
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 72px icon-only left rail (DECISION) sacrifices immediate label legibility for persistent spatial availability—every icon consumes identi…
steal: Icon-only fixed-narrow navigation at 72px width with 24px icons vertically stack…
4px colored top border as the sole card differentiation marker—assigns project i…

entries.critique: The 72px icon-only left rail (DECISION) sacrifices immediate label legibility for persistent spatial availability—every icon consumes identical vertical real estate regardless of label length, allowing dense stacking (EFFECT), rejecting the expandable/collapsible sidebar convention that requires animation states and causes layout reflow (REJECTION). The 4px colored top border on project cards (DECISION) assigns visual identity through hue alone without background fills, keeping card interiors identical white (EFFECT), avoiding the category-colored-background pattern that reduces text contrast and consumes attention even for inactive projects (REJECTION). The asymmetric 64px top margin above 'Good afternoon, Kat' (DECISION) creates breathing room that directs focus downward into the dashboard grid rather than upward toward navigation (EFFECT), discarding the center-vertical-alignment approach common in empty states that would misrepresent the density of content below (REJECTION). The #1facf0 underlined 'My tasks' link within body text (DECISION) uses browser-default link styling to signal interactivity through convention rather than custom button chrome (EFFECT), rejecting styled pill buttons or chips that would add visual weight and disrupt the sentence's readability as prose (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Icon-only fixed-narrow navigation at 72px width with 24px icons vertically stacked—preserves maximum canvas width while maintaining tap-target accessibility, eliminating label translation issues across languages and the expandable-drawer interaction tax

4px colored top border as the sole card differentiation marker—assigns project identity through edge treatment rather than background fill, keeping interiors uniform white for consistent text contrast regardless of brand color luminance

Three-tier text hierarchy using only size (#424242 at 32px, 18px, 14px) and a single muted state (#94bcc4)—removes the temptation to bold or uppercase headers, forcing vertical rhythm and whitespace to carry structural meaning

Asymmetric top margin (64px) above greeting text—pushes content downward into view rather than centering vertically, preventing the false impression of an empty state when dashboard cards exist below the fold
antiPatterns: The icon navigation provides zero textual labels or tooltips in the default state—users must either memorize 7+ abstract icons or hover each one repeatedly, creating a recognition-over-recall burden that violates the benefit of having persistent navigation in the first place, especially problematic for infrequent users or those with cognitive load constraints

entries.title: Home
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The italic 'Report' label in #025d82 creates a recessive posture within the navigation list, signaling a meta-action (generating output) rat…
steal: Reserve italic posture exclusively for secondary actions embedded in primary nav…
Use unpadded single-pixel vertical rules in muted palette colors as hard column …
avoid: The three status dots (green, yellow, red) float without explanatory labels or h…
entries.critique: The italic 'Report' label in #025d82 creates a recessive posture within the navigation list, signaling a meta-action (generating output) rather than a destination. This rejects the common practice of treating all navigation items as equal weight siblings, allowing users to distinguish between places-to-go and things-to-do without adding icons or explicit visual containers. The unpadded 1px #97bdca vertical rule at the column boundary enforces a hard content edge, producing a database-like segregation where the eye must fully commit to one region before engaging the other. This avoids the blended whitespace approach where columns feel negotiable or responsive, instead creating fixed territorial claims appropriate for tool-heavy dashboards. The status dots floating right of 'Reports' with no grid snap create a metrics-at-a-glance layer that works through positional tension rather than structured alignment, rejecting the typical badge-in-container pattern that would add visual weight and suggest interactivity where none exists.
whatToSteal: Reserve italic posture exclusively for secondary actions embedded in primary navigation lists—the recessive slant communicates 'different kind of click' without introducing new colors or icon systems, keeping the information architecture flat while signaling behavioral boundaries

Use unpadded single-pixel vertical rules in muted palette colors as hard column separators when the interface intentionally segregates tool regions from content regions—the zero-padding approach rejects negotiable layouts and forces users to treat each side as a committed context switch

Float status indicators to the right of parent navigation items without grid alignment when the metrics are glanceable metadata rather than interaction targets—the loose positioning creates a separate visual layer that doesn't compete with the clickable label's alignment system

Apply all-caps tracking to canvas headers while keeping navigation items sentence-case to establish a two-tier architecture without weight changes—the case contrast alone separates 'where am I' from 'where can I go' without needing size jumps or color shifts
antiPatterns: The three status dots (green, yellow, red) float without explanatory labels or hover affordances, assuming users already know what green/yellow/red quantify in this specific context. If Reports tracks three different metric types, the color-only encoding forces users to either memorize an arbitrary mapping or ignore the indicators entirely, making the dashboard less discoverable for infrequent users who lack the institutional knowledge to decode the dot semantics.

entries.title: 1-on-1
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The DECISION to render the 'Recurring' metadata as blue italic text rather than a tag creates an EFFECT where scheduling information reads a…
steal: Rendering recurring event metadata as italic blue inline text rather than pills …
Using tan (#e0dcc2) as the surface color for sidebar cards against white canvas …
avoid: The bright blue accent (#08aafb) appears on both the 'Recurring' metadata link a…
entries.critique: The DECISION to render the 'Recurring' metadata as blue italic text rather than a tag creates an EFFECT where scheduling information reads as inline editorial content, making the note feel like authored prose rather than database records — this REJECTS the convention of discrete pill-shaped tags that visually interrupt reading flow and treat metadata as distinct UI objects. The DECISION to use lowercase, conversational placeholder text ('Type a note, or drag a file here') produces an EFFECT of collapsing the distinction between system instruction and user content, making the empty state feel like a writing prompt rather than technical guidance — this REJECTS imperative title-case directives ('Enter Note Content') that establish authoritative system-user hierarchy. The DECISION to present 'Delete note' and 'Move note to...' as simple underlined text links delivers an EFFECT where destructive actions carry equal visual weight to benign ones, relying on reading comprehension rather than color-coded danger signals — this REJECTS the pattern of red warning buttons that pre-interpret risk for users and add visual noise to every action menu.
whatToSteal: Rendering recurring event metadata as italic blue inline text rather than pills or badges — treats scheduling attributes as editorial annotation within the content flow, not as UI furniture bolted onto records

Using tan (#e0dcc2) as the surface color for sidebar cards against white canvas — creates tactile depth through warm/cool contrast rather than relying on shadow intensity alone to establish layering hierarchy

Placing the primary action button ('+') in accent blue while keeping navigation icons in muted blue-gray (#6c94a4) — reserves saturated color for creative actions while letting wayfinding recede, making the color hierarchy reflect user intent rather than just importance

Writing placeholder text in lowercase conversational voice ('Type a note, or drag a file here') — transforms empty states from instructional voids into writing prompts that match the casual register of actual note content
antiPatterns: The bright blue accent (#08aafb) appears on both the 'Recurring' metadata link and the '+' creation button with identical saturation levels — dilutes the signal value of the accent color by applying it to both passive informational links and primary creative actions, making it unclear whether blue means 'clickable detail' or 'start something new'

entries.title: Antler
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The active navigation state combines a saturated green dot indicator with a desaturated pill background (#bcfcb4), creating redundant signal…
steal: Overlapping avatar stacking at 75% visibility — partial occlusion communicates '…
Muted gray (#BCBCBC) on table headers instead of bold black — pushes structural …
avoid: The green dot on 'Deal flow' adds a third indicator (dot + background + text wei…
entries.critique: The active navigation state combines a saturated green dot indicator with a desaturated pill background (#bcfcb4), creating redundant signaling where the dot alone would suffice — EFFECT: the pill dilutes the dot's precision by adding 40px+ of background noise — REJECTION: abandons the common single-indicator pattern (left border or background alone) which would maintain the density this data-heavy interface requires. The overlapping investor avatars use fixed offset stacking at roughly 75% visibility per avatar — EFFECT: communicates multiplicity while consuming minimal horizontal space in a constrained table cell — REJECTION: avoids the conventional full-circle spacing that would either truncate the list or force the column to be 3x wider. The cyan accent (#0CB6FB) appears only on user-initiated actions (buttons, configurators, menus) while status indicators use the green palette — EFFECT: creates a functional color grammar where cyan means 'you can do this' and green means 'this is where you are' — REJECTION: bypasses the single-accent-for-everything approach that would conflate navigation state with interaction affordance.
whatToSteal: Overlapping avatar stacking at 75% visibility — partial occlusion communicates 'there are N people' without requiring tooltip counts or expanding the column width beyond what the names themselves need

Muted gray (#BCBCBC) on table headers instead of bold black — pushes structural labels into the background so scanning behavior focuses on cell data, reducing cognitive load when hunting for specific company or founder names

Functional color separation between state (green palette) and action (cyan #0CB6FB) — prevents users from clicking non-interactive status indicators by reserving the action color exclusively for clickable elements

Italic text as a status modifier on 'Analytics' — communicates disabled or pending state without requiring a separate visual treatment like opacity reduction or strikethrough, keeping the navigation rhythm consistent
antiPatterns: The green dot on 'Deal flow' adds a third indicator (dot + background + text weight) for a binary state that only needs one, creating visual clutter in a nav system that claims to prioritize data density — either the pill or the dot should be removed

entries.title: Aboard 17
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The full-bleed yellow banner (#fcf4b4) with zero padding creates urgency through compression—the text touches the color field's edges with r…
steal: Zero-padding alert banners that let background color extend edge-to-edge while k…
Unstyled native form controls in technical/transactional interfaces—eliminates 4…
