Radio button labels lack any hover state or expanded click target beyond the tin…
entries.critique: The full-bleed yellow banner (#fcf4b4) with zero padding creates urgency through compression—the text touches the color field's edges with roughly 4px clearance, making the message feel temporally bounded rather than permanently architectural. This rejects the conventional 16-24px padded alert that reads as structural chrome, treating warnings as dismissible decoration instead of interruptive states. The unstyled radio buttons preserve browser-native interaction semantics—15-16px click targets with system-standard focus rings—which eliminates the cross-browser testing burden of custom form controls while maintaining WCAG compliance by default. This deliberately avoids the styled-checkbox pattern where designers replace native controls with div+SVG assemblies that require separate focus, hover, and checked state implementations, often breaking keyboard navigation or screen reader announcements. The 1px horizontal rule under 'Your order summary' (#c4bc84, a muted tone from the palette) defines sectional boundaries through color differentiation rather than weight—it's the hue shift from ink to muted that creates the visual break, not thickness. This rejects the 2-3px bold divider convention that competes for attention with actual content, treating structure as a whispered suggestion rather than a shouted partition.
whatToSteal: Zero-padding alert banners that let background color extend edge-to-edge while keeping text within reading margins—creates temporal urgency versus the 16px-padded 'system message' treatment that feels permanent and ignorable

Unstyled native form controls in technical/transactional interfaces—eliminates 40+ lines of custom CSS for checked/focus/hover states while guaranteeing accessibility compliance and familiar interaction patterns across devices

1px sectional dividers in muted palette tones rather than neutral grays—the color shift from ink (#444444) to muted (#c4bc84) performs the separating work, making structure feel integrated with the palette instead of overlaid as monochrome infrastructure

Label-to-control spacing (12px) that exceeds inter-item spacing within the control group (8px implied)—visually associates the label with its fieldset through proximity math, replacing the need for explicit fieldset borders or background fills
antiPatterns: The summary strip buries interactive elements (blue hyperlinks for 'Details' and 'Change') within paragraph-style prose, forcing users to parse sentence structure to locate click targets—a vertically-stacked action list or right-aligned link cluster would surface affordances without requiring reading comprehension

Radio button labels lack any hover state or expanded click target beyond the tiny native circle—the text 'Marmalade' is visually grouped but functionally inert, requiring precise 16px circle targeting instead of allowing the full label row to register clicks

entries.title: Jira
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation uses state-dependent fill inversion: selected items receive full #0f9cf1 background floods with white text, while unselected …
steal: State indication through complete fill inversion (full background flood vs. tran…
Uppercase + letterspacing for group headers instead of size increase—maintains v…
avoid: The 'Starred' label creates a filtering expectation that isn't met—it appears as…
entries.critique: The navigation uses state-dependent fill inversion: selected items receive full #0f9cf1 background floods with white text, while unselected items show only the icon in accent color against white (DECISION). This creates unambiguous location awareness through maximum contrast differential, not subtle border weight changes (EFFECT). It rejects the hover-only feedback pattern where selected and unselected states look nearly identical until mouse interaction, which fails keyboard users and creates spatial disorientation in deep navigation trees (REJECTION). The numeric badge merges count and visual weight into a single 16px circle, rendering both the quantity and its importance through geometric presence (DECISION). This lets peripheral vision register 'there are items here' before conscious reading occurs, accelerating triage decisions (EFFECT). It avoids the text-only count suffix approach ('Your work (5)') which requires full parse of every navigation label to detect activity, slowing repeated scanning patterns (REJECTION). Project card metadata uses weight reduction plus color shift to #bdbdbd simultaneously, compounding hierarchy signals (DECISION). This allows dense information packing—avatar, title, type, and contributor count in under 60px height—while maintaining parse speed through redundant encoding (EFFECT). It rejects size-only hierarchy where secondary text shrinks to 10-11px, forcing squinting and reducing accessibility, especially when metadata carries functionally important disambiguation between similar project names (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: State indication through complete fill inversion (full background flood vs. transparent) rather than border or underline changes—creates binary clarity that reads instantly in peripheral vision during rapid navigation scanning
Uppercase + letterspacing for group headers instead of size increase—maintains vertical rhythm in constrained sidebars while preserving scannable breaks between navigation sections
Concurrent weight and color shifts for metadata hierarchy (400-weight + #bdbdbd simultaneously)—allows information density without size reduction below 13px, keeping secondary content accessible
Circular numeric badges at icon-scale (16px diameter) positioned inline with labels—broadcasts presence of counted items to peripheral attention before text parsing begins
antiPatterns: The 'Starred' label creates a filtering expectation that isn't met—it appears as a section header but functions as a manual collection, meaning items don't auto-populate based on starring actions elsewhere in the product, creating a mental model mismatch between the star iconography's typical meaning (dynamic filter) and its actual behavior (static folder)

entries.title: Aboard 18
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The cyan selection pill (#97ddf7) fills the entire vertical block of the active nav item, creating a solid area beacon rather than an underl…
steal: Use a saturated, full-height background pill for active navigation states instea…
Render structural labels (field types, metadata) in a muted accent color rather …
avoid: Field type labels ("Short answer", "Long answer") appear in the same visual weig…
entries.critique: The cyan selection pill (#97ddf7) fills the entire vertical block of the active nav item, creating a solid area beacon rather than an underline or left border—this produces instant location recognition in peripheral vision without requiring the user to parse text, rejecting accent strokes that demand focused reading to confirm position. Navigation labels in dark maroon (#641c44) establish semantic permanence distinct from the transient cyan state layer, avoiding neutral grays that would falsely suggest these controls are contextually disabled. The exposed white gutters between form fields build separation through canvas color rather than ruled lines, letting each field breathe as a discrete object without introducing visual noise from borders that would compete with the input data itself.
whatToSteal: Use a saturated, full-height background pill for active navigation states instead of underlines—creates a shape-based beacon legible in peripheral vision without requiring text reading
Render structural labels (field types, metadata) in a muted accent color rather than disabled gray—establishes them as permanent descriptors instead of inactive controls awaiting enablement
Separate form containers with exposed canvas gaps rather than borders or dividers—builds hierarchy through negative space that doesn't add competing graphical elements to the data entry zone

Position destructive or additive actions ("Add field") flush-right in toolbars while keeping primary controls left-aligned—uses spatial isolation to prevent accidental activation of state-changing operations
antiPatterns: Field type labels ("Short answer", "Long answer") appear in the same visual weight and position as actual input data would occupy, creating ambiguity about whether these are placeholder hints or persistent metadata—users must learn through interaction whether this text disappears on focus or remains visible during entry

entries.title: ABOARD
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The beige card surfaces (#cdbe9c) against white canvas (#ffffff) establish a 1.3:1 contrast that visually separates actionable containers fr…
steal: Use a 1.3:1 contrast ratio between card surface and canvas when shadows/borders …
Size directional glyphs (arrows, chevrons) at body-text scale and color when the…
avoid: The vertical icon-only sidebar uses no labels, relying entirely on icon recogniz…
entries.critique: The beige card surfaces (#cdbe9c) against white canvas (#ffffff) establish a 1.3:1 contrast that visually separates actionable containers from the background without requiring drop shadows or borders—rejecting the ubiquitous card-with-shadow pattern that adds visual weight and depth cues this interface doesn't need. The cyan navigation icons (#1383ec) at 9.2:1 contrast against their dark sidebar create immediate wayfinding without relying on labels or tooltips, rejecting the common practice of muted iconography that requires hover states to decode. The arrow glyph (→) staying at body-text size and ink color treats forward navigation as typographic punctuation rather than a clickable target, rejecting the convention of visually prominent CTAs that would fracture attention across multiple competing signals on a dashboard meant for sequential onboarding.
whatToSteal: Use a 1.3:1 contrast ratio between card surface and canvas when shadows/borders are absent—it's sufficient for spatial grouping while maintaining the warmth of a limited palette, and it avoids the harsh separation that full-contrast or shadowed cards impose on tutorial content

Size directional glyphs (arrows, chevrons) at body-text scale and color when the entire card is clickable—this keeps them subordinate to the heading and prevents the visual fragmentation that occurs when every card has a bright, large CTA competing for attention

Apply high-contrast accent color (#1383ec at 9.2:1) exclusively to navigation elements in a fixed sidebar, reserving the rest of the interface for a narrow warm palette—this creates a single, unambiguous locus of wayfinding without scattering blue UI chrome throughout the workspace

Establish type hierarchy through size ratio alone (2:1 heading-to-body) when working within a single typeface—weight or family changes aren't necessary if the scale jump is decisive enough, and it reduces the typographic palette to one variable
antiPatterns: The vertical icon-only sidebar uses no labels, relying entirely on icon recognizability—this works only if the icon set is either universal (home, settings) or the user base is trained; for domain-specific functions, unlabeled icons create a guessing game that wastes cognitive load on decoding rather than task completion

entries.title: Blui
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The circular icon containers in the sidebar create a 1:1 aspect ratio constraint (DECISION), forcing each icon to occupy identical vertical …
steal: Circular icon containers with consistent diameter and soft shadow — creates pred…
Ink inversion for selected calendar dates (white text on accent fill vs. accent …
avoid: The search input field lacks a visible boundary or background differentiation fr…
entries.critique: The circular icon containers in the sidebar create a 1:1 aspect ratio constraint (DECISION), forcing each icon to occupy identical vertical and horizontal territory, which prevents visual clutter from asymmetric glyphs (EFFECT), rejecting the common pattern of naked icons that create ragged optical boundaries when mixing wide and tall symbols (REJECTION). The calendar's selected date uses ink inversion — white on blue rather than blue on white (DECISION) — providing a focal anchor that doesn't require additional chrome like rings or outlines (EFFECT), avoiding the weak affordance of border-only selection states that compete poorly with the grid's natural density (REJECTION). Appointment cards position the time stamp at the leading edge in a heavier weight (DECISION), establishing a scannable left-aligned vertical rhythm down the timeline (EFFECT), rather than centering metadata or using caps-based hierarchy that sacrifices scannability for symmetry (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Circular icon containers with consistent diameter and soft shadow — creates predictable touch targets and visual rhythm without requiring complex alignment logic, especially useful when icon glyphs have inconsistent natural bounds

Ink inversion for selected calendar dates (white text on accent fill vs. accent text on white) — provides unambiguous selection state without adding border weight that would compete with the grid structure
Vertical icon spacing that matches the icon container diameter — produces a 1:1 rhythm that feels stable and deliberate, preventing the sidebar from appearing cramped or arbitrarily spaced

Compressed or condensed typeface for day-of-week abbreviations — recovers horizontal space in narrow columns while preserving readability, avoiding the need for single-letter abbreviations that lose clarity
antiPatterns: The search input field lacks a visible boundary or background differentiation from the canvas, relying solely on the magnifying glass icon to signal interactivity — users scanning for input affordances may skip over it entirely since it presents no chromatic or tonal separation from surrounding whitespace

entries.title: Sitemob
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The butter-yellow banner (#fcf4c2) serves as a non-modal, persistent promotional space that contrasts with white canvas without using high-s…
steal: Use a low-saturation accent background (#fcf4c2 butter-yellow) for persistent pr…
Position add-actions as small inline icons (16px outlined circles with '+') flus…
avoid: The right rail file list shows no visual differentiation between file types or s…
The timestamp styling (#a4c8d0 muted color) relies solely on color to convey sec…
entries.critique: The butter-yellow banner (#fcf4c2) serves as a non-modal, persistent promotional space that contrasts with white canvas without using high-saturation warning colors — rejecting the common pattern of blue info banners that compete visually with primary actions. The right rail's inline '+' icons (16px outlined circles) position add-actions at the point of context rather than requiring a global menu or toolbar, rejecting the centralized-action pattern that forces users to mentally map actions back to targets. The 'Create New' button isolation in top-right creates a dedicated initiation zone with breathing room (appears to have 24-32px margins), rejecting the toolbar-clustering pattern where primary CTAs compete with secondary utilities. The vertical label-above-input layout sacrifices horizontal density to eliminate the truncation and alignment problems that plague side-by-side label-input pairs, particularly visible in fields like 'Business Name' where the label is 50% longer than 'Website'.
whatToSteal: Use a low-saturation accent background (#fcf4c2 butter-yellow) for persistent promotional banners instead of saturated info-blue — creates visual separation from primary content while avoiding the alert fatigue that bright blue or yellow warnings trigger

Position add-actions as small inline icons (16px outlined circles with '+') flush-right within each list row rather than in a global toolbar — reduces the cognitive load of action-to-target mapping and keeps interaction distance minimal

Isolate primary creation CTAs in top-right with generous surrounding whitespace (24-32px margins) — prevents visual competition with content-level actions and establishes a predictable initiation point users can return to without scanning

Stack form labels directly above inputs in vertical pairs instead of horizontal side-by-side alignment — prevents label truncation, eliminates alignment complexity for varying label lengths, and allows full-width utilization of container space
antiPatterns: The right rail file list shows no visual differentiation between file types or states beyond the filename text itself — users must read and parse each filename string to understand what they're looking at, rejecting iconography or type indicators that would allow scanning by shape and color

The timestamp styling (#a4c8d0 muted color) relies solely on color to convey secondary information hierarchy without size or weight variation — creates accessibility risk for color-impaired users and reduces scanability when multiple metadata types appear in the same context

entries.title: Atlassian
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 1:0 row alternation (header background present, data rows uniform white) rejects the conventional zebra stripe because it treats the tab…
steal: Use uppercase micro-labels in a muted hue (here #6a9f67 at 11px) for column head…
Apply table header background color only to the header row, leaving data rows un…
avoid: The avatar circles use a dark fill requiring white text, creating a contrast dep…
entries.critique: The 1:0 row alternation (header background present, data rows uniform white) rejects the conventional zebra stripe because it treats the table as a scanning surface rather than a counting instrument—users track horizontally within a row using the left-anchored avatar and key, not vertically across row boundaries. The uppercase column headers in #6a9f67 create a perceptual weight inversion: they're visually quieter than the data they label, allowing the eye to skip directly to content while still maintaining scannable column structure, rejecting the bold-header convention that competes with data for attention. The selective color application (blue links only on issue keys, green only on headers, sage only on header row) creates a three-tier information architecture where each hue owns one functional layer, avoiding the conventional monochrome-plus-accent approach that forces all interactive elements to share a single color channel.
whatToSteal: Use uppercase micro-labels in a muted hue (here #6a9f67 at 11px) for column headers instead of bold—it lets data dominate the visual field while preserving scannability, particularly effective when data cells contain mixed formatting (links, text, avatars)

Apply table header background color only to the header row, leaving data rows un-striped when each row contains strong left-aligned anchors (avatars, ID codes)—reduces cognitive load in high-frequency scanning scenarios where users re-enter the same row multiple times

Assign one dedicated hue to each functional layer in a data view (here: blue for navigation, green for metadata, sage for structure)—creates a self-documenting color grammar where users learn what each color does rather than parsing visual weight alone

Place the single primary action button in accent fill while leaving secondary actions as text-only in the same toolbar—creates a 10:1 contrast ratio in chromatic intensity that guides task initiation without requiring positional or size emphasis
antiPatterns: The avatar circles use a dark fill requiring white text, creating a contrast dependency that fails when users have dark-mode expectations or when the same avatar pattern needs to appear on non-white backgrounds—a background-aware avatar system using borders or tinted fills would preserve the identification function across surface colors

entries.title: Jira
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #2299e7 vertical accent bar positioned at the navigation item's left edge (DECISION) creates state awareness without disrupting the labe…
steal: 2px vertical accent bar at the navigation item's left edge in #2299e7: communica…
40px uniform clickable zones measured baseline-to-baseline: establishes predicta…
avoid: The surface color #efdb8d (pale yellow) used as the left navigation background c…
entries.critique: The #2299e7 vertical accent bar positioned at the navigation item's left edge (DECISION) creates state awareness without disrupting the label's left-alignment or requiring the entire row to change color (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional full-row highlight that forces labels to indent or shift horizontally when selected, destabilizing the vertical scan line users establish (REJECTION). The uniform 40px vertical rhythm across all navigation targets (DECISION) guarantees consistent motor memory and reduces mis-clicks in a high-frequency interaction zone (EFFECT), avoiding the temptation to compress spacing to show more items, which empirically increases error rates when users develop muscle memory for specific vertical positions (REJECTION). The all-caps treatment of 'RECENT' at reduced size (DECISION) signals non-interactive category metadata through typographic convention rather than spatial separation (EFFECT), rejecting the common pattern of using horizontal rules or background color blocks to create section boundaries, which add visual weight to what should be the least important element in the hierarchy (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: 2px vertical accent bar at the navigation item's left edge in #2299e7: communicates selection state without forcing label text to shift or re-align, preserving the vertical scan line users rely on when moving between items rapidly

40px uniform clickable zones measured baseline-to-baseline: establishes predictable motor targets in high-frequency navigation, preventing the spacing variance that breaks muscle memory when users navigate by spatial memory rather than reading labels

All-caps categorical labels ('RECENT') rendered at 11px with reduced opacity creating #5490a4 from base ink #3f3d38: signals metadata without requiring horizontal rules, background fills, or additional spacing, keeping section markers visually subordinate to the actionable items they categorize

Zero-margin adjacency between the 16px circular avatar and the first letter of 'Tobias Funke': creates a cohesive name-identity unit that reads as a single interactive target rather than two separate elements, appropriate when the avatar serves purely as visual reinforcement rather than an independent control
antiPatterns: The surface color #efdb8d (pale yellow) used as the left navigation background creates insufficient contrast with canvas #cfbab4, measuring approximately 1.2:1 luminance ratio — well below accessibility minimums and forcing reliance entirely on the #3f3d38 ink color for legibility, a fragility that fails users with reduced contrast sensitivity or those viewing under varied lighting conditions

entries.title: Akira
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The icon-only left navigation chooses 20×20px cyan glyphs without labels, forcing recognition through shape vocabulary alone; this decision …
steal: Uppercase micro-labels at 11px with 200-250% letter-spacing in muted color (#aca…
Single-color status system using opacity variation (15% fill, 100% text) from on…
avoid: The table rows lack any hover or selection state visual feedback: the uniform wh…
The metric cards ('Budget', 'New clients') display delta percentages (+12%, +2.4…
entries.critique: The icon-only left navigation chooses 20×20px cyan glyphs without labels, forcing recognition through shape vocabulary alone; this decision eliminates the 80-120px width tax that icon-plus-label patterns impose, allowing the three-column layout to allocate more horizontal space to data-dense regions while rejecting the false safety of redundant text that most dashboards use to avoid training users. The status pills apply the accent color at ~15% fill opacity with 100% opacity text, creating categorical distinction through tint variation rather than hue rotation; this produces seven visually separable states from a single base color while avoiding the semantic confusion of multi-hue systems where users must learn whether red means 'rejected' or 'urgent'. The right rail's percentage values right-align against client names with a fixed ~180px gap, establishing a consistent scan column for numeric comparison; this rejects the common flush-left number pattern that forces the eye to hunt horizontally across variable-width name strings, treating the number as the scannable entity and the name as contextual decoration.
whatToSteal: Uppercase micro-labels at 11px with 200-250% letter-spacing in muted color (#acacc4): they create structural boundaries that organize without competing, functioning as typographic rules that remain legible while visually receding below data hierarchy

Single-color status system using opacity variation (15% fill, 100% text) from one accent hue: generates 5-7 distinct categorical states without requiring users to decode a color-meaning map, and eliminates the design tax of choosing semantically appropriate hues for each state

Icon-only navigation with 20×20px glyphs at high contrast (cyan #09aafa on white): recovers 60-100px of horizontal canvas width while training users on shape recognition, appropriate when the icon set is constrained (6-8 items) and usage frequency is daily

Right-aligned numeric column in list layouts with fixed gap spacing (~180px): establishes a vertical scan line for comparison tasks, treating numbers as the primary scannable entity rather than forcing horizontal hunting across variable-width labels
antiPatterns: The table rows lack any hover or selection state visual feedback: the uniform white background provides no affordance that rows are interactive targets, forcing users to discover clickability through cursor changes or accidental activation rather than designing the invitation into the surface itself

The metric cards ('Budget', 'New clients') display delta percentages (+12%, +2.45%) without time context: '12% vs what baseline' is undefined, requiring users to either guess the comparison window (week/month/quarter) or maintain that context from elsewhere, rejecting the self-documenting pattern of inline temporal labels like 'vs last month'

entries.title: BLI
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The white icon rail (DECISION: #ffffff canvas for the navigation column) creates edge-to-edge chromatic separation from the #f8f8f8 surface …
steal: Encode binary state (complete/incomplete, active/inactive) through hue shift bet…
Use the canvas/surface lightness differential (here #ffffff vs #f8f8f8, an 8-uni…
avoid: The revenue chart bars extend to implied gridlines that aren't drawn — users mus…
The Projects section header '+' symbol sits at the same visual weight (color #24…
entries.critique: The white icon rail (DECISION: #ffffff canvas for the navigation column) creates edge-to-edge chromatic separation from the #f8f8f8 surface of the main canvas (EFFECT: the 8-unit lightness gap registers as a hard boundary even without a drawn border, making the navigation read as foreground furniture rather than page background) (REJECTION: conventional dashboards float nav on the same surface as content, relying on divider lines that add visual noise and require maintenance across breakpoints). The dual-hue bar encoding (DECISION: #0f7cea for complete tasks, #86d1f7 for incomplete, visible in the Projects chart) maps completion state to wavelength rather than transparency (EFFECT: the distinction survives both monochrome conversion and colorblind simulation because the two blues sit 40 units apart in lightness space) (REJECTION: standard practice dims incomplete bars with opacity, which collapses into ambiguity when layered over non-white backgrounds). The donut chart's interior label (DECISION: 'LAST 30 DAYS' placed in the structural void at chart center) repurposes negative space for temporal context (EFFECT: the reader gets time-range qualification without needing a separate header or legend entry, reducing total component count) (REJECTION: typical implementations leave the donut hole empty or fill it with a redundant percentage restatement, wasting the most scrutinized focal point). The uniform icon spacing (DECISION: 24px vertical rhythm applied to all navigation icons regardless of their internal complexity) creates predictable target zones (EFFECT: the flame, chart, and gear icons become equally hittable despite their different visual masses, and the rhythm establishes a 1-2-3 counting pattern that aids spatial memory) (REJECTION: dynamic spacing that adjusts to perceived icon weight feels organic but produces inconsistent interaction targets that punish muscle memory).
whatToSteal: Encode binary state (complete/incomplete, active/inactive) through hue shift between related colors rather than opacity change — the #0f7cea to #86d1f7 jump in the bar chart maintains distinction across rendering contexts where transparency fails, and the 40-unit lightness gap ensures accessibility compliance without additional passes

Use the canvas/surface lightness differential (here #ffffff vs #f8f8f8, an 8-unit gap) as a structural separator that eliminates drawn borders — this works because the eye registers even small lightness steps as depth cues when they span full-bleed regions, and you avoid the 1px hairline maintenance problem across zoom levels

Place temporal or contextual qualifiers in the geometric center of radial charts instead of external headers — the donut chart's 'LAST 30 DAYS' label claims the void that otherwise contributes nothing, cutting legend overhead while positioning metadata at the natural focal point where fixation naturally lands

Fix navigation icon spacing to a single rhythm value regardless of icon complexity — the 24px vertical step applied uniformly here makes every target equally predictable, which matters more for motor planning than matching spacing to visual weight, and the regularity reinforces position-counting as a wayfinding strategy
antiPatterns: The revenue chart bars extend to implied gridlines that aren't drawn — users must mentally interpolate the $2k, $4k, $6k marks without visual scaffolding, increasing cognitive load for precise value reads and slowing comparative judgments between non-adjacent bars

The Projects section header '+' symbol sits at the same visual weight (color #244464, no size differentiation) as the heading text itself, causing the add-action affordance to blend into the label rather than registering as an interactive control, which delays recognition and risks mis-clicks on the text

entries.title: Aboard 21
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation sidebar's teal background (#8bdaf3) paired with dark gray icons (#565958) creates a 4.8:1 contrast ratio that passes WCAG AA …
steal: Apply a single colored background field to the entire navigation zone rather tha…
Use identical border weight (1px) across all container types (sidebar divider, f…
avoid: The muted green tone (#b1c9bb) serving as both a border color and a background t…
entries.critique: The navigation sidebar's teal background (#8bdaf3) paired with dark gray icons (#565958) creates a 4.8:1 contrast ratio that passes WCAG AA but rejects the conventional white-background-with-accent-highlights pattern; this DECISION delivers persistent visual separation between navigation and content zones without requiring active state background fills, and the EFFECT is that users can identify navigation space through color field alone rather than through interaction-dependent feedback, avoiding the cognitive load of remembering which zone they're interacting with. The toggle switch's physical depth simulation—drop shadow on the thumb combined with recessed track appearance—explicitly REJECTS the flat-state-indicator approach of most modern toggles; this DECISION makes the off/on distinction readable through dimensional metaphor rather than color alone, and the EFFECT is that colorblind users can parse state through perceived depth, though this adds rendering complexity that flat alternatives avoid. The uniform 1px border weight on form inputs and the sidebar divider REJECTS the borderless-input pattern common in contemporary forms; this DECISION establishes explicit boundaries around every editable region, and the EFFECT is that users can identify interactive zones before hover states appear, avoiding the ambiguity of placeholder-only field identification where users must guess at clickable regions.
whatToSteal: Apply a single colored background field to the entire navigation zone rather than individual item backgrounds—this eliminates the need for active/inactive state management through fills and creates persistent spatial recognition through color field boundaries alone

Use identical border weight (1px) across all container types (sidebar divider, form inputs, modal edge) to establish a consistent separation language—this creates a unified vocabulary for 'boundary' that users learn once and apply everywhere, rather than decoding different stroke weights for different purposes

Render drop shadows at two distinct elevations (modal container and interactive controls) rather than a graduated system—this limits the vocabulary to 'base surface' and 'interactive element' without the parsing cost of distinguishing between multiple intermediate z-index levels

Position form labels outside and above their corresponding inputs with visible spacing rather than as placeholder text—this preserves label visibility during data entry and eliminates the label-disappearance problem that placeholder-only patterns create when users return to verify field purpose
antiPatterns: The muted green tone (#b1c9bb) serving as both a border color and a background tint creates ambiguity between 'inactive element' and 'boundary line'—users must parse context to determine whether this color signals a disabled state or a structural divider, increasing cognitive load where distinct colors for distinct roles would eliminate interpretation

entries.title: Rua
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The neumorphic navigation circles use inset shadows (approximately 2px inner shadow at 20% opacity) instead of elevation shadows, which keep…
steal: Inset-shadow neumorphism for navigation states: uses 2px inner shadow at low opa…
Selective rounding on chart geometry: apply border-radius only to the terminal 8…
avoid: The white canvas background (#ffffff) behind data visualizations offers zero con…
entries.critique: The neumorphic navigation circles use inset shadows (approximately 2px inner shadow at 20% opacity) instead of elevation shadows, which keeps the nav rail visually subordinate to the data canvas while still providing tactile affordance; this rejects the raised-button convention where navigation competes for z-index dominance with content. The bar chart applies rounded caps only to the top 8px of each column rather than rounding the entire rectangle, which preserves the geometric rigor of aligned baselines and vertical comparison while adding just enough softness to distinguish this from default chart library output; full-radius bars would sacrifice the crisp left-edge alignment that makes month-to-month comparison instantaneous. The dual-chart overlay combines bars and line without separating them into panels or adding a second y-axis, using the 2px stroke weight and #507a97 muted tone to establish the line as commentary rather than competitor; side-by-side charts would double the cognitive load by forcing users to translate between two spatial frames for data that shares the same time axis.
whatToSteal: Inset-shadow neumorphism for navigation states: uses 2px inner shadow at low opacity to create a 'pressed button' appearance that signals selection without elevating the element above the content plane—this keeps navigation contextual rather than dominant, ideal when the data deserves primary visual weight

Selective rounding on chart geometry: apply border-radius only to the terminal 8px of bars (top cap) while keeping sides and base at 0° corners—preserves the functional alignment and comparison value of hard edges while adding a signature visual detail that differentiates from stock chart libraries

Weight-only hierarchy for metrics: use 32pt bold for numbers and 13pt regular for labels without changing color or case—creates approximately 60% optical mass difference that works even when accent colors are reserved for interactive elements, and survives both light and dark mode shifts

Muted overlay for secondary data series: render supplementary chart lines in #507a97 with 2px stroke instead of introducing a second accent color—establishes information hierarchy through tone rather than hue, preventing the 'two primary colors fighting' problem common in multi-metric dashboards
antiPatterns: The white canvas background (#ffffff) behind data visualizations offers zero contrast buffer between the chart bars and the page surface, forcing the accent blue to do all the separation work; a tinted surface (even 2% gray) would create a perceptual container that groups the chart elements and reduces the optical load on the accent color to define boundaries

entries.title: Rai
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 64px sidebar width (DECISION) creates exactly enough room for 20px icons plus symmetric padding, forcing a single-column icon stack (EFF…
steal: Use geometric icon outlines at 2px stroke weight when the navigation column is n…
Set canvas background 14+ luminance points darker than the navigation surface to…
avoid: The label-free icon navigation assumes universal icon literacy — users unfamilia…
The 64px sidebar consumes fixed horizontal space regardless of viewport width, c…
entries.critique: The 64px sidebar width (DECISION) creates exactly enough room for 20px icons plus symmetric padding, forcing a single-column icon stack (EFFECT) that rejects the expandable sidebar pattern where labels slide in on hover (REJECTION), eliminating the layout shift and hover-state complexity that pattern demands. The #F2F2F2 canvas background (DECISION) provides a 14-point luminance step from the white sidebar (EFFECT), rejecting the flat single-surface approach (REJECTION) where sidebar and canvas share the same white, which would require borders or shadows to establish hierarchy. The 1px horizontal rule in #E0E0E0 (DECISION) separates navigation from content with minimal visual weight (EFFECT), rejecting heavier 2-4px dividers or drop shadows (REJECTION) that would compete with the content layer for attention. Outline-only icons with 2px stroke weight (DECISION) maintain visual presence at 20px without filling the 64px column with solid shapes (EFFECT), rejecting filled icon styles (REJECTION) that would create excessive visual density in the confined vertical space.
whatToSteal: Use geometric icon outlines at 2px stroke weight when the navigation column is narrow (sub-80px) — the linear style maintains legibility without the mass that filled shapes add, preventing the sidebar from becoming a dense visual anchor.

Set canvas background 14+ luminance points darker than the navigation surface to establish depth through brightness alone, eliminating the need for borders or shadows that add visual complexity and require additional color tokens.

Dimension icon navigation columns at exactly 3.2× the icon size (e.g., 64px for 20px icons) to create symmetric padding that accommodates touch targets without label text, forcing ruthless icon selection and avoiding the hover-expand pattern's layout shift.

Use 1px horizontal rules in a muted mid-tone (#E0E0E0 range) to separate navigation from canvas — the minimal weight divides regions without creating a heavy visual barrier that competes with content.
antiPatterns: The label-free icon navigation assumes universal icon literacy — users unfamiliar with the specific geometric shapes have no text fallback, creating a memorization tax that disproportionately affects infrequent users or those encountering the interface for the first time.

The 64px sidebar consumes fixed horizontal space regardless of viewport width, claiming 5.3% of a 1200px screen but 12.8% of a 500px mobile viewport, leaving no mechanism to reclaim that space when content density becomes critical.

entries.title: BLR
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The sky-gradient canvas (#79d8fb to #b3cddc) positions the lighter value at the bottom, inverting the atmospheric expectation where sky dark…
steal: Invert gradient direction so lighter values anchor the bottom: creates perceived…
Use muted ink (#7a776e, roughly 65% lightness) for secondary labels under primar…
avoid: The calendar widget uses #1e9fd8 fill for the current date (14) while all other …
entries.critique: The sky-gradient canvas (#79d8fb to #b3cddc) positions the lighter value at the bottom, inverting the atmospheric expectation where sky darkens toward the horizon — this creates a lifted, airy foundation that makes white cards appear backlit rather than dropped onto a surface. The navigation icons use solid rounded containers filled with #1e9fd8 instead of stroke outlines or ghost buttons, sacrificing some visual weight for immediate scannable recognition in the narrow column. The floating metric cards omit drop shadows entirely, relying on the 20-25 point lightness delta between #ffffff surfaces and #b3cddc canvas to achieve separation — this rejects the z-axis depth convention in favor of a flattened, layered-paper aesthetic that prevents shadow clutter when cards multiply.
whatToSteal: Invert gradient direction so lighter values anchor the bottom: creates perceived uplift for content above, making white surfaces read as luminous rather than weight-bearing, especially effective when canvas gradients span 15+ percentage points in lightness

Use muted ink (#7a776e, roughly 65% lightness) for secondary labels under primary data rather than reducing opacity on the main ink color: avoids the translucent-text effect that makes small type harder to parse, and establishes a stable two-ink hierarchy that survives theme changes

Fill icon containers with the accent color (#1e9fd8) and reverse glyphs to white instead of using colored glyphs on neutral backgrounds: the higher contrast ratio (4.5:1 minimum) makes icons parseable at 20px, and the color becomes a structural element rather than decorative tint

Omit shadows when lightness contrast between surface and canvas exceeds 20 points: the hard edge between #ffffff and #b3cddc (approximately 23-point delta) provides sufficient figure-ground separation, and eliminating the shadow layer prevents the soft-halo accumulation that degrades dense layouts
antiPatterns: The calendar widget uses #1e9fd8 fill for the current date (14) while all other dates remain neutral, but no hover or selection state is visible on neighboring dates — this makes it unclear whether dates are interactive targets or static labels, forcing users to mouse over the entire grid to discover affordances instead of signaling interactivity through consistent treatment of the date set

entries.title: SLUI
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation sidebar inverts the typical surface-to-canvas relationship by making the sidebar darker (#242c2c) than the main workspace (#c…
steal: Color-matched input fields: set the search box background to exactly match the c…
Single-accent navigation: apply the accent color (#8bb4f3) to exactly one call-t…
avoid: The search input's #c4bfc7-on-#c4bfc7 treatment eliminates all perceptual bounda…
entries.critique: The navigation sidebar inverts the typical surface-to-canvas relationship by making the sidebar darker (#242c2c) than the main workspace (#c4bfc7), which pushes the navigation forward in the z-order perception rather than recessing it as a supporting frame — this rejects the common pattern where toolbars sit on lighter surfaces to feel auxiliary. The search input achieves borderless integration by matching the canvas color exactly (#c4bfc7 on #c4bfc7) and relying solely on the search icon as the affordance marker, which eliminates the cognitive load of parsing yet another rectangular container — this avoids the standard sunken-field treatment that adds visual noise through strokes, shadows, or background tints. The icon column employs #607baa at 2.8:1 contrast against its #242c2c background, prioritizing spatial rhythm and tonal consistency over accessibility compliance — this rejects the safer choice of meeting WCAG AA (4.5:1) in favor of maintaining the design's strict five-color palette without introducing a sixth value for legibility.
whatToSteal: Color-matched input fields: set the search box background to exactly match the canvas color behind it, so the only visible affordance is the icon — this removes one rectangle from the user's perceptual load while maintaining functionality through icon recognition alone

Single-accent navigation: apply the accent color (#8bb4f3) to exactly one call-to-action item while rendering all other navigation in the muted tone (#c4bfc7) — this creates a binary attention system where one path is lit and all others are deliberately dimmed

Inverted sidebar z-order: make the sidebar surface darker than the main canvas rather than lighter, which repositions it as a control panel brought forward instead of a recessed frame — this works when the sidebar contains active tools rather than passive wayfinding

Padding-only separators: use consistent vertical padding (16px per item) with zero divider lines between navigation entries, letting whitespace alone create the grouping rhythm — this reduces stroke count while maintaining parseable boundaries
antiPatterns: The search input's #c4bfc7-on-#c4bfc7 treatment eliminates all perceptual boundaries, forcing users to rely entirely on icon recognition to locate the interaction target — users scanning for a traditional input field shape will miss it, especially if the magnifying glass icon is unfamiliar or gets lost in peripheral vision during rapid navigation tasks

entries.title: Run
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The inset shadow on the #bcacce canvas creates a recessed well that makes the white modal appear to float forward rather than sit on top, re…
steal: Inset shadows on the background plane instead of drop shadows on the foreground …
Sentence-case descriptive phrases ('Continue watching', 'Content you like') inst…
avoid: The three-column icon grid offers no visual weight differentiation between prima…
entries.critique: The inset shadow on the #bcacce canvas creates a recessed well that makes the white modal appear to float forward rather than sit on top, reversing the typical drop-shadow convention where foreground elements cast shadows backward; this inversion subtly suggests the modal pushed through the canvas from behind. The #89cefb accent applies exclusively to interactive glyphs (play button, likely active state indicators) rather than decorative containers, making color a reliable signifier of clickability instead of mere visual interest. The bottom navigation row commits to equal-width distribution across the full modal span rather than clustering around centered content or using auto-margins, which forces each icon into its own clearly-bounded territory and eliminates ambiguity about tap target boundaries.
whatToSteal: Inset shadows on the background plane instead of drop shadows on the foreground card — reverses depth perception so the modal feels like it emerged from beneath rather than dropped from above, reducing visual weight

Sentence-case descriptive phrases ('Continue watching', 'Content you like') instead of truncated nouns ('Watch', 'Liked') — adds one or two words but dramatically increases scannability by preserving natural reading rhythm
Single accent color reserved exclusively for interactive glyphs (#89cefb on play icon) while all static text remains ink-dark — turns hue into a reliable interaction cue rather than decorative noise
Bottom navigation with pixel-perfect even distribution rather than flex-end clustering — eliminates the guessing game of whether whitespace is tappable or just margin
antiPatterns: The three-column icon grid offers no visual weight differentiation between primary actions (like 'Continue watching') and secondary utility (like 'Your Library'), treating all nine items with identical icon size, spacing, and color — a user returning to resume playback must parse all options at equal priority rather than being guided to the most statistically likely task

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The dropdown chevron in #ad876c muted brown (DECISION) reduces mechanical affordance visibility so the field reads as content-first rather t…
steal: Color-coding dropdown affordances in the muted text tone (#ad876c) rather than h…
Isolating the primary action button with 40-50px vertical margin creates a psych…
avoid: The left illustration panel, while visually coordinated through palette sharing,…
entries.critique: The dropdown chevron in #ad876c muted brown (DECISION) reduces mechanical affordance visibility so the field reads as content-first rather than control-first (EFFECT), rejecting the standard dark chevron that announces 'this is a mechanical UI widget' before the user has decided what information to provide (REJECTION). The isolated 'Continue' button with 40-50px top margin (DECISION) separates decision-making from data entry by creating a deliberate pause (EFFECT), avoiding the conventional tight grouping where action buttons feel like just another field to fill (REJECTION). The left panel's overlapping geometric shapes with transparency blending in #c7a7be, #ad876c, and #104865 (DECISION) establishes tonal continuity between illustration and interface through shared material language (EFFECT), rejecting the typical photo-realistic illustration or brand mascot that lives in a separate visual universe from the form controls (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Color-coding dropdown affordances in the muted text tone (#ad876c) rather than high-contrast black makes the interaction reveal itself during task engagement instead of screaming 'clickable widget' before the user has context for the choice

Isolating the primary action button with 40-50px vertical margin creates a psychological checkpoint—users must finish the mental task of 'providing information' before encountering the separate task of 'committing that information', reducing premature clicks

Rendering geometric illustration elements in the exact extracted palette (#c7a7be, #ad876c, #104865) rather than decorative brand colors makes the visual panel feel like material exploration of the same design system, not a separate marketing artifact

Using sentence case for conversational prompts ('Tell us a bit about yourself') while keeping field labels properly capitalized ('First Name') separates human framing from data structure, making bureaucratic requests feel like dialogue turns
antiPatterns: The left illustration panel, while visually coordinated through palette sharing, consumes roughly 50% of horizontal screen real estate during an input-heavy task where users need to look at keyboards, reference documents, or recall personal information—the geometric shapes provide no task support and force the form into a narrow column that increases scrolling and reduces field visibility per viewport

entries.title: Juicebox
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The skip link occupies the bottom-left corner in accent purple (#9440c4), creating asymmetric balance against the right-aligned primary butt…
steal: Position the dismissal action (skip/cancel) in the opposite corner from the prim…
Render input field borders in mid-tone neutral (#c3c3c3 range) rather than accen…
avoid: The 'Next step' label on the primary button names the mechanism (pagination) rat…
entries.critique: The skip link occupies the bottom-left corner in accent purple (#9440c4), creating asymmetric balance against the right-aligned primary button—this REJECTS centered or right-stacked secondary actions that compete for the same visual column, reducing decision paralysis by giving exit and progress separate spatial domains. The input borders use #c3c3c3 neutral gray in their resting state, providing enough containment to define tappable zones without the aggressive presence of accent-colored or thick borders—this REJECTS the pattern of pre-highlighting empty fields, which falsely signals activity before user engagement. The password helper text renders as persistent static copy below the field rather than as placeholder text that vanishes on focus—this REJECTS the placeholder-as-instruction anti-pattern where critical context disappears exactly when users need it most, keeping security requirements constantly visible during character entry.
whatToSteal: Position the dismissal action (skip/cancel) in the opposite corner from the primary button using accent color for both—creates spatial separation that lets users distinguish 'move forward' from 'move away' by direction rather than parsing visual weight hierarchies

Render input field borders in mid-tone neutral (#c3c3c3 range) rather than accent color or invisible—defines interactive boundaries without the false urgency of pre-colored fields that suggest required attention before any user action

Display helper text as persistent visible copy below fields rather than tooltips or vanishing placeholders—keeps constraints and requirements in view during the exact moment users are typing, eliminating the need to remember instructions shown earlier
Use a muted ink color (#444444) for headings instead of pure black—reduces typographic aggression while scale alone maintains hierarchy, particularly effective when accent colors are already vibrant
antiPatterns: The 'Next step' label on the primary button names the mechanism (pagination) rather than the outcome (create account, see dashboard)—users must infer what happens after clicking instead of seeing a goal-oriented verb, increasing cognitive load during a decision moment that should feel like progress toward a known destination

entries.title: Gem
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The left sidebar uses icon-only navigation with no visible labels, requiring returning users to memorize icon meanings before muscle memory …
steal: Use filled background status chips with white text for pipeline stages (e.g., 'M…
Separate key-value pairs in detail cards by rendering keys in a secondary gray w…
avoid: Avoids using color alone for status differentiation without a supporting text la…
Avoids stacking the activity feed and detail rail on a single scrollable column …
entries.critique: The left sidebar uses icon-only navigation with no visible labels, requiring returning users to memorize icon meanings before muscle memory develops — first-time users must hover each icon to learn its function, which slows initial onboarding but rewards frequent use with faster scanning. The activity feed's status chips use filled backgrounds with white text (e.g., 'Matched' in green, 'In Review' in orange), creating a high-contrast visual anchor that lets returning users assess pipeline stage at a glance without reading text. The horizontal tab bar uses uppercase labels with an underlined active state, which adds visual weight to the selected tab but forces users to decode uppercase text — a conventional lowercase or sentence-case would be faster to read while preserving hierarchy. The detail rail's key-value pairs separate keys in secondary gray (smaller font) from values in bold primary text, letting users quickly locate the data point they need without parsing dense prose. The filter row uses bordered dropdown buttons with chevron icons and light backgrounds, making them clearly clickable but visually subordinate to the main content — this prevents accidental filter activation while keeping the controls discoverable.
whatToSteal: Use filled background status chips with white text for pipeline stages (e.g., 'Matched' in green, 'In Review' in orange) so returning users can assess state by color alone without reading. Avoid for more than 5 states — color differentiation fails beyond that.

Separate key-value pairs in detail cards by rendering keys in a secondary gray with smaller font size (2-3px smaller) than values, which speeds up data scanning by letting the eye skip to the value first. Do NOT use when keys are longer than values — that reverses the benefit.

Render filter controls as bordered dropdown buttons with chevron icons and light backgrounds, distinguishing them from primary actions (which should be filled). This reduces accidental clicks during rapid scanning while keeping filters accessible. Avoid for a single filter — a simple dropdown is faster.
antiPatterns: Avoids using color alone for status differentiation without a supporting text label — each status chip (e.g., 'Matched', 'In Review') has both a distinct hue and the word itself, so colorblind users can still read the state. A lazy approach would rely on hue-only dots, forcing cross-referencing.

Avoids stacking the activity feed and detail rail on a single scrollable column — instead, the three-column layout keeps the feed scannable while the rail holds persistent context. This prevents the common failure where a user must scroll back and forth between a list and its details, which increases cognitive load during rapid triage.

entries.critique: The inline italic '(person has left the company)' embeds status directly in the name field (DECISION), which preserves horizontal scan paths without forcing eye movement to a separate status column (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional dedicated status badge that fragments attention across multiple vertical tracks (REJECTION). The monochromatic purple navigation with white text (DECISION) achieves 8.6:1+ contrast ratio while maintaining brand presence (EFFECT), avoiding the weak grey sidebars that rely on subtle strokes or low-contrast icons for structure (REJECTION). Column headers match data row typography exactly (DECISION), creating a continuous text field that prioritizes content density over structural signposting (EFFECT), deliberately discarding the bold-header convention that treats interface chrome as more important than the data itself (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Inline parenthetical status indicators in italic — embeds metadata directly in the primary field rather than forcing a dedicated column, maintaining left-to-right reading flow while preserving sortability

Saturated single-hue navigation background with white foreground — delivers accessibility-compliant contrast without introducing a second brand color or relying on stroke-weight variations for hierarchy
Weight-identical headers and data rows — treats the table as a unified text block rather than a chrome-wrapped content area, letting whitespace and alignment do all hierarchical work

Three-saturation accent palette (#6915b0 full, #ba9fca tint, procurement tags desaturated) — creates visual rhythm through value shifts in a single hue rather than introducing multiple colors that compete for attention
antiPatterns: The '50 people' results counter sits directly below the search input with no weight change, size increase, or background differentiation — users scanning for result quantity must parse it from the same visual layer as body text, losing the instant recognizability that a distinct treatment (background pill, larger size, or position change) would provide

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 4px vertical active-state bar on #a9cad1 canvas creates a low-contrast edge indicator (purple-on-teal delivers roughly 3:1) that relies …
steal: Use background color shifts (dark teal to light teal) as the primary hierarchy s…
Apply active-state indicators as thick edge marks (4px bars) rather than full-ce…
avoid: The fixed-width column layout forces truncation on company names and titles with…
The right-detail rail consumes roughly 30% of viewport width even when showing o…
entries.critique: The 4px vertical active-state bar on #a9cad1 canvas creates a low-contrast edge indicator (purple-on-teal delivers roughly 3:1) that relies on width rather than luminance — this rejects the common high-contrast white or bright accent bar because it preserves the muted, analytical tone appropriate for prolonged data work. The table's hairline row separators in rgba(255,255,255,0.1) on dark teal produce subtle divisions that guide scanning without the jarring interruption of full-opacity borders — this avoids the zebra-striping pattern that fragments dense numeric data into alternating blocks. The coral icon paired with black text on the 'Export Enrichment' button uses hue differentiation instead of matching the button's text color to its icon — this rejects monochrome button anatomy because it lets the icon register as an actionable object class while the label remains readable, a critical distinction when icons carry non-obvious export metaphors.
whatToSteal: Use background color shifts (dark teal to light teal) as the primary hierarchy signal when showing 50+ rows of data — this preserves vertical density by avoiding size jumps while still establishing clear regions, particularly effective when combined with 12px row padding that's tight enough to show 8-10 rows without scroll

Apply active-state indicators as thick edge marks (4px bars) rather than full-cell backgrounds when the navigation sits on a colored canvas — this preserves the canvas color as part of the brand experience while still providing clear wayfinding, especially valuable in icon-only navigation where hover states can't explain meaning

Position critical metric badges (like '40 Contacts Available') above filter controls rather than in summary footers — this front-loads the scarcity or availability signal before users invest effort in configuring searches, reducing wasted interaction when limits are already reached

Use 0.1 opacity white overlays for table dividers on dark backgrounds instead of gray strokes — this creates adaptive separators that maintain consistent perceived weight regardless of the base color's luminance, avoiding the manual tuning required when gray strokes appear too heavy or too light
antiPatterns: The fixed-width column layout forces truncation on company names and titles without showing ellipses or tooltips — users see 'Senior Dir...' with no hover affordance, requiring them to open the detail panel to read full context, which breaks the table's promise as a scannable overview. This is especially problematic because the main canvas has flex width that could accommodate 15-20% wider columns without compromising the 3-column overall structure.

The right-detail rail consumes roughly 30% of viewport width even when showing only 4 form controls and a contact count — this allocates premium horizontal space to controls that could collapse into a modal or slide-over, particularly wasteful on laptop screens where the data table (the primary task surface) is squeezed into the remaining 50% after accounting for the two left navigation columns.

entries.title: Shortlisted Profiles
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 56px vertical rhythm between navigation icons (home, briefcase, user, charts, settings) creates predictable muscle-memory targets while …
steal: 56px vertical intervals between icon-only navigation targets — large enough for …
Interpunct separators ('·') for metadata pairs like 'Available · 30 days' — main…

entries.critique: The 56px vertical rhythm between navigation icons (home, briefcase, user, charts, settings) creates predictable muscle-memory targets while rejecting the common 40-48px mobile-derived spacing that forces desktop users into unnecessarily precise cursor movements. Setting 'Shortlisted' in 18px all-caps with 12px letter-spacing transforms a simple label into a spatial anchor, creating horizontal tension that conventional title-case typography at 16-20px would dissolve into blandness. The interpunct ('·') separator in 'Available · 30 days' occupies 8px of horizontal space but preserves the text as a single scannable unit, avoiding the fragmentation that slash or pipe separators introduce when the eye must parse them as semantic delimiters rather than pure visual punctuation. Profile thumbnails locked to 48×48px with 3px corners reject both the oversized 64-80px avatars common in social feeds (which waste vertical real estate in list contexts) and the sharp 0px corners that read as placeholder imagery rather than finished interface elements.
whatToSteal: 56px vertical intervals between icon-only navigation targets — large enough for confident cursor landing without the wasted space of 64-80px mobile-first patterns, tested against Fitts's Law for desktop pointer precision

Interpunct separators ('·') for metadata pairs like 'Available · 30 days' — maintains single-line cohesion while providing semantic separation without the visual weight of slashes, pipes, or comma-space pairs that fragment scan patterns

18px all-caps labels with 12px letter-spacing for section headers — creates horizontal tension and spatial differentiation from body text without increasing vertical footprint or invoking font-size changes that disrupt rhythm

48×48px profile thumbnails with 3px radius — balances recognition (faces remain identifiable) against density (three profiles visible without scrolling in a 400px rail), while subtle rounding signals 'finished component' instead of 'wireframe placeholder'
antiPatterns: Gray (#7c7c7c) metadata text at 11px sits near the threshold of legibility on the cream (#e2cfb9) background — WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 for small text, but this pairing may dip below that in certain lighting conditions, forcing users to lean forward or strain to parse availability status that should be instantly scannable

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The input field's 4px bottom-only border (#9496cc) registers as an underline primitive rather than a container, which reduces the cognitive …

Color-matching helper text to input border (#9496cc for both) creates a visual s…

entries.critique: The input field's 4px bottom-only border (#9496cc) registers as an underline primitive rather than a container, which reduces the cognitive overhead of 'field as box' and lets users treat it as a direct-write surface—this rejects the card-style input with 1px perimeter borders that creates visual weight around emptiness. The helper text shares the exact color (#9496cc) as the input underline, creating a visual set where instruction and interaction boundary are perceptually linked—this avoids the conventional #666 gray helper text that orphans instructions from their target. The modal's 60px top offset with 24px atmospheric shadow places it in the upper third rather than geometric center, keeping the search action in the natural reading start zone—this rejects the vertically-centered modal that forces downward eye travel before interaction begins.
whatToSteal: Single-edge border (4px bottom only) for text inputs eliminates perimeter weight while maintaining field recognition, creating a write-on-line metaphor that reduces form anxiety
Color-matching helper text to input border (#9496cc for both) creates a visual set that binds instruction to interaction surface without requiring proximity or leader lines
Upper-third modal positioning (60px from top) places primary interaction in the natural reading start zone, reducing the eye-travel tax that vertically-centered modals impose
Purple label with matching 1px underline ('Search' in #6314a5) converts static text into an interactive signal through minimal typographic furniture rather than button chrome
antiPatterns: The close X button uses identical ink color (#343435) and weight (2px stroke) as body text, making the dismiss affordance compete visually with content rather than establishing it as a distinct control layer—a 40% opacity or lighter stroke would separate utility from content

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 48×48px circular profile images with 1px gray borders establish fixed vertical rhythm across dense rows—DECISION: uniform circular crop …

Checkbox vertical-center alignment to profile photo instead of text baseline—gro…

entries.critique: The 48×48px circular profile images with 1px gray borders establish fixed vertical rhythm across dense rows—DECISION: uniform circular crop at 48px with hairline border. EFFECT: creates predictable scan-line targets even when photos vary wildly in source quality or aspect ratio. REJECTION: avoids unconstrained aspect ratios that would make each row a different height, destroying the ability to skim vertically. The checkbox placement 12px from edge and vertically centered to the photo—DECISION: align checkbox to photo center, not text baseline. EFFECT: makes the clickable target visually grouped with the person's identity (photo + name), not their metadata. REJECTION: top-alignment would pair the checkbox with job title text, implying you're selecting the role rather than the human. The middot separator consolidating title and company—DECISION: inline concatenation with ' · ' instead of stacked fields. EFFECT: cuts vertical space per record by 50%, doubling the number of results visible without scroll. REJECTION: conventional two-line treatment (title on one line, company below) wastes space on low-information-density content where both fields are often 2-4 words.
whatToSteal: All-caps purple (#7424b0) buttons with white text—creates unambiguous call-to-action signaling in data-dense contexts where every other element is sentence-case, letting users locate export/save functions in <200ms without reading any word

Checkbox vertical-center alignment to profile photo instead of text baseline—groups the selection affordance with the person's visual identity rather than their metadata, making bulk actions feel like 'choosing people' rather than 'choosing list items'

Middot-separated inline title+company (no line break)—halves the vertical space per record compared to stacked labels, doubling visible results per viewport while maintaining full information scannability

1px gray border on circular profile images—prevents light-background photos from bleeding into the white canvas, maintaining consistent visual weight across rows even when source images have zero contrast with the background
antiPatterns: The 'Exclude' text link in gray (#7c7c7c) with no surrounding visual container sits isolated in the header—makes a destructive filtering action look identical in weight and affordance to passive metadata, risking accidental clicks when users scan for more prominent controls

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation pills use 100% container width with full purple fill rather than minimal-width badges or transparent backgrounds, which force…
steal: 100% width filled navigation pills that consume their entire container rather th…
All-caps table headers with expanded letter-spacing set at smaller absolute size…

entries.critique: The navigation pills use 100% container width with full purple fill rather than minimal-width badges or transparent backgrounds, which forces vertical stacking and creates unmistakable tap targets while the solid color establishes absolute visual hierarchy over the beige canvas (#c6bda7). The table header employs all-caps with generous letter-spacing instead of mixed-case bold or larger size, which allows smaller absolute font size while maintaining legibility and creates uniform visual rhythm across variable-length column labels like 'NAME' versus 'EMPLOYEES'. The row dividers appear as single-pixel hairlines rather than alternating background fills or thicker strokes, which maintains maximum data density by minimizing vertical space consumption while still providing necessary scanline guidance across the wide horizontal table span.
whatToSteal: 100% width filled navigation pills that consume their entire container rather than auto-width badges — eliminates ambiguity about clickable area and creates vertical rhythm through uniform block shapes regardless of label length

All-caps table headers with expanded letter-spacing set at smaller absolute size than the data rows — achieves visual differentiation through transformation rather than weight or size, keeping vertical height minimal while maintaining distinct hierarchy

Single-pixel horizontal dividers between rows instead of zebra-striping or grouped whitespace — preserves maximum information density per viewport while still providing necessary scanline tracking across wide tables

Sentence-case secondary values (titles) displayed at same size but reduced visual weight compared to primary values (names) within the same row — creates intra-row hierarchy without adding columns or requiring icons
antiPatterns: The search input placeholder text 'Search for a company, person, or keyword...' explicitly enumerates three entity types, which creates maintenance debt when search scope changes and conditions users to think categorically rather than exploring the full search capability — a simple 'Search...' would avoid false constraints while the results themselves would teach scope

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The two-tone header ('34 Results' in black, 'from ~330M People' in gray) splits the statistic at the semantically critical boundary—DECISION…

Pipe-delimited metadata concatenation that keeps three data fields on one line: …

entries.critique: The two-tone header ('34 Results' in black, 'from ~330M People' in gray) splits the statistic at the semantically critical boundary—DECISION: same type size, color differentiation at word level—EFFECT: emphasizes actionable count while acknowledging total corpus without typographic escalation—REJECTION: avoids the conventional larger-number-in-bigger-font pattern that would compete with the action buttons for visual dominance. The pipe-separated metadata line—DECISION: single-line concatenation with '|' delimiters between location, title, company—EFFECT: maintains 8-10 result cards visible without scroll in a standard viewport—REJECTION: dismisses the two-line stacked layout (name/title on line 1, company/location on line 2) that most directory UIs default to, trading readability for information density. The outlined 'Enrich' button—DECISION: stroke-only purple with white fill instead of solid color—EFFECT: establishes secondary action hierarchy without introducing a third color—REJECTION: refuses both the ghost button (text-only, no border) that would disappear against varied avatar colors, and the solid secondary button that would create purple saturation across the vertical scan path.
whatToSteal: Two-color statistical header where chromatic weight (black vs. gray) replaces size hierarchy: lets you emphasize the primary number without typographic volume that competes with CTAs, especially useful when header stats and action buttons occupy the same horizontal plane.

Pipe-delimited metadata concatenation that keeps three data fields on one line: preserves vertical real estate in scrollable result sets where each extra line reduces visible-without-scroll count by 20-30%.

Outlined accent-color button for secondary actions in repetitive card layouts: creates visual rhythm down the right edge without the saturation fatigue that 10+ solid purple buttons would produce in a single viewport.

Circular avatar positioning at fixed left margin with consistent diameter: establishes a vertical scan line anchor that helps eyes skip between results when hunting for visual recognition cues (familiar faces) rather than reading sequentially.
antiPatterns: The all-caps treatment on 'EXPORT ALL' and 'SELECT ALL' buttons contradicts the sentence-case labels elsewhere in the interface: introduces typographic inconsistency without clear affordance benefit, since button shape and color already signal interactivity—all-caps typically signals permanence or system status, not user actions.

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: DECISION: Canvas uses #ead68c warm beige rather than white or neutral gray behind dense tabular data. EFFECT: The warm undertone reduces the…
steal: Warm beige (#ead68c) canvas behind dense data tables — reduces eye strain during…
Identical typographic treatment for headers and data cells with structure define…
avoid: Column headers receive no typographic differentiation (weight, size, or color ch…
entries.critique: DECISION: Canvas uses #ead68c warm beige rather than white or neutral gray behind dense tabular data. EFFECT: The warm undertone reduces the harshness of prolonged scanning across hundreds of rows while maintaining sufficient contrast for black ink, preventing eye fatigue during extended research sessions. REJECTION: White backgrounds maximize theoretical contrast ratios but create glare and visual fatigue when users spend hours filtering prospect lists. DECISION: Column headers use identical weight, size, and color (#47433c) as body text with only left-alignment to denote structure. EFFECT: This eliminates visual noise in the header row, keeping attention on the data values themselves rather than creating a heavy top border that competes with every subsequent row. REJECTION: Bold or all-caps headers announce themselves as 'different' but add visual weight that pulls focus upward repeatedly during vertical scanning. DECISION: Navigation badges display counts in #745c84 purple circles rather than red notification dots or inline numbers. EFFECT: The muted purple registers as informational metadata rather than urgent alerts, preserving the badge as a utility feature without creating artificial pressure to act. REJECTION: Red badges exploit anxiety to drive engagement but create false urgency in analytical tools where counts represent research scope, not missed messages.
whatToSteal: Warm beige (#ead68c) canvas behind dense data tables — reduces eye strain during extended scanning sessions without sacrificing contrast, directly addressing the fatigue problem inherent in white-background research tools

Identical typographic treatment for headers and data cells with structure defined purely by left-alignment — removes header row as a competing visual element, keeping cognitive load on parsing values rather than navigating decorative hierarchy

Muted purple (#745c84) count badges instead of high-contrast or red notification indicators — frames numerical metadata as informational context rather than urgent alerts, appropriate for analytical workflows where counts represent scope not anxiety triggers

Avatar circles as the only consistent left-edge element in each table row — creates a vertical rhythm that anchors the eye's return sweep during horizontal scanning without adding grid lines or zebra striping
antiPatterns: Column headers receive no typographic differentiation (weight, size, or color change) from body text, forcing users to infer column boundaries purely from spatial alignment — this fails when column content varies in length or when users enter mid-scroll, as there's no persistent structural signal to re-orient against

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The full-width pill selection indicator in the sidebar (DECISION: #167bb3 background extending edge-to-edge with white text reversal) create…
steal: Full-width background pill for navigation selection state instead of left-border…
Monogram avatars with palette-coordinated backgrounds as the default pattern — g…
