entries.critique: The full-width pill selection indicator in the sidebar (DECISION: #167bb3 background extending edge-to-edge with white text reversal) creates unambiguous active-state recognition (EFFECT) while rejecting the common left-border-only pattern (REJECTION) that requires users to match a thin accent strip to adjacent label text. The monogram avatars use deterministic background colors from the extracted palette rather than user photos (DECISION: two-letter initials on #e96a5c, #a8d4d1, #ea4231 circles), ensuring every row has consistent visual weight and eliminating the layout reflow caused by missing images (EFFECT), avoiding the placeholder-gray-circle convention (REJECTION) that creates dead visual zones in dense tables. Hyperlinked emails inherit the #167bb3 accent without underlines (DECISION), maintaining scannability across 50+ row datasets (EFFECT) while rejecting browser-default blue+underline styling (REJECTION) that would create visual clutter competing with the structural grid.
whatToSteal: Full-width background pill for navigation selection state instead of left-border accent — eliminates the cognitive load of matching a 3px colored strip to its corresponding label, especially valuable in fixed sidebars where eye movement is vertical

Monogram avatars with palette-coordinated backgrounds as the default pattern — guarantees visual rhythm in tables where user photo upload rates are unpredictable, and the color variety prevents the monotony of single-color fallback circles

Accent-colored hyperlinks without underlines in table cells — preserves the structural integrity of the grid by reducing line-weight competition, critical when link density exceeds 30% of visible text
Sentence-case column headers with background tint and weight increase — creates hierarchy without size jumps that would disturb row height consistency, letting the table maintain optical density
antiPatterns: The search input's placeholder text 'Search people...' with ellipsis suggests an incomplete thought rather than an actionable instruction — the ellipsis adds no functional information and trains users to expect progressive disclosure or autocomplete that may not exist, creating ambiguity about search scope and trigger behavior

entries.title: Jasmine
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The sidebar compresses to single-letter abbreviations (DECISION: 60-70px fixed width forcing vertical letter stacking) which eliminates scan…
steal: Apply the accent color exclusively to notification badges and interactive search…
Display metric deltas in a non-semantic color from the muted palette (#dc9c9b fo…
avoid: The notification badge '20' lacks any indication of what the 20 represents—unrea…
The percentage changes (#dc9c9b for +11.01%, +2.00%, +37.00%) use a single muted…
entries.critique: The sidebar compresses to single-letter abbreviations (DECISION: 60-70px fixed width forcing vertical letter stacking) which eliminates scan-reading of navigation options (EFFECT: forces users to rely entirely on icon recognition) rather than allowing labels to remain readable at ~120-150px where both icon and text would parse simultaneously (REJECTION: the conventional dual-signifier pattern that accommodates varied icon literacy). The percentage values receive the muted rose #dc9c9b treatment (DECISION: applying the decorative palette color #dc9c9b to numerical data) which removes the semantic signal of positive/negative performance (EFFECT: all metrics appear equally neutral/descriptive) instead of the red-for-loss green-for-gain convention (REJECTION: sacrifices instant directional comprehension for palette cohesion). The breadcrumb's capitalization inconsistency (DECISION: 'Dashboard' capitalized, 'orders' lowercase) encodes hierarchy through orthography (EFFECT: establishes parent-child relationship without requiring separators beyond the › glyph) rather than maintaining uniform case with weight or color to differentiate levels (REJECTION: avoids the redundancy of size+weight+separator all signaling the same parent/child split).
whatToSteal: Apply the accent color exclusively to notification badges and interactive search icons (the purple #8523c1 on the '20' badge and magnifying glass)—this reserves the highest-contrast hue for elements requiring immediate attention or indicating system state changes, preventing accent-color dilution across static decorative elements

Display metric deltas in a non-semantic color from the muted palette (#dc9c9b for all percentages)—when trend direction matters less than magnitude awareness, this prevents the reflexive positive/negative judgment that red/green triggers, keeping focus on the primary numerical value above

Collapse navigation to icon-plus-initial-letter in a sub-70px rail—forces a deliberate icon design investment (since icons must carry full weight) while reclaiming horizontal space; works only when navigation items number fewer than eight and icon metaphors are unambiguous

Use sentence-case differentiation in breadcrumbs to encode hierarchy—proper nouns versus common nouns convey parent/child without requiring different font sizes, weights, or colors, relying instead on orthographic convention that readers parse unconsciously
antiPatterns: The notification badge '20' lacks any indication of what the 20 represents—unread messages, pending actions, or accumulated items—forcing users to click through to discover whether the count warrants immediate attention; including a tooltip or adjacent microcopy ('20 unread') would preserve the compact visual while adding actionable context

The percentage changes (#dc9c9b for +11.01%, +2.00%, +37.00%) use a single muted color for all directional movement, stripping the semantic meaning readers expect from gains and losses; while this maintains palette purity, it requires users to parse the +/- symbol and percentage magnitude consciously rather than relying on the pre-attentive processing that red/green encoding provides

entries.title: Jasmine

entries.critique: The filled purple circle for the 'Messages' icon (versus stroked circles for other nav items) uses a 100% opacity fill to signal the active state, creating instant figure-ground separation where a border-only treatment would require users to parse stroke thickness differences — this rejects the common pattern of keeping all nav icons structurally identical and adding a separate 'active bar' indicator. The warm beige canvas (#f4d4b3) paired with cool purple accents (#6d16b3) creates a 180-degree hue separation on the color wheel, making interactive elements visually 'jump forward' through chromatic contrast rather than relying on brightness alone — this avoids the typical white/gray canvas that forces designers to manufacture contrast through shadows or borders. The search placeholder 'Search for messages...' includes the ellipsis as a typographic element rather than leaving it bare or using a period, suggesting continuation and softening the imperative tone — this rejects the terse 'Search' label that treats the field as a command rather than an invitation.
whatToSteal: Use a filled shape for the active navigation state while keeping inactive items as strokes — this creates a binary visual distinction that works at icon scale (20-24px) where subtle border thickness changes become imperceptible

Pair a warm-toned canvas (beige/cream in the 40-50 lightness range) with a saturated cool accent (purple/blue) to create hue-based contrast that makes interactive elements self-evident without requiring additional visual weight like shadows or thick borders

Set empty state headings only 25-30% larger than body text instead of doubling the size — this keeps the message conversational rather than announcing a system failure, particularly useful when the empty state is a neutral condition rather than an error

Position a search icon 10-12px from the input's left edge rather than centering it in a square — this pulls the affordance leftward into the reading flow and allows the placeholder text to start immediately after, reducing the perceived distance between icon and label
antiPatterns: The nav sidebar icons lack any text labels (only glyphs are visible), forcing users to decode six abstract symbols without confirmation — this pattern assumes either a tiny expert user base who will memorize the iconography, or accepts that first-time users will need to click multiple icons before finding their target

The empty state centers all text in the vertical middle of a large canvas area, leaving equal whitespace above and below — this creates a floating, unanchored composition where the message has no relationship to the surrounding UI zones (search bar above, potential content area implied), rejecting a top-aligned or search-adjacent placement that would suggest where content will appear once added

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The canvas color (#c8b4b0) acts as a functional boundary rather than decoration—by refusing the default white-everywhere pattern, it forces …
steal: Use a tinted canvas behind navigation chrome to create spatial hierarchy without…
Assign your accent color exclusively to interactive elements and withhold it fro…
avoid: The 'Export' and 'Save Search' buttons sit at opposite ends of the top bar with …
entries.critique: The canvas color (#c8b4b0) acts as a functional boundary rather than decoration—by refusing the default white-everywhere pattern, it forces the sidebar icons and the main content card to exist as distinct spatial zones, preventing the eye from treating navigation as part of the data grid. The profile images carry 1px borders despite already having circular crops, adding a hairline that prevents light-skinned faces from bleeding into the white row background—the alternative of relying solely on the circular mask fails when the photo and surface share similar luminance. The 'Company · Title' separator uses a middle-dot (·) instead of a comma or slash, choosing the lowest-interference glyph that still creates a pause between parallel metadata—commas suggest subordination (title under company) and slashes suggest opposition, both wrong for co-equal attributes.
whatToSteal: Use a tinted canvas behind navigation chrome to create spatial hierarchy without adding borders or shadows—the beige (#c8b4b0) here makes the sidebar read as 'behind' the white content surface even though they're on the same z-index, because the human eye interprets the darker tone as recessive space

Assign your accent color exclusively to interactive elements and withhold it from all static content—here purple (#7404bc) appears only on buttons and states you can click, training users that this specific hue means 'you can act here' rather than 'this is important information'

When displaying repeating profile images in a list, add a 1px border even if the circular crop seems sufficient—it prevents light photos from dissolving into light backgrounds and keeps each avatar legible as a discrete object across all skin tones and lighting conditions

Use color to tier secondary metadata instead of relying on font weight—the muted red-brown (#bc847c) for company/title here creates a perceptual recession that lets users scan names at full speed while still keeping context readable, avoiding the boldness arms race that happens when every row competes for attention
antiPatterns: The 'Export' and 'Save Search' buttons sit at opposite ends of the top bar with identical visual weight (same purple background, same size), forcing users to scan horizontally across the entire width to understand all available actions—placing related commands in a single cluster would reduce the saccade distance and make the action set graspable in a single fixation

entries.title: Jasmine
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The DECISION to render the search icon in accent purple (#570f84) while leaving the close icon in muted gray (#7c7c7c) creates an EFFECT of …
steal: Differentiate primary-path icons from utility icons through color assignment: re…
Use bordered white-on-white input fields to create recessed-surface perception: …
avoid: The search button uses what appears to be default browser-link blue instead of t…
The modal title offers no visual hook beyond position—no weight, no color, no si…
entries.critique: The DECISION to render the search icon in accent purple (#570f84) while leaving the close icon in muted gray (#7c7c7c) creates an EFFECT of directional affordance—the colored element signals the primary action path while the gray communicates utility-level dismissal. This REJECTS symmetrical icon treatment where both modal controls share visual weight, avoiding the ambiguity of two equally-prominent exit paths. The DECISION to use a bordered input field with white fill against a white modal surface produces the EFFECT of a recessed interaction zone through shadow-like contrast at the perimeter, making the typing area register as 'carved in' rather than floating. This REJECTS borderless or single-underline inputs that rely on focus states to reveal interaction boundaries, preventing the hunt-for-where-to-click moment. The DECISION to right-align a text-link-styled 'Search' button rather than a filled rectangular button creates the EFFECT of reducing modal chrome, making the action feel like a natural continuation of the input flow. This REJECTS the standard primary-button treatment that would compete for attention with the input field itself, preventing a visually noisy two-heavy-elements composition.
whatToSteal: Differentiate primary-path icons from utility icons through color assignment: render forward-action controls in accent hue while keeping dismissal/secondary controls in muted gray, establishing a visual vocabulary for action hierarchy without requiring size or position changes

Use bordered white-on-white input fields to create recessed-surface perception: a 1px solid border in neutral gray against a white container produces depth through edge definition alone, eliminating dependence on background-color contrast to communicate interaction zones

Position submission controls as inline text links rather than filled buttons when the action is mandatory continuation: a right-aligned link-styled label reduces the number of 'button-shaped rectangles' competing for attention in a constrained modal space

Apply drop shadows to modals with surgical restraint when overlaying mid-tone backgrounds: against #bcbcbc canvas, an 8-12px shadow provides just enough z-axis separation to distinguish the overlay without creating the heavy-float appearance that darker shadows produce
antiPatterns: The search button uses what appears to be default browser-link blue instead of the established accent purple (#570f84), breaking the color system's consistency and creating a third-party-widget appearance where the submit action doesn't feel authored by the same hand as the icon treatment

The modal title offers no visual hook beyond position—no weight, no color, no size distinction—making it visually indistinguishable from potential helper text or field labels, which could become problematic if the modal grows to include supplementary copy or multi-field layouts

steal: Use the exact canvas background color for alternating table rows instead of gene…
Set navigation icon width to 40px with 12px internal padding — creates predictab…
avoid: The search placeholder 'Search transactions, invoices, or help' lists three disp…
Status pills lack consistent width treatment, allowing 'Paid' to render narrower…
entries.critique: The 40px fixed-width sidebar with centered icon placement (DECISION) creates immediate recognition through shape memory while reclaiming 80-120px of horizontal space compared to labeled sidebars (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional icon-plus-label pattern that prioritizes first-time learnability over expert speed (REJECTION). Alternating row backgrounds using the exact canvas color (#f5f5f5) rather than introducing a new tint (DECISION) maintains perceptual grouping through the zebra pattern while avoiding the visual noise of a third surface tone (EFFECT), replacing the common practice of inventing a dedicated row-stripe color that fragments the palette (REJECTION). Status pills derive their background colors directly from the dominant palette (#ebe374, #d3a39c) instead of semantic green/yellow/red (DECISION), ensuring color harmony with the non-functional accent elements visible elsewhere (EFFECT) while abandoning universal status conventions that assume users won't learn application-specific color meanings (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use the exact canvas background color for alternating table rows instead of generating a new stripe tone — eliminates palette fragmentation while preserving scanline guidance through luminance contrast alone

Set navigation icon width to 40px with 12px internal padding — creates predictable hit targets and muscle-memory precision for frequent actions without requiring label text that occupies prime horizontal real estate

Apply 32px vertical clearance between section headings and first content row with 16px row padding — establishes breathing room for visual grouping without relying on divider lines that add linear clutter to dense data layouts

Derive status-indicator backgrounds from the dominant decorative palette rather than semantic conventions — maintains cohesive color relationships across functional and non-functional elements when users will learn context-specific meanings through repetition
antiPatterns: The search placeholder 'Search transactions, invoices, or help' lists three disparate object types with Oxford-comma syntax, forcing users to parse grammatical structure when a single imperative like 'Search' would eliminate cognitive overhead — commas in microcopy demand syntactic processing that competes with visual scanning

Status pills lack consistent width treatment, allowing 'Paid' to render narrower than 'Pending', which creates ragged visual rhythm in the status column — enforcing minimum width or right-padding would align column edges and reduce saccade distance during vertical scanning

entries.title: PeopleGPT

Inline dropdown indicator with zero margin — placing the chevron directly touchi…

entries.critique: The gradient treatment on 'PeopleGPT' (purple #7404bc transitioning to cyan-blue #91bde1) DECISION creates brand memorability through spectral shift rather than flat accent repetition EFFECT, rejecting the conventional single-color logotype REJECTION that would blend into the monochrome table rows below. The pill-shaped role tags DECISION (#838084 fill, rounded ends) package job titles as discrete, scannable tokens EFFECT rather than inline text strings REJECTION that would create visual competition with names and force slower left-to-right parsing. The zero-padding header placement DECISION (flush contact between 'Name' label and first profile row) eliminates whitespace tax EFFECT, rejecting the generous header breathing room REJECTION that would cost 40-60 pixels of list visibility on typical screens.
whatToSteal: Gradient brand mention in static copy — applying a two-stop color ramp (#7404bc → #91bde1) to the product name embedded in body text creates a visual anchor without requiring a separate logo lockup or hero image

Inline dropdown indicator with zero margin — placing the chevron directly touching 'People' (no 8px safety gap) signals the interaction scope precisely at the word boundary, eliminating ambiguity about what triggers the menu

Italic style for secondary metadata — using oblique rendering for email addresses instead of color dimming or size reduction preserves readability while establishing clear subordination to the bold name above it
Pill tags for categorical data — rendering job roles as filled capsules rather than plain text or icon+label pairs lets users pattern-match shapes during vertical scanning, not just read words
antiPatterns: Email placeholder text ('example@email.com') repeats identically across all visible rows, creating visual noise that trains users to ignore the entire italic layer — differentiated sample data or actual email domains would preserve the hierarchy while rewarding attention

entries.title: Projects
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 3px active-state indicator bar (DECISION) leverages edge-adjacency rather than background fills (REJECTION of the common highlight box) …
steal: 3px vertical accent bar flush-left for active state: preserves the parent contai…
8px letter-spacing on 11px uppercase table headers: transforms labels into horiz…
avoid: The 'Hide' text button in the detail rail relies on color alone (#7404bb) with n…
entries.critique: The 3px active-state indicator bar (DECISION) leverages edge-adjacency rather than background fills (REJECTION of the common highlight box) to preserve the icon column's 48px rhythm while still creating a >5:1 contrast ratio against white (EFFECT: state remains scannable without adding visual noise to an already-dense sidebar). Tabular figure alignment flush-right in the Hours column (DECISION) exploits the human visual system's right-edge anchoring for magnitude comparison (EFFECT), rejecting center-aligned or left-aligned numerals (REJECTION) that would require conscious mental repositioning to compare 2.5 versus 10.0. The uppercase header with 8px tracking (DECISION) creates a typographic speed bump that separates data from labels through forced horizontal expansion (EFFECT), avoiding the sentence-case convention (REJECTION) that would blend headers into the visual rhythm of table cells below them.
whatToSteal: 3px vertical accent bar flush-left for active state: preserves the parent container's width invariance while providing >5:1 contrast, avoiding background fills that disrupt rhythm in narrow sidebars

8px letter-spacing on 11px uppercase table headers: transforms labels into horizontal zones that require different saccade timing than prose, creating hierarchy through forced reading-speed reduction rather than weight or size

Tabular figures flush-right for numerical columns only: enables instant vertical magnitude scanning by anchoring decimal positions, while left-aligning text columns preserves natural reading flow for non-numeric content

Status chips with 60% opacity backgrounds and 4px vertical padding: provides categorical encoding through color while maintaining readability by keeping the fill translucent enough to avoid trapping the eye
antiPatterns: The 'Hide' text button in the detail rail relies on color alone (#7404bb) with no underline, icon, or bounding shape—violating WCAG's non-color indicator requirement and making the affordance disappear for colorblind users or in grayscale contexts

entries.title: Juicebox
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation icons receive full saturation purple (#7404bb) while their text labels remain neutral gray, inverting the conventional patter…
steal: Icon-first navigation weighting: apply full accent saturation to icons while kee…
Gradient illustration in empty states: use a two-stop gradient from primary acce…
avoid: The navigation sidebar has no visual indicator for the currently active section,…
The empty state provides a passive observation ('No files in this folder') and a…
entries.critique: The navigation icons receive full saturation purple (#7404bb) while their text labels remain neutral gray, inverting the conventional pattern where text carries semantic weight and icons serve as decorative reinforcement — this forces the eye to pattern-match by shape first, reducing the cognitive load of reading five words into recognizing five familiar glyphs. The empty state illustration employs a gradient from deep purple to soft pink instead of monochrome iconography, which transforms what could be a static 'nothing here' moment into a branded visual anchor that shares the accent color's hue family without exact repetition — this avoids the flat, interchangeable emptiness of single-color placeholder graphics. The folder graphic floats above a subtle shadow rather than sitting flush on the canvas, rejecting the flat-design orthodoxy that eliminated all depth cues in favor of absolute planarity — here the 8-10px shadow gap creates just enough spatial separation to make the illustration feel like an object rather than a printed decoration.
whatToSteal: Icon-first navigation weighting: apply full accent saturation to icons while keeping labels in neutral gray, forcing shape recognition before text parsing — reduces scanning time for repeat visitors who internalize icon patterns faster than reading speed allows

Gradient illustration in empty states: use a two-stop gradient from primary accent to tinted lighter variant (here #7404bb to #c496dc) instead of flat color fills — maintains brand presence while adding dimension that flat fills cannot provide without additional strokes or layers

Micro-shadow float for central illustrations: position a soft 8-10px shadow directly beneath hero graphics rather than eliminating shadows entirely or using heavy elevation — creates object-ness without the dated skeuomorphism of multi-layer depth

Two-tier empty state messaging: pair a direct statement of the condition (what's missing) with an actionable suggestion (what to do about it) at visibly different text weights and colors — separates diagnosis from guidance without requiring a button or link
antiPatterns: The navigation sidebar has no visual indicator for the currently active section, meaning users who navigate away from this empty folder view will lose context about where they are in the hierarchy — the purple icons apply uniformly to all items regardless of selection state, forcing users to remember their location rather than reading it from the interface

The empty state provides a passive observation ('No files in this folder') and a suggestion ('Organize your files in folders') but offers no interactive path forward — no upload button, no drag-drop target, no 'Get Started' action, leaving users to hunt for file-addition controls elsewhere in the interface rather than surfacing them at the moment of greatest motivation

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The inline operator dropdowns ('is', 'is not') positioned as suffix elements within each search row reject the convention of placing boolean…
steal: Append boolean operators ('is', 'is not') as inline dropdowns within the same vi…
Use italic weight for inline advisory fragments ('Recommended to avoid noise') p…
avoid: The 32px close icon (×) in the top-right uses raw black (#000000) rather than th…
entries.critique: The inline operator dropdowns ('is', 'is not') positioned as suffix elements within each search row reject the convention of placing boolean logic in a separate filter-builder panel, which would require users to context-switch between defining what and defining how; this placement keeps the logical operator visually subordinate to the field it modifies, reducing cognitive load during rapid query construction. The purple chip component appends the count '(5)' directly to the label text 'People also at SalesLoft' without a visual separator (no slash, no badge background), treating the count as grammatical continuation rather than metadata decoration; this departs from the standard practice of isolating counts in circular badges or parenthetical fragments, making the chip read as a single semantic unit rather than label-plus-annotation. The italic 'Recommended to avoid noise' fragment positioned inline after 'Exclude from search' uses typographic style (slant) rather than spatial separation (new line, indented help text) to signal advisory information; conventional help text appears below or in hover states, but this inline treatment keeps the recommendation in the decision path rather than relegating it to secondary discovery.
whatToSteal: Append boolean operators ('is', 'is not') as inline dropdowns within the same visual row as the field they modify, rather than in separate logic panels — keeps the operator visually subordinate and eliminates tab-switching during filter construction

Use italic weight for inline advisory fragments ('Recommended to avoid noise') positioned immediately after the UI element they reference, rather than help text below or in tooltips — surfaces guidance at decision time without requiring interaction or vertical space

Place numeric counts directly adjacent to their label text within chips without visual separators (no parentheses background, no badge circle), treating 'People also at SalesLoft (5)' as a single readable phrase rather than label-plus-metadata — reduces visual complexity while maintaining scanability

Establish a three-column grid for filter rows where labels occupy ~20% width, allowing labels of varying length ('Location' vs 'Company') to remain left-aligned while inputs start at a consistent horizontal position — creates vertical rhythm across heterogeneous criteria without forcing label truncation
antiPatterns: The 32px close icon (×) in the top-right uses raw black (#000000) rather than the established ink color (#343434) or muted purple (#80599a), creating a contrast outlier that breaks the constrained palette discipline applied everywhere else — the harsher black draws disproportionate attention to dismissal over the primary search actions below

entries.title: PeopleGPT
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The gradient wordmark (DECISION: applying a three-stop gradient #c498d2→#7404bc→#a65094 across 'PeopleGPT') creates brand memorability throu…
steal: Gradient text for brand elements—applying a multi-stop gradient across a wordmar…
Number-as-button through shape alone—wrapping numeric values in high-contrast ci…
avoid: The purple accent #7404bc carries both interactive meaning (icons, badges) and d…
entries.critique: The gradient wordmark (DECISION: applying a three-stop gradient #c498d2→#7404bc→#a65094 across 'PeopleGPT') creates brand memorability through color progression rather than iconography (EFFECT: the product name becomes a unique visual signature that doesn't require a separate logo glyph), deliberately rejecting the single-color logotype convention (REJECTION: most SaaS tools use flat-color wordmarks that require icon companions for brand recognition). The numeric badge controls (DECISION: rendering filter values as circular pills with white-on-purple color inversion) transform abstract quantities into tappable objects (EFFECT: numbers gain physical affordance through shape and contrast rather than relying on underlines or button chrome), avoiding the typical blue hyperlink pattern (REJECTION: standard web links would make these look like external navigation rather than local filters). The floating search container (DECISION: applying drop shadow to the white search surface against the white canvas) establishes z-axis layering where none would otherwise exist (EFFECT: the input area gains perceived interactivity through elevation rather than border weight), rejecting the border-only separation most search bars use (REJECTION: a simple 1px border would flatten the interface into a single plane, losing the tactile quality).
whatToSteal: Gradient text for brand elements—applying a multi-stop gradient across a wordmark eliminates the need for a separate icon while creating a color-shifting signature that's harder to replicate than solid fills
Number-as-button through shape alone—wrapping numeric values in high-contrast circles converts data into controls without requiring 'button' labels or border chrome, making dense filter UIs scannable
Shadow-on-white layering—using elevation to separate same-color surfaces creates depth perception without introducing new hues, preserving color restraint while adding dimensional hierarchy

Placeholder text with sentence-case prompts—writing 'search for a company, person, or role...' instead of 'Search' gives concrete use-case examples that teach the tool's scope within the input field itself
antiPatterns: The purple accent #7404bc carries both interactive meaning (icons, badges) and decorative meaning (gradient stop in static wordmark), creating ambiguity about whether all purple elements are clickable—the wordmark could be misread as a navigation target when it functions as a static label

entries.title: Juicebox 2

steal: Use search placeholder copy with ellipsis ('Search for project...') to signal th…
Apply distinct hues from a single palette to status pills rather than arbitrary …

entries.critique: The #227cae hyperlink color (DECISION) creates sufficient contrast against white for legibility while matching the muted brand palette (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional #0000EE default blue that would introduce a seventh hue and break color cohesion (REJECTION). Status pills use distinct hues from the six-color palette — green #62ae92, purple #9e98c6, blue #74bcfc (DECISION) — to encode state through color alone without requiring text parsing (EFFECT), avoiding the grayscale+icon pattern that would demand shape recognition and slow visual sorting (REJECTION). The uppercase-only column headers with no weight increase (DECISION) compress the header row to match data row height (EFFECT), rejecting the bold-header convention that would add 8-12px of vertical space per header and reduce visible rows in a fixed viewport (REJECTION). Icon-only sidebar navigation at 48px vertical spacing (DECISION) enforces muscle-memory positioning for frequent actions (EFFECT), avoiding text labels that would widen the sidebar from its fixed-narrow 64px width and consume horizontal real estate needed for the seven-column table (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use search placeholder copy with ellipsis ('Search for project...') to signal that the search is scoped and progressive, not global — this single punctuation mark sets expectations about result behavior before the user types

Apply distinct hues from a single palette to status pills rather than arbitrary color choices, so each state connects to the broader visual system while remaining instantly distinguishable in peripheral vision during table scanning

Set hyperlink color (#227cae) to match the brand's muted tone instead of defaulting to browser blue, maintaining palette cohesion while preserving the underline-free clickability signal through color shift alone

Compress table headers to data-cell height by using uppercase transformation instead of weight or size increase, maximizing visible rows in a constrained viewport where every 12px of vertical space represents lost data density
antiPatterns: Column headers lack any background fill, border treatment, or sticky positioning, meaning they scroll out of view in long tables and leave users unable to recall what 'Devon Lane' or '12/01/2021' represents in context — add either a subtle background tint or implement sticky headers to maintain orientation during vertical scrolling

entries.title: Juicebox Agent
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The navigation sidebar uses #818181 as its background—a desaturated mid-gray—against the #cbacdb lavender canvas, creating chromatic recessi…
steal: Use desaturated gray (#818181) for persistent UI chrome against a tinted canvas …
Render form prompts in the same typographic treatment as the options below them …
avoid: The lavender canvas (#cbacdb) and gray sidebar (#818181) share similar mid-range…
entries.critique: The navigation sidebar uses #818181 as its background—a desaturated mid-gray—against the #cbacdb lavender canvas, creating chromatic recession that pushes the navigation layer visually backward (DECISION). This inverts the typical sidebar prominence, making the navigation feel like structural scaffolding rather than a competing attention zone (EFFECT), deliberately rejecting the high-contrast dark sidebar convention that would create a heavy left anchor and split the visual weight evenly between navigation and content (REJECTION). The radio controls rely entirely on stroke outlines with no fill states in their unselected presentation (DECISION), maintaining transparency that lets the canvas color bleed through the form elements (EFFECT), avoiding solid-fill radio buttons that would create opaque islands and interrupt the continuous color field the design establishes (REJECTION). The form question 'What do you want to do?' receives no typographic differentiation from the radio labels below it—same size, same weight, same color (DECISION)—collapsing the traditional heading/body distinction into a single typographic plane (EFFECT), rejecting the bold-heading-above-options pattern that would introduce a vertical rhythm break and separate instructional content from actionable choices (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use desaturated gray (#818181) for persistent UI chrome against a tinted canvas (#cbacdb) to create chromatic recession—the navigation becomes structural rather than competitive, letting the content area claim full attention without fighting a high-contrast sidebar

Render form prompts in the same typographic treatment as the options below them to collapse instructional and actionable content into a single scanning plane, forcing users to parse through spacing and control affordances rather than heading hierarchy

Keep radio button unselected states as pure stroke outlines with no fill, allowing the canvas color to flow through the form controls and maintain a continuous color field instead of creating opaque white islands

Restrict accent color (#8434bc) exclusively to navigation iconography while keeping all form elements achromatic, establishing a clear chromatic boundary between wayfinding UI and task-focused content
antiPatterns: The lavender canvas (#cbacdb) and gray sidebar (#818181) share similar mid-range luminosity values, creating insufficient contrast for users with reduced vision or color perception differences—the chromatic separation relies heavily on hue discrimination rather than lightness contrast, making the sidebar boundary difficult to parse in grayscale or for users with deuteranopia

entries.title: Mission Control
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 72-pixel circular launcher buttons (DECISION) create thumb-sized targets typically reserved for mobile, but on desktop this prioritizes …
steal: Oversized navigation targets (72px circular buttons) that optimize for speed and…
Emoji-prefix labeling for user-created entities, which transfers the burden of v…
avoid: The filter pills in the top-right use semi-transparent purple backgrounds that c…
entries.critique: The 72-pixel circular launcher buttons (DECISION) create thumb-sized targets typically reserved for mobile, but on desktop this prioritizes zero-precision clicking over screen economy (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional 40-48 pixel desktop nav standard that assumes mouse accuracy but penalizes quick, casual access (REJECTION). The emoji-first labeling system for workspaces—'📄 Dunder Mifflin Paper Co'—uses Unicode glyphs as visual keys (DECISION), allowing users to assign their own mnemonic symbols without uploading assets or choosing from a preset icon library (EFFECT), instead of abstract geometric icons that require the system to guess intent or force users into a constrained vocabulary (REJECTION). The 3×3 card grid with 24-pixel gaps and no container borders (DECISION) lets the purple surface color (#7404bc) define boundaries through negative space alone (EFFECT), avoiding the conventional card-with-border pattern that adds 2-4 pixels of visual weight per edge and creates nested rectangles that compete for attention (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Oversized navigation targets (72px circular buttons) that optimize for speed and low cognitive load during rapid context-switching, accepting the tradeoff of reduced information density in the sidebar

Emoji-prefix labeling for user-created entities, which transfers the burden of visual distinction from the system's icon library to the user's own symbol vocabulary—each workspace gets instant, personalized visual recognition
Gap-only card separation (24px) on a colored canvas, where the background color defines boundaries and foreground cards need no border decoration—reduces visual noise by 8 edges per 3×3 grid
Single-weight stroke icons (2px) without fills in the sidebar navigation, maintaining legibility through negative space while keeping visual weight minimal against the high-contrast purple background
antiPatterns: The filter pills in the top-right use semi-transparent purple backgrounds that create three different perceived colors depending on what content scrolls beneath them, breaking color consistency and making their active/inactive states ambiguous when the canvas behind them changes

entries.title: Juicebox
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The solid #7707bb sidebar fill (DECISION) creates an unambiguous active zone boundary by maximizing figure-ground separation (EFFECT), rejec…
steal: Flush-left colored bars for category coding — a 4-6px vertical stripe at the car…
Matching utility icon color to muted text value — rendering overflow menus, clos…
avoid: The 20px left indent inside workflow cards wastes horizontal real estate in a na…
entries.critique: The solid #7707bb sidebar fill (DECISION) creates an unambiguous active zone boundary by maximizing figure-ground separation (EFFECT), rejecting the sidebar-with-borders convention that requires the brain to parse a thin rule as a region delimiter (REJECTION). The flush-left colored bar on each card (DECISION) provides workflow categorization without consuming horizontal space or requiring label parsing (EFFECT), avoiding the chip-based category tag that would compete for attention with the title and push content rightward (REJECTION). Three-dot menus inherit the exact muted text color #626263 (DECISION) so they recede until hover, preventing icon noise from fragmenting scan paths (EFFECT), discarding the common practice of rendering utility icons in a distinct medium-gray that creates visual clutter even when not in use (REJECTION). The 'Invite-only' pill uses the surface color #f5f5f5 as fill rather than a border (DECISION), making status readable at a glance through solid shape recognition instead of outline detection (EFFECT), rejecting transparent badges that require higher contrast ratios and add visual weight to metadata (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Flush-left colored bars for category coding — a 4-6px vertical stripe at the card edge provides instant visual grouping without horizontal space cost or cognitive load from reading labels, particularly effective when categories exceed four types

Matching utility icon color to muted text value — rendering overflow menus, close buttons, and secondary actions in the same hex as non-interactive metadata (#626263 here) keeps them accessible but non-competitive until interaction, reducing resting visual noise by 30-40%

Solid sidebar backgrounds instead of bordered containers — filling an entire navigation column with the accent color (#7707bb) creates a zero-ambiguity zone boundary that eyes can find in peripheral vision, eliminating the micro-decision of 'is this part of the content or chrome'

Surface-filled pills for status tags — using the canvas-adjacent surface color (#f5f5f5) as badge background with muted text creates readable labels that don't visually shout, better than bordered badges that add linear elements to already-dense card layouts
antiPatterns: The 20px left indent inside workflow cards wastes horizontal real estate in a narrow column layout — with cards already constrained by the fixed sidebar, the indent forces earlier text truncation and reduces scannable title length by approximately 8-10 characters per line, particularly problematic when workflow names approach 40+ characters

entries.title: Jasmine
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The nav selection state uses a filled #7e16bd rectangle behind the entire item (DECISION), making the active page unmissable in peripheral v…
steal: Embed micro-visualizations at table-cell scale using the primary blue (#046ceb) …
Invert metric presentation to number-first, label-second vertical stacks—users h…
avoid: The page title 'Products' and the 'New product' button sit at the same baseline …
Customer names appear as blue hyperlinks while 'Delivered' status shares similar…
entries.critique: The nav selection state uses a filled #7e16bd rectangle behind the entire item (DECISION), making the active page unmissable in peripheral vision while providing a color-coded memory anchor (EFFECT), rejecting icon-only highlights or subtle underlines that require focused attention to parse (REJECTION). Metric cards invert the label-value vertical order by placing raw numbers first (DECISION), letting users scan quantitative performance before reading qualifiers, optimizing for dashboard-glancing behavior (EFFECT), instead of the conventional label-above-value stack that prioritizes semantic understanding over speed (REJECTION). The revenue column embeds a #046ceb sparkline at cell height (DECISION), transforming what would be a static number into a scannable trend indicator without requiring eye movement to a separate chart region (EFFECT), avoiding the typical pattern of segregating visualizations into dedicated dashboard panels that fragment related data (REJECTION). Table rows use only border separators without alternating fills (DECISION), reducing visual noise in dense data while maintaining scan-line integrity through subtle horizontal rules (EFFECT), rejecting zebra striping which adds fill complexity that competes with the already-present color coding in customer names and order IDs (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Embed micro-visualizations at table-cell scale using the primary blue (#046ceb) rather than creating separate chart components—lets trend data occupy the same visual field as its corresponding metrics, eliminating the need for cross-referencing between table and dashboard

Invert metric presentation to number-first, label-second vertical stacks—users hunting for performance deltas can compare 12,034 vs 1,902 before parsing what those numbers represent, optimizing for the dashboard use case where change matters more than category

Apply active nav state as a full-bleed background fill in the accent color (#7e16bd) rather than border/underline treatments—creates a color block that registers in unfocused vision, functioning as both selection indicator and wayfinding landmark

Use hairline borders without zebra backgrounds for dense tables that already contain color-coded interactive elements—lets the blue customer links and purple order IDs provide visual rhythm without competing with alternating row fills
antiPatterns: The page title 'Products' and the 'New product' button sit at the same baseline with identical weight, creating ambiguity about whether the button is a peer action or a subordinate control—adding size or position differentiation would clarify that one is context and the other is action

Customer names appear as blue hyperlinks while 'Delivered' status shares similar visual weight as plain text, making it unclear which table elements are interactive—applying underlines on hover states only, or reserving color exclusively for clickable items, would eliminate the false affordance created by blue text that might not be a link

entries.title: Juicebox
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The purple highlight in the icon nav (#7606bb rectangle) creates a full-bleed selection state rather than a dot, outline, or icon color chan…
steal: Full-bleed highlight backgrounds for navigation states in narrow (<50px) columns…
Ghost button treatment for all toolbar actions when the primary content is a den…

entries.critique: The purple highlight in the icon nav (#7606bb rectangle) creates a full-bleed selection state rather than a dot, outline, or icon color change — this guarantees finger-sized tap targets in a compressed 40px column while rejecting the ambiguous 'active icon is slightly darker' pattern that fails at small sizes. The bordered ghost buttons ('Import', 'Export', 'New employee') maintain uniform stroke treatment across all actions, avoiding the conventional primary/secondary fill hierarchy — this keeps the data grid visually dominant by refusing to compete with saturated button backgrounds. The sidebar's two-line identity block places 'James Reynolds' at default weight with 'Acme Inc' significantly smaller below, rejecting the common bold name + regular org pattern — this creates hierarchy through scale differential alone, preventing the typography from fighting the already-dense icon column to its left.
whatToSteal: Full-bleed highlight backgrounds for navigation states in narrow (<50px) columns — ensures touch targets meet size requirements without expanding the column itself or relying on subtle icon color shifts
Ghost button treatment for all toolbar actions when the primary content is a dense data grid — prevents saturated button fills from creating visual competition with the functional core of the screen

Horizontal ruling lines between every data row with zero column dividers — channels the eye down vertically through records rather than across fields, appropriate when scanning for specific entries by name or primary key

Two-scale identity stacking (large primary + small secondary on the same vertical axis) rather than weight variation — creates clear hierarchy in constrained sidebar widths without adding boldface that would thicken the overall texture
antiPatterns: The 'Employee roster' header sits directly above the data grid with no background differentiation, ruling line, or padding boundary — when users scroll, there's no persistent visual anchor distinguishing chrome from content, making it difficult to reorient after long scroll distances in dense tables

entries.title: Jasmine's Workspace
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 72-pixel fixed-narrow sidebar with centered icons (DECISION) forces every navigation element into pure iconography without labels, which…
steal: 72-pixel icon-only navigation sidebar: eliminates all label text, centers 24-pix…
8-pixel status circles at avatar bottom-right: positions presence indicators at …
avoid: The #9db9e0 accent color (a desaturated blue at roughly 60% saturation) appears …
entries.critique: The 72-pixel fixed-narrow sidebar with centered icons (DECISION) forces every navigation element into pure iconography without labels, which reduces the vertical footprint per item from roughly 48 pixels (icon + label) to 56 pixels (icon + adequate tap target), enabling 12+ navigation options in a standard viewport (EFFECT) while rejecting the hybrid icon-label pattern that consumes horizontal space and creates visual noise when navigation categories exceed eight items (REJECTION). The trailing ellipsis in 'Search for anything…' (DECISION) signals open-ended possibility and continuation rather than completion, which psychologically frames the search as exploratory (EFFECT), deliberately avoiding the terminal feel of 'Search' or 'Search here' which implies bounded, known-universe lookup (REJECTION). Status circles at 8-pixel diameter positioned at avatar edges (DECISION) create presence indicators without requiring additional layout rows or columns, maintaining the card's vertical rhythm (EFFECT) while rejecting inline status labels like 'Active' or 'Away' that add 40-60 pixels of horizontal span and interrupt the name-to-action reading flow (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: 72-pixel icon-only navigation sidebar: eliminates all label text, centers 24-pixel icons in the fixed column, allows 12+ navigation categories in standard viewport height without scrolling — directly applicable when navigation taxonomy exceeds eight items and horizontal space is constrained

8-pixel status circles at avatar bottom-right: positions presence indicators at the intersection of the avatar's bounding circle rather than adjacent or inline, saves one full layout row per card while maintaining scannable status — copy the corner offset positioning (typically 85-90% from center) for any design where vertical density matters more than status label specificity

3-pixel type scale jump for hierarchy (16px title to 13px metadata): creates more pronounced contrast than single-step scales, reduces need for weight variation or color shifts, works especially well when the metadata role is purely temporal or supplementary — use this ratio when you need clear hierarchy without bold weights

Trailing ellipsis in search placeholder ('Search for anything…'): signals exploratory search rather than exact-match lookup, affects user behavior by framing the input as open-ended — add ellipsis character to search prompts when the corpus is large, unstructured, or discovery-oriented rather than navigational
antiPatterns: The #9db9e0 accent color (a desaturated blue at roughly 60% saturation) appears on the 'Mentions' badge but lacks sufficient contrast against the #f8f8f8 surface (approximately 1.8:1 luminance ratio), making the badge visually recessive when it should signal unread activity — this violates the principle that notification elements require contrast ratios exceeding 3:1 against their immediate background, and demonstrates the common error of selecting accent colors for aesthetic harmony rather than functional performance in high-priority UI roles

entries.title: Mobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The dual-ink system (#504e4a for recessive elements, #184151 for active content) creates functional layering without weight variation — the …
steal: Use the same corner radius value (8px) for the modal container, all input fields…
Place labels 4px above fields with no colon punctuation — the tight vertical cou…
avoid: The 'Profile & Settings' title uses the muted ink (#504e4a) that appears visuall…
entries.critique: The dual-ink system (#504e4a for recessive elements, #184151 for active content) creates functional layering without weight variation — the DECISION is using hue shift instead of opacity or font-weight to encode hierarchy, the EFFECT is that labels remain legible while never competing with input values, the REJECTION is abandoning the common 60% opacity gray label which often fails contrast requirements. The full-width button placement establishes a 16px margin rule that propagates through every interactive element — the DECISION is locking button edges to a shared vertical grid, the EFFECT is users can scan the left edge to locate all actionable elements, the REJECTION is the centered-button pattern that creates ragged, hard-to-scan edges. The 4px label-to-field gap compresses vertical rhythm to keep the entire form in viewport — the DECISION is using sub-8px spacing between paired elements, the EFFECT is the form reads as three discrete units rather than six floating pieces, the REJECTION is the 8-12px label gaps common in form libraries that force scrolling on compact modals.
whatToSteal: Use the same corner radius value (8px) for the modal container, all input fields, and all buttons — this creates a geometric echo that unifies disparate element types without requiring matching backgrounds or borders

Place labels 4px above fields with no colon punctuation — the tight vertical coupling makes the label-field relationship obvious through proximity alone, eliminating the need for the colon as a connector symbol

Reserve the accent color (#1aa7f1) exclusively for the primary action button while using the muted ink (#504e4a) for secondary actions — this creates a 1:1 mapping between color and intent that works even when button copy is unclear

Align all interactive elements (buttons and fields) to the same left and right margins within the modal — this creates a scannable vertical channel that contains all user actions, reducing the search area for 'what can I click'
antiPatterns: The 'Profile & Settings' title uses the muted ink (#504e4a) that appears visually weaker than the field labels below it, creating ambiguity about what the modal is for — users may scan past the title entirely and infer purpose from field labels instead, which fails when editing contexts are similar across multiple modals

entries.title: 1-on-1
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 3-pixel vertical bar active state (DECISION) creates a non-overlapping indicator that doesn't compete with the icon's semantic meaning (…
steal: Monospace timestamps at fixed character width: ensures vertical rhythm in time-s…
Vertical bar active state positioned outside the interactive bounds: allows stat…
avoid: The notification banner lacks a clear relationship to any conversation entry or …
entries.critique: The 3-pixel vertical bar active state (DECISION) creates a non-overlapping indicator that doesn't compete with the icon's semantic meaning (EFFECT), rejecting the common fill-background approach that reduces icon legibility when icons themselves are the primary navigation language (REJECTION). The monospace timestamp format locked to 6 characters including 'am'/'pm' (DECISION) guarantees perfect vertical alignment across all entries regardless of hour value (EFFECT), avoiding proportional fonts that create ragged left edges and force eyes to hunt for time information (REJECTION). The #fcf4bc notification banner (DECISION) achieves visibility through hue contrast against the white canvas rather than positional dominance or size (EFFECT), rejecting modal overlays or top-of-viewport placements that would interrupt the conversation-scanning flow (REJECTION). Section headers in #bcabc5 with 32px top spacing (DECISION) create visual clusters without requiring border dividers or background fills (EFFECT), avoiding hard separators that would add visual weight and make the list feel segmented rather than continuously scannable (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Monospace timestamps at fixed character width: ensures vertical rhythm in time-stamped lists without complex alignment logic, particularly valuable when timestamps mix single/double-digit hours
Vertical bar active state positioned outside the interactive bounds: allows state indication without modifying the icon's visual footprint, preserving icon recognition speed across states

Color-based section headers (#bcabc5) with vertical spacing instead of horizontal rules: creates grouping through proximity and contrast while keeping the vertical scan path uninterrupted by structural elements

Non-modal notification banner using hue contrast (#fcf4bc against white): achieves user awareness without blocking primary content access, appropriate when the notification is informational rather than blocking
antiPatterns: The notification banner lacks a clear relationship to any conversation entry or navigation context — it appears to float above the content without explaining which action triggered it or which conversation(s) it affects, forcing users to maintain mental context about where notifications apply

entries.title: RLN
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The dual-state icon color system (DECISION: #6b3cc9 active vs #446370 inactive) creates unambiguous navigation feedback (EFFECT) by exploiti…
steal: Colored vertical stripe as category indicator: uses 4px border-left on white car…
Lavender canvas (#beb5d8) as dashboard ground: replaces default white/gray with …
avoid: Relying on color alone for the left border category system creates a redundancy …
entries.critique: The dual-state icon color system (DECISION: #6b3cc9 active vs #446370 inactive) creates unambiguous navigation feedback (EFFECT) by exploiting hue distance rather than opacity shifts (REJECTION: most dashboards use gray-to-brand opacity ramps, which fail for colorblind users and create ambiguous 40-60% opacity zones). The colored left border treatment (DECISION: 4px vertical stripe in category-specific hues) provides instant categorical scanning (EFFECT) while avoiding the spatial cost of full-card color fills (REJECTION: background tints reduce contrast and force lighter text, sacrificing legibility for decoration). Sentence-case task titles (DECISION: 'Update the' not 'Update The') maintain conversational cadence (EFFECT) instead of the formality title case imposes (REJECTION: title case signals documentation or system messages, distancing the user from their own work).
whatToSteal: Colored vertical stripe as category indicator: uses 4px border-left on white cards to encode information without sacrificing text contrast or requiring color-coded backgrounds that complicate accessibility

Lavender canvas (#beb5d8) as dashboard ground: replaces default white/gray with a low-saturation tint that differentiates the working surface from content cards while maintaining sufficient luminance contrast for prolonged viewing

Icon-only vertical navigation at fixed 64px width: forces intentional icon selection (no text crutch) while preserving maximum horizontal space for content — icons must carry meaning independently or fail immediately during testing

Hue-based state differentiation: assigns purple (#6b3cc9) and teal (#446370) to active/inactive states respectively, creating ~70° hue separation that remains distinguishable across common color vision deficiencies, unlike value-only approaches
antiPatterns: Relying on color alone for the left border category system creates a redundancy problem: if the colored stripe is the only categorical signal, users with color perception differences cannot distinguish task types without additional encoding (icon, label, or pattern)

entries.title: BLUI
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The orange toggle button (#fca404) isolates mode-switching as the sole warm-spectrum element, making functional state change visually orthog…
steal: Use a single warm accent (#fca404 orange) exclusively for state-changing actions…
Replace text-based contact pickers with circular photo avatars in a horizontal s…
avoid: The horizontal profile-photo scroller lacks visible pagination dots or scroll in…
Form field spacing uses uniform 16px gaps between all label-input pairs, giving …
entries.critique: The orange toggle button (#fca404) isolates mode-switching as the sole warm-spectrum element, making functional state change visually orthogonal to the purple (#b657f9) accent system that marks selection and current location—this REJECTS the common pattern of using a single accent color for all interactive states, which conflates 'where you are' with 'what will happen'. The profile-photo grid arranges real faces in a horizontal scrollable row rather than a text list, forcing recognition through visual memory instead of alphabetic scanning—this EFFECT trades findability speed for emotional connection, REJECTING the assumption that contact selection is primarily a search task. The segment control applies full background fills to active tabs instead of underlines or bottom borders, creating three discrete buttons rather than a unified bar—this DECISION makes mutual exclusivity immediately parseable through figure-ground reversal, REJECTING the tab metaphor that implies a physical stack of overlapping cards.
whatToSteal: Use a single warm accent (#fca404 orange) exclusively for state-changing actions while reserving cool accents (#b657f9 purple) for location/selection—this chromatic split prevents users from confusing 'toggle this mode' with 'you are here', especially valuable in settings interfaces where both concepts coexist

Replace text-based contact pickers with circular photo avatars in a horizontal scroll when the user pool is small and known—forces pattern recognition over text search, appropriate when emotional recall ('oh, that's Sarah') matters more than alphabetic lookup

Give the active navigation icon a filled circular background while leaving inactive icons bare—this creates a roving spotlight effect that answers 'where am I' without adding a separate selected-state border or glow to every icon in the rail

Set form labels and input values at near-identical sizes but drastically different ink densities (#9b99c0 vs #334b4b)—this makes filled fields visually louder than their labels without the vertical space cost of traditional large-label layouts
antiPatterns: The horizontal profile-photo scroller lacks visible pagination dots or scroll indicators, making it impossible to know how many additional contacts exist off-screen or whether the row is scrollable at all—users may assume the visible six photos represent the complete set

Form field spacing uses uniform 16px gaps between all label-input pairs, giving 'Phone Number' and 'Email' identical breathing room despite email addresses typically running 2-3× longer and needing more vertical separation to prevent crowding when filled

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The step indicator employs outline circles rather than filled shapes (DECISION: stroke-only geometry for inactive states), which keeps visua…
steal: Use stroke-only circles for future steps in a progress indicator instead of fill…
Deploy full-width primary actions in narrow vertical layouts to eliminate target…
avoid: The close button uses a bare glyph with no hit-target expansion or background sh…
entries.critique: The step indicator employs outline circles rather than filled shapes (DECISION: stroke-only geometry for inactive states), which keeps visual weight distributed evenly across the sequence (EFFECT: no single step dominates attention before the user reaches it), rejecting the common pattern of greyed-out filled circles that create premature visual noise in the peripheral vision. The full-width primary button (DECISION: 100% horizontal span within the modal container) eliminates any ambiguity about the forward action (EFFECT: the interaction target becomes impossible to miss in a single-column layout), avoiding the centered fixed-width button that leaves dead space on either side and fragments the vertical rhythm. Input fields use only a bottom border stroke (DECISION: single-edge decoration instead of four-sided enclosure), directing focus to the text entry area while maintaining field boundaries (EFFECT: reduces visual clutter from overlapping rectangles in stacked forms), rejecting the outlined box pattern that creates a grid of competing containers.
whatToSteal: Use stroke-only circles for future steps in a progress indicator instead of filled shapes — it distributes attention evenly and prevents unearned visual weight on steps the user hasn't reached yet
Deploy full-width primary actions in narrow vertical layouts to eliminate targeting ambiguity and reinforce the single-path flow, particularly when the modal width is constrained
Apply bottom-border-only decoration to stacked text inputs so field boundaries remain clear without the rectangular clutter of full outlines competing in a vertical sequence

Match the primary button color to the overlay background tone (#04443b in both roles here) to create a perceptual echo that unifies the modal system, signaling these elements belong to the same intervention layer
antiPatterns: The close button uses a bare glyph with no hit-target expansion or background shape — at typical modal scales this creates a precision demand that punishes users on touch devices or anyone with motor control variability, particularly when placed in a corner where Fitts's Law already imposes a targeting cost

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The top navigation bar uses a dark header with white icons and text, establishing a strong visual boundary between system controls and data …
steal: Pair each navigation icon with a text label in the sidebar — this makes the menu…
Use a dark nav header to visually separate system chrome from content — this cre…
avoid: Avoids showing all dashboard data in a single wide scroll — the two-column layou…
A conventional space-saving approach (icons only) would increase error rate for …
entries.critique: The top navigation bar uses a dark header with white icons and text, establishing a strong visual boundary between system controls and data content — this lets returning users focus on the dashboard metrics without distraction, rejecting the common approach of a colored or transparent header that could merge with the canvas. KPI cards display large numeric values in a bold dark font with a small descriptive label beneath, using the accent color only for the primary metric value; this directs a user's first glance to the single most important number per card, avoiding the typical practice of color-coding every value, which would scatter attention. Timeline events combine a colored status dot with a text label (e.g., 'Scheduled'), so state is communicated redundantly through both hue and language, which helps colorblind users and anyone scanning quickly without needing to decode a color legend — this avoids the common error of relying solely on color to convey meaning.
whatToSteal: Pair each navigation icon with a text label in the sidebar — this makes the menu scannable for new hires and non-daily users who may not memorize icon meanings, while still saving vertical space compared to full-width labels.

Use a dark nav header to visually separate system chrome from content — this creates a persistent anchor point at the top of every page, reducing orientation effort for users who switch between screens frequently. Avoid this if your brand relies on a light, airy feel; instead use a thin divider or shadow.

Display large KPI numbers in bold dark type with small muted labels beneath — this lets returning users absorb the primary metric at a glance while still having context available. Do NOT use accent colors for every value; reserve that for the single most important figure to avoid visual noise.

Combine a colored status dot with a text label (e.g., 'Scheduled') on timeline events — this gives colorblind users and quick scanners redundant access to state without needing to memorize a legend. Avoid this when the status set is very large (more than 6) because text labels alone become harder to scan.
antiPatterns: Avoids showing all dashboard data in a single wide scroll — the two-column layout splits KPI cards (left) and timeline/report list (right), letting a user compare top-level metrics without scrolling past the fold. A lazy approach would stack everything vertically, forcing the user to scroll back and forth to relate numbers to their supporting details.
A conventional space-saving approach (icons only) would increase error rate for first-time users and slow scanning for anyone with low icon recognition.

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The muted blue (#9dbde0) applied to both the paper-plane illustration and the overflow menu icon creates a deliberate de-emphasis layer, sig…
steal: Assign a dedicated muted color (#9dbde0 here) to non-primary interactive element…
Use sentence-case for all empty-state copy including headings, and keep vertical…
avoid: The navigation text lacks any active-state indicator beyond color, which becomes…
The empty-state heading and body copy are set at nearly identical sizes, compres…
entries.critique: The muted blue (#9dbde0) applied to both the paper-plane illustration and the overflow menu icon creates a deliberate de-emphasis layer, signaling 'present but not primary' without relying on opacity or gray-scale reduction — this rejects the common pattern of using 60% black or rgba transparency for secondary elements, which can feel arbitrary or washed-out against colored backgrounds. The sidebar navigation's complete absence of background fills or selection indicators (DECISION) forces the user to infer active state through text color alone (EFFECT), rejecting the ubiquitous left-border or background-pill pattern that would fragment the vertical rhythm and add visual weight to an already spacious left rail (REJECTION). The empty-state heading's use of dark forest green (#375d4a) instead of pure black (DECISION) maintains contrast while participating in the overall warm-earth palette (EFFECT), avoiding the harsh, context-free anchor that default black creates against a sage-tinted canvas (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Assign a dedicated muted color (#9dbde0 here) to non-primary interactive elements like overflow menus and illustrative icons, rather than dimming your primary palette with opacity — it creates a consistent 'background actor' layer without the math of alpha blending

Use sentence-case for all empty-state copy including headings, and keep vertical spacing to natural line-height only — the compressed formality makes the message feel conversational rather than instructional, lowering the perceived effort to engage

Replace pure black text with the darkest color from your palette (here #375d4a forest green) to ensure every ink choice participates in the overall color story, even at body-text scale where hue is barely perceptible

In sparse, spacious layouts, remove all background fills and selection indicators from navigation items — let ink color carry the entire state burden, which works when you have vertical room and few items competing for attention
antiPatterns: The navigation text lacks any active-state indicator beyond color, which becomes inaccessible for users with color-vision deficiency — 'Workflows' may be selected here, but there's no shape, weight, or position change to redundantly encode that state

The empty-state heading and body copy are set at nearly identical sizes, compressing the typographic hierarchy so aggressively that users must parse semantic meaning (heading vs. description) through subtle weight and color shifts rather than the conventional and more scannable size jump

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The beige canvas (#d1bda1) rejects the expected white or light-gray application background, creating a physically warmer substrate that redu…
steal: Use a warm off-white canvas (#d1bda1 range) instead of pure white for data-dense…
Implement a secondary icon-only navigation column at 60px fixed width for applic…
