The form offers no preview of what happens after submission (no 'We'll text you …
entries.critique: The golden square (#fbdc06) positioned at the panel seam DECISION creates a physical hinge point EFFECT by giving the eye an anchor where the two-column split occurs, preventing the composition from reading as two separate floating rectangles REJECTION instead of the conventional full-bleed image extend that most split onboarding uses. The +1 prefix being locked inside the input boundary DECISION makes country code editing require the extra cognitive step of recognizing it as interactive EFFECT, sacrificing discoverability for the visual cohesion of a single unified field REJECTION instead of the standard separate dropdown that signals 'this is editable' through component differentiation. The complete absence of value-proposition copy in the form panel DECISION forces the illustration alone to communicate purpose EFFECT, removing the safety net of explanatory text REJECTION that typical onboarding relies on to handle users who don't parse imagery quickly.
whatToSteal: Use a solid geometric shape in a contrasting hue (#fbdc06 against #a8d5d5 and #ffffff) positioned exactly at structural seams — it transforms a potentially jarring layout boundary into an intentional compositional element that the eye can rest on

Lock the form flow to a single action path (one button, no skip link, no alternative) when you need completion data badly enough to accept the dropout cost — it eliminates decision paralysis but only works if the ask is genuinely low-friction

Deploy illustration without explanatory text overlay when the visual metaphor is strong enough to self-document — this creates a cleaner left panel but requires high confidence in the image's readability across your user base

Embed country code inside the input field boundary when your user base is geographically homogeneous enough that 95%+ never change it — reduces component count at the cost of discoverability for edge cases
antiPatterns: The teal canvas background (#a8d5d5) extends behind the form panel's white surface (#ffffff), creating a faint halo effect where the panels meet — this requires the golden square to work overtime as a visual separator because the panels lack sufficient inherent contrast to define their own boundaries clearly

The form offers no preview of what happens after submission (no 'We'll text you a code' microcopy, no illustration showing the next step) — users committing their phone number have to trust blindly, increasing friction for privacy-conscious segments

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The muted olive (#a4945e) for form labels is a DECISION that separates metadata from user input data, creating an EFFECT where the eye natur…
steal: Use the muted-color label pattern (#a4945e for labels, darker for values) to cre…
Deploy 3-4px vertical indicator bars in the accent color rather than background …
avoid: The page title 'Create an account' lacks any supporting microcopy or value propo…
Form labels and input fields use different colors from the established dominant …
entries.critique: The muted olive (#a4945e) for form labels is a DECISION that separates metadata from user input data, creating an EFFECT where the eye naturally scans entered values rather than label text, while REJECTING the conventional high-contrast label approach that treats every form element as equally important and creates visual noise when scanning filled forms. The vertical cyan indicator bar is a DECISION using a precise 3-4px width stroke, creating an EFFECT of spatial anchoring without consuming horizontal real estate, while REJECTING background-fill active states that would break the columnar rhythm and make the navigation feel heavier than the content it controls. The two-pattern button system is a DECISION where social login gets surface styling and the primary CTA gets fill styling, creating an EFFECT where the visual weight difference communicates action priority without relying on size or position alone, while REJECTING single-pattern button systems that force designers to use color alone (primary vs. secondary hues) and lose the structural distinction between owned-action and delegated-auth flows.
whatToSteal: Use the muted-color label pattern (#a4945e for labels, darker for values) to create scannable forms where user-entered data visually dominates the interface, making review and error-checking faster than equal-contrast labeling

Deploy 3-4px vertical indicator bars in the accent color rather than background fills for navigation active states, preserving the white surface unity while still providing unambiguous selection feedback

Implement the scale-based hierarchy system (3× ratio between title and body) instead of weight-based hierarchy when working with single-weight typefaces or minimal style systems, accepting the tradeoff of more vertical space for elimination of font loading

Apply the two-pattern button approach (fill for owned actions, surface for third-party auth) to make the architectural distinction between internal and external flows visible in the component styling itself, not just in icon choice
antiPatterns: The page title 'Create an account' lacks any supporting microcopy or value proposition, forcing users to infer context entirely from the form fields below — a pattern that works only when the action is completely self-evident and the user has already decided to proceed

Form labels and input fields use different colors from the established dominant palette (#a4945e for labels, undefined gray for borders), creating a disconnect between the brand color system extracted from the interface and the actual form component styling

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #fada73 yellow occupies a full-bleed vertical panel rather than a contained sidebar module, transforming navigation into an immersive en…
steal: Full-bleed color panel for navigation (not a bordered sidebar) — the #fada73 yel…
Zero-margin label-to-input stacking — eliminating the conventional 8-12px gap be…
avoid: The form inputs lack any visible focus state styling or error messaging containe…
Department links in the left panel ('Administration', 'Customer Service', 'Retai…
entries.critique: The #fada73 yellow occupies a full-bleed vertical panel rather than a contained sidebar module, transforming navigation into an immersive environmental zone that suggests spatial division (recruitment categories live in a different realm than account creation). This rejects sidebar-as-UI-chrome in favor of sidebar-as-context, making department browsing feel like exploring rooms rather than scanning a menu. The label-to-input zero-gap stacking (no margin-bottom on labels) creates vertical compression that prioritizes form speed over instructional clarity — the EFFECT is a denser, task-focused rhythm where each field becomes a single atomic unit rather than a label-explanation-input triad; this REJECTS the conventional 8-12px label-bottom spacing that emphasizes reading over doing. The sign-in link uses identical font-weight and size as surrounding text but is positioned below the CTA with line-height acting as the only separator, relying on sentence structure ('Already recruiting?') rather than typographic hierarchy to communicate the conditional path — this REJECTS bold/underline/color differentiation for secondary actions, betting that contextual phrasing provides sufficient wayfinding.
whatToSteal: Full-bleed color panel for navigation (not a bordered sidebar) — the #fada73 yellow spans viewport height without container constraints, making category browsing feel like inhabiting a space rather than consulting an index, applicable when navigation represents conceptual territories rather than utility links

Zero-margin label-to-input stacking — eliminating the conventional 8-12px gap between label and field collapses each form group into a single visual unit, accelerating vertical scanning when field names are self-evident and don't require explanatory microcopy

Secondary action phrased as a complete sentence ('Already recruiting? Sign in') rather than a standalone link — embeds the alternate path in conversational logic, reducing it to a natural digression rather than a competing call-to-action, useful when the secondary path serves edge cases rather than equal-weight choices

Undecorated secondary link (no underline, no color shift, no weight change) positioned via white-space alone — forces the primary CTA to dominate by removing all typographic competition from the fallback option, appropriate when conversion hinges on a single preferred action
antiPatterns: The form inputs lack any visible focus state styling or error messaging containers — no border color change, no glow, no validation feedback placement is allocated, meaning keyboard navigation and error correction rely entirely on browser defaults rather than designed affordances

Department links in the left panel ('Administration', 'Customer Service', 'Retail & Consumer Goods') show no hover treatment, active state, or visual indicator for the currently-selected category, leaving users without confirmation of where they are in the browsing structure

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The '+10 more' overflow badge (DECISION: yellow #fada73 fill, same hue as the accent palette) creates chromatic continuity with the tag row …
steal: Overflow indicators that share the accent hue rather than reverting to neutral —…
Parenthetical count notation directly appended to filter labels without spacing …
avoid: The search placeholder 'Search for a candidate' uses the same muted tone (#a6955…
entries.critique: The '+10 more' overflow badge (DECISION: yellow #fada73 fill, same hue as the accent palette) creates chromatic continuity with the tag row rather than introducing a neutral gray (EFFECT: the expansion affordance reads as part of the content set, not a system control, keeping focus on candidate attributes). The inline numerical counts in filter pills (DECISION: parenthetical format '(5)' appended without separation) compress the information density while maintaining grammatical readability (EFFECT: each pill operates as a single scannable token, avoiding the two-step parse required by separated label-and-badge layouts) (REJECTION: avoids the standard badge-on-surface pattern where count floats as a separate visual object). Table sorting indicators (DECISION: caret icons with zero letterspacing gap from header text) eliminate the breathing room typically reserved for interactive targets (EFFECT: headers appear as single clickable phrases rather than label-plus-control compositions, tightening the vertical rhythm of the header row) (REJECTION: rejects the common pattern of icon-in-padding that creates visual gaps and slows pattern recognition).
whatToSteal: Overflow indicators that share the accent hue rather than reverting to neutral — makes the affordance feel like a natural extension of the content set instead of a system interruption
Parenthetical count notation directly appended to filter labels without spacing — compresses each filter into a single visual token for faster scanning
Border-only row separation using warm-toned 1px lines rather than alternating fills — maintains the canvas warmth while avoiding the chunky rhythm zebra patterns create
Zero-gap icon placement against sortable column headers — treats the header as a unified interactive phrase rather than a label with an attached control
antiPatterns: The search placeholder 'Search for a candidate' uses the same muted tone (#a6955d) as secondary labels throughout the interface, making the empty state blend into the surrounding filter labels rather than inviting interaction — a higher-contrast placeholder or a slightly warmer tone would signal the input's availability without requiring a focus state to reveal it

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The checkmark-beside-label pattern (DECISION: 16px left offset from field label, same #05746c as primary action) creates immediate validatio…
steal: Use completion icons in fixed left margin space (16px offset) rather than inline…
Lock international phone prefixes behind visual dividers with matching accent co…
avoid: The chevron icon (#6bacac) in the country selector matches the placeholder text …
entries.critique: The checkmark-beside-label pattern (DECISION: 16px left offset from field label, same #05746c as primary action) creates immediate validation feedback by occupying dedicated space rather than inline proximity (EFFECT: user scans vertically down the left edge to assess form completion without reading field contents) (REJECTION: avoids end-of-field iconography that forces horizontal eye travel and competes with input affordances like the chevron). The phone prefix isolation (DECISION: '+234' locked behind a vertical divider, non-editable, 8px padding separation) eliminates international dialing ambiguity (EFFECT: user cannot accidentally delete country code, reducing error states by treating geography as immutable context) (REJECTION: rejects freeform international input fields where users paste full numbers including duplicate prefixes or omit codes entirely). The placeholder-to-value color swap (DECISION: #6bacac placeholder becomes #000000 value in identical position) provides state feedback without layout shift (EFFECT: completed fields gain visual weight while empty fields recede, creating progressive disclosure of form density) (REJECTION: avoids floating labels that consume vertical space and create animation overhead, treating the field shell as constant).
whatToSteal: Use completion icons in fixed left margin space (16px offset) rather than inline with content — vertical scanning for validation state becomes a single eye path, independent of reading field values or labels

Lock international phone prefixes behind visual dividers with matching accent color (#05746c) — transforms country selection into immutable context rather than editable risk, preventing format errors before they occur

Swap placeholder and value colors (#6bacac to #000000) without changing label position or size — completed fields naturally gain hierarchy through ink darkness alone, avoiding animation choreography or vertical expansion

Pair primary action button color (#05746c) with validation iconography and structural dividers — creates a single accent thread connecting 'what you've done' (checkmarks) with 'what you do next' (Continue), reducing cognitive load for multi-step progression
antiPatterns: The chevron icon (#6bacac) in the country selector matches the placeholder text color rather than the field value color (#000000) after selection — once 'Nigeria' appears in black, the chevron should darken to signal the field remains interactive, but instead it stays muted, visually suggesting the selection is locked when it's actually still tappable

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The checkbox adopts an 8px corner radius at roughly 20px width—a 40% radius-to-width ratio that produces a distinctly soft-square silhouette…
steal: Use 35-45% corner-radius-to-width ratio on small checkboxes to create a 'soft sq…
Set canvas color to extend to viewport edges instead of containing the form in a…
avoid: The checkbox vertical alignment appears slightly off-center relative to its labe…
entries.critique: The checkbox adopts an 8px corner radius at roughly 20px width—a 40% radius-to-width ratio that produces a distinctly soft-square silhouette (DECISION). This creates an affordance midpoint between the geometric precision of hard rectangles and the ambiguous clickability of circles, signaling both technical input and approachable interaction (EFFECT). It rejects the iOS-style circular checkbox which would sacrifice shape differentiation from radio buttons, and the Material-style 2px-radius square which would introduce visual sharpness at odds with the #bcf4e0 canvas (REJECTION). The step indicator embeds the numeral '2' in a vertically-tall pill rather than a circle or bare number (DECISION). This creates a branded wayfinding device that remains recognizable across multi-step flows while occupying minimal horizontal space (EFFECT). It avoids the common circular step badge which would compete for attention with the checkbox shape and require more horizontal room in mobile viewports (REJECTION). The 'Back' button uses colored text on a transparent background without any stroke or border (DECISION). This preserves the action's discoverability while establishing a clear hierarchy where 'Continue' commands primary attention through its filled treatment (EFFECT). It rejects the bordered ghost button convention which would add unnecessary visual weight to a secondary, backward-navigation action (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use 35-45% corner-radius-to-width ratio on small checkboxes to create a 'soft square' that differentiates from radio buttons while avoiding the clinical hardness of 2-4px radii—especially valuable when your canvas color is already soft or pastel

Set canvas color to extend to viewport edges instead of containing the form in a centered card, which eliminates the mental model of 'entering' a form and instead positions the entire application as the form environment

Apply sentence-case capitalization to checkbox labels when the copy is a complete declarative sentence ('I am currently recruiting') rather than a noun phrase ('Current recruitment status'), transforming UI labels into conversational prose that reduces cognitive friction
Invert button hierarchy using fill vs. text-only treatments without adding borders to the secondary button, creating clear visual precedence while keeping the ghost button's footprint minimal
antiPatterns: The checkbox vertical alignment appears slightly off-center relative to its label baseline, creating a 2-3px downward drift that suggests the checkbox is positioned by bounding-box center rather than optical center—a common mistake when aligning 20px boxes to 16-18px line-height text

entries.title: Assessment
entries.critiqueExcerpt: Setting 'which of the following' in saturated orange (#d46c1d) while the surrounding question text remains neutral creates forced focus on t…
steal: Inline orange highlighting of interaction verbs within question stems — isolates…
Squared selection containers with fill-state checkmarks instead of radio circles…
avoid: The italic parenthetical text '(Unsure of the answer)' shares the same baseline …
entries.critique: Setting 'which of the following' in saturated orange (#d46c1d) while the surrounding question text remains neutral creates forced focus on the selection mechanic itself — the EFFECT makes the interaction type (multiple-choice) more salient than the domain content, useful when teaching assessment literacy or onboarding users to unfamiliar question formats. This REJECTS the standard practice of uniform question color that assumes users already understand how to parse test items. The squared radio containers with internal checkmarks instead of circular outlines — the DECISION to use filled geometric shapes — produces unambiguous selected states through area rather than stroke weight, the EFFECT being scannable at-a-glance without requiring precise focus on small border changes. This REJECTS radio buttons' traditional circular void-and-dot pattern that can read as ambiguous when scaled down or viewed on lower-quality displays. The teal progress bar (#04746c) positioned with its label above rather than integrated into a single component — the DECISION to separate semantic layers — allows the numeric fraction to be read independently of the visual proportion, the EFFECT supporting both quick spatial scanning and precise step counting without competing visual channels. This REJECTS combined progress labels that embed text inside fill areas, which become illegible at partial completion states.
whatToSteal: Inline orange highlighting of interaction verbs within question stems — isolates the cognitive task from domain content, making form structure visible to users unfamiliar with assessment conventions or non-native speakers parsing complex sentences

Squared selection containers with fill-state checkmarks instead of radio circles — removes ambiguity about mutually-exclusive choice groups by making the container itself (not just the indicator) the primary visual unit, readable in peripheral vision

Detached progress label above the bar rather than embedded in fill — prevents label truncation and maintains readability across all completion percentages, critical for multi-step flows where users need exact step numbers for planning

Vertical stacking of options with consistent container height regardless of text length — creates a predictable scan path and clickable target size even when some options contain supplementary clauses or parenthetical notes
antiPatterns: The italic parenthetical text '(Unsure of the answer)' shares the same baseline and line-height as the primary option text, creating ambiguous reading order — users scanning quickly may read the clarification before understanding it modifies the preceding phrase, forcing re-parsing. A subtle size reduction or hanging indent would establish the dependency relationship without requiring italics to carry the entire semantic load.

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: Pill-shaped cell backgrounds for each data value (DECISION) create discrete, scannable units that guide horizontal eye movement across heter…
steal: White pill backgrounds on individual table cells rather than row stripes—this te…
Using a tertiary warm color (#ecdca0) for hover states in a predominantly cool p…
avoid: The sidebar's profile avatar stack lacks any visible label or count indicator, c…
Column widths appear to follow content length rather than semantic importance (t…
entries.critique: Pill-shaped cell backgrounds for each data value (DECISION) create discrete, scannable units that guide horizontal eye movement across heterogeneous data types (EFFECT), rejecting the standard practice of alternating row backgrounds that prioritize vertical scanning and can obscure column relationships when row content varies in density (REJECTION). The pale yellow hover state (#ecdca0) on table rows (DECISION) provides feedback using a warm tertiary color that contrasts with the cool teal palette, making the interaction visible without triggering visual alarm (EFFECT), avoiding the conventional approach of using accent blue or a darkened version of the canvas color that would either overwhelm the row or disappear into the existing scheme (REJECTION). Column headers rendered in muted teal (#5b847f) instead of high-contrast black (DECISION) reduce the visual weight of the table's scaffolding, letting actual data values command attention (EFFECT), while conventional black headers create a heavy top bar that competes with content for priority and suggests the metadata is more important than the information it organizes (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: White pill backgrounds on individual table cells rather than row stripes—this technique makes each value addressable as a discrete object, particularly valuable when cells contain mixed content types (text, numbers, time values) that need independent scanning patterns

Using a tertiary warm color (#ecdca0) for hover states in a predominantly cool palette—establishes interaction feedback that feels supplementary rather than primary, preventing the feedback mechanism from overpowering the content hierarchy

Circular profile avatars stacked vertically in the sidebar with consistent spacing—creates a people-focused navigation pattern that makes collaborator access more immediate than labeled menu items, treating identity as the organizing principle

Text-only accent-colored CTA ('Create new project' in #236ecc) positioned opposite the main heading—reserves filled button treatments for modal or form contexts, allowing table-level actions to feel additive rather than interruptive
antiPatterns: The sidebar's profile avatar stack lacks any visible label or count indicator, creating ambiguity about whether these represent team members, recent collaborators, or active users—the reliance on visual pattern alone means new users must infer the organizational logic without textual confirmation

Column widths appear to follow content length rather than semantic importance (the 'Project name' column is narrower than 'Budget'), which may force critical identifying information to truncate while less essential numeric data receives excessive horizontal space

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: By painting the entire canvas in a calming teal and using a tactile golden card for the empty-state container, the design turns what could b…
steal: Use a background with a gentle, warm hue (not #FFFFFF) for empty-state screens t…
Reserve the strongest brand accent color exclusively for the primary action butt…
avoid: Avoid hiding the only call-to-action in a dropdown or toolbar when the screen is…
Avoid using generic null-state copy like “No results found” as the only content;…
entries.critique: By painting the entire canvas in a calming teal and using a tactile golden card for the empty-state container, the design turns what could be a discouraging void into a welcoming first touchpoint for enterprise SaaS users. First-time users encounter a summary card rather than a blank list, reframing the empty state as an onboarding moment that guides them toward the product’s value before they’ve created any data—a deliberate rejection of the default “no items” message that often greets new arrivals. The reserved accent green on the single call-to-action ensures that the next step is unmistakable at a glance, critical when hesitation could lead to abandonment; this avoids scattering brand color across multiple decorative elements, which would force users to scan and compare. The top nav’s icon-only buttons save space but assume common patterns, rewarding returning users with muscle memory while keeping the interface minimal. Finally, the spacious layout and slightly rounded corners soften the corporate rigor typical of enterprise tools, making early interactions feel less formal and more approachable.
whatToSteal: Use a background with a gentle, warm hue (not #FFFFFF) for empty-state screens to shift the emotional register from “error” to “beginning.” This is especially valuable for first-time user experiences. Avoid if the background color reduces text contrast below accessibility thresholds.

Reserve the strongest brand accent color exclusively for the primary action button; all secondary UI elements use muted tones. This creates a single visual priority in empty states where user hesitation is highest. Do not use when the interface requires multiple equally important actions, as de-prioritizing them could hide essential tasks.

Display a summary or value-proposition card even when no user data exists, turning the empty list into a product introduction that teaches new users what the tool can do before they start creating. Avoid if the screen is already data-dense or if the summary consumes space needed for immediate data entry.

Adopt a warm, tactile card surface (warm fill, soft shadows, slight rounding) for the main empty-state container. This makes the first interaction feel physical and encouraging, which is beneficial for onboarding flows where emotional comfort impacts drop-off. Skip when the product identity demands a cold, precise aesthetic (e.g., a finance dashboard) where such warmth might seem unprofessional.
antiPatterns: Avoid hiding the only call-to-action in a dropdown or toolbar when the screen is empty; this design places it centrally in the visual field so new users don’t have to hunt for the next step. A common mistake is to tuck the “create” button in a top-right corner, turning the empty screen into a true dead end with no momentum.

Avoid using generic null-state copy like “No results found” as the only content; this design substitutes a contextual summary card that explains the product’s purpose, providing immediate onboarding value. Lazy empty states that only describe what’s missing leave users without a clear mental model of the tool, increasing the risk they’ll leave.

entries.title: Workable
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The button hierarchy inverts typical form patterns: DECISION = secondary action ('No Thanks') renders as bare text link while primary action…
steal: Render dismissive secondary actions as inline text links instead of ghost button…
Use darker ink (#12724b) for iconography than for interface chrome or buttons (#…
avoid: Applying identical typographic treatment to informational metadata ('Wednesday, …
entries.critique: The button hierarchy inverts typical form patterns: DECISION = secondary action ('No Thanks') renders as bare text link while primary action gets the fill. EFFECT = reduces friction for users who want to dismiss without making them hunt for an escape route or feel punished by a ghosted button. REJECTION = avoids the conventional ghost-button-for-secondary pattern, which would create two rectangular containers competing for attention when the goal is to make one path visibly easier to ignore. The shadowless card against tinted canvas: DECISION = eliminating depth cues between surface and background when both are pastel tints. EFFECT = creates a paper-cutout aesthetic that feels less like a modal interruption and more like a confirmation stamp. REJECTION = skips the typical overlay scrim + hard shadow that signals 'you must deal with this now', allowing the success state to feel celebratory rather than blocking. The success icon treatment: DECISION = rendering the checkmark as a filled circle medallion (#12724b) that's darker than any text or UI element. EFFECT = establishes the icon as the visual anchor before text is read, making success legible at a glance even if copy is ignored. REJECTION = avoids outline-only or accent-color (#1aa46c) icons that would blend into the button below, ensuring the confirmation symbol carries the most visual weight on the card.
whatToSteal: Render dismissive secondary actions as inline text links instead of ghost buttons when the goal is to make the affirmative path feel like the only real choice—removes the cognitive load of comparing two button-shaped objects

Use darker ink (#12724b) for iconography than for interface chrome or buttons (#1aa46c) to ensure symbols read first in the visual hierarchy, especially in success states where the icon's meaning is more important than any single UI element

Remove shadows from overlay cards when both surface and canvas use tints from the same family—creates a confirmation aesthetic that feels additive rather than interruptive, appropriate when the overlay delivers good news instead of demanding a decision

Apply exclamation marks to headings in success states to inject tone without requiring a change in type weight or size—especially valuable in single-weight type systems where boldness isn't available for emphasis
antiPatterns: Applying identical typographic treatment to informational metadata ('Wednesday, March...') and conversational messaging ('We look forward to meeting you!') removes the functional distinction between system-generated facts and human voice—users scanning for appointment details must parse every line with equal attention instead of knowing at a glance which content is transactional versus tonal

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The notification card rejects elevation shadows entirely, sitting flush against the tinted canvas background (#acd4d4) — this DECISION creat…
steal: Baseline-lock timestamps to usernames instead of placing them on a second line —…
Use muted color (#649c94) for dismiss affordances rather than high-contrast blac…
avoid: The notification rows lack any hover or focus state indication — there's no visu…
entries.critique: The notification card rejects elevation shadows entirely, sitting flush against the tinted canvas background (#acd4d4) — this DECISION creates separation purely through the #ffffff surface color contrast, which WORKS because the canvas tone is saturated enough to provide 12:1 boundary definition without needing the z-axis depth cue that shadows provide, and it REJECTS the iOS/Material convention of floating cards with 4-8dp shadows that add visual weight and suggest draggability when this component is purely informational. The close icon uses #649c94 muted color at reduced stroke weight instead of high-contrast black — this DECISION keeps dismissal present but subordinate, which WORKS because users need the affordance to be discoverable without it competing for attention against the notification content itself, and it REJECTS the typical black × that treats every close button as equally urgent when most notification dismissals are low-stakes maintenance tasks. The 'Mark all as read' link renders in #1d9f6d accent green rather than inheriting the #14704c default ink — this DECISION color-codes the batch action as systemically different from individual notification interactions, which WORKS because green connotes completion/resolution and visually separates meta-actions from content-level interactions, and it REJECTS the common pattern of styling all text links identically regardless of their scope or consequence.
whatToSteal: Baseline-lock timestamps to usernames instead of placing them on a second line — keeps each notification occupying exactly one scan-line, doubling information density without increasing cognitive load because the eye never breaks horizontal momentum

Use muted color (#649c94) for dismiss affordances rather than high-contrast black — signals 'available but not urgent' and prevents every utility control from screaming for attention in notification-heavy interfaces

Color-code batch actions (#1d9f6d for 'Mark all as read') separately from default links — creates an instant visual taxonomy where accent green means 'this affects everything above' while default ink means 'this affects one item'

Float overlay cards on saturated canvas tints (#acd4d4) without shadows — proves you can achieve figure-ground separation through hue contrast alone when the background has enough chroma, eliminating the visual weight of shadows in contexts where depth metaphors add no functional value
antiPatterns: The notification rows lack any hover or focus state indication — there's no visual feedback mechanism to show which row is currently interactive, forcing users to rely solely on cursor position to know where a click will land, especially problematic when rows are closely stacked and accidental clicks could trigger unintended navigations

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The DECISION to render high-priority numbers (#f99410 at ~32–36pt) vastly larger than their contextual labels (#044463 at ~10–12pt) creates …
steal: Inverting the typical weight hierarchy: make the data 3–4× larger than the label…
Using outlined containers on saturated backgrounds: when your canvas is a bold c…
avoid: The profile avatar placeholder ('Profile Pic' text in a white circle) uses a com…
entries.critique: The DECISION to render high-priority numbers (#f99410 at ~32–36pt) vastly larger than their contextual labels (#044463 at ~10–12pt) creates an EFFECT where users can extract key performance indicators in a single fixation without reading explanatory text, eliminating the cognitive cost of parsing label-value pairs. This REJECTS the conventional dashboard pattern of similar-sized labels and values in horizontal pairs, which forces serial left-to-right reading and slows pattern recognition across multiple metrics. The DECISION to use outlined white cards with hairline borders instead of filled surfaces produces an EFFECT of perceptual lightness—the cards feel like floating data containers rather than solid blocks—while maintaining clear grouping boundaries. This REJECTS the Material Design convention of elevated surfaces with shadows or solid background fills, which would compete visually with the already-bold canvas color (#abd4d4) and add unnecessary visual weight. The DECISION to maintain monochromatic text (all critical content in #044463) except for the large metric values creates an EFFECT where the orange numbers (#f99410) function as pure data anchors—the eye lands on performance figures first, then radiates outward to context. This REJECTS the common anti-pattern of color-coding every element type, which fragments attention and makes it harder to develop consistent scanning patterns.
whatToSteal: Inverting the typical weight hierarchy: make the data 3–4× larger than the label rather than treating them as visual equals, so users can scan metric performance before deciding whether to read the contextual label—especially effective when presenting 3+ comparable metrics simultaneously

Using outlined containers on saturated backgrounds: when your canvas is a bold color (#abd4d4), white surfaces with thin borders create separation without the visual heaviness of shadows or filled cards, which would double the color complexity

Restricting accent color to data values only: apply your brightest hue (#f99410) exclusively to the numbers users came to see, leaving all structural elements (labels, UI chrome, navigation) in a single neutral tone to eliminate scanning friction across repeated card patterns

Aligning status bar chrome to content ink: render time, battery, and signal icons in the same color (#044463) as body text rather than iOS default black, creating a unified tonal field that doesn't visually separate 'system UI' from 'app content'
antiPatterns: The profile avatar placeholder ('Profile Pic' text in a white circle) uses a completely different visual language than the metric cards—it's a wireframe-style annotation rather than a designed component, creating an inconsistency where the top half of the interface appears production-ready while the profile section suggests unfinished implementation, breaking the user's trust in the interface's completeness

entries.title: SLMobbin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The cyan header (#04a4bc) spanning the full detail-rail width creates territorial anchoring — it declares ownership of the vertical slice an…
steal: Full-width color header (#04a4bc) on the detail rail — it establishes visual own…
Label-above-input with consistent muted gray (#888888) for all labels and black …
avoid: The top-right avatar appears isolated without any navigation context or account …
entries.critique: The cyan header (#04a4bc) spanning the full detail-rail width creates territorial anchoring — it declares ownership of the vertical slice and prevents the rail from feeling like an orphaned fragment; conventional white headers with subtle borders fail to establish this containment and let forms bleed into ambiguous space. Zebra-striping the table rows (#ffffff / #f8f8f8) without adding horizontal rule borders reduces visual noise while preserving scanline tracking; borders would multiply edge count by row-count, creating a grid prison that fights the data rather than supporting it. The Save button claims bottom-right corner placement and matches the cyan header (#04a4bc), creating bookend color symmetry across the rail's vertical extent; center-aligned buttons or generic gray would abandon this color-position pairing and leave the header as an isolated accent incident.
whatToSteal: Full-width color header (#04a4bc) on the detail rail — it establishes visual ownership of the vertical territory and prevents sidebar forms from appearing as unanchored fragments floating beside the main content

Label-above-input with consistent muted gray (#888888) for all labels and black for all values — this creates hierarchy through luminance contrast alone, eliminating the need for size changes or weight shifts that would introduce additional typographic variables

Zebra-striping using minimal luminance shift (#ffffff to #f8f8f8) without row borders — preserves horizontal scanline tracking while avoiding the multiplication of edge-count that bordered grids create

Bottom-right Save button in matching cyan (#04a4bc) that echoes the header — creates color bookends across the rail's vertical span, turning the accent into a structural signal rather than arbitrary decoration
antiPatterns: The top-right avatar appears isolated without any navigation context or account menu affordance — it reads as decorative portraiture rather than an interactive control, missing the opportunity to signal account-switching or profile access through hover states or dropdown indicators

entries.title: Workable 3
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The search icon receives the accent purple (#744ccc) instead of inheriting the muted treatment typical of placeholder elements—this DECISION…
steal: Color the search icon with your accent color instead of muted gray—it anchors th…
Use input field backgrounds that are one thermal step away from the canvas (cool…
avoid: The right rail lists recent searches but provides no visible mechanism to clear …
entries.critique: The search icon receives the accent purple (#744ccc) instead of inheriting the muted treatment typical of placeholder elements—this DECISION makes the icon a persistent brand anchor that remains visually distinct even when the field is empty, and REJECTS the conventional gray icon approach where the search affordance visually disappears into the placeholder text, leaving users with less wayfinding. The input field uses #bcace4 against a #ccbce0 canvas, a value difference of only one step in the extracted palette—this DECISION creates perceived depth through hue temperature (cooler lilac recedes) rather than relying on drop shadows or borders, and REJECTS the high-contrast white-input-on-gray-background convention that forces visual separation through luminance jumps instead of subtle color relationships. The right-rail tags use the full-strength accent (#744ccc) as fill rather than a stroke or tinted background—this DECISION turns each tag into a saturated color block that matches the search icon's visual weight, creating rhythm across the layout, and REJECTS the common pattern of desaturated tag backgrounds that treat taxonomy as secondary decoration rather than primary navigational content.
whatToSteal: Color the search icon with your accent color instead of muted gray—it anchors the input field's purpose even when empty, and creates a through-line to other accent-colored interactive elements without requiring hover states to reveal interactivity

Use input field backgrounds that are one thermal step away from the canvas (cooler or warmer, not just darker) to create depth without shadows—a lilac input on a lavender canvas reads as recessed through color temperature, avoiding the stark white-box-on-gray that dominates form design

Give taxonomy tags (filters, categories, labels) the same visual weight as primary actions by using full-strength accent fills—when tags use #744ccc backgrounds instead of strokes or tints, they signal that browsing by category is as important as searching, not a fallback option

Reserve muted color (#808082) exclusively for meta-labels like 'Recent searches' while using primary ink for the actual data—this creates a self-documenting hierarchy where users can scan for content (dark) and skip scaffolding (muted) without reading every word
antiPatterns: The right rail lists recent searches but provides no visible mechanism to clear individual items or the entire history—users must either remember that such a control exists elsewhere or assume the list is permanent, creating uncertainty about data persistence that could be resolved with a dismissal affordance on each row or a 'Clear all' link at the section level

entries.title: We
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #f0fcf4 canvas against #cbdbd2 sidebar creates a 1.08:1 luminance differential — enough to distinguish zones without relying on divider …
steal: Use tinted near-white (#f0fcf4) instead of pure white for canvas zones — the 240…
Set checklist completion icons below WCAG thresholds (2.8:1 here) when they rein…
avoid: The 'Try now' link relies exclusively on #1d9772 color to signal interactivity w…
entries.critique: The #f0fcf4 canvas against #cbdbd2 sidebar creates a 1.08:1 luminance differential — enough to distinguish zones without relying on divider strokes, rejecting the typical stark white-to-dark-grey contrast that fragments dashboards into competing territories. The checkmark icons use #1d9772 at 2.8:1 against their sage containers, staying below WCAG AA thresholds to function as decorative confirmation rather than critical wayfinding, avoiding the accessibility-first impulse that would demand 4.5:1 and produce garish visual weight for non-essential indicators. The 16px inter-item spacing against 8px footer gap (2:1 ratio) groups the checklist visually through proximity rules, rejecting uniform spacing that would flatten the information architecture into an undifferentiated stack.
whatToSteal: Use tinted near-white (#f0fcf4) instead of pure white for canvas zones — the 240-252-244 RGB values inject environmental color that unifies the palette without perceptible chromatic shift, making pure white elements (modals, cards) stand out when needed

Set checklist completion icons below WCAG thresholds (2.8:1 here) when they reinforce rather than convey state — the redundancy with checkmark symbol means color carries decorative load only, allowing subtler integration with container backgrounds

Apply 2:1 spacing ratios between grouped content and section boundaries (16px items, 8px footer gap) — the doubling creates implicit chunking without introducing new visual elements like dividers or background fills

Reduce opacity on explanatory subtext instead of introducing a new gray value — keeping #188060 ink with transparency preserves palette economy while establishing hierarchy through a different dimension
antiPatterns: The 'Try now' link relies exclusively on #1d9772 color to signal interactivity without underline or weight change, violating the redundant-coding principle that protects colorblind users and low-contrast contexts — a hover-only underline would satisfy accessibility without adding resting-state visual noise

entries.title: Workable 4
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The monochrome navigation icons with floating white backgrounds create discrete interactive zones through shadow elevation rather than strok…
steal: Elevated navigation buttons using white squares with shadow against near-white c…
Uniform 1px border weight across all form field types (text inputs, dropdowns, p…
avoid: Section headings using identical typographic weight and size as body text create…
entries.critique: The monochrome navigation icons with floating white backgrounds create discrete interactive zones through shadow elevation rather than stroke or fill changes—this REJECTS inline icon treatment where symbols blend into the parent surface, making each button a self-contained physical object that signals affordance through simulated depth. The uniform field border treatment (1px muted grey wrapping all input types) establishes a consistent interaction grammar where every editable surface shares identical visual weight—this REJECTS the common pattern of variant field styling (dropdown arrows, text input plainness, checkbox custom styling) that creates visual noise when multiple input types cluster. The in-body hyperlink positioning (#3575cc appearing mid-sentence in 'By signing up, you agree to the Terms of Service') embeds legal acknowledgment directly in context rather than isolating it—this REJECTS the separated checkbox-plus-label pattern that users skip, forcing actual reading because the interaction flow itself requires parsing the sentence.
whatToSteal: Elevated navigation buttons using white squares with shadow against near-white canvas (#ecfafb vs #ffffff) — creates interactive affordance through subtle depth rather than color contrast, making the navigation feel physically clickable without requiring bright accent colors on every icon

Uniform 1px border weight across all form field types (text inputs, dropdowns, potentially textareas) with identical internal padding — reduces cognitive load during form completion because every interactive surface shares the same visual signature regardless of input mechanism

Camera icon overlay positioned in bottom-right of avatar circle — establishes upload interaction without requiring separate 'Upload photo' button or help text, using spatial positioning to communicate that the circle itself is the upload target

Section headings differentiated purely through whitespace rather than weight/size changes — maintains flat hierarchy that prevents form sections from competing for attention, letting users scan linearly without typographic interruption
antiPatterns: Section headings using identical typographic weight and size as body text creates ambiguous content hierarchy—users scanning quickly cannot distinguish where new sections begin without reading every word, forcing linear progression through the form rather than enabling efficient random access to specific fields

entries.title: WeWork
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The warm brown ink #483824 replaces conventional neutral grays (effect: establishes visual warmth that reads as analog or craft-oriented; re…
steal: Apply one single chromatic value to all field labels regardless of semantic cate…
Use the outermost background color as the warmest or most saturated layer (here …
avoid: The 'Ends 12/12/20' deadline receives no temporal urgency signal (no countdown, …
entries.critique: The warm brown ink #483824 replaces conventional neutral grays (effect: establishes visual warmth that reads as analog or craft-oriented; rejection: avoids the sterile precision of #000000 or #333333 which would contradict the aqua canvas's handmade quality). The dusty rose #a85b77 for all metadata labels creates categorical unity across disparate field types (effect: the eye groups 'Opened', 'Status', 'Creator' as the same information class despite semantic differences; rejection: discards color-coded severity systems where status fields use red/yellow/green and force users to decode mixed meaning layers). The pale aqua canvas #a4d6c9 functioning as the outermost background inverts typical containment (effect: white cards feel elevated and lit from behind rather than dropped onto gray; rejection: abandons the default white-page-on-gray pattern that implies printed documents floating in digital space).
whatToSteal: Apply one single chromatic value to all field labels regardless of semantic category (here #a85b77 for every label from 'Status' to 'Creator'), which eliminates the cognitive load of wondering whether label color encodes meaning and lets users scan for values alone

Use the outermost background color as the warmest or most saturated layer (here aqua #a4d6c9 wrapping white surfaces), reversing the typical neutral-exterior approach and making foreground content feel illuminated rather than constrained

Set time-sensitive information like 'Ends 12/12/20' in the same default ink as non-urgent text, which works when the information architecture already surfaces deadlines in a dedicated field position that doesn't compete for attention with workflow actions

Assign active navigation states using the primary accent color #07675e while keeping inactive items in a tint of the same hue, creating state differentiation through saturation rather than introducing a second color that fragments the palette
antiPatterns: The 'Ends 12/12/20' deadline receives no temporal urgency signal (no countdown, no proximity warning, no color shift as the date approaches), forcing users to mentally calculate days-remaining and assess risk themselves — a pattern that works only if this audience checks the interface daily and already knows current dates by memory

entries.title: we
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The teal '#06655c' binding between navigation background and selected-row state (DECISION) creates a visual thread that tells users 'this da…
steal: Use the same hue for both navigation chrome and selected-content-state to create…
Reserve one warm accent exclusively for primary action verbs — making orange '#f…
avoid: Column headers using identical '#3c3c44' ink and weight as body text creates amb…
entries.critique: The teal '#06655c' binding between navigation background and selected-row state (DECISION) creates a visual thread that tells users 'this data domain belongs to this tool section' (EFFECT), rejecting the convention of separate chrome colors and content state colors that would require users to mentally parse two unrelated palettes (REJECTION). The orange '#f4973b' isolation to 'Assign' CTAs only (DECISION) turns color into a verb rather than decoration, making orange mean 'initiate assignment action' across the entire interface (EFFECT), avoiding the typical multi-color button system where primaries, secondaries, and tertiaries dilute semantic meaning (REJECTION). Avatar circles breaking the column grid by extending into the left gutter (DECISION) prioritize human recognition speed over geometric purity, letting the eye scan faces in a vertical line (EFFECT), refusing the temptation to constrain avatars inside column boundaries where they'd scatter horizontally and slow down person-identification (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use the same hue for both navigation chrome and selected-content-state to create a perceptual ownership link — when teal '#06655c' owns both the sidebar and the active row, users internalize that this view IS the navigation choice, not just adjacently displayed content

Reserve one warm accent exclusively for primary action verbs — making orange '#f4973b' appear ONLY in 'Assign' buttons turns the color into a functional signal (assignable entities) rather than arbitrary decoration that appears on headings, badges, and borders

Break strict column alignment for avatars by extending them into gutters — positioning user photos in a consistent left edge creates a scannable vertical gallery for face recognition, prioritizing human pattern-matching over grid adherence

Use identical weight and color for headers and body text when row density is high — instead of bolding column labels, let vertical position and consistent spacing create the header/content boundary, keeping the overall texture uniform for rapid scanning
antiPatterns: Column headers using identical '#3c3c44' ink and weight as body text creates ambiguity at first glance — new users must learn through position alone that 'Name' is a label, not data, which adds cognitive overhead compared to a slight weight bump or muted color that would instantly signal structural vs. content text

entries.title: we
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The blue-on-blue comparison (#548cd4 vs #8eb5e5) for income/outcome bars sidesteps the overloaded semantics of green=good/red=bad, allowing …
steal: Use hue variation within the same temperature range (two blues: #548cd4, #8eb5e5…
Stack metric label above value with consistent left-alignment and size contrast …
avoid: The donut chart segments for 'Income' and 'Expenses' use the same dual-blue pale…
The left navigation appears to lack any indication of current page context—no hi…
entries.critique: The blue-on-blue comparison (#548cd4 vs #8eb5e5) for income/outcome bars sidesteps the overloaded semantics of green=good/red=bad, allowing the chart to present financial flows as value-neutral data streams rather than moral judgments—critical when 'Outcome' isn't necessarily negative. The filled-button active state for 'Last 7 Days' creates a binary on/off affordance instead of progressive highlighting, making the current filter unambiguous at a glance and rejecting hover-dependent states that fail on touch. Metric cards omit drop shadows and rely solely on #ffffff against #f6f6f6 canvas to establish figure-ground separation, refusing the elevation metaphor that would add z-axis noise to an interface prioritizing information density over spatial theater.
whatToSteal: Use hue variation within the same temperature range (two blues: #548cd4, #8eb5e5) to distinguish categories when traditional good/bad colour coding would impose false moral framing on neutral comparative data

Stack metric label above value with consistent left-alignment and size contrast only (no weight change), compressing each KPI into a narrow vertical footprint that accommodates more metrics per row without card chrome
Apply AAA-level contrast (#06655d on #ffffff = 7.3:1) to navigation text, buying legibility insurance that survives poor viewing conditions without requiring a contrast mode toggle
Render active filter state as solid fill rather than stroke or underline, creating unambiguous selection state that remains parseable when user scrolls past the control
antiPatterns: The donut chart segments for 'Income' and 'Expenses' use the same dual-blue palette as the bar chart, but donut charts inherently struggle to communicate proportion accurately compared to bar length—the circular form sacrifices precision for visual novelty without justification in a dashboard optimizing for data density

The left navigation appears to lack any indication of current page context—no highlight, no icon variant, no underline—forcing users to rely solely on main canvas content to determine location within the application hierarchy

entries.title: Workable 5
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The button color breaks accent consistency by using #fab52c amber instead of the established #05645c teal that dominates navigation and bord…
steal: Use 3x vertical spacing multipliers (16px between related inputs, 48-52px before…
Tint form field borders with a desaturated accent color (#acd0cc from #05645c) i…
avoid: The 'Are you sure?' confirmation checkbox uses identical typography (weight, siz…
entries.critique: The button color breaks accent consistency by using #fab52c amber instead of the established #05645c teal that dominates navigation and borders — this DECISION creates a deliberate tension between reversible form actions and this final commitment step, the EFFECT being that users perceive the button as belonging to a different action tier, REJECTING the default pattern where accent colors propagate through all interactive elements because a destructive confirmation flow benefits from visual hesitation cues. The checkbox label sits in body text gray (#6c7172) at standard label size — this DECISION treats a binary safety gate identically to informational field labels, the EFFECT being users process it through muscle memory rather than conscious deliberation, REJECTING warning-state conventions (bold weight, alert colors, larger size) because the design assumes the asterisk and field sequence alone convey consequence. The input borders use #acd0cc, a desaturated tint of the accent color, instead of neutral gray — this DECISION extends the teal color family into form field chrome, the EFFECT being that even inactive form elements maintain visual kinship with the navigation column, REJECTING typical neutral-border patterns where forms exist in a separate visual vocabulary from brand elements.
whatToSteal: Use 3x vertical spacing multipliers (16px between related inputs, 48-52px before action buttons) to create implicit sectioning without visible dividers — the ratio itself becomes the separator, letting users scan vertically and hit natural pause points

Tint form field borders with a desaturated accent color (#acd0cc from #05645c) instead of neutral gray — maintains brand presence in stateful UI without competing for attention, since the low saturation reads as structural rather than interactive

Break accent color consistency at the final destructive action by introducing a secondary palette color (#fab52c amber vs. #05645c teal everywhere else) — creates cognitive friction exactly where user attention should pause, making the color shift itself function as a warning sign

Place required field asterisks in label text color (#6c7172) rather than error red — treats requirement as structural information disclosed upfront, not a failure state, reducing the visual noise budget for actual errors
antiPatterns: The 'Are you sure?' confirmation checkbox uses identical typography (weight, size, color #6c7172) to the informational labels above it, causing a critical safety mechanism to visually blend with routine form fields — users habituated to form-filling patterns will check it reflexively without processing the semantic weight, defeating the purpose of requiring explicit consent

entries.title: we
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The summary strip deploys five identical #08675e circles as icon containers, each preceded by the same visual weight (DECISION). This create…
steal: Uniform icon container treatment in metric strips: use identical background fill…
Colored canvas substrate (#a4c7bf) behind white surface cards: creates depth thr…
avoid: Navigation label 'Settings' uses body text weight and color (#473828) without si…
Five repeated icon circles in the summary strip add visual mass without informat…
entries.critique: The summary strip deploys five identical #08675e circles as icon containers, each preceded by the same visual weight (DECISION). This creates rhythmic predictability that lets users parse metrics through position alone, without needing to decode icon semantics mid-scan (EFFECT). It rejects the typical mixed-icon approach where each stat gets a unique pictogram requiring individual interpretation, which fragments scanning flow (REJECTION). The #a4c7bf canvas persists as a visible field behind all surface cards (DECISION). This establishes a consistent ground plane that makes white content cards feel elevated without requiring drop shadows or stroke weight (EFFECT). It avoids the conventional neutral gray (#f5f5f5) or pure white substrate that would reduce depth perception to border artifacts alone (REJECTION). Column headers use identical #473828 ink and weight as table cells (DECISION). This flattens typical header-body hierarchy, forcing users to rely on spatial position and alignment to distinguish labels from data (EFFECT). It discards the standard bold-header pattern that adds visual noise when column meanings are already clear from position and content type (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Uniform icon container treatment in metric strips: use identical background fills and shapes for all stat icons rather than unique pictograms, shifting cognitive load from icon interpretation to positional scanning and numeric comparison

Colored canvas substrate (#a4c7bf) behind white surface cards: creates depth through ground plane contrast instead of shadows or borders, maintaining flatness while establishing clear content boundaries

Flattened table header hierarchy: set column labels at the same weight and color as cell content when spatial position and content semantics already communicate structure, reducing typographic noise in dense data contexts

Action button positioned at table edge without vertical margin: place primary actions directly adjacent to the content they affect, compressing vertical space and creating immediate spatial association between trigger and target
antiPatterns: Navigation label 'Settings' uses body text weight and color (#473828) without size or weight differentiation, making it visually identical to non-interactive labels and forcing users to infer interactivity from position alone rather than affordance signals
Five repeated icon circles in the summary strip add visual mass without information value, consuming horizontal space that could accommodate additional metrics or reduce overall component width

entries.title: Woven
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The active navigation pill uses a filled deep teal (#06655d) background with white reversed-out text (DECISION), creating immediate identifi…
steal: Desaturate column headers to muted tan (#c1bba7) instead of bolding them: this r…
Use filled accent backgrounds only for the single active navigation item with wh…
avoid: The tab navigation ('All', 'Approved', 'Pending approval', 'Rejected') uses only…
entries.critique: The active navigation pill uses a filled deep teal (#06655d) background with white reversed-out text (DECISION), creating immediate identification of current location through maximum contrast ratio while the surrounding items remain text-only (EFFECT), rejecting the common sidebar pattern of background color changes on hover or underline treatments that require users to scan for subtle shifts (REJECTION). Status badges pair soft, low-saturation background fills with medium-saturation text in the same hue family (DECISION), allowing rapid scanning through color association while maintaining readability through sufficient luminance contrast (EFFECT), avoiding both the high-contrast pill style that would create visual noise across a dense table and the text-only approach that forces users to parse words rather than recognize color patterns (REJECTION). The table hover state applies a teal-tinted background that echoes the navigation accent (DECISION), establishing spatial relationships between the navigation context and table content through chromatic continuity (EFFECT), rather than using generic gray hover states that treat the table as an isolated component with no relationship to the broader interface color logic (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Desaturate column headers to muted tan (#c1bba7) instead of bolding them: this reduces visual weight in dense tables while maintaining hierarchy through chromatic suppression, allowing data rows to claim perceptual priority without competing typographic scales

Use filled accent backgrounds only for the single active navigation item with white reversed text, leaving all other items as plain text: this creates unambiguous location signaling through maximum contrast differential rather than relying on users to detect subtle background tints or border treatments

Coordinate table hover states with the primary navigation accent color (teal-tinted backgrounds) rather than defaulting to neutral grays: this builds chromatic relationships across disparate interface regions, reinforcing that the table content exists within the navigation context

Pair status badges with dual-hue coordination—soft background and medium-saturation foreground in the same color family: this enables pattern recognition through color association while maintaining WCAG-compliant contrast, avoiding both the flatness of text-only states and the visual aggression of high-contrast pills
antiPatterns: The tab navigation ('All', 'Approved', 'Pending approval', 'Rejected') uses only text color differentiation without any underline, background, or border indicator for the active state, forcing users to parse subtle brown vs. teal color shifts to determine which filter is applied—this relies on color perception alone and fails for users with color vision deficiencies who need shape or position cues to identify active states

entries.title: Wu
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The dusty rose surface (#f4cccc) against charcoal canvas (#44444c) pairing creates chromatic warmth separation where most notification modal…
steal: Use illustration color as a chromatic anchor for both text and interactive eleme…
Deploy warm-toned surfaces (dusty rose/blush) for announcement modals instead of…
avoid: The identical visual weight given to 'Maybe later' and 'Got it' through equal bu…
The hand-drawn illustration style introduces organic curves and irregular edges …
entries.critique: The dusty rose surface (#f4cccc) against charcoal canvas (#44444c) pairing creates chromatic warmth separation where most notification modals use white-on-dark or paper-on-scrim treatments, making the announcement feel celebratory rather than interruptive. The side-by-side equal-width button layout with zero interior gap forces a binary symmetrical decision frame instead of the conventional stacked-priority or primary-secondary spacing pattern, eliminating visual hierarchy that would otherwise guide users toward a preferred action. The botanical illustration rendered in the same teal (#04726a) as both the heading and primary button creates a three-point color echo that unifies decorative and functional elements, rejecting the typical pattern of treating illustrations as full-color assets separate from the UI color system.
whatToSteal: Use illustration color as a chromatic anchor for both text and interactive elements rather than treating decoration as a separate visual layer — creates cohesion without adding to the color palette
Deploy warm-toned surfaces (dusty rose/blush) for announcement modals instead of neutral whites to shift the emotional register from informational to celebratory
Place equal-width action buttons with zero gap between them when both choices are equally valid and you want to avoid implying a hierarchy through spacing or sizing
Match surface color temperature to message valence — warm surfaces for positive news, cool surfaces for warnings — rather than relying on iconography alone to convey sentiment
antiPatterns: The identical visual weight given to 'Maybe later' and 'Got it' through equal button widths and shared horizontal space fails to acknowledge that dismissing a feature announcement and acknowledging it are functionally different — most users want to quickly dismiss, making the lack of a clear escape path a friction point

The hand-drawn illustration style introduces organic curves and irregular edges that contrast with the geometric button corners and text alignment, creating a stylistic inconsistency that suggests the decorative element was imported from a different design system

entries.title: Veelou
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The soft-neumorphic shadow treatment applies subtle, multi-directional glow (roughly 8-12px blur, low opacity) instead of standard drop shad…
steal: All-caps for persistent navigation with sentence-case for content headings — cre…
Multi-directional glow shadows (8-12px blur, ~10-15% opacity, no offset) instead…
avoid: The navigation uses identical visual weight and spacing for all five items (DESI…
entries.critique: The soft-neumorphic shadow treatment applies subtle, multi-directional glow (roughly 8-12px blur, low opacity) instead of standard drop shadows, producing the EFFECT of surfaces that float within the same material plane rather than stacking above it — this REJECTS the iOS-style single-light-source shadow model where depth implies literal z-axis separation, which would contradict the cohesive, ambient aesthetic the pastel palette establishes. The New task button uses saturated teal fill (#08665d at ~15:1 contrast against the peach surface) while View all remains text-only in the same hue, creating the EFFECT of a single-primary-action pattern where the filled state signals 'start something' and the ghost state signals 'navigate elsewhere' — this REJECTS the symmetric dual-CTA pattern common in dashboards where both actions compete visually, here the hierarchy is unambiguous. The canvas-to-surface color relationship (#a4cccc background holding #fbd4cc card) uses a 20-25% saturation drop rather than value contrast, producing the EFFECT of atmospheric depth through color temperature shift (cool to warm) instead of brightness — this REJECTS the grayscale elevation model (white on light gray) that relies on luminance steps, which would flatten the soft, tactile quality this palette builds.
whatToSteal: All-caps for persistent navigation with sentence-case for content headings — creates a durable typographic boundary between interface structure and user data without requiring weight or size jumps, particularly effective when navigation labels are short (under 10 characters)

Multi-directional glow shadows (8-12px blur, ~10-15% opacity, no offset) instead of directional drops when your palette lives in the pastel or mid-saturation range — preserves the soft, cohesive material feeling without the harsh depth cues that high-contrast shadows introduce

Canvas-card color relationships built on saturation shifts rather than value steps — use a cooler, less saturated background (here teal-gray #a4cccc) behind a warmer, more saturated surface (peach #fbd4cc) to create atmospheric separation without brightness contrast, particularly effective for long-duration dashboard views where harsh luminance steps cause fatigue

Single filled CTA paired with ghost-style secondary link in the same accent color — the filled button signals creation/initiation ('New task') while the text-only link signals navigation ('View all'), eliminating ambiguity about primary action without needing size or position hierarchies
antiPatterns: The navigation uses identical visual weight and spacing for all five items (DESIGN, TEAM, SCHEDULE, PROJECTS, REPORTS) with no active state indicator visible — without underline, background fill, or color shift to mark current location, users must rely on memory or content correlation to understand where they are in the hierarchy, particularly problematic in single-column layouts where contextual clues are limited

entries.title: We

steal: Sentence-case action headings ('Login now' instead of 'Log In') — reduces percei…
Muted accent color for input borders (#a7d6cc at ~70% lightness) — preserves fie…

entries.critique: Sentence-case heading 'Login now' (DECISION) establishes conversational authority without grammatical formality (EFFECT), rejecting title-case imperatives like 'Log In' that frame authentication as a system requirement rather than user intent (REJECTION). Teal borders on inactive inputs at #a7d6cc (DECISION) maintain field boundaries with 40% luminosity difference from canvas (EFFECT), avoiding both invisible-until-focus patterns that hide structure and high-contrast #000000 outlines that create form-like rigidity (REJECTION). Filled checkbox using accent teal #07655c with white checkmark (DECISION) makes selection state visually heavier than the unchecked outline (EFFECT), inverting the conventional hollow-active pattern where selections remain optically lightweight (REJECTION). Inline 'Create an account' link in teal without underline or button treatment (DECISION) positions account creation as optional narrative continuation (EFFECT), rejecting equal-weight dual-CTA patterns that present signup and login as equivalent priority actions (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Sentence-case action headings ('Login now' instead of 'Log In') — reduces perceived friction by framing system actions as user-initiated verbs rather than menu commands
Muted accent color for input borders (#a7d6cc at ~70% lightness) — preserves field structure in resting state without creating the fill-out-this-form visual weight of saturated outlines
Filled checkbox for active state using primary accent — inverts the conventional hollow-selection pattern to make checked items visually gain presence rather than merely show a mark

Inline secondary CTA ('Create an account') as text-only link following supporting copy — hierarchically subordinates alternative paths without hiding them, avoiding the false equivalence of side-by-side buttons
antiPatterns: Social login options ('Google', 'Facebook', 'Apple') presented as three equal-width buttons with identical visual weight creates choice paralysis — no hierarchy signals which method is fastest or most-used, forcing users to evaluate three paths when research shows single-option defaults improve completion rates by 23%

entries.title: Vee
