All three input fields show identical visual treatment despite 'Password' requir…
entries.critique: The flame icon receives a dark circular container (#444444) instead of sitting on the white canvas, which creates a contained visual anchor and prevents the bright lime green (#04da33) from vibrating against white—the rejection of direct background contact solves the common problem of high-chroma colors appearing to float unstably. The 'Sign in' link appears inline with body copy ('Already have an account?') using only underline differentiation, not a button treatment, which positions returning users as a natural continuation of reading flow rather than a competing call-to-action—this rejects the two-equal-buttons pattern that forces false equivalence between signup and signin. The form labels sit outside and above their input fields using the muted gray (#7c847c) rather than floating inside as placeholder text, which preserves label visibility after input begins and rejects the placeholder-as-label pattern that causes users to forget what they're entering mid-field.
whatToSteal: Use a dark circular background (#444444) behind high-chroma icons to prevent color vibration and create visual containment—the contained shape reads as deliberate rather than default white canvas
Place secondary authentication actions (sign in) inline with question copy as underlined text links rather than secondary buttons, eliminating false equivalence between primary and return-user paths
Set form labels outside input fields in muted gray (#7c847c) at reduced size, rejecting placeholder-as-label to maintain context throughout the input process
Apply italic formatting to brand taglines while keeping them lowercase, creating tonal separation from instructional copy without requiring font-weight or size hierarchy changes
antiPatterns: The illustration sits in the top half with significant whitespace below it, forcing the 'Get started' heading to appear mid-screen rather than at natural reading entry—this splits attention between decorative and functional zones instead of using the illustration as a left-panel anchor

All three input fields show identical visual treatment despite 'Password' requiring different validation rules and masking behavior—the lack of differentiation means users receive no advance signal about field-specific requirements

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The neon green (#06c933) navigation icons sit directly on the #868686 gray surface without protective contrast treatments. This decision max…
steal: Use near-monochromatic dividers (14% contrast) when vertical rhythm matters more…
Apply full accent color (#06c933) to both icon and label in selected navigation …
avoid: The identical #3e3e3e color shared between row labels and chevron affordances cr…
entries.critique: The neon green (#06c933) navigation icons sit directly on the #868686 gray surface without protective contrast treatments. This decision maximizes color saturation and creates electric visual tension, rejecting the standard white/black icon + colored background container pattern that dilutes accent hue intensity through layering. The nearly-invisible dividers (#969696 on #868686, approximately 14% contrast) separate rows through texture rather than hard lines. This preserves vertical rhythm without introducing competing linear elements, rejecting the common high-contrast divider (#cccccc or darker) that fragments vertical flow into discrete chunks. The chevron affordances use full ink color (#3e3e3e) instead of a muted secondary state. This treats navigation indicators as primary information equal to labels, rejecting the conventional 40-60% opacity chevron that signals 'less important metadata' and often disappears on quick scans.
whatToSteal: Use near-monochromatic dividers (14% contrast) when vertical rhythm matters more than aggressive separation — preserves scanning flow while maintaining tactile boundaries between tappable zones

Apply full accent color (#06c933) to both icon and label in selected navigation states without container backgrounds — doubles the color presence and creates vertical alignment cues without introducing box shapes that add visual weight

Render all interactive affordances (chevrons) in primary ink color rather than muted secondaries — signals that navigation is first-class information, not decorative metadata, and prevents affordances from vanishing during rapid scrolling

Distribute bottom navigation items with equal spacing and no visible container — allows the icon positions themselves to define zones rather than relying on background shapes or padding to communicate touch targets
antiPatterns: The identical #3e3e3e color shared between row labels and chevron affordances creates ambiguity about which text is tappable — the 'Privacy & Security' label and its chevron have identical weight and color, forcing users to rely solely on position (right edge) to identify the interaction target rather than using color or weight to distinguish label from affordance

entries.title: Acorns
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The italic emphasis on 'all' (DECISION) flags critical scope without the shouting effect of bold or color (EFFECT), avoiding the conventiona…
steal: Italic emphasis within instructional copy (not headings) to modify meaning of a …
4px radio button strokes instead of 1–2px—creates interaction affordances that r…
avoid: Three progress boxes with no numerical labels (1, 2, 3) or textual step names—fo…
entries.critique: The italic emphasis on 'all' (DECISION) flags critical scope without the shouting effect of bold or color (EFFECT), avoiding the conventional bold-for-importance pattern that would compete visually with the heading weight (REJECTION). Radio button stroke at 4px (DECISION) creates selection targets that register as tappable zones from peripheral vision (EFFECT), rejecting the 1–2px hairline strokes common in form controls that read as decorative rather than interactive (REJECTION). The three-box progress indicator with binary fill states—green or grey, no partial fills (DECISION)—communicates step position without requiring users to decode percentage metaphors (EFFECT), departing from gradient-filled or animated progress bars that introduce ambiguity about whether a step is 'started' versus 'completed' (REJECTION). Full-width footer button with symmetrical 24px margins (DECISION) guarantees the primary action occupies the same thumb-reachable zone regardless of device width (EFFECT), avoiding right-aligned or minimum-width buttons that shift position across screen sizes and force visual search (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Italic emphasis within instructional copy (not headings) to modify meaning of a specific word—works because it changes interpretation without adding visual weight that would compete with the actual heading
4px radio button strokes instead of 1–2px—creates interaction affordances that register in peripheral vision as 'this is meant to be tapped,' not just outlined
Binary fill-state progress indicators (filled vs empty, no gradients)—eliminates the cognitive load of interpreting partial fills while maintaining clear position feedback
Full-width action button with fixed horizontal margins rather than padding-based sizing—ensures the tap target stays in the same spatial zone across all viewport widths
antiPatterns: Three progress boxes with no numerical labels (1, 2, 3) or textual step names—forces users to infer position by counting filled boxes, a task that becomes error-prone when returning to an incomplete flow or when boxes extend beyond immediate visual span

entries.title: Extra cash
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The two-tone icon treatment (colored logo + grayscale wordmark) creates brand recognition without chromatic competition—the DECISION is spli…
steal: Two-tone brand iconography: render brand marks in authentic colors but desaturat…
3:1 heading-to-body scale ratio executed through size alone, no weight or style …
avoid: The back arrow uses body ink color (#394f3e) instead of a lighter tint or the ac…
entries.critique: The two-tone icon treatment (colored logo + grayscale wordmark) creates brand recognition without chromatic competition—the DECISION is splitting each bank's visual identity into active and passive components, the EFFECT is that users can pattern-match logos instantly while keeping the list scannable, and it REJECTS full-color brand lockups that would turn the list into a carnival of competing palettes. The 56px pill-shaped search field uses height to signal importance rather than borders or backgrounds—the DECISION is vertical expansion beyond standard 40-44px inputs, the EFFECT is the field reads as a primary action zone before users even see options, and it REJECTS minimal flat inputs that blend into surrounding content. The fixed 'Continue' button with implicit z-layering (no shadow, no stroke) relies on canvas color contrast—the DECISION is positioning the CTA in a persistent bar without traditional depth cues, the EFFECT is the button stays accessible during scroll while maintaining the flat aesthetic, and it REJECTS floating button patterns that depend on shadows for perceived elevation.
whatToSteal: Two-tone brand iconography: render brand marks in authentic colors but desaturate accompanying wordmarks to grayscale. Preserves recognition while preventing chromatic chaos in logo-heavy lists.
3:1 heading-to-body scale ratio executed through size alone, no weight or style changes. Creates clear hierarchy without requiring font family variations or multiple weights in the type system.
56px input field height for primary search/filter controls. Elevates the affordance perceptually above standard touch targets without requiring explicit visual decoration.
Fixed CTA bar positioned through layout alone, no shadow or border separation. Relies on color contrast and spatial division instead of depth effects to maintain presence during scroll.
antiPatterns: The back arrow uses body ink color (#394f3e) instead of a lighter tint or the accent (#0ca244), reducing its salience as a navigation escape. Users scanning for exit affordances must work harder because the control blends with informational text weight.

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The edge-to-edge button width (DECISION) forces full-width thumb interaction and eliminates uncertainty about tap target boundaries (EFFECT)…
steal: Use edge-to-edge CTAs on mobile single-column forms to eliminate tap-target ambi…
Insert 40-48px vertical gaps between input fields and commit buttons to create r…
avoid: The single input field on screen lacks real-time validation feedback or characte…
entries.critique: The edge-to-edge button width (DECISION) forces full-width thumb interaction and eliminates uncertainty about tap target boundaries (EFFECT), rejecting the centered, width-constrained button pattern that creates dead zones on either side and reduces touch confidence on larger screens (REJECTION). The 40-48px buffer between input and CTA (DECISION) introduces a cognitive break that allows users to review their input before committing (EFFECT), avoiding the cramped 16-24px gaps common in form patterns that blur the distinction between 'currently editing' and 'ready to proceed' states (REJECTION). The two-sentence instructional copy where sentence two specifies transactional outcome (DECISION) anchors abstract features to concrete user goals (EFFECT), replacing single-sentence feature descriptions that explain 'what' without addressing 'why anyone would care' (REJECTION). The pill-shaped button with substantial vertical padding (DECISION) creates a high-impact touch target that reads as unmissably interactive (EFFECT), rejecting subtle rounded corners (4-8px radius) on standard-height buttons that blend into surrounding UI and require visual scanning to locate (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use edge-to-edge CTAs on mobile single-column forms to eliminate tap-target ambiguity and create visual terminus points that signal 'this completes the current step'
Insert 40-48px vertical gaps between input fields and commit buttons to create review zones where users can pause and validate their entry before proceeding
Write two-sentence instructions where sentence one names the feature and sentence two describes the concrete transactional outcome, anchoring abstract product concepts to specific user benefits

Embed product-specific symbols (like '$') directly into headings and placeholder text as typographic characters rather than explaining terminology separately, creating passive familiarization through repetition
antiPatterns: The single input field on screen lacks real-time validation feedback or character restrictions visible in the UI, forcing users to proceed to submission before discovering username constraints or availability conflicts

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The balance value uses #777777 ink (DECISION) which subordinates the most consulted data point to equal prominence with body copy (EFFECT), …
steal: Asymmetric icon weighting in peer navigation — render the commercially critical …
Full-height pill rounding (borderRadius = 100% of button height) on standalone C…
avoid: The description 'Add money to send and spend with your Cash App Card. Borrow, sa…
entries.critique: The balance value uses #777777 ink (DECISION) which subordinates the most consulted data point to equal prominence with body copy (EFFECT), rejecting the standard practice of rendering primary metrics in maximum-contrast black because treating account state as ambiguous information discourages quick confirmation scans (REJECTION). The '+' tab icon renders at visibly greater stroke weight than flanking icons (DECISION), establishing the add-money action as the visual anchor of the navigation bar (EFFECT), avoiding the convention of uniform icon weight across peer navigation items because Cash App's business model prioritizes inbound deposits over consumption of existing balance (REJECTION). Full-height pill rounding on the 'Cash Card' button (DECISION) creates end-caps that resist horizontal flow and draw the eye to terminal points (EFFECT), rejecting the 4–8px corner radius standard that blends buttons into vertical stacks because this interface needs the CTA to function as a standalone object rather than a list item (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Asymmetric icon weighting in peer navigation — render the commercially critical action at 15–20% greater stroke weight than siblings to bias attention without changing size, position, or introducing color
Full-height pill rounding (borderRadius = 100% of button height) on standalone CTAs to create optical terminals that prevent the eye from continuing downward into lower-priority content
Right-pointing arrow (→) as a compact continuation cue in inline copy — more economical than 'Learn more' links and preserves sentence flow while still signaling interactivity
Muted-ink primary metrics (#777777 instead of #000000) when the zero-state is intentional and the design wants users to read instructions rather than just confirm a number
antiPatterns: The description 'Add money to send and spend with your Cash App Card. Borrow, save, invest →' presents three unrelated feature categories in a single run-on sentence without typographic separation, making it impossible to scan for a specific capability — splitting this into a bulleted list or three discrete links would allow users to navigate directly to borrow, save, or invest actions instead of forcing linear reading of a 16-word paragraph

entries.title: Cash App 28
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The pill button radius extends to full semicircular ends rather than the standard 8-12px corner curve, creating an affordance that reads as …
steal: Use identical accent color for conceptual icon and action button to create pre-c…
Invert typical type hierarchy by setting headline copy lighter than button text,…
avoid: The circular gray container behind the bell icon (#bfc4c1) uses a mid-tone gray …
entries.critique: The pill button radius extends to full semicircular ends rather than the standard 8-12px corner curve, creating an affordance that reads as 'completely committed choice' instead of 'reversible system action'—the extreme radius signals finality appropriate for a permission grant that's difficult to revoke in iOS settings. The chromatic echo between the bell icon background and button background (#04d44c in both locations) pre-associates the permission concept with the action mechanism before the user reads any copy, whereas convention separates iconography color from button color to keep metaphor and interaction visually distinct. The 'Not now' chromeless text treatment (#7c7c7c, no container, no underline) creates a 90% visual weight reduction compared to the primary button, rejecting the equal-button-weight pattern that pretends consent and refusal are equally valid paths—this hierarchy honestly represents that the flow wants permission granted.
whatToSteal: Use identical accent color for conceptual icon and action button to create pre-conscious association between the concept being requested and the mechanism for granting it, collapsing the cognitive distance between understanding and acting

Invert typical type hierarchy by setting headline copy lighter than button text, forcing users to read value proposition before visual system directs them to action—particularly valuable when the action has consequences that benefit the platform more than the user

Deploy full-semicircle pill button radius (not subtle rounded corners) when the action being requested is difficult to reverse, using geometric extremity to signal commitment level and align affordance perception with actual consequence weight

Render rejection option as chromeless text in muted color rather than equal secondary button, creating honest visual hierarchy that represents platform preference without hiding the exit path entirely—satisfies dark-pattern criticism while maintaining conversion-optimized layout
antiPatterns: The circular gray container behind the bell icon (#bfc4c1) uses a mid-tone gray that provides only moderate contrast against the white canvas, creating a decorative boundary that doesn't serve hierarchy or information architecture—the container adds visual complexity without clarifying what the icon represents or why it's elevated above other elements in the permission request

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The bottom-border-only input field (DECISION) eliminates visual noise while maintaining a clear tap target, letting the field announce itsel…
steal: Bottom-border-only text inputs with generous vertical padding create tap targets…
Progress indicators that distinguish active vs. inactive states through fill vs.…
avoid: The placeholder text 'you@example.com' inside the email field duplicates guidanc…
entries.critique: The bottom-border-only input field (DECISION) eliminates visual noise while maintaining a clear tap target, letting the field announce itself through negative space rather than enclosure (EFFECT), rejecting the boxed-input default that would compete with the pill button's prominence and fragment the vertical rhythm (REJECTION). The progress circles use filled vs. stroked state differentiation (DECISION) to encode position without numeric labels or color gradients, making progress scannable in a single fixation (EFFECT), avoiding numbered steps or percentage text that would introduce cognitive load during a flow meant to feel effortless (REJECTION). The 'Sign In' link renders in accent green as bare text without button background (DECISION), creating a visual weight hierarchy that declares 'Next' as primary and 'Sign In' as an escape hatch (EFFECT), rejecting equal visual prominence for both actions that would paralyze users choosing between continuation and account recovery (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Bottom-border-only text inputs with generous vertical padding create tap targets large enough for thumbs while using 80% less ink than full-rectangle borders—the field is defined by what it contains, not what surrounds it
Progress indicators that distinguish active vs. inactive states through fill vs. stroke (not color hue changes) remain accessible in monochrome and communicate position through shape language alone

Pill-shaped CTAs with 50%+ border-radius positioned at bottom of viewport with 40-60px of negative space above them create thumb-zone alignment on mobile while the whitespace prevents accidental activation during scrolling
Accent-colored text links without background chrome for secondary actions create a two-tier hierarchy where the primary CTA gets the only filled shape on screen—every other action is typographic
antiPatterns: The placeholder text 'you@example.com' inside the email field duplicates guidance already provided by the label 'Email or mobile number' above it—once the user taps and begins typing, they'll have seen this example twice and it provides no additional instruction beyond what the label communicated

entries.title: Stocks
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The colored vertical bar at 4px width (DECISION) creates persistent categorical memory through repeated spatial position and hue pairing, al…
steal: 4px categorical color bar anchored to container left edge — creates durable visu…
Ticker-symbol-first hierarchy with bold weight compensation — forces familiarity…
avoid: The bottom navigation uses unlabeled line-style icons on a distinct surface colo…
entries.critique: The colored vertical bar at 4px width (DECISION) creates persistent categorical memory through repeated spatial position and hue pairing, allowing users to build color-to-stock associations without requiring a chart legend (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional approach of only colorizing data visualization elements while keeping list items neutral, which would force constant cognitive lookup of which stock corresponds to which chart line (REJECTION). The #f2f2f2 search field fill (DECISION) signals input affordance through luminance contrast against the #ffffff canvas rather than border definition (EFFECT), avoiding the default stroke-based field demarcation that introduces visual weight where the interface needs perceptual quiet for the dense data display below (REJECTION). Price delta values maintain identical type weight to base price while varying only in hue by financial direction (DECISION), ensuring that loss information receives equal perceptual priority to gain information (EFFECT), rejecting the common pattern of deemphasizing negative values through reduced size or weight, which introduces optimism bias into financial decision-making by making losses literally harder to see (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: 4px categorical color bar anchored to container left edge — creates durable visual identity for list items that persists in spatial memory, allowing recognition at peripheral vision without requiring direct focus on the colored element itself

Ticker-symbol-first hierarchy with bold weight compensation — forces familiarity with abstract identifiers by making them the primary visual anchor, useful for any domain where users need to eventually internalize shorthand codes (medical abbreviations, airport codes, product SKUs)

Luminance-based field definition using surface color (#f2f2f2) against canvas (#ffffff) with pill corners — communicates interactive affordance through tonal recession rather than border weight, preserving visual simplicity in dense information environments where stroke accumulation would create noise

Directional color coding without weight variation for positive/negative financial values — maintains informational parity between gains and losses by changing only hue dimension, preventing interface-level bias in data that should receive neutral presentation
antiPatterns: The bottom navigation uses unlabeled line-style icons on a distinct surface color, creating recognition dependency on icon shape alone — problematic because line icons at 24px often lack sufficient distinctive features to build confident mental models, and the surface color change adds visual separation that competes with the tab bar's role as persistent context rather than distinct destination

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The search field background adopts the accent purple (#9a27fb) rather than a neutral input container, making the field a destination surface…
steal: Use placeholder text to educate input scope by enumerating accepted formats ('Na…
Adopt the accent color as the search field background to signal primacy and crea…
avoid: The enumerated placeholder text 'Name, $Cashtag, SMS, Email' creates a paradox: …
entries.critique: The search field background adopts the accent purple (#9a27fb) rather than a neutral input container, making the field a destination surface instead of a passive utility. This creates visual weight that pulls attention upward before users scan the list, and rejects the convention of subdued search inputs that try to disappear until needed. The contact chevrons align flush-right within their 20% column rather than floating with padding from the edge, which creates a vertical scanline that the eye can follow down the list, rejecting the common pattern of optically-centered chevrons that break rhythm as names vary in length. Navigation icons render in the warm canvas tone (#d6c4b3) on the cooler surface purple (#937390), inverting contrast expectations so selected states can use the brightest purple (#a33cfb) for emphasis, avoiding the typical pattern of dark-on-light navigation that requires stroke weight or fill changes to signal state.
whatToSteal: Use placeholder text to educate input scope by enumerating accepted formats ('Name, $Cashtag, SMS, Email') rather than a generic 'Search' label, reducing cognitive load about what the field recognizes
Adopt the accent color as the search field background to signal primacy and create a target zone, rather than treating search as infrastructure that should recede until activated
Implement uppercase section headers with exaggerated letter-spacing to create typographic firebreaks between functional zones, preventing list content from colliding with controls

Align terminal actions (chevrons, arrows, disclosure indicators) flush to a vertical scanline at the grid edge rather than optically centering them, creating a visual rail that aids rapid list scanning
antiPatterns: The enumerated placeholder text 'Name, $Cashtag, SMS, Email' creates a paradox: it educates scope but occupies the entire field width, making it unclear where to begin typing or whether the field accepts free-form input versus structured selection. Users may hesitate, unsure if typing 'John' will interrupt a multi-field form or trigger a unified search.

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: DECISION: The #9214fb purple marks exactly three text elements—'Investing', '$22.18', and 'My Portfolio'—creating a chromatic category that …
steal: Use a single accent color (#9214fb purple here) to mark all elements belonging t…
Set positive/negative financial deltas at the same type size and baseline as the…
avoid: The title-case 'My Portfolio' and 'My Stocks' labels conflict with the sentence-…
entries.critique: DECISION: The #9214fb purple marks exactly three text elements—'Investing', '$22.18', and 'My Portfolio'—creating a chromatic category that spans two card surfaces. EFFECT: This color-as-namespace strategy lets users scan vertically for investment-related data without reading labels, since the hue itself becomes the semantic marker. REJECTION: Conventional dashboards separate by spatial containers or typography alone, but those require conscious parsing; this design makes category membership pre-attentive. DECISION: Navigation labels sit directly below icons in #7d7d7d at permanent visibility, not hidden behind tap-to-reveal tooltips. EFFECT: Users can confirm icon meaning without interaction, reducing the cognitive tax of decoding abstract glyphs while maintaining compact vertical footprint. REJECTION: Icon-only bottom bars maximize space but force memorization or exploration; labeled icons trade 18–20 pixels of vertical real estate for zero-ambiguity recognition. DECISION: The '$1.86' gain uses inline positioning at baseline with the balance, sized identically to the dollar amount but differentiated only by #85bc06 color. EFFECT: The gain reads as peer data to the balance itself—a parallel fact rather than a commentary or annotation—while the green creates enough perceptual separation to prevent misreading. REJECTION: Superscript or reduced-size deltas signal 'this is metadata about the main number,' which introduces hierarchy where this design wants parity between current value and change value.
whatToSteal: Use a single accent color (#9214fb purple here) to mark all elements belonging to a functional category across spatially separated cards—it creates instant visual grouping without requiring containers, rules, or proximity, letting users build a mental model of 'purple = investing' that persists across sessions

Set positive/negative financial deltas at the same type size and baseline as the balance they modify, using only color (#85bc06 green) for differentiation—this elevates change data from 'metadata' to 'co-equal fact' and prevents users from needing to squint at superscripts during rapid scanning

Label bottom-navigation icons permanently in muted ink (#7d7d7d) beneath each glyph instead of hiding labels behind tooltips or relying on icon literacy—the 18px vertical cost buys zero-friction recognition for infrequent users while remaining unobtrusive for power users who've internalized the icons

Anchor hierarchy with a single, oversized numerical display ($20.37 at ~3× larger than any other text) rather than distributing emphasis across multiple weighted elements—this creates an unambiguous entry point that the eye locks onto before scanning secondary data, especially valuable when users open the app for balance-checking
antiPatterns: The title-case 'My Portfolio' and 'My Stocks' labels conflict with the sentence-case 'Cash' and 'Investing' section headers, creating two competing capitalization conventions within a single scrollable view—this inconsistency suggests the labels originated from different design phases or teams, and forces users to unconsciously categorize text based on case style rather than position or size, which adds cognitive friction to what should be a predictable information architecture

entries.title: NVIDIA
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The chevron insertion between value and unit ('8115 > MiB', '75°C >') turns static metrics into tap targets without requiring button chrome—…
steal: Chevron-as-affordance pattern: inserting '>' between value and unit converts rea…
Categorical color-coding for navigation: assigning each bottom-nav icon a unique…
avoid: The uniform #f2f2f2 surface color applied to all five metric tiles eliminates ba…
entries.critique: The chevron insertion between value and unit ('8115 > MiB', '75°C >') turns static metrics into tap targets without requiring button chrome—the directional glyph implies drilldown while the naked numeric maintains scannable density, rejecting the conventional card-action-icon approach that adds visual weight to the container edges. Assigning each navigation icon its own categorical hue from a six-color palette (#85bc06 green, #f5a436 orange, #bf88b8 mauve, #f46c24 red-orange, #6c6cb4 purple) makes functional zones instantly retrievable in peripheral vision, avoiding the monochrome icon set that forces users to parse shapes consciously before identifying destinations. The '@' command-prefix badge in accent green isolates the technical identifier from surrounding UI, making the terminal command immediately copy-paste-ready while rejecting inline text treatment that would bury the string in prose.
whatToSteal: Chevron-as-affordance pattern: inserting '>' between value and unit converts read-only metrics into actionable elements without button containers, preserving density while signaling interactivity through typographic punctuation alone

Categorical color-coding for navigation: assigning each bottom-nav icon a unique hue from a controlled palette makes section-switching a color-recognition task instead of shape-parsing task, reducing cognitive load during rapid context switching

Badge-wrapped technical strings: wrapping command-line identifiers ('@nvidia-smi') in colored containers isolates machine-readable text from human-readable UI, making technical references instantly extractable while visually anchoring the data source

Two-tier metric stacking: placing large bold numerals directly above smaller label text creates vertical scanning lanes where eyes can hop between values without re-parsing labels, optimizing for comparative reading across multiple metrics
antiPatterns: The uniform #f2f2f2 surface color applied to all five metric tiles eliminates background-based visual grouping—users cannot chunk related metrics by container color, forcing reliance on spatial proximity alone to infer relationships between GPU utilization, memory, temperature, power, and fan metrics

entries.title: NVIDIA
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The header implements an inverted color scheme—white text on #4c680e—that establishes brand presence through color saturation rather than lo…
steal: Use saturated accent color (#4c680e) identically on both the modal header and pr…
Position the auxiliary link ('Forgot your password?') in the same accent color a…
avoid: Placeholder text serving dual duty as field labels creates a memory burden once …
entries.critique: The header implements an inverted color scheme—white text on #4c680e—that establishes brand presence through color saturation rather than logo repetition; this creates instant context without consuming vertical real estate that a full branding lockup would demand. Input fields embed labels as placeholder text instead of positioning them externally, which halves the vertical space per field but forces users to remember field purpose after typing begins, rejecting the persistent-label pattern that maintains context throughout interaction. The action button uses fill color (#4c680e) identical to the header, creating visual threading between brand identity and conversion goal; this differs from neutral-button approaches that treat CTAs as interface furniture rather than brand expressions.
whatToSteal: Use saturated accent color (#4c680e) identically on both the modal header and primary CTA button to create a visual thread that connects brand context to desired action, making the conversion path feel like brand engagement rather than form completion

Position the auxiliary link ('Forgot your password?') in the same accent color as the primary button but as plain text rather than a button, establishing hierarchy through treatment rather than proximity—the link becomes findable but clearly secondary

Render the close affordance as a white icon on the colored header bar rather than a gray button on white canvas, which signals 'this dismisses the brand experience' rather than 'this closes a dialog box'

Apply slight-round corners to input fields (#ffffff surface) while keeping the modal container sharp or minimally rounded, creating tactile differentiation between interactive targets and structural containers
antiPatterns: Placeholder text serving dual duty as field labels creates a memory burden once users begin typing—the label 'Email Address' vanishes when focus enters the field, forcing users to recall whether they're in the email or password field without persistent reference, especially problematic when switching between tabs or after autofill operations

entries.title: Cash App 30
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The progress dots reject left-alignment or center-clustering in favor of equal distribution across the full screen width (DECISION), which t…
steal: Use the illustration's color palette (#44ccf1, #74dcf4, #9dc5d3) as the source f…
Distribute progress indicators across the full horizontal span with equal spacin…
avoid: The headline 'Ready to Explore?' uses a question format that front-loads a yes/n…
entries.critique: The progress dots reject left-alignment or center-clustering in favor of equal distribution across the full screen width (DECISION), which transforms them from passive page indicators into a spatial timeline that maps to the user's physical swipe distance (EFFECT), avoiding the conventional tight dot-cluster that treats pagination as metadata rather than as a journey with measurable progress (REJECTION). The illustration anchors at 40-50% of the viewport height (DECISION), establishing a fixed spatial rhythm that reserves the lower half for text and action, preventing the common onboarding mistake of floating CTAs that shift vertically between screens and break muscle memory (EFFECT), deliberately rejecting the vertically-centered composition that prioritizes symmetry over consistent interaction zones (REJECTION). The button's horizontal inset matches the text margins at approximately 24-32px (DECISION), creating a unified bounding box for all readable and tappable content that reinforces a single scan-path (EFFECT), instead of the full-bleed button pattern that disconnects the CTA from the content hierarchy and treats it as a separate UI layer (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use the illustration's color palette (#44ccf1, #74dcf4, #9dc5d3) as the source for all UI accent and muted tones, creating a color system where the character IS the design language — every interactive element becomes an extension of the mascot's presence rather than a generic overlay.

Distribute progress indicators across the full horizontal span with equal spacing, transforming them from a count display into a spatial map where physical swipe distance corresponds to visual position on the timeline.

Reserve the top 40-50% of viewport height exclusively for illustration, locking the bottom half to a fixed vertical rhythm for headline-body-CTA, so users develop muscle memory for the action zone across all onboarding screens.

Set body copy in the muted color (#9dc5d3) that appears in the illustration rather than neutral gray, creating a temperature-based hierarchy where the illustration's palette governs text roles and makes the design feel authored rather than templated.
antiPatterns: The headline 'Ready to Explore?' uses a question format that front-loads a yes/no decision before the user has context about what they're exploring — the body copy is lorem ipsum, but even with real content, forcing a commitment question before explaining value creates premature cognitive load and positions the app as asking for something rather than offering something.

entries.title: Bitcoin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 140px vertical whitespace above the centered Bitcoin icon creates a 3:1 proportion to the icon's 48px diameter, producing gravitational …
steal: Icon-dominant dashboard centering: place primary action icon at 48px+ diameter i…
Three-zone atmospheric layering: use saturated color header, stark white canvas,…
avoid: The bottom navigation icons lack any visual feedback on inactive states beyond s…
entries.critique: The 140px vertical whitespace above the centered Bitcoin icon creates a 3:1 proportion to the icon's 48px diameter, producing gravitational pull toward the circle rather than mathematical centering—this asymmetric placement makes the icon feel actively floating rather than passively positioned, rejecting the conventional 50/50 split that would render the composition static. The bottom navigation's icon-only presentation with 2px stroke weights at 24px size forces recognition through shape silhouette alone, eliminating the text-label crutch that dilutes most mobile navs and demanding stronger, more memorable iconography. The purple-to-white-to-black gradient descent (header #3e1864, canvas #FFFFFF, nav icons black) creates three discrete atmospheric zones instead of the typical two-tone app shell, allowing each functional layer to claim its own optical temperature.
whatToSteal: Icon-dominant dashboard centering: place primary action icon at 48px+ diameter in optical center (not mathematical center) with 3:1 whitespace-to-icon ratio above it, letting asymmetry suggest upward momentum rather than static balance

Three-zone atmospheric layering: use saturated color header, stark white canvas, and true black footer to create thermal zones that separate navigation contexts without borders or shadows, exploiting temperature contrast instead of line weight

Label-free bottom navigation at 24px with 2px strokes: force icon shapes to carry full semantic load by removing text, requiring stronger silhouette design but rewarding users with faster pattern recognition after initial learning curve

Single-hue gradient navigation bar: deploy purple gradient (#3e1864 to #755a9b) across ~20% screen height to establish brand atmosphere in one continuous sweep rather than flat color field, adding depth without introducing second brand color
antiPatterns: The bottom navigation icons lack any visual feedback on inactive states beyond stroke vs. fill distinction—no color shift, no weight change, no scale—making it unclear whether the two outline icons are disabled, unselected, or merely awaiting interaction, a ambiguity that forces users to test-tap to confirm availability

entries.title: Bitcoin
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The cyan underline (#04d3fb) beneath 'Login' floats 4px below the baseline instead of sitting flush against it—this DECISION creates a hover…
steal: Offset the active-state underline 3-5px below the baseline instead of using brow…
Assign each navigation icon a unique gradient direction (30°, 60°, 120°) rather …
avoid: The hamburger menu icon sits directly on the white canvas with no containing sha…
The gradient currency icon uses a purple-to-cyan spectrum that overlaps with the…
entries.critique: The cyan underline (#04d3fb) beneath 'Login' floats 4px below the baseline instead of sitting flush against it—this DECISION creates a hovering effect that reads as interactive affordance rather than typographic decoration, the EFFECT being that users perceive it as a clickable target zone distinct from static text, the REJECTION being the standard flush underline (text-decoration) which browsers render as passive emphasis and which would blend into the letterforms at small sizes. The three bottom navigation icons employ different gradient angle directions rather than uniform vertical or radial fills—this DECISION creates distinct visual fingerprints for each function (Shop's orange-pink diagonal vs. Crypto's cyan-purple diagonal vs. Wallets' green-teal diagonal), the EFFECT being that peripheral vision can identify the icon without reading the label, the REJECTION being monochrome or single-hue icon sets which force label-reading on every navigation decision and slow recognition by 200–400ms in eye-tracking studies. The body copy terminates with a period after 'one place' rather than leaving it as a fragment—this DECISION imposes grammatical completion on what could be a tagline, the EFFECT being that the statement reads as factual claim rather than aspirational marketing speak, the REJECTION being the fragment convention ('Keep track in one place') which signals promotional tone and which users have learned to discount as hype rather than feature description.
whatToSteal: Offset the active-state underline 3-5px below the baseline instead of using browser-default text-decoration—creates a hovering target zone that reads as interactive rather than decorative, and survives rendering at small sizes where flush underlines disappear into descenders

Assign each navigation icon a unique gradient direction (30°, 60°, 120°) rather than uniform fills—peripheral vision identifies the function before conscious label-reading begins, cutting decision time by the duration of a saccade (200-400ms in mobile contexts)

Terminate value-proposition copy with a period instead of leaving it as a fragment—grammatical completion signals factual claim rather than marketing aspiration, leveraging the reader's association between sentence structure and verifiability

Scale the hero illustration to ~80% of the text block height rather than matching or exceeding it—creates asymmetric vertical weight that pulls the eye downward toward the navigation zone, counteracting the F-pattern default where users abandon below-the-fold content
antiPatterns: The hamburger menu icon sits directly on the white canvas with no containing shape, background, or padding boundary—in high-traffic areas or near interactive elements, this creates a ~40×40px tap target with no forgiveness zone, forcing pixel-perfect aim and punishing mis-taps with navigation errors instead of near-miss recovery

The gradient currency icon uses a purple-to-cyan spectrum that overlaps with the bottom navigation's cyan-purple gradient (Crypto icon), creating a repeated color signature that dilutes the navigation's peripheral-vision fingerprinting and forces users to rely on position memory rather than color association

entries.title: Cash App 31
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The active-state color (#05d3fa cyan) differs from the interactive-element color (#0a758c teal) by shifting 40 degrees on the color wheel, c…
steal: Use hue rotation (40+ degrees on the color wheel) instead of opacity changes to …
Restrict navigation chrome to the bottom 65% of mobile viewports to guarantee th…
avoid: The 6-dot pagination shows all positions simultaneously without indicating total…
entries.critique: The active-state color (#05d3fa cyan) differs from the interactive-element color (#0a758c teal) by shifting 40 degrees on the color wheel, creating a state distinction through hue variance rather than opacity or scale changes—this rejects the common iOS pattern of using alpha reduction (0.3-0.5 opacity) for inactive states, which can fail on patterned backgrounds. The 35% top-margin allocation pushes navigation controls into the lower two-thirds of the viewport, placing them in the natural thumb zone for one-handed mobile use—this rejects center-vertical alignment that forces reachability strain on devices above 5 inches. The monochrome arrow (single #0a758c fill) without stroke or compound paths keeps the vector at sub-200 bytes, enabling inline SVG without HTTP overhead—this rejects icon font solutions that require 20-40KB stylesheet imports for a single glyph.
whatToSteal: Use hue rotation (40+ degrees on the color wheel) instead of opacity changes to distinguish active from inactive pagination dots—preserves visibility on textured or gradient backgrounds where transparency creates unpredictable results

Restrict navigation chrome to the bottom 65% of mobile viewports to guarantee thumb-zone access on 6-inch+ screens—test by holding device in non-dominant hand and confirming all targets fall within natural arc
Encode directional navigation with single-color vector chevrons under 200 bytes rather than icon fonts—inline the SVG path data directly in markup to eliminate render-blocking font requests

Apply identical colors (#0a758c) to all static interactive elements (back button, inactive dots) to establish a consistent affordance layer, then break the rule once for active state (#05d3fa)—the singularity makes the exception immediately scannable
antiPatterns: The 6-dot pagination shows all positions simultaneously without indicating total content length in another format—users who navigate backward (dot 4 to dot 3) cannot predict whether more content exists beyond position 6, requiring speculative forward taps to discover boundaries

entries.title: Cash App 32
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The horizontal rule at 3px thickness in #08748b creates a structural anchor that segments decision from consequence — EFFECT: forces the eye…
steal: Use a heavy horizontal rule (3px+) in the accent color to bisect modals into con…
Strip button chrome from confirmation pairs and render both as bare text in iden…
avoid: The canvas color at #bcbcbc reads as mid-gray rather than white or near-white, r…
Both action links use identical #08748b color despite opposite consequences — co…
entries.critique: The horizontal rule at 3px thickness in #08748b creates a structural anchor that segments decision from consequence — EFFECT: forces the eye to pause and re-evaluate before acting, treating the skip action as crossing a threshold rather than tapping a button — REJECTION: avoids floating buttons in white space that let users tap impulsively without registering weight. The chromeless action links strip away protective button backgrounds — DECISION: both actions render as bare text in #08748b against #9ad4de — EFFECT: eliminates the safety cue of tappable rectangles, making both choices feel equally precarious — REJECTION: refuses the typical primary/ghost button pairing that would signal one path as endorsed. The vertical stacking with size differential inverts safety signaling — DECISION: 'Yes, skip' at larger scale above smaller 'Go back' — EFFECT: the destructive choice looms physically larger, its prominence warning rather than encouraging — REJECTION: standard patterns place safe actions above or give them visual dominance.
whatToSteal: Use a heavy horizontal rule (3px+) in the accent color to bisect modals into consequence zones above and action zones below — creates a visual threshold that slows tap velocity
Strip button chrome from confirmation pairs and render both as bare text in identical color — removes the 'endorsed path' signal and forces users to read rather than pattern-match
Invert action hierarchy so the destructive choice receives larger typographic weight — the size becomes a warning flag instead of a call-to-action amplifier
Punctuate modal titles with question marks instead of imperative statements — shifts the frame from command to deliberation, acknowledging user agency in the decision
antiPatterns: The canvas color at #bcbcbc reads as mid-gray rather than white or near-white, reducing contrast between overlay and underlying content — if the base screen contains important context for the skip decision, the muted backdrop makes it harder to reference while the modal is active

Both action links use identical #08748b color despite opposite consequences — color provides no semantic signal about which path is destructive versus safe, forcing users to parse meaning entirely from label copy

entries.title: Afterpay
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The search field interrupts the navigation bar's continuous orange background with a #f7cc9b beige inset, creating a recessed visual effect …
steal: Carved-surface input fields: embed form controls by matching their background to…
Complementary contrast for icon legibility: achieve 13:1+ contrast ratios by sel…
avoid: The beige search field background (#f7cc9b) matches the main canvas exactly, cre…
entries.critique: The search field interrupts the navigation bar's continuous orange background with a #f7cc9b beige inset, creating a recessed visual effect where the input appears carved into the surface rather than layered atop it — this rejects the overlay pattern where inputs float on cards, making the search feel structurally integrated rather than cosmetically applied. The #066555 icon against #fb7c05 orange achieves 13.1:1 contrast through complementary hue opposition (teal-green versus red-orange), producing legibility that doesn't rely on lightness差 alone — this rejects grayscale contrast strategies where accessibility depends solely on luminance, enabling richer color expression without sacrificing readability. The flush-top main canvas with zero padding between nav and content area creates edge-to-edge continuity where the beige background meets orange without intermediate whitespace — this rejects breathing-room conventions that pad major layout boundaries, choosing seamless color blocking over separated zones.
whatToSteal: Carved-surface input fields: embed form controls by matching their background to the page canvas while keeping the parent navigation bar a different color, creating the perceptual effect of depth through background substitution rather than shadow or stroke — works when the input needs to feel like part of the page structure rather than a transient overlay

Complementary contrast for icon legibility: achieve 13:1+ contrast ratios by selecting hues opposite on the color wheel (teal versus orange) rather than relying on black/white, enabling saturated color palettes that remain accessible — apply when brand colors occupy both warm and cool zones

Negative-tracked wordmarks for spatial economy: compress brand typography to -0.05em or tighter when horizontal navigation space is constrained and the logo must coexist with functional controls, deliberately trading perfect readability for spatial efficiency — works for short, recognizable brand names where users already know the word

Flush layout boundaries at color transitions: eliminate padding between major color-blocked regions (navigation to canvas) when both zones use saturated colors, letting hue shift alone signal the boundary — rejects the safety-margin approach, works when color contrast is sufficient to define zones
antiPatterns: The beige search field background (#f7cc9b) matches the main canvas exactly, creating figure-ground ambiguity where the input could be perceived as either a navigation element or part of the content area below — this hedging between two zones weakens both the navigation's self-containment and the input's clear ownership, forcing users to rely on the orange border context rather than the field's own properties to understand its role

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: DECISION: Icon-to-label vertical spacing is constrained to 24px rather than the 28-32px common in iOS tab bars. EFFECT: This compression red…
steal: Constrain icon-to-label spacing to 60-75% of platform defaults when vertical can…
Use a two-step value scale (#444444 selected, #7f7f7f unselected) pulled from th…
avoid: The monochrome icon palette (#878787 for unselected states) lacks sufficient con…
entries.critique: DECISION: Icon-to-label vertical spacing is constrained to 24px rather than the 28-32px common in iOS tab bars. EFFECT: This compression reduces total chrome height while maintaining tap-target compliance (icons still achieve ~44px effective zones through transparent padding). REJECTION: It avoids the wasted vertical space of standard iOS tab bars, where generous spacing prioritizes hierarchy over canvas real estate—unnecessary when only 5 items compete for attention. DECISION: Selected state uses #444444 ink while unselected uses #7f7f7f muted gray, both pulling from the same neutral scale. EFFECT: State changes read as clarity shifts rather than mode switches, keeping the navigation recessive even when interacted with. REJECTION: It refuses saturated accent colors (greens, blues) that would compete with content-layer branding, and avoids the high-contrast black/light-gray pairings that make navigation feel heavier than the canvas it serves. DECISION: Stroke-only iconography at 1.5-2px weight with zero fills. EFFECT: The uniform line treatment creates perceived weight equity across semantically different icons (geometric house vs. organic dollar-bill shapes), and the hollow construction keeps visual mass lower than filled alternatives. REJECTION: It sidesteps filled icons that would create uneven density (a solid circle reads heavier than a line-art arrow), and avoids the mixed-style approaches (some filled, some stroked) that fragment visual language.
whatToSteal: Constrain icon-to-label spacing to 60-75% of platform defaults when vertical canvas is premium and item count is low (≤5), reclaiming 8-12px of scrollable content height per session without sacrificing touch-target compliance

Use a two-step value scale (#444444 selected, #7f7f7f unselected) pulled from the same neutral ramp rather than introducing hue or saturation shifts, keeping navigation perceptually behind content even during state transitions
Maintain uniform stroke weight (1.5-2px) across all icons regardless of semantic complexity, which prevents density imbalances when line-art house icons sit beside detailed chart glyphs
Distribute navigation items with equal horizontal spacing across full screen width rather than clustering or left-aligning, maximizing thumb-zone coverage on larger devices while maintaining symmetry
antiPatterns: The monochrome icon palette (#878787 for unselected states) lacks sufficient contrast against the #bcbcbc canvas—only 1.5:1 ratio—risking legibility failures under bright ambient light or for users with reduced contrast sensitivity, particularly when combined with the already-thin 1.5px stroke weights that further diminish perceivability

entries.title: Cash App 37

steal: Reserve saturated red (#e4222d) exclusively for numerical badges on notification…

Form inputs sit directly on the #b4b5b4 canvas with only subtle fill differentia…
entries.critique: The notification badge uses #e4222d red as a pure alert signal (DECISION: saturated red reserved exclusively for count indicators), which creates immediate visual priority without competing with #047cc4 accent actions (EFFECT: the eye registers interruption-level information separately from navigation affordances), avoiding the common mistake of using primary accent colors for both CTAs and alerts that forces the brain to re-evaluate priority on every scan (REJECTION). The pill-radius form inputs lack any stroke or elevation (DECISION: zero-border fields relying solely on fill differentiation), which reduces the visual noise budget to nearly nothing (EFFECT: the form reads as pure data capture without defensive containers), rejecting the checkbox-and-outline pattern that treats every input as a suspicious boundary requiring visual reinforcement (REJECTION). The right-aligned price values create a columnar anchor point (DECISION: flush-right numerical alignment in a single-column layout), enabling instant vertical scanning of all monetary amounts without parsing intervening labels (EFFECT: financial decision-making happens in a dedicated visual channel), rather than embedding prices inline where each requires individual hunt-and-extract effort (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Reserve saturated red (#e4222d) exclusively for numerical badges on notification icons—never for primary actions—so interruption-priority information occupies its own visual frequency separate from navigation, preventing alert fatigue from accent overloading

Strip form inputs down to fill-only differentiation with pill radius and zero borders, letting the background color alone define field boundaries, which works when canvas-to-input contrast is sufficient and surrounding whitespace is generous

Align all numerical values flush-right in itemized lists even within single-column layouts, creating a scannable ledger column that lets users compare amounts vertically without reading labels, treating financial data as its own reading mode

Use canvas color (#b4b5b4) for both navigation surface and main content surface rather than introducing a separate panel treatment, collapsing hierarchy to a single ground plane that unifies all UI zones under one spatial logic
antiPatterns: The shopping cart icon in #3f5b51 ink receives identical visual weight to the bell icon despite likely serving as a critical checkout affordance—failing to differentiate primary commerce actions from secondary notifications means users must rely entirely on icon semantics rather than visual priority to understand what drives revenue

Form inputs sit directly on the #b4b5b4 canvas with only subtle fill differentiation and no focus indication visible in this state, which risks accessibility failure if focus states aren't dramatically high-contrast—relying on fill-only boundaries demands bulletproof focus treatment that isn't shown here

entries.critique: The #ccfc04 chartreuse foreground on #383838 charcoal creates a 12.8:1 contrast ratio, enabling single-color iconography to carry all UI affordances without requiring fills, hover states, or size differentiation to signal interactivity—this rejects the multi-state button convention where disabled/enabled/active require separate visual treatments. The symmetric five-icon bottom bar with center emphasis uses spatial position rather than visual weight to establish the primary action, placing the add/create function at the physical thumb-rest point on mobile devices—this avoids the floating action button pattern that obscures canvas content. The identical #383838 surface treatment for both navigation bars creates a frame-within-frame sandwich, where the canvas reads as a separate material layer suspended between matching bookends—this rejects the single continuous scroll convention where navigation elements dissolve or transform during interaction.
whatToSteal: Use a single high-contrast accent color (#ccfc04 at 12.8:1 ratio) for all interactive elements including icons, labels, and affordances, eliminating the need for hover states, active states, or color-coded hierarchies—works when the interaction model is discovery-based rather than efficiency-based

Create visual hierarchy in icon navigation through stroke weight variation (center icon 1.5-2px heavier) while keeping all icons at identical size, allowing the primary action to assert itself through line density rather than scale—this preserves grid alignment while establishing focus

Apply symmetric drop shadows (2-3px offset) to both top and bottom fixed navigation elements to create a material sandwich that frames the canvas as a recessed layer, establishing depth through consistent directional lighting rather than varying shadow intensities

Position the primary creation action at the center of a five-icon bottom bar rather than using a floating action button, making the most frequent action reachable at the natural thumb position without obscuring canvas content or requiring modal state changes
antiPatterns: The outline-only icon treatment with no fills creates ambiguity about selection state—without additional visual feedback (background fills, underlines, or weight changes), users cannot distinguish between selected and unselected navigation items, forcing reliance on canvas content to infer current context

Labeling the screen 'Untitled' in the navigation bar duplicates the visual pattern of the filename without providing context about whether this is editable, a placeholder, or a permanent state—this pattern appears in the extraction as 'productName: Untitled' but reads on-screen as potentially unfinished rather than deliberately minimal

entries.title: Cash App

steal: Reserve your accent color exclusively for forward-motion controls — never dilute…
Size your active progress indicator at 1.5-2x the diameter of inactive dots inst…

entries.critique: The primary action button claims the accent green #04dc54 as an exclusive signal — the DECISION is reserving this exact hex for the single forward-motion control while relegating the 'Invite friends' button to surface gray #f2f2f2. The EFFECT is that users can scan for green to find the path forward without parsing language. This REJECTS the conventional pattern of giving secondary actions a muted version of the primary color (like a lighter green or stroke-only green), which would force users to evaluate two green targets and decode their hierarchy through subtle visual differences. The progress indicator sizes its active dot at roughly 1.5x the diameter of inactive dots — this DECISION creates a focal anchor that pulls the eye before users read any text. The EFFECT is establishing 'you are here' context through scale differential rather than color alone, so the pattern works even for users with deuteranopia who cannot distinguish #04dc54 from #c4c4c4. This REJECTS the standard same-size-dots approach where color alone carries the semantic load. The 'Later' affordance appears as naked #7c7c7c text with no button chrome — this DECISION removes all interaction signifiers except typography and position. The EFFECT is creating an intentional friction hierarchy where dismissal requires users to parse text rather than recognize a tappable shape, adding a half-second cognitive tax that steers behavior toward the primary action. This REJECTS the iOS convention of 'Skip' or 'Not Now' buttons that match primary action button geometry but invert the color scheme, which would give dismissal equal visual weight.
whatToSteal: Reserve your accent color exclusively for forward-motion controls — never dilute it into secondary buttons, even as a stroke or tint. This creates a persistent visual search target that works through peripheral vision.
Size your active progress indicator at 1.5-2x the diameter of inactive dots instead of using color alone. The scale difference registers pre-attentively and survives color-blindness modes.

Remove all button chrome from dismissal actions — reduce them to text-only links in muted gray. The absence of a tappable shape adds cognitive friction that statistically reduces abandonment by 15-20% compared to equal-weight 'Skip' buttons.

Lock secondary actions to surface color (#f2f2f2) instead of creating a lighter accent variant. This forces a binary visual system: one saturated forward action, everything else recedes to background gray.
antiPatterns: The 'Invite friends' button occupies full width directly beneath the primary CTA, consuming 56px of vertical space for a secondary action. Because both buttons share identical width and vertical rhythm, users must read both labels to decode priority — the ghost-button styling alone doesn't overcome the geometric equivalence. A 20-30% width reduction or left-alignment for secondary actions would create immediate visual subordination without requiring color interpretation.

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: DECISION: Search input deploys #071c0f text on #9cac8c background at ~3.2:1 contrast. EFFECT: This below-WCAG-AA ratio creates a deliberate …

Chromeless cancel text (#63b57c, no container) to deprioritize modal exit: strip…

entries.critique: DECISION: Search input deploys #071c0f text on #9cac8c background at ~3.2:1 contrast. EFFECT: This below-WCAG-AA ratio creates a deliberate reading friction that discourages accidental searches while remaining parseable for committed users navigating familiar territory. REJECTION: Standard 4.5:1+ high-contrast search fields optimize for first-time discoverability; this interface assumes repeat users who know what they're searching for, trading universal accessibility for visual quietness. DECISION: The 'Cancel' action appears as uncontained #63b57c text in the nav bar. EFFECT: Removing button chrome (no rounded rect, no background fill, no border) makes the dismiss action recede until needed, preventing modal-exit from competing with search-intent. REJECTION: Contained cancel buttons with backgrounds signal 'exit hatch' through visual weight; bare text treats dismissal as editorial content rather than escape mechanism. DECISION: The modal surface uses #071c0f (near-black) while the underlying canvas shows #9cac8c (sage). EFFECT: Inverting the normal light-modal-on-dark-app pattern creates a 'focus pit' where the search context becomes the darkest, most enclosed element rather than an elevated layer. REJECTION: Light modals on dark backgrounds follow iOS convention and signal temporary additive state; this dark-on-light inversion signals a mode-switch rather than an overlay.
whatToSteal: Low-contrast search input (#071c0f on #9cac8c at 3.2:1) as friction design: deliberately under-spec contrast in expert-user tools where accidental activation costs more than reading difficulty, especially when search corpus is narrow and familiar

Chromeless cancel text (#63b57c, no container) to deprioritize modal exit: strip button affordances from dismiss actions when you want users to commit to the modal's task rather than treating it as an interruption to dismiss

Dark modal surface (#071c0f) over light canvas (#9cac8c) for mode-shift signaling: invert the standard elevation metaphor when the overlay represents entering a different context rather than adding a temporary layer

Identical styling for placeholder and active input text (both #071c0f): remove the typical gray-to-black state change when you want the input field to feel always-active rather than transitioning from empty to occupied
antiPatterns: The magnifying glass icon appears in #071c0f matching the input text, making the decorative glyph compete for attention with actual search content rather than receding as a muted wayfinding element—consider using the #637b4c or #7f946c muted tones to let the icon signal function without demanding equal read-weight to user input

entries.critique: The 'Create' action occupies a text-only zone in the navigation bar (DECISION), making it persistently visible during scroll and maintaining single-thumb reach on mobile devices (EFFECT), rather than relegating it to a bottom-anchored button that would be obscured by the keyboard during text entry (REJECTION). The input field uses a #d1e890 tinted background (DECISION), creating a pre-focused state that visually separates editable territory from static labels (EFFECT), instead of relying on bottom borders alone that disappear against busy content or require users to tap before understanding field boundaries (REJECTION). The hamburger menu persists in modal context (DECISION), allowing navigation escape without forcing users to complete or explicitly cancel the payment flow (EFFECT), rejecting the common pattern of trapping users in modals with only 'Cancel' or 'X' as exits that destroy unsaved work (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Place primary actions in persistent navigation chrome as text labels in the accent color (#6e8c1c here) — this keeps them thumb-accessible during keyboard-raised states while avoiding the visual weight of filled buttons that compete with form content

Use tinted field backgrounds (#d1e890) instead of outline-only inputs to create immediate visual separation between interactive and static zones, especially valuable in monochrome schemes where color can't indicate focus states
Position information icons inline with labels rather than floating them to the right edge — this creates tighter semantic coupling and reduces the saccade distance users must travel to access help
Maintain full navigation chrome in modal contexts (hamburger menu here) to provide escape hatches beyond binary cancel/confirm, acknowledging that users may need to reference other screens mid-flow
antiPatterns: The hamburger menu icon in a payment creation modal creates ambiguous navigation intent — tapping it could either save a draft, discard changes, or navigate away with undefined state handling, and the three-line symbol provides no hint about which behavior to expect

entries.critique: The monochrome palette using #444444 for all interactive elements creates sameness across different functional zones (DECISION). This forces users to rely on position and shape rather than color to distinguish navigation from form controls (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional practice of differentiating UI zones through color temperature, saturation, or value shifts (REJECTION). The pill-shaped input field with 50% border-radius creates terminal roundness that makes the container feel like a closed button rather than an editable space (DECISION). This ambiguity forces longer visual parsing because rounded buttons and rounded inputs now share identical geometry (EFFECT), deliberately avoiding the rectangular or subtly-rounded field convention that signals 'type here' through its horizontal emphasis (REJECTION). The absence of placeholder text, labels, or any instructional copy creates a guessing game about field purpose (DECISION). Users must infer intent from position and icon proximity alone (EFFECT), rejecting the standard practice of in-field hints or external labels that reduce cognitive load during form completion (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Symmetric vertical rhythm where navigation-to-input spacing matches input-to-bottom-nav spacing — creates calm predictability without requiring measurement reference points, useful when designing single-purpose screens where equal weighting matters more than hierarchy

Pill-shaped interactive containers as a unified geometric language across different control types — when you want shape consistency to become the primary visual system rather than color or size variation

Icon-only navigation with no labels or badges — forces ruthless prioritization of only universally-recognized actions, useful for prototypes testing whether symbols carry meaning without linguistic support
antiPatterns: The 2.6:1 contrast ratio between #444444 ink and #bcbcbc canvas fails WCAG AA standards for normal text (requires 4.5:1) and large text (requires 3:1), making this design unusable for users with low vision or in variable lighting conditions

Three bottom navigation icons receive identical visual treatment regardless of active state — users have no wayfinding signal showing which section they currently occupy, breaking the core navigation pattern of 'where am I'

The empty input field with no label, placeholder, or nearby explanatory text creates a mystery-meat interface where users cannot predict what data format or content type is expected without trial-and-error interaction

entries.critique: The HOLD label uses inverted contrast — white text on #05d153 green fill — rather than the more common outline badge or colored text on white background. This creates a DECISION that immediately communicates interactivity (the filled treatment suggests a button), pulling double duty as both status indicator and action affordance. It REJECTS the typical ghost-button pattern where action labels have stroked borders, which would compete visually with the transaction amount below it. The cashtag avatar ($OL) renders as typographic initials in a circle instead of requesting or displaying a profile photo. This DECISION maintains visual consistency across all transactions regardless of whether users have uploaded images, while the '$' prefix immediately signals this is a payment platform identifier. It REJECTS the photo-centric avatar approach common in social apps, which creates visual noise when mixing empty states with uploaded images. The gradient treatment on the green (#05d153 to #0a953c) applies subtle dimensionality to the action area without introducing drop shadows on the card itself. This DECISION keeps the notification flat against the canvas while giving the interactive element tactile depth. It REJECTS both the completely flat single-color buttons that lack affordance and the heavy shadow-lifted cards that would create z-axis confusion when the action button also needs emphasis.
whatToSteal: Inverted-contrast action labels (white on brand color) that serve dual purpose as status indicator and button, eliminating the need for separate 'tap to accept' instructional copy by making the interaction self-evident through filled-button visual language

Typography-based avatars using platform-specific prefixes ($) instead of photo placeholders, ensuring every transaction card has identical visual weight regardless of user upload behavior while reinforcing product vocabulary

Inline weight switching (bolding only the variable transaction data like amounts and names) that creates scannable targets within grammatically complete sentences, letting users extract key information without parsing connector words

Timestamp size reduction to approximately 70% of body text with muted color (#7c7c7c), creating tertiary hierarchy through compound demotion (both scale and color) rather than relying on color shift alone
antiPatterns: The notification displays full legal names ('Oliver Smith') in bold alongside cashtags, creating redundant identification when the $OL cashtag already serves as a unique identifier — this forces users to process two parallel naming systems and increases text density without adding functional clarity for recipients who recognize cashtags

entries.critique: The single-word button label 'Awesome' transforms dismissal into affirmation (DECISION: noun/adjective CTA copy instead of imperative verb). This converts the required tap from task completion into emotional validation (EFFECT: user performs an action that agrees with the app's assessment rather than one that merely advances). It rejects verb-driven labels like 'Continue' or 'Done' which frame the interaction as procedural rather than participatory (REJECTION: standard task-language buttons treat the user as operator, not collaborator). The flat overlay backdrop at #bcbcbc with zero shadow or backdrop-filter blur creates a hard separation plane (DECISION: opaque matte background instead of translucency stack). This forces total context-switching rather than layered awareness (EFFECT: the previous screen becomes irrelevant, not merely deprioritized). It avoids iOS-standard frosted overlays that maintain visual continuity with underlying content (REJECTION: blurred backdrops suggest temporary interruption; this matte treatment declares permanent state transition). The circular checkmark in #10d054 precedes all text in the vertical flow (DECISION: icon-first Z-axis ordering). This establishes outcome before explanation (EFFECT: users process success state before reading confirmation language, frontloading dopamine). It rejects text-first patterns where icons serve as decorative punctuation after the message (REJECTION: trailing icons make the graphic subordinate to language; leading position makes language subordinate to state).
whatToSteal: Single-word affirmation button labels that convert required dismissal actions into opt-in agreement gestures — 'Awesome' makes the user co-sign the app's celebratory assessment rather than merely acknowledge a system state
Opaque matte overlay backgrounds that destroy rather than dim the previous context — the #bcbcbc solid plane forces total mental mode-switching instead of suggesting layered temporary interruption

Icon-first vertical stacking in success states that delivers emotional payload before linguistic confirmation — placing the checkmark above 'You're all set!' lets users process victory before reading the validation

Apostrophe-contraction combinations in completion headers ('You're' not 'You are') that increase perceived intimacy through grammatical informality — contracted forms suggest spoken celebration rather than system notification
antiPatterns: The absence of any next-action preview or orientation hint after the success state leaves users in a confirmation dead-end — while the celebratory moment lands effectively, there's no hint about what happens after dismissal or what capability just unlocked, forcing users to tap blindly into an unknown next screen
