entries.critique: The pill-corner buttons (DECISION: border-radius set to 50% of height) eliminate the hard visual stop of squared corners (EFFECT), making tap targets feel expansive and reducing the button's perceived weight within the constrained modal. This REJECTS the conventional 4-8px radius that would create discrete rectangular zones, which would compete for visual dominance with the already-constrained modal frame. The mid-gray success icon (DECISION: #7c7c7c, matching the muted color role) signals affirmation without introducing chromatic hierarchy (EFFECT), keeping attention on the copy itself. This REJECTS the expected green or blue success state that would anchor the eye to the icon first, forcing users to read the symbol before the explanation. The solid-fill secondary button (DECISION: #7f7f7f background rather than outline treatment) creates uniform button mass across both actions (EFFECT), preventing the common visual imbalance where an outlined secondary looks like an afterthought or error state. This REJECTS the primary-outlined pattern that would make the secondary feel optional or dismissive.
whatToSteal: Use pill-radius buttons (50% height) in constrained containers to eliminate corner collision with frame edges — the rounded caps create visual breathing room without requiring additional padding
Apply italic to the one variable word in templated messages ('succeeding') rather than bolding the entire phrase — this isolates the dynamic content without shouting the whole sentence
Give secondary actions solid fills in muted tones rather than strokes — this maintains button parity in monochrome systems where color can't differentiate hierarchy
Match success icons to the muted text color instead of introducing semantic green — this prevents icon-first reading patterns when the message copy carries more specific information than the symbol
antiPatterns: The modal appears to lack a visible close affordance (no X, no dismiss gesture hint, no background scrim visible) — users who want to defer the action without choosing either button have no explicit exit path, forcing a binary choice or requiring learned platform gestures

entries.title: Cash App Taxes
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The logo splits 'TAXES' into high-luminance yellow (#f2e306) while keeping 'Cash App' in low-luminance purple (#362d5c), forcing the product…
steal: Split logo color treatment by semantic weight — apply high-contrast accent color…
Engineer CTA button aspect ratios independently of text length — set extreme hor…
avoid: The hamburger menu icon in dark purple #362d5c against the #c3c3c4 canvas achiev…
entries.critique: The logo splits 'TAXES' into high-luminance yellow (#f2e306) while keeping 'Cash App' in low-luminance purple (#362d5c), forcing the product category to dominate the parent brand — this inverts typical brand hierarchy where logos maintain unified treatment, and works here because tax-filing context demands immediate category recognition over brand recall. The CTA button's extreme horizontal elongation (roughly 4:1 aspect ratio) with identical corner radius at all breakpoints creates a pill that reads as a single gestural target rather than a text-wrapped rectangle — rejecting the conventional approach of proportional padding that shrinks buttons on mobile, this maintains tap-target primacy regardless of viewport. The flat #c3c3c4 canvas with no imagery, gradient, or depth cues keeps all cognitive load on the copy and conversion path — where consumer finance often uses aspirational photography or trust-signaling textures, this erases environmental detail to eliminate decision friction.
whatToSteal: Split logo color treatment by semantic weight — apply high-contrast accent color only to the category term (TAXES) while keeping parent brand in standard ink, forcing users to recognize service type before brand, useful when category clarity drives conversion more than brand equity

Engineer CTA button aspect ratios independently of text length — set extreme horizontal padding (3-4x the vertical padding) so the button shape remains a wide gestural pill across all breakpoints, preventing mobile tap targets from collapsing into narrow rectangles

Use flat mid-tone canvas (#c3c3c4 sits at ~76% lightness) without any imagery or texture when the conversion goal is binary and immediate — eliminating visual environmental context removes browsing behavior and forces focus on the zero-cost value proposition

Deploy weight contrast within a single headline rather than size hierarchy — set the value claim ('100% free') in heavy weight and the service description in regular weight at the same size, creating a two-stage parse where benefit registers before scope
antiPatterns: The hamburger menu icon in dark purple #362d5c against the #c3c3c4 canvas achieves only ~3:1 contrast, falling below WCAG AA requirements for graphical objects (3:1 minimum) and risking invisibility in bright ambient conditions — this is especially problematic because mobile navigation is the only path to secondary content, making the low-contrast icon a potential dead-end for users who need account access or help documentation

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The selective chromatic emphasis—coloring only the action verb 'Send' in #05d454 while leaving 'Invest' and 'Spend' in black—creates an impl…
steal: Apply accent color to a single verb within parallel feature lists rather than di…
Position category icons inline at sentence start rather than in separate columns…
avoid: The uniform black treatment of 'Invest' and 'Spend' after the green 'Send' creat…
Placing explanatory copy directly beneath action verbs without vertical spacing …
entries.critique: The selective chromatic emphasis—coloring only the action verb 'Send' in #05d454 while leaving 'Invest' and 'Spend' in black—creates an implicit priority ladder without typographic hierarchy, guiding attention through wavelength difference (DECISION). This exploits pre-attentive color processing to establish 'Send' as the primary affordance before conscious reading occurs (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional approach of equal visual weight across peer feature lists that forces users to sequentially parse each item (REJECTION). The emoji-first inline positioning—placing 💸 directly before 'Send' rather than above or to the left in a separate column—collapses icon and label into a unified reading unit (DECISION). This compression forces single-fixation scanning where both elements are processed simultaneously (EFFECT), avoiding the two-step decode pattern of separated icon grids where users must visually shuttle between symbol and meaning (REJECTION). The pagination dots' 3:2 gap-to-diameter ratio—12px spacing for 8px circles—creates breathing room that prevents the indicator from competing with content hierarchy (DECISION). This expanded negative space keeps wayfinding passive and peripheral (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional tight dot clusters that pull focus downward and create visual tension with bottom-aligned CTAs (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Apply accent color to a single verb within parallel feature lists rather than distributing it equally, manufacturing priority through selective chromatic weight that pre-attentive vision processes before semantic parsing begins
Position category icons inline at sentence start rather than in separate columns, forcing icon and text into a unified saccade target that halves fixation count during vertical scanning

Set pagination dot spacing to 1.5× element diameter minimum, keeping wayfinding indicators recessive enough that they register only in peripheral vision and never compete with primary content for foveal attention
Use imperative present-tense verbs under 8 characters as feature labels, eliminating gerunds and articles to create action-forward framing that implies immediate capability rather than future promise
antiPatterns: The uniform black treatment of 'Invest' and 'Spend' after the green 'Send' creates an inconsistent emphasis system—either all action verbs should receive chromatic priority or none should, as the current pattern suggests two features are secondary without establishing why

Placing explanatory copy directly beneath action verbs without vertical spacing ('Send money instantly' follows 'Send' with no buffer) creates vertical cramping that forces each feature block to compete for distinct boundaries, making scanning more effortful

entries.title: Cash App Taxes
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The illustration's flat-color zone approach (DECISION: hard-edged color fills with no gradients or shadows between the four dominant hues) c…
steal: Flat-color illustration zones with hard edges between saturated hues (#c8bd17, #…
Numeric step indicator ('1 of 5') instead of dot pagination—provides concrete ef…
avoid: The body copy 'We'll guide you step-by-step so you can file confidently—for free…
entries.critique: The illustration's flat-color zone approach (DECISION: hard-edged color fills with no gradients or shadows between the four dominant hues) creates spatial separation through hue alone rather than depth cues (EFFECT), which maintains the playful tone while keeping cognitive load minimal for users anxious about tax complexity (REJECTION: dimensional illustration with shading and highlights would introduce visual hierarchy that competes with the actual UI controls and suggests complexity). The 1-of-5 pagination placement (DECISION: top-aligned with 40-50px margin below before content begins) anchors progress tracking in persistent real estate (EFFECT) while conventional bottom or dot-indicator patterns would either obscure action buttons or provide less concrete information about remaining effort (REJECTION). The two-button stack (DECISION: equal-width rectangular buttons with primary/secondary color roles vertically arranged) forces sequential cognitive evaluation (EFFECT) rather than a side-by-side split that would imply equal-weight choices and create pressure to compare options simultaneously (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Flat-color illustration zones with hard edges between saturated hues (#c8bd17, #9280df, #06c933) separated by value contrast alone—eliminates gradient rendering costs and maintains visual energy without introducing false depth hierarchy that would compete with interactive elements

Numeric step indicator ('1 of 5') instead of dot pagination—provides concrete effort estimation that reduces abandonment anxiety in multi-step flows where users need to know total commitment before proceeding

Oversized decorative objects (coins, cards at 1.5-2x human scale) in onboarding illustration—establishes aspirational framing by making reward tokens visually dominant over the user figure, priming expectation of gain rather than loss

Sentence-case headline with concrete numeric claim ('every dollar') rather than abstract benefit language—grounds the value proposition in measurable outcomes that tax filers can mentally calculate against their specific situation
antiPatterns: The body copy 'We'll guide you step-by-step so you can file confidently—for free' front-loads the process description before the cost qualifier, burying the most compelling differentiator (free filing) at the end of a 13-word sentence where anxious users scanning for price signals will encounter friction before relief

entries.title: Afterpay
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The active navigation state applies color (#04d454) to both the icon and label while increasing only the label's weight, creating a three-si…
steal: Apply three simultaneous signals (color + luminance + weight) to active navigati…
Use stroke-only outlines for secondary navigation (profile, settings) when the a…
avoid: The profile icon stroke weight (approximately 2px) is noticeably thinner than th…
entries.critique: The active navigation state applies color (#04d454) to both the icon and label while increasing only the label's weight, creating a three-signal reinforcement (hue + value + weight) that survives in monochrome screenshots and for colorblind users, rejecting the single-signal convention where only color shifts and the inactive state remains ambiguous in grayscale. The profile icon uses a stroke-only outline at roughly 2px weight instead of a filled circle or avatar photo, which prevents it from competing for attention with the neon accent used in CTAs below, accepting a hierarchy trade-off where account access recedes behind primary actions. The option cards place icons at a fixed 16px margin with 12px text offset rather than centering them in a leading column, which aligns all text to a single vertical rhythm and allows the eye to scan labels without jumping between centered and left-aligned elements, rejecting the centered-icon pattern that prioritizes icon symmetry over reading speed.
whatToSteal: Apply three simultaneous signals (color + luminance + weight) to active navigation states so the hierarchy survives in any color mode or accessibility transformation, not just the designer's default palette

Use stroke-only outlines for secondary navigation (profile, settings) when the accent color is high-saturation and used elsewhere for CTAs, preventing visual weight competition between different interaction tiers
Left-align all option-card icons to a fixed margin (16px) with consistent text offset (12px) instead of centering them, which creates a scannable vertical text column and faster label comparison

Place the primary action prompt ('Select an option to continue') 40-48px below a large illustrative element rather than immediately after it, giving the illustration breathing room and preventing the heading from feeling like a caption
antiPatterns: The profile icon stroke weight (approximately 2px) is noticeably thinner than the option-card icon strokes (approximately 3-4px), creating an unintentional hierarchy suggestion that profile access is less sturdy or less important than the primary actions, when it should match or exceed them to signal account-level control

entries.title: Cash App Taxes
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The two-tone pagination ('5' in #000000, 'of 8' in #beb9c3) makes the current step number a high-contrast anchor rather than treating '5 of …
steal: Split color/weight within pagination text to make current position high-contrast…
Allocate a full horizontal band (120-140px vertical) to progress indicators rath…
avoid: The exclamation mark in 'Great!' followed by a formal period-ended sentence ('Le…
entries.critique: The two-tone pagination ('5' in #000000, 'of 8' in #beb9c3) makes the current step number a high-contrast anchor rather than treating '5 of 8' as a single neutral label; this prioritizes 'where am I now' over 'how much remains', which matters when users return mid-session and need instant re-orientation instead of calculating remaining burden. The dedicated 120-140px vertical band for progress chrome elevates navigation state to primary wayfinding real estate rather than tucking it into a header corner; this signals that step position is as important as the content itself, rejecting the assumption that progress is mere metadata. The bare '<' glyph without button chrome makes the back action feel like document-level navigation rather than a trapped modal flow; treating it as a typographic element instead of an enclosed button implies the user is moving through pages they own, not completing a vendor-imposed wizard.
whatToSteal: Split color/weight within pagination text to make current position high-contrast (#000000 bold) and total count recessive (#beb9c3 light)—users re-entering a flow need 'where am I' before 'how much left', and this makes the anchor instant while keeping context available

Allocate a full horizontal band (120-140px vertical) to progress indicators rather than inline header metadata—when step position affects what users can do (skip, exit, return), giving it primary spatial real estate signals it's not disposable chrome

Render navigation glyphs as bare typography ('<') without button containers when the action is document-level (back/forward through owned content) rather than transactional (submit/cancel)—the absence of chrome implies freedom of movement instead of modal constraint

Use filled circles (#5d05e9) for completed steps and unfilled outlines (#beb9c3) for future steps to create a left-to-right 'colored-in' metaphor—this makes progress feel like filling a container rather than climbing a ladder, which is cognitively lighter for long sequences
antiPatterns: The exclamation mark in 'Great!' followed by a formal period-ended sentence ('Let's move on to income.') mixes celebration grammar with instructional grammar in a single two-sentence unit—this creates tonal whiplash because the exclamation primes for playful informality but the period-declarative immediately shifts to procedural neutrality, forcing the user to recalibrate voice expectations mid-read instead of maintaining a consistent register across the transition

entries.title: Afterpay partners
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The alphabetical navigation strips away all container chrome—no pill backgrounds, no stroke outlines, no hover states visible—forcing the le…
steal: Use actual brand assets in monochrome reproduction when displaying partner lists…
Match placeholder text color precisely to explanatory subheadings rather than us…
avoid: The search input lacks any visual weight or container definition beyond the clea…
entries.critique: The alphabetical navigation strips away all container chrome—no pill backgrounds, no stroke outlines, no hover states visible—forcing the letterforms themselves to carry the entire interactive signal (DECISION). This reduction places full trust in typographic weight and the user's familiarity with alphabet-based filtering, eliminating visual noise that would compete with the partner logos below (EFFECT). It rejects the common pattern of pill-shaped category chips with background fills and borders, which would add six to eight additional visual elements fighting for hierarchy in an already content-dense modal (REJECTION). The in-field clear button positions the X icon with zero padding between icon and input boundary (DECISION), maximizing the tappable zone within the constrained mobile modal width while keeping the affordance visually subordinate to the search content (EFFECT). This avoids the typical approach of padding the icon away from edges, which would either shrink the touch target or push the input text uncomfortably leftward (REJECTION). The partner presentation uses authentic brand wordmarks rendered in their native typefaces rather than logo lockups or generic text labels (DECISION), allowing instant brand recognition through typographic signature while maintaining tonal consistency in a monochrome environment (EFFECT). This sidesteps the placeholder-box approach common in wireframes-turned-production, which would require color to differentiate brands and break the grayscale discipline (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use actual brand assets in monochrome reproduction when displaying partner lists—the authentic typeface variations (Nike's weight, Urban Outfitters' condensed style) provide recognition cues without requiring color, preserving visual system constraints while delivering brand fidelity

Match placeholder text color precisely to explanatory subheadings rather than using a lighter tint—this creates a consistent vocabulary for 'non-primary information' that users learn once and apply everywhere in the interface

Strip alphabetical filters to bare letterforms without container UI when the interaction model is universally understood—users who've seen A-Z navigation don't need button-like affordances to grasp the function, and the reduction lets content breathe

Position in-field action icons (clear, search, etc.) flush to input boundaries with zero interior padding—this treats the input's edge as the natural touch-target limit rather than artificially shrinking interactive zones or wasting horizontal space
antiPatterns: The search input lacks any visual weight or container definition beyond the clear icon, making it challenging to identify the interactive field boundary in a scan—users accustomed to stroked inputs or background-filled fields may skip past this as static text rather than an editable control, especially when the placeholder gray matches non-interactive copy exactly

entries.title: Dollar Tree
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The 4px green divider bar (#08953f at 100% width) creates a categorical boundary that prevents the search function from blending into the na…
steal: The 4px accent bar at 100% width as a categorical divider — it's thick enough to…
Locking all left edges of interactive elements (search input, nav items) to a si…
avoid: The question-based placeholder 'What can we help you find?' optimizes for friend…
entries.critique: The 4px green divider bar (#08953f at 100% width) creates a categorical boundary that prevents the search function from blending into the navigation list — rejecting the common pattern of letting white space alone separate functional zones, which would force users to infer the difference through trial. The symmetrical 16px horizontal padding on both the search input and navigation items locks the left edge of all interactive targets into a single vertical line — this produces a predictable tap zone that eliminates the micro-adjustments users make when interactive elements indent unpredictably, as seen in implementations that center-align some items or vary padding per row. Positioning the search icon inside the input field at a fixed 16px from the right edge (rather than as a separate button outside the field) collapses two tap targets into one continuous surface — this reduces decision paralysis at the top of the modal where users expect immediate action, avoiding the iOS Settings pattern of external magnifying glass buttons that fragment the interaction into 'focus field, then submit.'
whatToSteal: The 4px accent bar at 100% width as a categorical divider — it's thick enough to register as a boundary (not a decorative underline) but thin enough to avoid dominating the vertical rhythm, and the solid #08953f ensures it reads as structure rather than content

Locking all left edges of interactive elements (search input, nav items) to a single vertical line via consistent 16px horizontal padding — this eliminates the cognitive load of tracking multiple alignment columns and makes every tap zone predictable from the first interaction

Embedding the search icon inside the input field at a fixed right offset instead of creating a separate submit button — this treats search as a single atomic action and removes the ambiguity of whether the icon or the field is the primary target

Using a 1.125:1 size ratio (18px header vs 16px body) for hierarchy — it's the minimum perceptible step that still registers as structural difference without creating the visual debt of large headlines that need rebalancing across viewports
antiPatterns: The question-based placeholder 'What can we help you find?' optimizes for friendliness over scannability — users seeking the search function must parse a full sentence to confirm the field's purpose, whereas a noun phrase like 'Search products' or 'Find items' would communicate function in the first two words, reducing time-to-recognition for repeat visitors who already know what the input does

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The underlined text link 'Sign Up Without Investing' uses #7c7b7c muted gray instead of defaulting to blue hyperlink convention (DECISION), …
steal: Color-based title hierarchy where the product name appears in full black while t…
Exaggerated CTA button padding that consumes 15-20% of viewport height, transfor…
avoid: The legal disclaimer 'Brokerage services by Cash App Investing LLC, member FINRA…
entries.critique: The underlined text link 'Sign Up Without Investing' uses #7c7b7c muted gray instead of defaulting to blue hyperlink convention (DECISION), which keeps the visual weight on the primary green CTA while still signaling interactivity through the underline (EFFECT), rejecting the standard blue-link paradigm that would compete with the already-colorful feature cards and accent green (REJECTION). The primary CTA's extreme vertical padding—approximately 18-20px top and bottom—creates a touch target that dominates the lower third of the screen (DECISION), ensuring motor accessibility and eliminating mis-taps during a critical conversion moment (EFFECT), rather than following mobile button standards of 44-48px total height which would feel timid against the bold feature cards above (REJECTION). The three feature cards use discrete icon colors—purple, green, blue-teal—that map to different product categories (DECISION), creating visual separation that helps users parse distinct value propositions in a single glance (EFFECT), instead of monochromatic icons that would force users to read every label to differentiate features (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Color-based title hierarchy where the product name appears in full black while the feature descriptor uses muted gray—maintains readable size while creating weight difference that keeps brand recognition primary
Exaggerated CTA button padding that consumes 15-20% of viewport height, transforming the action into an unmissable focal point rather than treating it as one element among many
Underlined text link in muted gray positioned immediately below primary CTA—preserves visual hierarchy by avoiding high-contrast link blue while underline preserves affordance clarity

Multi-color icon system within identical card containers where each icon's hue maps to a product domain, letting color alone differentiate features without requiring shape variation or card-level styling changes
antiPatterns: The legal disclaimer 'Brokerage services by Cash App Investing LLC, member FINRA/SIPC' appears at small scale in muted gray, requiring users to parse dense regulatory text at low contrast—the combination of small size and reduced opacity creates a readability barrier for required disclosure information that regulations demand users access

entries.title: Cash App 53
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The chevron icons receive #7c7c7c instead of matching the #424242 body text, reducing their visual authority to suggestion-level rather than…
steal: De-emphasize directional chevrons to #7c7c7c (one step lighter than body text) w…
Lock all settings rows to a single height value (72px here) regardless of conten…
avoid: The hamburger menu icon at top-left forces left-handed users to reach across the…
Every row shows a chevron-right regardless of whether the destination is a new s…
entries.critique: The chevron icons receive #7c7c7c instead of matching the #424242 body text, reducing their visual authority to suggestion-level rather than command-level — this acknowledges that the entire row is tappable, making the chevron a directional hint rather than the sole clickable element, whereas conventional settings UIs color their chevrons identically to labels, falsely implying they're the primary tap target. The uniform 72px row height across both single-line and two-line entries creates predictable rhythm and eliminates the ragged vertical spacing that results from auto-fitting each row to its content — conventional layouts let rows collapse to 56px or expand to 88px based on text wrap, producing stutter-step scanning. The 16px inset applied equally to left padding, right padding, and inter-row vertical spacing establishes a consistent spatial grid where every element lands on 8px increments, rejecting the common practice of using different values for horizontal (12px) and vertical (8px) spacing that creates visual tension between the two axes.
whatToSteal: De-emphasize directional chevrons to #7c7c7c (one step lighter than body text) when the entire row is tappable — this visual de-ranking clarifies that the chevron indicates outcome rather than serving as the interaction zone, preventing users from precise-targeting the icon instead of tapping anywhere

Lock all settings rows to a single height value (72px here) regardless of content length — this produces scannable rhythm and prevents the accordion effect where some rows feel cramped and others feel loose, which happens when row height auto-adjusts to text length

Use weight drops (600 to 400) instead of size drops to separate primary and secondary text within the same row — this keeps all text optically aligned to the same baseline grid while still establishing clear hierarchy, avoiding the baseline-shift problems that come from mixing 16px and 13px type

Apply identical padding values horizontally and vertically (16px on all four sides of the layout grid) — this creates spatial consistency where every element snaps to the same unit, rejecting the asymmetric padding systems (like 16px horizontal, 12px vertical) that introduce competing spatial rhythms
antiPatterns: The hamburger menu icon at top-left forces left-handed users to reach across the screen width for primary navigation, while right-handed users can thumb-tap it comfortably — this handedness bias could be mitigated by offering a settings option to flip navigation chrome to either edge, or by placing the hamburger in a centered position accessible to both grip styles

Every row shows a chevron-right regardless of whether the destination is a new screen or an inline toggle, eliminating the affordance distinction between navigation actions and state changes — conventional settings correctly vary the trailing icon (chevron for navigation, switch for toggles) to telegraph the interaction model before the tap occurs

entries.title: Cash App 54
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The progress dots encode state through fill value contrast (dark #494949 versus light #bcbcbc) rather than using position, size, or accent c…
steal: Use three distinct gray values for semantic separation (ink #494949, placeholder…
Place primary action buttons on a contrasting surface (#c1c1c1 vs white canvas) …
avoid: The inline 'Login' link at the bottom receives no visual differentiation from th…
entries.critique: The progress dots encode state through fill value contrast (dark #494949 versus light #bcbcbc) rather than using position, size, or accent color, which makes the current step immediately scannable in monochrome contexts where color cues would disappear. The email placeholder 'example@email.com' uses a distinct mid-tone gray (#7c7c7c) different from both the ink and the muted values, creating a third-level hierarchy that prevents the example text from being mistaken for user input while remaining legible enough to serve as a format guide — rejecting the common pattern of ultra-light placeholders that vanish before users can reference them. The full-width button on a contrasting gray surface (#c1c1c1) creates a tappable field that extends beyond text boundaries, maximizing the touch target while the pill corners prevent the button from appearing to bleed into the canvas — avoiding the floating-rectangle pattern that requires additional shadows or strokes to separate layers.
whatToSteal: Use three distinct gray values for semantic separation (ink #494949, placeholder #7c7c7c, muted #bcbcbc) to create a legibility ladder that works without color, letting each element find its hierarchy position through luminance alone

Place primary action buttons on a contrasting surface (#c1c1c1 vs white canvas) rather than floating them, so the button's container defines the interaction boundary and eliminates the need for shadows or heavy strokes to separate elements

Encode stepper/pagination state through fill density rather than color or position — a dark filled dot versus light filled dot creates instant recognition of progress without requiring users to decode sequential positioning

Break heading text with visible line characters at meaningful phrase boundaries to control vertical rhythm and pair the title with form fields as a unified block, rejecting the convention of treating headings as isolated display elements with excess bottom margin
antiPatterns: The inline 'Login' link at the bottom receives no visual differentiation from the surrounding 'Already have an account?' text — no underline, no color shift, no weight change — making it indistinguishable from plain copy and forcing users to discover interactivity through trial-and-error tapping rather than visual signaling

entries.title: Cash App 55
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The full-width primary button forces a binary interaction model where the user must complete or abandon the form with equal prominence given…
steal: The 1px border weight in a muted gray (#bcbcbc) for input fields—provides just e…
The label-above-field with permanent visibility in a mid-tone gray (#7c7c7c)—eli…
avoid: The monochrome palette with no accent color creates a system where all interacti…
entries.critique: The full-width primary button forces a binary interaction model where the user must complete or abandon the form with equal prominence given to both paths. This DECISION (equal visual weight for 'Cancel' text versus 'Continue' button) creates an EFFECT of reduced pressure to complete, as both paths feel like legitimate choices rather than one being stigmatized. This REJECTS the common pattern of making cancel actions visually recessive (ghosted buttons, underlined text, smaller size) which creates artificial urgency through limited-feeling options. The hairline border treatment on the input field (single-pixel #bcbcbc stroke) sets up a DECISION where the field boundary is suggested rather than declared, creating an EFFECT of breathing room that separates the input area from surrounding white space without introducing visual weight. This REJECTS the heavier 2-3px borders or filled background patterns that assert themselves as objects rather than letting the typed content become the focal point. The label-always-visible positioning makes a DECISION to consume vertical space with persistent 'Email' text, generating an EFFECT where users never lose context about what they're typing even mid-entry. This REJECTS the space-efficient placeholder-only pattern that collapses label and input into one line but creates a memory tax once the user begins typing.
whatToSteal: The 1px border weight in a muted gray (#bcbcbc) for input fields—provides just enough definition to establish the interactive region without competing for attention against the user's typed content, especially effective when the canvas and surface share the same white value

The label-above-field with permanent visibility in a mid-tone gray (#7c7c7c)—eliminates the placeholder vanishing problem while establishing a clear vertical rhythm where informational text and input space occupy distinct horizontal bands

The unadorned text 'Cancel' action in body ink color (#3c3c3c) without button styling—removes the visual hierarchy between proceed and abandon paths, creating a low-pressure interaction where backing out feels as legitimate as continuing forward

The generous negative space ratio where the modal content area occupies roughly 40% of viewport width—forces each form element to stand alone rather than clustering, making each decision point feel like a discrete commitment rather than one item in a rushed checklist
antiPatterns: The monochrome palette with no accent color creates a system where all interactive elements must rely on weight, size, or placement for differentiation—this works for binary forms but would collapse under complexity when multiple action priorities need simultaneous expression, as users have no color-based shorthand for 'destructive' versus 'confirmatory' versus 'neutral' actions

entries.title: Cash App 56
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The single-hue activation pattern (DECISION: #14d45c green applied exclusively to ON-state toggles, with OFF states remaining gray #7c7c7c) …
steal: Single-hue activation: apply your accent color only to the ON state of binary co…
Italic as metadata flag: reserve italic (not bold, not color) for labels that ne…
avoid: The italic 'Invites' label uses type style to signal meaning, but without any su…
entries.critique: The single-hue activation pattern (DECISION: #14d45c green applied exclusively to ON-state toggles, with OFF states remaining gray #7c7c7c) creates binary legibility through color presence versus color absence (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional approach of using two distinct hues for ON/OFF which would compete for attention and double the accent budget (REJECTION). The asymmetric horizontal anchoring (DECISION: labels 24px from left, toggles 16px from right edge) establishes clear functional zones without centering elements (EFFECT), avoiding centered alignment which would create visual tension as toggle states change width and disturb optical balance (REJECTION). The lineless row separation (DECISION: 20px vertical spacing with no stroke dividers between settings) relies on whitespace alone to define tap boundaries (EFFECT), rejecting hairline dividers that would add 7+ visual elements to a screen already carrying 9+ interactive components and increase perceived density (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Single-hue activation: apply your accent color only to the ON state of binary controls, leave OFF states in muted gray — this cuts your accent usage by half and makes every active control instantly scannable without training users on a second accent hue

Italic as metadata flag: reserve italic (not bold, not color) for labels that need distinction without hierarchy change — works especially well for beta features, optional settings, or secondary toggles that don't deserve weight promotion

Asymmetric edge anchoring: lock labels to one margin (24px left) and controls to another (16px right) using different values — the unequal spacing prevents centered-layout rigidity and accommodates variable control widths without reflowing the entire row

Equal-height navigation zones: give bottom nav icons a fixed-height container (64px) with equal top/bottom padding — this creates uniform tap targets even when icon artwork varies in intrinsic height, without enforcing a viewBox on the SVG itself
antiPatterns: The italic 'Invites' label uses type style to signal meaning, but without any supporting visual grammar (no icon, no subtext, no color shift) users must infer what the slant represents — if italic means 'new feature' or 'beta' or 'organizational setting', that semantic load belongs in a secondary label or icon, not hidden in font posture alone

entries.title: Borrow
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The dual-button pattern assigns #17c709 solid fill to 'Turn on notifications' and outline-only treatment to 'Not now' (DECISION), establishi…
steal: Outline-only secondary buttons using the same hue as filled primaries—maintains …
Letter-spacing on completion-state headlines to create temporal deceleration—for…
avoid: The pill-radius corners on buttons combined with pill-radius modal itself create…
entries.critique: The dual-button pattern assigns #17c709 solid fill to 'Turn on notifications' and outline-only treatment to 'Not now' (DECISION), establishing action hierarchy through fill density rather than size or position (EFFECT), rejecting same-visual-weight buttons that force users to parse meaning before understanding priority (REJECTION). The #4a4841 modal surface against #e0f4d6 canvas creates 82-point luminance separation (DECISION), ensuring the overlay reads as a distinct interaction layer even on low-contrast displays (EFFECT), avoiding semi-transparent scrims that blend foreground and background into ambiguous spatial zones (REJECTION). The 'SKIP' link receives no button chrome or padding (DECISION), reducing its visual weight to barely-present so committed users discover it without competing against primary conversion paths (EFFECT), rejecting equal-prominence exit options that undermine the modal's persuasive intent (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Outline-only secondary buttons using the same hue as filled primaries—maintains color system cohesion while achieving hierarchy through fill density, avoiding the need for a separate 'secondary color' in your palette

Letter-spacing on completion-state headlines to create temporal deceleration—forces a visual pause that reinforces arrival at a milestone, distinguishing end-of-flow messaging from mid-flow instructions
Bare text links for tertiary actions positioned in corners without padding or backgrounds—grants escape hatches to power users while preserving focal hierarchy for primary conversion goals

High-luminance canvas colors paired with low-luminance modal surfaces (80+ point separation)—creates unambiguous spatial layering without requiring drop shadows or blur effects that complicate rendering
antiPatterns: The pill-radius corners on buttons combined with pill-radius modal itself creates shape redundancy—every interactive element shares identical geometry, eliminating shape as a differentiating variable and making the button feel like a detached piece of the container rather than an independent control

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The notification bell icon rendered in neon green (#04e314) against charcoal (#444444) creates a 13:1 luminance ratio—well beyond WCAG AAA—e…
steal: Render permission-request icons in the primary accent color at extreme contrast …
Use sentence case for permission modal titles ('Allow Notifications' not 'Allow …
avoid: The body copy runs edge-to-edge within the modal's content area with no inset ma…
entries.critique: The notification bell icon rendered in neon green (#04e314) against charcoal (#444444) creates a 13:1 luminance ratio—well beyond WCAG AAA—establishing instant visual priority before any text is read; this rejects the typical iOS-style monochrome glyph approach where the icon simply decorates the message rather than commanding attention. The 'Not Now' dismissal action appears as bare text in the bottom-left corner without button chrome or underline styling, requiring the user to recognize it as interactive solely through position and color (#7c7c7c); this abandons the conventional secondary-button pattern (outlined or ghost button) that would give it equal affordance weight, correctly deprioritizing rejection in a flow where opt-in is the desired path. The Continue button extends to within 24px of both modal edges while the body text above it uses the same horizontal bounds, creating a unified content column; this rejects the common pattern of centering a narrow button that would visually disconnect it from the message it confirms, instead treating the call-to-action as the structural conclusion of a single reading path.
whatToSteal: Render permission-request icons in the primary accent color at extreme contrast ratios (13:1+) rather than neutral grays—the neon green bell immediately signals 'this is a Cash App decision' and prevents the modal from feeling like an OS-level interruption the user must navigate around

Use sentence case for permission modal titles ('Allow Notifications' not 'Allow Notifications?') to reduce the perceived weight of the interruption—title case adds formality that amplifies user resistance to granting access

Position the dismissal action as plain text in the bottom-left corner without button styling—this creates asymmetric visual weight that subtly guides toward the primary action while still offering an exit, rejecting the false equivalence of side-by-side buttons

Extend the primary button width to match the text column above it rather than centering a narrow button—this treats the CTA as the final line of a single vertical narrative instead of a separate decision element
antiPatterns: The body copy runs edge-to-edge within the modal's content area with no inset margins, creating line lengths of approximately 60-65 characters—optimal for reading—but this only works because the modal itself is width-constrained; in a wider viewport or different orientation, this same full-width text rule would produce unreadable 90+ character lines, revealing a layout that relies on the container's physical size rather than typographic max-width constraints

entries.title: Square
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The disabled button state (#7c7c7c) forces explicit selection before progression — users cannot accidentally advance with incomplete data, a…
steal: Disable primary CTAs until required input is complete, using the same muted valu…
Use discrete circle indicators (1, 2, 3...) instead of percentage bars for multi…
avoid: The back arrow uses the same muted gray (#7c7c7c) as disabled form elements, vio…
entries.critique: The disabled button state (#7c7c7c) forces explicit selection before progression — users cannot accidentally advance with incomplete data, and the visual state communicates that the form awaits input rather than allowing ambiguous "maybe this works" taps. This rejects the optimistic validation pattern where buttons remain enabled and show errors after tap, trading discoverability for certainty. The 1-of-6 circle indicator uses discrete units rather than a progress bar, making each step feel like a complete unit of work instead of arbitrary points along a continuum — users know they'll answer six distinct questions, not inch toward 100%. The full-width radio containers expand tap targets beyond the small circles themselves, letting users tap anywhere in the white rectangle to select an option; this rejects the minimal-chrome approach where only the label or circle is tappable, acknowledging that thumbs on glass need forgiving hit areas. The back arrow's muted treatment (#7c7c7c) creates unintended ambiguity by matching the disabled button color, suggesting it might not be tappable when it actually is — this contradicts the elsewhere-consistent pattern of using full ink (#444444) for interactive elements.
whatToSteal: Disable primary CTAs until required input is complete, using the same muted value (#7c7c7c here) already established for inactive elements — this creates a two-state system (muted = wait, full ink = proceed) that users learn once and apply everywhere, rather than inventing yellow warning states or red error borders

Use discrete circle indicators (1, 2, 3...) instead of percentage bars for multi-step flows where each screen collects a single, complete piece of information — circles make each step feel like answering one question rather than filling arbitrary progress toward a distant goal

Expand radio button tap targets to full-width containers with ample vertical padding (appears ~48-60px tall here), letting users tap the entire row rather than hunting for the small circle — especially critical when options are stacked vertically and users are tapping with thumbs while holding the device one-handed

Differentiate hierarchy through weight and color shifts rather than size jumps — the question text here stays readable without becoming a billboard, using heavier weight + darker ink (#444444) while supporting copy drops to muted (#7c7c7c), avoiding the teenage-resume problem of making headings 3x larger than body text
antiPatterns: The back arrow uses the same muted gray (#7c7c7c) as disabled form elements, violating the screen's own rule that muted = inactive — users must guess whether navigation chrome follows different color logic than form controls, creating a split mental model where chrome might mean something different than content

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The full-bleed #05dc35 green button with pill corners (DECISION) creates a single, unavoidable tap target that occupies the entire width of …
steal: Apply secondary information color (#7c7c7c versus #444444 title) to soften deliv…
Use edge-to-edge button width on mobile overlay cards to eliminate tap-target am…
avoid: The white card on black backdrop creates harsh edge contrast without any transit…
entries.critique: The full-bleed #05dc35 green button with pill corners (DECISION) creates a single, unavoidable tap target that occupies the entire width of the card (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional centered, width-constrained button that would introduce hesitation about hit-area boundaries and require more precision from the user (REJECTION). The #7c7c7c gray applied to deprecation messaging (DECISION) subordinates bad news below the neutral-tone title, letting users parse urgency hierarchy before emotional reaction (EFFECT), avoiding the typical red/warning color treatment that would frontload anxiety and potentially trigger immediate dismissal before comprehension (REJECTION). The QR code icon rendered in #05dc35 (DECISION) maintains brand color continuity while identifying the deprecated feature visually (EFFECT), rejecting a grayscale or muted treatment that would fail to connect this modal to the feature being discontinued or create visual disconnection from the action button (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Apply secondary information color (#7c7c7c versus #444444 title) to soften delivery of negative news while preserving readability, letting users absorb context before emotional weight
Use edge-to-edge button width on mobile overlay cards to eliminate tap-target ambiguity and reduce the cognitive load of precision aiming during dismissal flows
Echo the accent color (#05dc35) in both the feature icon and primary action to create visual bracketing that guides the eye from problem identification to resolution path
Set deprecation dates in running body copy rather than emphasized callouts to lower panic response and encourage full-sentence reading of transition details
antiPatterns: The white card on black backdrop creates harsh edge contrast without any transition treatment—no blur, no scrim opacity variation, no shadow—making the modal feel digitally superimposed rather than spatially layered, potentially undermining the urgency signal the interruption intends to convey

entries.title: Cash App 57
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The magenta bottom-border-only input field (#fa63c4) creates a floating label effect that visually anchors the text cursor position without …
steal: Single-border input styling using only a colored bottom stroke (#fa63c4) — remov…
Circular action button that physically overlaps the input field boundary rather …

entries.critique: The magenta bottom-border-only input field (#fa63c4) creates a floating label effect that visually anchors the text cursor position without boxing in the content — this rejects the conventional four-sided border or filled background approach that would compete with the circular action button for visual weight. The circular purple button (#b43cfc) positioned to overlap the right edge of the input establishes a directional flow where the search field appears to feed directly into the action trigger — conventional right-aligned buttons separated by padding force users to parse two distinct zones rather than one integrated gesture. The tan placeholder color (#cecc9d) at roughly 40% opacity equivalent provides just enough contrast to be legible while fading compared to actual input text — this rejects high-contrast placeholder text that competes with user-entered content and fails to signal the transient nature of instructional copy.
whatToSteal: Single-border input styling using only a colored bottom stroke (#fa63c4) — removes visual clutter from inactive borders while maintaining a clear baseline for text entry, particularly effective when the action button provides the primary visual terminus

Circular action button that physically overlaps the input field boundary rather than sitting in isolated padding — creates a connected flow where the input visually feeds into the action, reducing the cognitive load of parsing separate interactive zones

Dramatically desaturated placeholder text (#cecc9d against #ffffff) that reads as roughly 40% opacity — signals ephemeral instructional content without competing for attention once users begin typing, maintaining clear hierarchy between temporary hints and permanent input

Generous vertical spacing (spacious density) between the label 'Search' and the input field itself — allows each element to occupy its own breathing room without requiring additional visual separators or background panels
antiPatterns: The circular search button uses an icon-only affordance with no visible text label or tooltip indicator — users unfamiliar with magnifying glass iconography or those using screen readers receive no redundant confirmation of the button's purpose, creating potential accessibility and discoverability barriers that a dual icon-label approach would eliminate

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #dcf4fc canvas background (DECISION: a near-white cyan at 95% luminosity) keeps the screen from reading as stark paper (EFFECT: maintain…
steal: Use a 95% luminosity tint of your accent color for canvas backgrounds — it maint…
Omit navigation dividers entirely when your active state uses a high-chroma colo…
avoid: The identical type weight and size across all navigation labels means users must…
entries.critique: The #dcf4fc canvas background (DECISION: a near-white cyan at 95% luminosity) keeps the screen from reading as stark paper (EFFECT: maintains brand color presence without competing with content) while avoiding pure white which would make the navigation bar feel like an afterthought (REJECTION: white canvases force navigation to assert itself through heavy shadows or thick borders). The absence of any navigation-to-content divider (DECISION: no top border, gradient, or shadow on the nav bar) lets tappable zones extend to their natural bounds (EFFECT: maximizes hit areas and removes visual noise) rather than adding a stroke that would slice 1-2px off the tappable region and telegraph unnecessary visual weight (REJECTION: bordered navbars treat the navigation as a separate document when it's actually a persistent control layer). The direct icon-to-label vertical stacking with no intervening space (DECISION: labels sit immediately beneath glyphs with ~4px gap) creates single-unit tap targets (EFFECT: each item reads as one cohesive button) instead of the split-zone problem where icons and labels feel like separate clickable regions (REJECTION: extra padding between icon and label suggests two interactive elements or creates dead space users avoid tapping).
whatToSteal: Use a 95% luminosity tint of your accent color for canvas backgrounds — it maintains brand presence without creating contrast competition, letting interactive elements (buttons, cards) assert themselves against a subtly branded field rather than fighting for attention on pure white

Omit navigation dividers entirely when your active state uses a high-chroma color (#07d3fa here) — the color contrast between selected and unselected icons (4.7:1 ratio) provides sufficient boundary definition without adding structural lines that reduce tappable area and add visual weight

Stack navigation labels directly beneath icons with minimal gap (4-6px) to create single-unit tap targets — this prevents the psychological split where users wonder if the icon or text is the 'real' button, and eliminates dead zones between the two elements

Reserve icon fills exclusively for the active state while keeping inactive icons as strokes — this creates a binary on/off signal that's faster to parse than color-only changes, because filled vs. outlined is a shape-based distinction processed pre-attentively
antiPatterns: The identical type weight and size across all navigation labels means users must scan upward to the icon to determine which tab is active — adding a semibold weight or 1pt size increase to the active label would create a redundant signal that doesn't require eye movement, especially valuable for users who navigate by text rather than iconography

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The repeating cyan (#06d3fa) icon in the left margin of every settings row creates a rhythmic vertical beat that functions as a scan line fo…
steal: Use identical placeholder icons across all list rows (rather than semantically v…
Match your interactive affordance color (chevrons, arrows, carets) to your secon…
avoid: The settings list offers no visual preview or consequence indicator for what tap…
entries.critique: The repeating cyan (#06d3fa) icon in the left margin of every settings row creates a rhythmic vertical beat that functions as a scan line for the eye. This decision produces two simultaneous effects: it segments the list into parseable chunks (each icon marks a new entry point) and it establishes a strong left alignment column that guides downward reading momentum. The rejection here is the conventional approach of using icons only where semantically meaningful (profile icon for profile, phone icon for phone)—Cash App instead uses identical visual weight across all rows, which prevents the semantic meaning of individual icons from creating false hierarchy or causing users to hunt for "the right icon." The chevron-right symbols in #848484 muted gray (matching the secondary text color, not the #2f2f2f primary ink) deliberately suppress their visual prominence. This produces a functional effect: users recognize the affordance (tappable row) without the chevrons competing for attention against the primary content (your actual name, phone, email). The rejection is the common pattern of making interactive affordances visually loud (bright accent colors, heavy weight)—here, de-emphasizing the chevron keeps focus on the data itself, which is what users came to verify or edit. The bottom navigation applies accent cyan only to the active 'Settings' state while rendering inactive labels in #848484, but crucially all five icons maintain identical size and spatial weight. This decision creates state indication through color alone, which produces instant recognition of current location without requiring users to decode size changes or icon transformations. The rejection is the pattern of using multiple attributes (size + color + weight) for selected states, which often results in layout shift and visual noise—this single-attribute approach maintains grid stability.
whatToSteal: Use identical placeholder icons across all list rows (rather than semantically varied icons) to create a consistent left-edge scan column—eliminates the cognitive cost of decoding icon meanings while preserving visual rhythm and alignment

Match your interactive affordance color (chevrons, arrows, carets) to your secondary text color instead of primary ink—users will still recognize tappability, but the affordance won't compete with the actual content for attention priority

Establish type hierarchy through size reduction alone (20% drop between tiers) when you can pair it with a simultaneous color shift from dark to muted—the dual-attribute change makes weight variation unnecessary and keeps the type texture uniform

Apply accent color to only one navigation attribute (the label text) while keeping icon size and weight identical across states—this produces instant location recognition without layout shift or visual instability in the nav bar
antiPatterns: The settings list offers no visual preview or consequence indicator for what tapping a row will do—every row looks identically tappable regardless of whether it opens a simple text field, launches a complex verification flow, or triggers an external email client. This creates a uniform interaction model that feels predictable but provides no scent of information: users must tap to discover scope, which punishes exploration when someone just wants to scan what's editable versus what's a deep configuration path.

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The decision to fill the primary CTA with the brand’s brightest green (#05e315) and shape it as a pill ensures that even a first-time user’s…
steal: Reserve the brightest accent color exclusively for the single action that comple…
Create a 'verification block' by wrapping the transaction total and recipient in…
avoid: This design avoids burying the primary action inside a list of stacked, isomorph…
It deliberately rejects the common approach of using placeholder text inside inp…
entries.critique: The decision to fill the primary CTA with the brand’s brightest green (#05e315) and shape it as a pill ensures that even a first-time user’s gaze anchors on the pay button within the first half-second scan, a triumph over the typical outline button that fades into the visual noise of the form. Placing the summary card on a distinct green surface (#92ec9c) before the final tap creates a micro-pause that forces verification — returning users who routinely miss inline totals will no longer skip this last checkpoint, reducing mistaken payments. The icon-only back button exploits mobile convention so deeply that it disappears from the task flow, allowing users with muscle memory to navigate away without any mental cost, but still offering an exit for new users who recognize the standard arrow. Meanwhile, the pill-shaped input fields with color-shifting borders convert an anxiety-prone moment (typing financial data) into a softer, guided sequence, rejecting the rigid rectangles that make many users feel they’re filling out a tax form rather than sending money.
whatToSteal: Reserve the brightest accent color exclusively for the single action that completes the task (here, the pay button). This ensures that the visual hierarchy is unambiguous even under time pressure; avoid this when the screen has multiple equally important primary actions, as users will be confused about which to tap first.

Create a 'verification block' by wrapping the transaction total and recipient in a background card of a low-saturation variant of your brand color (here, #92ec9c). This functions as a speed bump in the scanning path. Only apply this pattern when the action is costly or irreversible — for low-stakes flows, it feels like unnecessary friction.

Use a pill radius and a muted border on form fields, and shift only the border to the accent color on focus. This teaches the user where they are without introducing a persistent colored outline that can look cluttered. Do not use this if your form has no visual separation between fields, as the color shift alone may not be enough to distinguish which field is active.

Rely on platform-standard icon-only navigation (back arrow) to streamline the header, trusting users’ learned mobile patterns. Do not do this if your audience includes users unfamiliar with OS conventions or if the same icon could mean something else in your context (e.g., a swipe-to-dismiss flow).
antiPatterns: This design avoids burying the primary action inside a list of stacked, isomorphic text links, which would force users to scan the entire form looking for the one that matters. Instead, the filled pill button stands alone, making the decision point obvious.

It deliberately rejects the common approach of using placeholder text inside input fields as labels, which disappears when users start typing and leaves them without context. Here, labels likely sit outside the fields, so information never vanishes.

It avoids using a white or same-color background for the summary card, which would blend it with the rest of the form and cause many users to skip it. The colored surface acts as an intentional visual interruption.

entries.title: Cash App 59
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The stepped horizontal alignment — where the header reaches edge-to-edge while the form content respects lateral padding — creates a structu…
steal: Use stepped horizontal alignment where the container's header breaks the margins…
Deploy low-contrast modal backdrops (under 2:1 ratio) when the action is routine…
avoid: The textarea lacks visible focus indication treatment — with slight-round corner…
The 'Name' and 'Email' fields sit directly adjacent with no visible divider or s…
entries.critique: The stepped horizontal alignment — where the header reaches edge-to-edge while the form content respects lateral padding — creates a structural anchor that prevents the modal from floating ambiguously in space. EFFECT: The header becomes an architectural lid that contains the form, establishing clear ownership of the content region. REJECTION: Uniform edge treatment (where all content shares the same margins) would make the header feel like just another form row instead of a containing element. The low-contrast backdrop (#ffffff modal on #c4c4c4 canvas at 1.8:1) forces the modal's borders and internal hierarchy to carry all the separation work instead of relying on a darkened scrim. EFFECT: The form remains embedded in the interface's tonal range, suggesting an in-context action rather than a context-breaking interruption. REJECTION: High-contrast overlays (60-80% black scrims) would create modal-as-emergency visual language inappropriate for routine messaging. The dual-blue button scheme — where the primary action uses #06748b and the header uses #39848c — segments interactive intent from spatial framing through hue-shifted siblings. EFFECT: The button earns perceptual priority through its 10.5:1 contrast ratio while the header recedes to 4.9:1, creating a tappability gradient within the same color family. REJECTION: Single-blue systems would collapse the distinction between 'where am I' and 'what do I do next,' forcing size or position alone to carry that semantic load.
whatToSteal: Use stepped horizontal alignment where the container's header breaks the margins of its content — full-bleed headers create containment while inset bodies establish focus hierarchy without additional visual weight

Deploy low-contrast modal backdrops (under 2:1 ratio) when the action is routine and contextual — forces the design to earn separation through structure and borders rather than dramatic dimming that signals interruption

Assign contrast-ratio hierarchy within a single hue family by shifting lightness for interactive vs structural elements — the 10.5:1 button vs 4.9:1 header split creates tappability priority while maintaining color coherence
Reserve ALL-CAPS typography exclusively for action-completion text ('SEND MESSAGE') rather than applying it broadly — creates a visual contract that capitalization means commitment, not decoration
antiPatterns: The textarea lacks visible focus indication treatment — with slight-round corners and borders already present, there's no affordance showing how the field will respond when activated, leaving users to discover interaction states through trial

The 'Name' and 'Email' fields sit directly adjacent with no visible divider or spacing differential — when both are unfilled, the vertical rhythm creates ambiguity about where one field ends and the next begins, especially problematic for autofill detection

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The canvas color (#04e414) serves as both background AND brand identifier, eliminating the need for a separate logo or color accent bar—this…
steal: Use your brand's signature color as the modal canvas rather than relegating it t…
Stack buttons with identical widths but contrasting fill strategies (solid vs. s…
avoid: The 3.5:1 contrast between headline text (#0f7815) and background (#04e414) fall…
entries.critique: The canvas color (#04e414) serves as both background AND brand identifier, eliminating the need for a separate logo or color accent bar—this works because the green is luminous enough to command attention yet saturated enough to avoid appearing washed-out, rejecting the typical white-modal-on-dimmed-background formula that requires additional brand signaling. The button hierarchy inverts the expected pattern by making the affirmative action (Continue) visually heavier through a filled dark treatment while the dismissive action (Not now) gets a lighter surface—this succeeds because the filled button reads as the path forward without needing underlines or arrows, avoiding the increasingly stale pattern of transparent-outline primary buttons that lack sufficient affordance weight. The bell icon uses stroke rendering instead of a solid fill, maintaining legibility against the bright background through preserved negative space—a filled icon would merge into an indistinct blob at this contrast ratio, demonstrating why line-weight glyphs outperform solid shapes when figure-ground contrast is compressed.
whatToSteal: Use your brand's signature color as the modal canvas rather than relegating it to accent dots—lets the background itself do brand work while eliminating redundant logo placements, but only viable when the brand color has sufficient luminosity (L* above 75) to support readable text overlays

Stack buttons with identical widths but contrasting fill strategies (solid vs. surface-light) to create hierarchy without resorting to size differences or ghost-button outlines—the filled button naturally reads as primary through visual weight alone, provided the fill color maintains 4.5:1+ against its label

Set body copy 2-3 steps lighter in hue rather than dropping to 60% opacity—targeting a mid-value gray (#848484 at approximately L*55) preserves glyph sharpness while achieving hierarchy, avoiding the washed-out antialiasing artifacts that opacity-based muting introduces on colored backgrounds

Apply tighter corner radii to the container (20px) than to the button endcaps (24px producing full pills)—creates a subtle shape-language distinction where interactive elements feel more gestural than their containing frame, rejecting the flat-radius approach where every rectangle shares identical corner treatment
antiPatterns: The 3.5:1 contrast between headline text (#0f7815) and background (#04e414) falls below WCAG AA requirements for body text—works here only because the text is large (17pt) and limited to a short headline, but demonstrates the risk of brand-color-as-canvas strategies when the brand palette lacks sufficient internal contrast range for arbitrary text lengths

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The #04e314 pure electric green at 100% saturation on the CTA rejects the standard practice of slightly desaturated brand greens (typically …
steal: Apply selective word bolding within sentence-case headers rather than bolding en…
Use page indicator dots rendered in a darker shade of the accent color (#224a24 …
avoid: The page indicator dots use a dark green (#224a24) fill that ties to the brand p…
The navigation tiles are presented in a static grid without visual preview or sc…
entries.critique: The #04e314 pure electric green at 100% saturation on the CTA rejects the standard practice of slightly desaturated brand greens (typically 85-92% saturation) because the neon intensity creates retinal persistence that holds attention even in peripheral vision, useful when competing against screen-scroll momentum. The sentence-case header with selective word bolding ('bank') rejects title-case convention because it mirrors natural speech rhythm while the bold acts as a visual anchor for scanning, letting users decode the value proposition in under 0.5 seconds rather than parsing a uniform string. The two-line navigation labels with weight differentiation (bold primary, muted secondary) reject single-line icon labels because the hierarchy allows instant recognition from the bold keyword while the gray descriptor prevents misinterpretation, reducing navigation errors without requiring users to tap-and-learn.
whatToSteal: Apply selective word bolding within sentence-case headers rather than bolding entire phrases — isolate the single highest-value keyword to create a scannable anchor that works even during fast scrolling, particularly effective when the bolded word represents the product's core differentiator

Use page indicator dots rendered in a darker shade of the accent color (#224a24 vs #04e314) rather than neutral gray, because it maintains brand continuity across micro-elements while avoiding the visual competition that would occur if accent-colored dots fought with the CTA button

Structure navigation tiles with bold primary labels and muted (#7c7c7c) secondary descriptors on separate lines rather than forcing all information into a single-weight label, allowing users to recognize categories from bold keywords in under 200ms while descriptors prevent category confusion

Position the primary CTA as a fixed bottom element with maximum contrast (white text on #04e314) against a white canvas, creating a persistent conversion path that remains visible regardless of scroll position and avoids the drop-off from buried CTAs
antiPatterns: The page indicator dots use a dark green (#224a24) fill that ties to the brand palette but creates poor contrast against the white canvas compared to the established muted gray (#7c7c7c) used elsewhere in the interface — users must focus directly on the dots to perceive which slide is active, whereas higher-contrast indicators support peripheral awareness during scrolling

The navigation tiles are presented in a static grid without visual preview or scent of the content behind each option, forcing users to rely solely on icon metaphor and text labels rather than providing a glimpse of the actual interface they'll land on, which increases navigation uncertainty for first-time users unfamiliar with fintech feature categories

entries.title: Moneybot
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The progress indicator implements three visual states (filled circle for active, outline for future, checkmark for complete) rather than the…
steal: Three-state progress dots (active filled, future outline, past checkmark) — impl…
Shadow-casting modal cards with 24-32px blur radius on darker backgrounds — use …
avoid: The 'Skip' link relies solely on position (top-right) and label text to communic…
entries.critique: The progress indicator implements three visual states (filled circle for active, outline for future, checkmark for complete) rather than the conventional two-state dot system (active/inactive). This EFFECT allows users to distinguish between 'not yet seen' and 'already completed' when navigating backward, providing spatial memory of their journey. It REJECTS the assumption that onboarding is purely linear-forward, acknowledging that users may need to reference previous screens. The non-traditional piggy bank colorway (#8a6f12 body, #72b63c accents) discards the expected pink-ceramic aesthetic. This EFFECT separates the product from children's banking apps and toys, signaling financial tooling for adults despite the playful illustration style. It REJECTS the pink-means-savings convention that would infantilize the experience. The raised card surface casts a dark, blurred shadow of roughly 24-32px spread against the #b7c6a0 canvas. This EFFECT creates the perception of physical proximity — the modal appears to float 2-3cm above the background rather than existing in flat z-index space. It REJECTS the flat-overlay approach where modals feel like interruptions rather than deliberate objects you can mentally 'pick up' and examine.
whatToSteal: Three-state progress dots (active filled, future outline, past checkmark) — implement whenever onboarding allows backward navigation or when users benefit from seeing their completed path, not just their current position

Shadow-casting modal cards with 24-32px blur radius on darker backgrounds — use when the modal content deserves object-like presence rather than feeling like a blocking overlay; particularly effective for educational or high-consideration flows

Single-axis typographic hierarchy (scale only, no weight or family variation) — deploy when you want body text to feel like a direct explanation of the headline rather than a separate content block; forces tighter conceptual coupling between heading and description

Non-default illustration color palettes that break category conventions (golden piggy bank instead of pink) — apply when your product needs to escape established category aesthetics; test whether conventional colors carry unwanted associations (childlike, gendered, toy-like) that contradict your positioning
antiPatterns: The 'Skip' link relies solely on position (top-right) and label text to communicate interactivity, lacking any visual affordance like underline, color differentiation from body text, or hit-state preview — users accustomed to explicit button treatments may not recognize it as tappable, particularly in the top corner where attention is rarely directed during content reading

entries.title: Cash App
entries.critiqueExcerpt: The inline hyperlink in 'Customize your Cash Card and get free discounts' isolates the product name in accent color (#00D54B range), making …
steal: Inline hyperlink colorization on product nouns within value propositions — not o…
Ink-weight differentiation between primary/secondary actions using only grayscal…

Body copy front-loads feature explanation ('Customize your Cash Card') before ou…
entries.critique: The inline hyperlink in 'Customize your Cash Card and get free discounts' isolates the product name in accent color (#00D54B range), making it the only chromatic element in grayscale copy. This works because it anchors the value proposition to a tangible object users can visualize, rather than letting 'customize' or 'discounts' compete for attention. It rejects the conventional approach of making the entire phrase a link or highlighting action words, which would diffuse focus across multiple concepts. The button pair uses ink-weight asymmetry (#4c4c4c vs #838383) instead of stroke borders to establish hierarchy, letting the 'Order' button claim visual dominance through darkness alone. This avoids the typical outlined-secondary pattern that adds perceptual noise and makes both options feel equally valid. The compact line-height (≈1.4) on body copy forces each benefit phrase into immediate proximity with the next, creating urgency through density. Conventional ~1.6 spacing would suggest leisurely consideration; this compression implies 'read fast, decide now.'
whatToSteal: Inline hyperlink colorization on product nouns within value propositions — not on action verbs or full phrases — to anchor abstract benefits to concrete, visualizable objects
Ink-weight differentiation between primary/secondary actions using only grayscale values (#4c4c4c vs #838383), eliminating stroke borders that introduce competing visual systems
Sub-1.5 line-height on short persuasive copy blocks to weaponize vertical rhythm as urgency signal, making users process benefits as a rapid sequence rather than browsable list
Modal corner radius sized to match button radius (appears 12-16px both), creating geometric consistency between container and controls without declaring an explicit design system
antiPatterns: The 'Not now' dismissal lives at equal hierarchical weight to 'Order' through identical size and proximity, when asymmetric placement (bottom-left vs centered, or footer vs body) would better signal their unequal strategic importance

Body copy front-loads feature explanation ('Customize your Cash Card') before outcome ('get free discounts'), burying the financial incentive in a dependent clause when leading with savings would answer the implicit user question 'why should I care' faster
