entries.critique: The 2px purple underline beneath 'Spend' (DECISION) creates state indication through linear accent rather than background fill (EFFECT), allowing the navigation to remain optically lightweight while still communicating selection—this rejects the common tab pattern with solid background pills that add visual noise and make the entire row heavier. The serif-only treatment of '$34,506.93' (DECISION) leverages typographic voice shift to signal data importance (EFFECT), avoiding the typical bold-weight-on-same-family approach that creates thickness without creating enough perceptual distance from surrounding elements. The account balances rendering in muted purple #865388 instead of ink (DECISION) subordinates secondary numbers through color temperature (EFFECT), rejecting the standard opacity-reduction technique that often produces muddy, accessibility-hostile grays on complex backgrounds.
whatToSteal: Use a 2px linear underline in your accent color for selected navigation states instead of background fills—it preserves vertical rhythm and keeps the navigation bar optically transparent

Switch to a serif typeface exclusively for high-importance numbers while keeping all other text sans-serif—the style contrast creates hierarchy without requiring larger sizes that break layout balance

Assign secondary numerical data to your muted color role rather than using opacity on your ink color—this maintains consistent color temperature across the interface instead of introducing unpredictable gray values

Position tertiary actions like 'View all accounts' as text-only links in your accent color at layout edges—this creates functional density without adding button chrome that would compete with primary content cards
antiPatterns: The warm canvas color #c49da1 hosts purple text links without sufficient luminance contrast—the 'View all accounts' link in #8650ec likely fails WCAG AA against this background, forcing users to rely on position rather than color to identify interactive elements

entries.critique: The 2px border weight on unselected pills (DECISION) creates a mechanical precision that registers as 'financial tool' rather than 'lifestyle app' (EFFECT), rejecting the 1px hairline default that would read as digital ephemera instead of institutional trust (REJECTION). The 24px vertical padding inside each pill (DECISION) expands the touch target beyond mobile minimums to create a rhythm of considered pauses between options (EFFECT), avoiding the 12-16px compression that treats selection as rapid data entry rather than consequential choice (REJECTION). The purple fill state #7f51ad with white text reversal (DECISION) isolates color exclusively to the moment of commitment (EFFECT), refusing the pattern of purple labels or purple borders throughout that would dilute the accent into decoration rather than preserve it as a state-change signal (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Sentence-case questions with punctuation ('What brings you to Wealthsimple?') instead of title-case commands — shifts power dynamic from system instructing user to peer asking peer, appropriate when requesting personal financial decisions

First-person option labels ('I want to…') that frame radio buttons as user declarations rather than system categories — transforms selection into intention-setting, raising psychological weight of the choice

Single accent color reserved exclusively for selected state, with all unselected UI in grayscale — ensures color operates as state indicator rather than decorative theme, reducing cognitive load during multi-step flows
Thick 2px borders on unselected interactive elements paired with pill corners — communicates 'financial precision' through crisp geometric boundaries while warm beige canvas prevents coldness
antiPatterns: The beige-on-beige surface treatment (canvas #c9beac, card surface also #c9beac) collapses figure-ground separation — the 16px margin frame is only visible because of the drop shadow, meaning the spatial hierarchy depends entirely on a shadow effect rather than explicit color contrast, which fails in high-contrast accessibility modes or bright sunlight conditions

entries.critique: The navigation icons receive full-saturation lime fill (#7fdc22) against near-black (#34342d), a decision that pushes contrast beyond WCAG AAA thresholds and makes the sidebar visually aggressive rather than neutral chrome. This trades conventional grayscale navigation (where icons sit at 40-60% opacity until activated) for constant, high-energy signaling—effective when the product wants every tool to feel immediately actionable, but prevents the sidebar from ever receding. The portfolio card isolates the dollar amount in a serif face while rendering the gain in sans-serif, a typographic split that prevents the two numbers from competing for primacy; this rejects the single-font-family convention where hierarchy comes only from weight and size, making the main balance feel like editorial content while gains read as data annotation. The investment rows stack name-over-percentage in a 16px + 13px arrangement with 4px gap, a decision that keeps each row under 40px tall and allows 8+ holdings to appear without scrolling; this rejects the card-per-holding pattern where each asset gets breathing room and visual weight, prioritizing density so users scan the entire portfolio in a single glance.
whatToSteal: Apply full-saturation accent color to navigation icons instead of greyscale-until-active, making every tool feel charged and available rather than dormant—works when the product has few enough navigation items (5-7) that constant visibility doesn't create noise

Use serif-for-primary / sans-for-secondary typographic split within a single data display, letting font style (not just size or weight) carry hierarchy—effective when one number is the hero and others are supporting context

Set action buttons as stroked pills with no fill, allowing them to sit on any background value without requiring state-dependent fill colors—solves the problem of buttons that need to work over cards, images, and varied canvas tones

Render time-period selectors as plain text links with increased letter-spacing rather than segmented controls or button groups, reducing UI weight when the interaction is frequent and users need to see all options simultaneously without chrome interference
antiPatterns: The time period selector '1D 1W 1M 3M 1Y ALL' presents as unstyled text with no visual indication of which period is currently active, forcing users to infer state from the chart rather than reading the control—missing hover states or active indicators means the UI provides no feedback loop for a primary filtering action

entries.critique: The 8px left border for selection state (DECISION) creates a spatial anchor without obscuring content or requiring color fills (EFFECT), rejecting the conventional full-background highlight that reduces text contrast and creates visual noise when multiple items need simultaneous selection states (REJECTION). The 48px square touch target for navigation icons with 32px vertical spacing (DECISION) ensures accessible tap areas while maintaining vertical rhythm (EFFECT), avoiding the cramped 24px icon-only pattern that prioritizes screen real estate over ergonomic interaction (REJECTION). The justified row layout for asset line items with coin icon + ticker left and value right (DECISION) creates scannable columns that let users compare values without horizontal eye movement (EFFECT), rejecting the grouped left-aligned pattern that forces the eye to hunt for numbers across varying text widths (REJECTION). The +2% return display in cyan #14a5c8 directly beneath the portfolio total (DECISION) delivers time-sensitive performance data in the primary visual hierarchy (EFFECT), avoiding the tucked-away detail screen pattern that requires navigation to see account health (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use a thin (8px) colored left border on cards for selection state instead of background fills — it creates a clear selection indicator while preserving the card's original background color and maintaining text contrast across all states

Set financial values at 2.6× the size of supporting metadata (42pt vs 16pt) using weight alone — this scale jump makes the critical number instantly parseable without needing color coding or italics to denote hierarchy

Place performance indicators (+2%) directly beneath totals at the same indent level rather than inline — this vertical stack prioritizes the absolute value first while keeping change data immediately accessible without competing for horizontal space

Use all-caps tracking for non-interactive section labels while keeping interactive elements in sentence case — this creates a clear affordance distinction where tracked headers signal information zones and normal case signals clickable actions
antiPatterns: The overlay card lacks a close affordance — no visible × icon, 'Cancel' text, or escape instruction, forcing users to guess whether clicking outside, pressing Esc, or tapping a navigation item will dismiss it

The 'CASH' section header uses identical typography to 'OVERVIEW' and 'PORTFOLIO' despite being nested within the Portfolio card, flattening the information architecture by making a subsection visually equivalent to a top-level section

entries.critique: Tri-chromatic headline splitting (DECISION: applying three discrete color values—'#34342c', '#89734c', '#cd9c52'—across a single sentence) guides attention through semantic zones without scale changes (EFFECT: reader naturally decodes 'what' → 'what kind' → 'benefit' as three cognitive beats) while rejecting monochrome hierarchy that would require size or weight jumps to differentiate meaning (REJECTION: single-color headlines force dimensional contrast, which here would compete with the already-bold statement length). Double-border button treatment (DECISION: 2px yellow stroke outside solid dark fill) creates a third-state affordance (EFFECT: reads as both 'selected' and 'primary' simultaneously, implying the action is both available and recommended) instead of flat fills that encode binary on/off states (REJECTION: single-border buttons communicate availability but not preference order). Textured photographic backdrop at '#bdbab9' (DECISION: using tactile, warm-surface photography as canvas rather than flat color) embeds the interface in material reality (EFFECT: financial service feels grounded in physical trust rather than abstract digital promise) rather than gradient or solid backgrounds that signal pure software (REJECTION: immaterial backdrops make fintech feel speculative, not stewarded).
whatToSteal: All-caps label in hairline border above headline: creates a 'metadata' layer for context (geography, eligibility) that doesn't compete with primary message for emphasis—useful when you need to communicate scope without derailing narrative flow

Tri-chromatic semantic splitting within a single sentence: assign three related hues to subject/modifier/benefit segments so readers parse meaning in stages without requiring punctuation breaks or size shifts—works when your hierarchy is conceptual rather than structural

Double-border button (solid fill + contrasting outline): encodes 'recommended default action' rather than just 'available action' by combining two visual states into one affordance—steal when you want a single CTA to feel pre-selected without disabling alternatives

Textured material backdrop for trust-category products: photograph something tactile (paper, fabric, ceramic) as your canvas to anchor digital promises in physical reliability—works for finance, healthcare, legal services where trust is the product
antiPatterns: Input field and button share nearly identical widths but misalign by a few pixels—the email field appears fractionally wider, creating a 'which edge do I trust' problem where neither left nor right alignment feels intentional

The warm gold '#cd9c52' used for headline emphasis and button outline creates split duty—same hue signals both 'read this part of the message' and 'click here to act', forcing users to decode whether gold means information or interaction

entries.critique: The button uses surface-on-surface color (#d58036 on #d58036), eliminating the perimeter definition most forms rely on, which forces the eye to recognize actionability through the subtle tonal shift and 'Get started' label alone—this rejects the high-contrast button convention because the surrounding border and white space already frame the clickable zone. The checkmark icons align to text baseline instead of optical center, creating a left-edge rhythm where each icon's vertical position shifts slightly based on whether the adjacent text has descenders—this produces a staggered, organic column rather than the rigid grid most checklists enforce. The muted disclaimer paragraph (#a2bec8) maintains body text sizing while reducing only chromatic intensity, preserving readability for legally-required content that conventional forms would shrink to 0.75em and relegate to footers.
whatToSteal: Surface-colored buttons within a bordered modal: when the card edge already defines interactive space, matching button fill to modal background reduces visual weight while the border provides sufficient affordance—lets you remove one contrast layer without losing clarity

Baseline-aligned iconography for text-heavy lists: aligning checkmarks or bullets to the alphabetic baseline instead of vertical center creates a hand-typeset feel where each line's position responds to its content rather than enforcing mechanical uniformity

Same-size color-only de-emphasis for disclaimers: keeping secondary copy at full body size but shifting it to a muted ink color (#a2bec8 instead of #3b3a34) lets legal or conditional text remain accessible without the patronizing font-size reduction that signals 'this is fine print'

Condensed heading proportions for vertical UI: using a tall, narrow typeface for headings in constrained modal spaces increases prominence without consuming horizontal real estate, making the label feel stronger than a same-point-size standard-width face would
antiPatterns: The eligibility requirements use full sentences ('You're a Canadian resident', 'You're at least 18 years old') where noun phrases ('Canadian residency', 'Age 18+') would reduce scan time—the grammatical completeness adds cognitive load in a checklist context where users seek fast validation, not narrative prose

entries.critique: The canvas color (#3d4021) chooses a saturated warm olive over neutral charcoal or black, producing a dark interface that reads as earthy rather than technical — this rejects the standard dark-mode assumption that 'professional' requires desaturation. The pastel card palette (#df9da9, #6791a2, #b4acd4) uses distinct hues at medium saturation for each account type instead of opacity-based neutral grays, creating categorical color-coding that remains gentle enough not to compete with data — this avoids both the sterility of single-tone cards and the aggression of fully saturated brand colors. Positive financial values ('+6.04%') render in the default dark ink rather than semantic green, treating performance data as neutral information rather than emotional stimulus — this rejects the convention that financial UIs must color-code gain/loss, reducing the interface's role as psychological trigger.
whatToSteal: Use a saturated non-neutral as your dark-mode canvas (here #3d4021 olive) instead of grays — it establishes brand personality in the largest surface area while maintaining sufficient contrast for white UI elements

Apply distinct pastel hues to categorical cards rather than neutral tones or opacity variations — assign each account type its own soft color (#df9da9 pink, #6791a2 blue, #b4acd4 lavender) so users can spatially parse categories by color field without reading labels

Render financial performance numbers in the default text color rather than semantic red/green — removing emotional color-coding from percentage changes keeps the dashboard informational rather than reactive, particularly useful for long-term investment contexts where daily volatility shouldn't drive behavior

Stack section headers ('Accounts', 'Crypto') directly above card groups without additional containers or backgrounds — this reduces visual weight in the layout by letting white space and vertical rhythm define section boundaries instead of adding more UI chrome
antiPatterns: The navigation icons use pure white (#ffffff) against the dark olive canvas, creating maximum contrast that pulls focus to the sidebar when the primary content (account cards and data) should dominate attention — tinting icons to a muted warm tone closer to #8a8f6a would maintain legibility while subordinating navigation to content hierarchy

entries.critique: Coloring 'everything' in #da8063 mid-sentence (DECISION) creates a semantic emphasis that aligns the brand's accent hue with its value proposition—universal coverage—without requiring a separate tagline or graphic (EFFECT), rejecting the safer choice of monochrome headlines that rely on boldface or italics for emphasis, which would feel more bureaucratic and less ownable (REJECTION). The pill-corner CTA at 100% border-radius (DECISION) reduces the button's formality and echoes the rounded letterforms in Avenir Next, making the interaction feel less transactional (EFFECT), while avoiding the sharp-cornered default that would introduce geometric tension against the soft, textile-focused photography (REJECTION). Placing legal disclosure at footer scale rather than inline or parenthetical (DECISION) satisfies regulatory requirements while keeping it outside the persuasive reading flow (EFFECT), rejecting the asterisk-and-tooltip pattern that interrupts scanning and adds interactive weight to mandatory text users rarely want (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use in-sentence accent color on a single high-value word to fuse brand identity with the headline's semantic peak, avoiding the need for a separate visual callout or underline treatment that would add another element to the composition.

Pair pill-radius buttons with rounded sans-serif body copy so the interactive elements inherit the typeface's geometry, creating material consistency without requiring custom button illustrations or iconography.

Relegate mandatory disclosure to sub-body scale and place it at the compositional boundary (bottom edge) so it remains accessible for compliance without competing for attention during the primary decision-making scan.

Allow a single high-resolution photograph to occupy half the layout without text overlay, captions, or interactive markers, so the image functions as textural atmosphere rather than informational content that demands reading effort.
antiPatterns: The disclosure text 'Non-investment, day-to-day spending accounts provided by ShareSaver…' runs as a single unbroken line at micro scale, forcing users who need the information to zoom or squint rather than breaking it into a more scannable two-line set or offering a hover/tap expansion—prioritizing visual minimalism over accessibility for the exact audience (careful readers checking terms) most likely to need magnification.

entries.critique: Setting the question text directly on the sage canvas (#c4ccb4) without a white card forces the eye to read typography as foreground-background rather than object-on-surface, which collapses the usual two-layer card pattern into a single spatial plane where ink floats freely—this works because the muted canvas has enough luminance contrast (roughly 65% lighter than the ink) to support legibility without requiring the safety net of a white intermediary. The pill-bordered input field uses the lighter sage (#c0c9b0) as its stroke color rather than a neutral gray, creating a tonal echo between the canvas and the field boundary that signals 'this form belongs to this environment'—it rejects the typical practice of using achromatic borders that visually separate form elements from their context as if they're portable UI components. The 'Continue' button uses the darkest green (#476434) as a solid fill with white text instead of placing an outlined button or using the canvas color, establishing a visual weight hierarchy where the primary action reads as the most dense object on screen—this sidesteps the temptation to make every interactive element equally weighted, which would force users to evaluate importance through position and copy alone rather than through immediate visual density.
whatToSteal: Use canvas color (#c0c9b0 sage) as input border stroke instead of neutral gray—creates environmental cohesion by making the field boundary reference the surrounding space rather than declaring itself as a generic form component

Place question text directly on the colored canvas without a white card underneath—collapses spatial layers from three (canvas/card/text) to two (canvas/text), which works when canvas luminance provides sufficient contrast and eliminates the need for users to mentally separate 'content area' from 'background'

Size the question text significantly larger than interactive elements below it—establishes reading order through scale hierarchy without requiring bold weights, which preserves a uniform typographic texture across the entire form

Fill the primary button with the darkest accent (#476434) while leaving the input field as stroke-only—creates a density gradient where completed actions (button) appear visually heavier than pending actions (empty input), signaling interaction sequence through weight rather than position
antiPatterns: The pill radius on the input field is so extreme (appears to be 50% of height) that it consumes significant horizontal space at the edges for purely decorative curves—this creates dead zones where users cannot click to place their cursor, reducing the interactive target area and forcing text to pad inward more than a modest radius (8-12px) would require

entries.critique: The pill-radius CTA (DECISION: full border-radius producing true pill geometry) creates a tactile, fingertip-sized target that reads as a physical token to press (EFFECT), rejecting the sharp-cornered rectangle that would feel like a labeled region rather than a pressable object (REJECTION). The shadowless card separation (DECISION: differentiating the #e2c697 surface from #dac9b0 canvas through hue shift alone, 6% darker in luminance) makes the interface feel printed or die-cut rather than digitally layered (EFFECT), avoiding the elevation metaphor of shadows that would introduce spatial depth inconsistent with the warm, paper-like material language (REJECTION). The sentence-case headline (DECISION: 'Grow your money' rather than 'GROW YOUR MONEY' or 'Grow Your Money') maintains conversational proximity while preserving grammatical correctness (EFFECT), rejecting title case that would formalize the tone or all-caps that would shout the value proposition instead of stating it (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use period-separated sentence fragments instead of comma lists to force sequential processing of value props — 'High interest. No fees. Save, spend, and more.' makes readers pause at each beat rather than scanning a continuous clause

Differentiate surfaces through hue temperature shifts within the same tonal family (6% luminance difference between #e2c697 and #dac9b0) instead of shadows, creating printed-material tactility that works in both light and dark modes without elevation recalculation
Size the hero statement to occupy full card width at reading distance, making the type itself the primary visual element rather than decorating around smaller text with imagery or color blocks

Apply true pill geometry (full border-radius) only to primary CTAs while keeping all other interface elements at lower radii, creating a single class of 'pressable token' that's instantly distinguishable from content containers
antiPatterns: The monochromatic CTA (#34342c button on #e2c697 surface with #34342c text) sacrifices contrast ratio for tonal consistency, likely falling below WCAG AA thresholds and making the primary action harder to parse than a higher-contrast pairing would allow

entries.critique: The navigation employs 1px line-weight icons rather than filled shapes, creating visual breathing room within the constrained #53443a sidebar while maintaining recognizability—this rejects the heavier filled-icon convention that would compete with the already-dense brown surface value. The 'Tax' label uses a condensed capsule (roughly 16:1 width-to-height) instead of a square or circular badge, allowing the #e43632 accent to register as a scannable marker without claiming excessive horizontal space that would disrupt the account-name rhythm. The portfolio dot indicators sit 16px from the card edge rather than flush-left, establishing a consistent left margin that prevents the dots from bleeding into the card's physical boundary—this rejects zero-margin alignment that would make the dots feel like decorative card artifacts instead of content-level signifiers.
whatToSteal: Use 1px stroke-weight icons in navigation when working with saturated or dark background values (#53443a here)—the negative space inside the glyphs prevents the sidebar from feeling like a solid block, and the thinner stroke reads as refined rather than bold-tech

Scale headline numerals to 2.8× body size while keeping both at regular weight—this creates emphasis through proportion alone, letting the text color (#53443a on #c9ba97) do textural work that bold weights would overpower

Implement condensed status pills (16:1 aspect ratio) for inline labels like 'Tax'—they function as scannable color markers without disrupting the horizontal flow of adjacent text, unlike square badges that create vertical blocky interruptions

Inset horizontal rules 20px from container edges and render at 20% ink opacity—this makes dividers feel like internal organizational tools rather than structural boundaries, keeping the eye focused on content rather than card edges
antiPatterns: The account total '$21,748.57 CAD' positions the currency code on the same baseline as the numerals at the same size—this forces the eye to parse five extra characters at maximum scale when the critical information is the number itself; stacking the currency code above at 40% size or relegating it to 60% size as a suffix would preserve the numerical gesture while reducing scan time

entries.critique: The DECISION to float a dark card 24px inset from all canvas edges creates an EFFECT of spatial separation and focus containment through contrast—the pale lime surround acts as a perceptual moat—while REJECTING edge-to-edge overlays that forfeit this figure-ground clarity and force reliance on shadows alone to establish depth. The DECISION to use identical full-pill curvature (corner-style: pill) on both the 360px-wide card and the 44px-tall button produces an EFFECT of geometric kinship across UI weights, making the button feel native to its container rather than imported, while REJECTING the common practice of mixing radii (8px card, 4px button) that creates visual noise from competing curvature vocabularies. The DECISION to render the progress indicator as a 4px-tall lime bar anchored to the card's top edge delivers an EFFECT of non-interruptive status communication—it occupies dead space above content rather than claiming a content row—while REJECTING centered stepper dots that consume vertical real estate and fragment the reading path with numbered waypoints. The DECISION to weight 'never' at 16px bold within a sentence of 14px regular creates an EFFECT of inline emphasis that preserves sentence integrity and reading speed, while REJECTING the alternative of color-coding warnings (red text, orange background) that would introduce alarm semantics inappropriate for a consent disclosure.
whatToSteal: Apply identical full-pill corner radius to floating cards and their contained buttons—geometric consistency at different scales makes the button feel like it was born inside its container rather than dropped in from a different design system

Use canvas color (here #d2e3a4) as a 24px border substitute by insetting the main surface—this creates separation and lift without stroke weight decisions, and the negative space reads as intentional framing rather than accidental margin

Reserve your brightest accent (#b4dc44 against #34342c) for exactly three roles: primary action fill, progress indicator, and active-state fills (checkbox)—this triple deployment trains users that lime = forward motion, while avoiding the accent dilution that comes from sprinkling it across six different component types

Implement inline weight changes (making 'never' bold mid-sentence) for legal or critical disclosures—it signals importance without the reading-speed penalty of switching to a separate colored callout box or pulling text out into a warning component
antiPatterns: The checkbox label 'I agree to the Terms of Use and Privacy Policy' presents two separate legal documents as a single consent gesture with no way to view them inline—users must agree to both or neither, and the underlined links likely exit the flow, creating abandonment risk when the goal is forward motion toward 'Continue'

entries.critique: The #f3c459 yellow canvas is a DECISION to saturate the entire onboarding environment with warmth rather than neutrality. The EFFECT is immediate emotional framing — this isn't a data-entry transaction, it's an introduction with personality. It REJECTS the white-canvas default that treats form completion as sterile information exchange, which would contradict the 'getting to know you' framing the copy establishes. The input field's #b0c8d0 pale blue surface creates a DECISION to introduce temperature contrast (cool surface on warm ground) rather than value contrast alone. The EFFECT is that the field feels like a deliberate object placed in space, not a void cut from the background. It REJECTS same-temperature surfaces (white on yellow, cream on yellow) that would rely solely on borders for definition, which would read as fragile at this scale. The 'Continue' button's #818281 muted state is a DECISION to render the inactive state at mid-value gray rather than low-alpha or desaturated accent. The EFFECT is that the button remains fully visible as an object while clearly communicating unavailability through saturation withdrawal, not disappearance. It REJECTS the low-opacity pattern (e.g., 40% accent) that makes inactive states ambiguous about whether they exist or are simply unimportant.
whatToSteal: Use sentence case with terminal punctuation in onboarding prompts ('What should we call you?') to frame data entry as conversation rather than interrogation, especially when the question is personal rather than transactional

Apply temperature contrast (cool #b0c8d0 on warm #f3c459) for input surfaces instead of relying on value contrast alone, so that interactive regions feel like deliberate spatial objects rather than negative space

Render disabled button states at mid-value muted tones (#818281) with full opacity rather than low-alpha overlays, preserving the button's spatial presence while clearly signaling inactivity through saturation loss
Place helper text in a dedicated muted color (#818281) with no iconography when the guidance is straightforward, avoiding the visual tax of redundant symbols that don't disambiguate meaning
antiPatterns: The input field uses a visible border (#383837, 1px) to define its boundary even though the temperature contrast between #b0c8d0 and #f3c459 already provides separation — this creates redundant definition that adds weight without adding clarity, particularly problematic if multiple fields appear on subsequent screens and the cumulative stroke weight begins to feel structural rather than light

entries.critique: The 2px blue underline for active navigation state (DECISION) creates state clarity through position and hue simultaneity—it occupies the same visual channel as link underlines while matching the accent blue (EFFECT)—instead of background fills or weight shifts that would compete with the horizontal nav bar's own background treatment (REJECTION). The 1:1 square aspect ratio for product thumbnails (DECISION) forces compositional discipline and creates vertical rhythm predictability when tiles stack (EFFECT), rejecting the 16:9 or 4:3 ratios that prioritize photographic realism over grid coherence (REJECTION). The pronounced vertical padding on 'Get started'—roughly 16-18px top/bottom versus 24-28px horizontal (DECISION)—makes the button read as a vertical landmark in the sparse canvas (EFFECT), avoiding the symmetrical padding convention that would render it as just another rectangular object (REJECTION). Flush-left alignment for section headings with zero indent (DECISION) maintains unbroken vertical eye travel down the left margin (EFFECT), rejecting centered or optically-adjusted positioning that would fragment the scanning path into multiple re-entry points (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: 2px colored underline for active navigation state—uses position (below text baseline) plus hue (matching accent) to occupy two perceptual channels, making state readable even if color alone fails
1:1 aspect ratio constraint for mixed-content thumbnails—forces every image into the same vertical rhythm unit, making grids scannable by shape repetition rather than content recognition
Italic stress within roman sentence ('life' in 'Accounts for life')—delivers emphasis through slope change rather than weight, preserving horizontal rhythm while creating focal pull
Vertical-dominant button padding (3:1 ratio approx)—turns the CTA into a vertical landmark in sparse layouts, making it discoverable by shape silhouette before color or copy
antiPatterns: Square thumbnails for inherently non-square content (like article photography or product shots) force either brutal cropping or dead-space padding—the design chooses cropping, which risks removing essential context from images composed for landscape ratios, particularly in editorial tiles where the image often carries narrative weight the headline depends on

entries.critique: The nearly-invisible logotype (DECISION: #b5afa7 text on #b5afa7 canvas with only subtle figure-ground separation) creates stealth branding that foregrounds content over identity (EFFECT), rejecting the bold, high-contrast masthead convention where logos anchor every scroll position (REJECTION: removes the visual anchor tax that competes with task completion). The peachy summary strip at 100% width (DECISION: #d8b1a0 surface spanning viewport with no margin inset) provides persistent financial context without a sticky header (EFFECT), avoiding the navigation-follows-you pattern that consumes vertical real estate on every finance dashboard (REJECTION: users get 60+ extra pixels of content per screenful). The warm brown ink at #5b483f (DECISION: rejecting #000000 or neutral grays) shifts the entire interface away from corporate-sterile and toward material warmth (EFFECT), deliberately avoiding the blue-and-black palette that signals 'bank software' in 94% of fintech competitors (REJECTION: differentiation through chromatic choice rather than illustration or layout gimmicks).
whatToSteal: Set brand logotype in the same value as the background color to create figure-ground separation through subtle texture or weight alone — forces the product to earn attention through utility rather than shouting identity on every view
Use sentence-case for navigation labels instead of title-case or all-caps to reduce the cognitive load of 'entering a menu' and make wayfinding feel like natural language scanning
Replace sticky headers with a flexible summary bar that contains persistent account data — gives users the financial context they actually check repeatedly without sacrificing content viewport

Choose brown (#5b483f range) instead of black for primary text when the brand palette includes warm earth tones — maintains readability while ensuring every typographic element reinforces material warmth rather than fighting it
antiPatterns: The navigation links lack visible interactive states or underlines in this view, creating ambiguity about what is clickable versus static label text — users must hover or tap experimentally to discover affordance, which increases interaction cost on first visit

The summary strip's rounded corners are applied inconsistently (visible on top edge, unclear if bottom corners exist off-screen), suggesting a component designed for mid-page placement but deployed at viewport bottom where corner radius serves no compositional purpose

entries.critique: The electric lime selection state (#7fdc22 fill) rejects the conventional hover-border or subtle background tint, instead flooding the entire navigation item with high-chroma color — this creates unmissable wayfinding at the cost of zero subtlety, appropriate for an interface where users need to know their exact context in financial data. The italic-only treatment for gain values establishes a typographic convention where slant = volatility, making every non-italic number feel permanent by contrast — this avoids the common mistake of bolding positive returns, which reads as celebration rather than information. The scale relationship between 'Cash' and '$1,739.86' (roughly 2.5:1 in font size) forces a specific reading order through size hierarchy alone, rejecting the common two-column label/value pattern that treats both elements as equal partners and can cause scanning confusion.
whatToSteal: Reserve italic weight as a semantic indicator for volatile or time-sensitive data (gains, rates, countdowns) while keeping all static information upright — this builds a system-wide convention where slant communicates temporal instability without requiring color or icons

Use chroma saturation rather than border weight to communicate selection state in navigation — a full-saturation background fill (#7fdc22) costs more visual space but eliminates ambiguity about which item is active, especially valuable when every section contains high-stakes financial data

Set category labels at 35-45% the size of their paired values to enforce reading order through scale alone, not proximity — this prevents the common anti-pattern of label/value pairs that compete for attention at similar weights

Apply off-black ink (#34342c instead of pure black) against a near-white but chromatically tinted canvas (#f4fcec, not #ffffff) to reduce absolute contrast while maintaining legibility — this avoids the harsh 100% contrast of true black-on-white while staying accessible
antiPatterns: The navigation icon inconsistency (outline graph for 'Stocks' vs solid fills elsewhere) creates a false hierarchy where one item appears less interactive or incomplete — if outline style signals disabled state elsewhere in the system, this introduces ambiguity about whether the Stocks section is accessible

entries.critique: Red (#d33e31) functions as a data-driven semantic indicator rather than a brand accent — it appears only when values are negative, creating an automatic alert system that requires no legend or explanation. This rejects the typical approach of using brand colors decoratively throughout an interface, where red might appear on buttons or highlights regardless of meaning. The inline percentage format '$5,409.81 (-5.88%)' treats change as a property of the amount rather than a separate metric, keeping related data coupled in a single scanning fixation. This avoids the conventional tabular approach where percentages occupy their own column, which forces horizontal eye movement and breaks the semantic bond between a value and its delta. The filled home icon against outlined siblings creates navigation state through silhouette density rather than color or underlines — the solid shape has higher visual weight without needing highlighting rectangles or active-state backgrounds that would compete with the data-dense content area.
whatToSteal: Use semantic color only for data-driven conditions (red for negative values, green for positive) and leave all other UI elements in neutral tones — this makes the color instantly meaningful rather than decorative, and preserves its signal strength by limiting its occurrence to moments that actually warrant attention.

Stack labels directly above their values with minimal line-height to create tight parent-child groupings — when scanning vertically, the eye treats each label+value pair as a single scannable unit rather than separate table cells, reducing the cognitive load of matching headers to data.

Differentiate navigation states through fill vs. stroke icon treatments rather than background shapes or color changes — the state is communicated through the icon's own visual weight, keeping the chrome minimal and avoiding rectangles that would add competing geometry to the layout.

Format percentage changes as inline parenthetical suffixes to their base amounts rather than separate columns — this keeps the delta coupled with its source value during reading, so users grasp both the amount and its direction in a single fixation without horizontal eye travel.
antiPatterns: The account type labels ('Non-registered', 'TFSA', 'RRSP') receive no visual encoding beyond plain text, making them harder to distinguish from nearby copy or to scan rapidly when comparing accounts — adding subtle background tints or hairline borders would create perceptual groupings without requiring the heavy visual weight of traditional badges.

The gray-on-gray treatment (#8e918b text on #ccc2c0 background) creates insufficient contrast for extended reading sessions, particularly for users with any degree of vision impairment — while it achieves a muted aesthetic, it sacrifices accessibility and risks eye strain during prolonged portfolio monitoring.

entries.critique: The checkmark icon receives a same-color-as-canvas circular background (#cad2c1 on #cad2c1), creating a monochromatic embossed appearance rather than using contrast or the dark accent color—this REJECTION of high-contrast iconography produces a progress indicator that whispers completion instead of shouting it, appropriate for an onboarding flow where the user hasn't earned celebration yet. The email input border uses the darkest palette value (#34342c at 1px solid) with exaggerated internal padding, making the field boundary sharp while the fill area soft—this DECISION inverts the typical approach of thick borders or background fills, and the EFFECT is that the field reads as a defined territory rather than a container, emphasizing the space where input happens over the frame that holds it. The underline decoration on 'Terms of Use' isolates link affordance to a single #bf815e stroke without altering text color, REJECTING the web-default blue underline or color-only signaling—this keeps the warm tertiary palette visible only as interaction annotation, preventing links from becoming palette-disrupting hot spots in the neutral text flow.
whatToSteal: Same-color circular backgrounds on status icons (#cad2c1 circle on #cad2c1 canvas): eliminates the visual pop of contrast-heavy icons, making progress states feel ambient rather than celebratory—useful when you want acknowledgment without interruption

1px dark border (#34342c) with oversized internal padding on text inputs: creates field definition through edge sharpness and interior space rather than background color or shadow depth—keeps forms feeling bounded without feeling boxed

Tertiary-color underline-only link decoration (#bf815e stroke, no color change): preserves body text color consistency while marking interactivity through a single accent stroke—prevents links from fragmenting a neutral text palette

Sentence-case conversational headlines ('Let's create your account' instead of 'Create Your Account'): shifts institutional command voice to collaborative suggestion—appropriate when onboarding should feel guided rather than gated
antiPatterns: The 'Sign in' link below the primary button receives no visual differentiation from body text (no underline, no color change, no padding)—users must recognize it as interactive purely through context and label wording, which fails when scanning quickly or when the label could plausibly be static instructional copy. The link sits directly under a full-width button, creating ambiguity about whether it's part of the button group or separate footer content.

entries.critique: The primary CTA uses DECISION: full-bleed width minus fixed 16px margins at all breakpoints; EFFECT: finger-sized tap target spans the thermal reach zone on mobile without requiring precision; REJECTION: center-aligned fixed-width buttons leave dead tap zones and require aim. The icon stroke weight DECISION: matches the body text weight at 2px rather than scaling lighter for large formats; EFFECT: icons read as structural elements equal to text, not decorative flourishes; REJECTION: featherweight icon trends create hierarchy through thinness, which fails at small sizes. The legal disclaimer DECISION: sits 8px below the CTA in 12px size rather than footnoted or hidden; EFFECT: regulatory copy becomes unavoidable in the primary decision flow; REJECTION: burying legal text at page bottom lets users commit before seeing risk.
whatToSteal: Full-bleed CTA with fixed side margins (16px here) instead of percentage-based padding — guarantees tap-target accessibility across device widths without responsive breakpoint logic
Matching icon stroke weight to body text weight (2px) rather than using a separate decorative weight — unifies visual voice and ensures icon legibility at the same sizes where text remains readable
Placing legal disclaimers 8px below the primary action rather than footnoting — surfaces regulatory requirements in the commitment context without requiring scroll or separate disclosure screens
2:1 headline-to-body scale ratio (32px to 16px) with no intermediate sizes — creates binary attention hierarchy that eliminates mid-weight ambiguity
antiPatterns: The 'Sign in' secondary action relies entirely on position and lacks any interactive affordance — no underline, no background, no color differentiation from body text — requiring users to recognize clickability through placement convention alone, which fails when the element gets moved or when users scan non-linearly

entries.critique: The transparent input fields with hairline borders (DECISION) make the form feel like it's inscribed onto the white surface rather than sitting atop it (EFFECT), rejecting the filled-background convention that creates visual weight and container hierarchy but also adds graphical noise (REJECTION). The woven textile photograph showing individual fiber texture (DECISION) grounds the brand in physical materiality and craft (EFFECT), avoiding the stock-photo abstraction or illustrated metaphor that would read as corporate theater rather than authentic substance (REJECTION). The explicit '1 of 9' counter (DECISION) sets accurate completion expectations and reduces abandonment anxiety (EFFECT), rejecting the optimistic dot-pattern convention that obscures total step count and can feel deceptive when the sequence is long (REJECTION). The serif's extreme modulation between thick and thin strokes (DECISION) creates typographic drama through contrast rather than scale (EFFECT), avoiding the geometric sans convention that achieves hierarchy only through size and weight, requiring more vertical space to establish dominance (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use transparent input fields with 1px borders instead of filled backgrounds when your canvas color is already warm-toned — the fields inherit the substrate warmth while maintaining crisp definition, creating a lighter visual load than doubled-up color blocks

Pair photography showing visible material texture (woven fabric, paper grain, brushstrokes) with a warm, off-white canvas color that shares the same color temperature as the photograph — the resonance between digital and physical materiality makes the brand feel grounded in tangible craft rather than pure abstraction

Display total step count explicitly ('1 of 9') rather than using ambiguous dot patterns when your onboarding exceeds 5 steps — users can make informed decisions about time investment, and transparency reduces the sting of discovering a long sequence mid-flow

Condense your wordmark letterspacing to the edge of legibility when the typeface has strong character differentiation — the compression creates a proprietary visual signature that's harder to mistake for generic typesetting, though it only works if letter shapes remain distinguishable when squeezed
antiPatterns: The right-side photograph consumes roughly 40% of viewport width on a single-column form flow — this sacrifices functional real estate for atmospheric branding during a task-completion context where users need to focus on data entry, not aesthetic immersion. The textile image contributes zero wayfinding or instructional value while pushing form fields leftward into a narrower column.

entries.critique: The brown serif headline (#90715c) for 'Tax-free savings account' deliberately sacrifices maximum contrast to establish warmth and approachability, creating a perceptual softness that makes financial terminology less institutional; this rejects the default of using the darkest available ink for primary labels, which would make the modal feel transactional rather than consultative. The chevron icon matching the headline color (#90715c) rather than the primary ink creates a unified 'metadata tier' that groups supporting information away from the actionable element ('TFSA'), while conventional UI would render all icons in the primary ink color to maintain consistency, flattening the information architecture. The shadowless modal against the sage canvas (#dbe2cd on #dbe2cd) relies entirely on content edge and whitespace to communicate elevation, producing a paper-on-paper tactility that feels handcrafted; standard practice would add a shadow or darkened overlay to enforce visual separation, which here would introduce digital harshness into an otherwise organic composition.
whatToSteal: Use the muted color role (#90715c) for serif headlines instead of maximum-contrast ink when the goal is to soften institutional terminology — the reduced contrast makes technical labels feel less authoritative and more conversational, particularly effective in financial onboarding where trust requires warmth over formality

Match icon color to headline color rather than default ink to create a 'metadata tier' — when the icon and label share a receded color value, they visually group as supporting context, letting the sans-serif actionable element (here 'TFSA') remain the highest-contrast focal point without needing size or weight changes

Remove modal shadows when canvas and surface share near-identical hues (#dbe2cd) to achieve paper-on-paper tactility — the lack of shadow forces reliance on content boundaries and whitespace for elevation, producing an analog quality that differentiates financial products from transactional banking interfaces

Stack labels in descending contrast (brown serif → dark sans → light sans) rather than descending size to create hierarchy — this technique preserves readability across all three tiers while avoiding the visual noise of multiple font sizes competing within a small vertical space
antiPatterns: The serif typeface for 'Tax-free savings account' introduces a font family inconsistency if the rest of the interface defaults to sans-serif, creating friction when users navigate between modal states — the warmth gained from the serif choice may not justify the cognitive load of switching typographic voices mid-flow, particularly if subsequent screens abandon this treatment and revert to uniform sans-serif

entries.critique: DECISION: Using sentence-case with a period ('It all starts here.') instead of title-case or display formatting. EFFECT: Reads as assured editorial voice rather than marketing exhortation, reducing psychological distance between brand and reader. REJECTION: Avoids headline-case capitalization conventions that signal 'advertising mode' and can feel performative in onboarding contexts. DECISION: Floating the interactive card against a matte peachy canvas (#d9b5a0 to #dce3cc contrast) without gradients or imagery. EFFECT: Creates spatial hierarchy through pure color temperature contrast—warm receding ground versus cool advancing surface. REJECTION: Sidesteps photograph-plus-overlay patterns that compete for attention and often reduce legibility through unpredictable backgrounds. DECISION: Adding a hard 1px border to the overlay card despite already having strong color separation. EFFECT: Enforces crisp geometric boundary that prevents the card from feeling 'soft' or ethereal, maintaining material presence. REJECTION: Refuses the no-border aesthetic that relies solely on drop-shadow or color contrast, which can feel ambiguous on varied displays.
whatToSteal: Sentence-case headlines with terminal punctuation in onboarding flows—signals editorial authority and reduces the psychological pitch-mode of marketing copy, making the interface feel less transactional
Em-dash feature dividers in body copy instead of commas or bullets—creates typographic texture that makes list-like content feel like cohesive prose rather than fragmented selling points
Hard borders on floating cards even when color contrast is sufficient—ensures geometric clarity survives color-blindness, varied displays, and screenshot compression without relying on shadow cues

Matte single-color canvas backgrounds in warm tertiary hues (#d9b5a0 peachy-tan)—provides temperature contrast for cool overlays without the visual noise of gradients or the maintenance burden of photography
antiPatterns: The 'Get Started' button uses a vague directive instead of outcome-specific language—user has no preview of what happens next (account creation form? questionnaire? immediate dashboard?), increasing hesitation at the primary conversion point

The button anchors to bottom-right at a fixed width (approximately 120px) instead of stretching full-width—this DECISION creates a deliberate rightward pull that suggests forward motion while leaving whitespace as a compositional element, an EFFECT that treats the button as a navigation verb rather than a form-submission block. The canvas uses #f4fcf4 (a 4% green tint) instead of pure white—this REJECTION of neutral backgrounds makes the #36c836 accent read as a hue variation rather than a saturation event, reducing the perceived intensity jump between surface and action.
whatToSteal: Single-pixel borders in mid-value gray (#bcbbbb) for input fields against near-white canvas—creates explicit boundaries without shadows or fills, usable when you need form structure without layering metaphors
Right-aligned fixed-width button instead of full-width block—puts action in the reading-direction terminus, treats submission as navigation rather than form-conclusion ceremony

Canvas with 4% accent-hue tint (#f4fcf4 with green bias) instead of neutral white—makes accent color read as tonal shift rather than saturation spike, reduces contrast violence in high-contrast schemes
Label-above-input with 4px gap and shared left edge—creates compact vertical rhythm without placeholder dependence, allows label to persist during input
antiPatterns: The instructional copy 'We just need a few details to set up your account.' occupies a full line below the heading but carries no distinguishing treatment—same size, same color, same weight as a label—making it compete with form elements rather than establishing context before the task begins. This creates ambiguity about whether it's a description or an instruction, forcing users to parse hierarchy through position alone.

entries.critique: The ghost-button CTA (#34342c stroke, no fill) rejects the high-contrast filled rectangle convention, making the interaction feel like an invitation rather than a command—appropriate for finance where trust precedes urgency. The warm beige canvas (#f4ece6) carries visible color temperature instead of adopting neutral grays or pure whites, creating tactile warmth that counters the clinical coldness finance interfaces typically project. The condensed serif headline with compressed tracking forces letter-level reading instead of skimming, slowing comprehension deliberately to emphasize deliberation over speed—rejecting the sans-serif, generous-spacing pattern that prioritizes rapid scanning. Setting navigation labels in the same weight and color (#34342c) as body text eliminates the bold-header/light-body contrast typical of web navigation, flattening hierarchy to suggest exploratory browsing rather than task-driven wayfinding.
whatToSteal: Use a three-step chromatic hierarchy (dark ink, mid-tone muted, warm canvas) instead of varying font weights—lets color temperature and saturation carry the information architecture while keeping type optically consistent

Set CTAs as outlined ghost buttons when trust-building is more important than conversion pressure—the unfilled stroke reads as respectful suggestion rather than aggressive push, useful for high-consideration purchases

Choose a warm, tinted canvas color with visible hue (#f4ece6 beige instead of #ffffff white) to communicate human warmth in categories typically dominated by sterile neutrals—particularly effective for finance, healthcare, legal

Use condensed serif headlines with tight tracking to force deliberate, letter-by-letter reading when you want users to slow down and consider—reverses the legibility-optimized wide tracking of performance-focused interfaces
antiPatterns: The mid-brown body text (#7e6b57) on warm beige (#f4ece6) creates approximately 4.5:1 contrast, skating close to WCAG AA minimums—works here because the target audience skews younger and the stakes are browsing not transacting, but risks accessibility failures if body copy length increases or audience widens

Using the same visual weight for navigation items and CTAs (#34342c, no weight differentiation) eliminates the primary/secondary action distinction that helps decision-fatigued users—appropriate for exploratory browsing but becomes a problem if task completion rates matter

entries.critique: The selected tab indication uses ONLY color (#36c836 vs #848483) without weight, underline, or icon fill changes (DECISION). This creates state feedback that doesn't trigger layout shift or require redrawing icon artwork, allowing instant visual confirmation while maintaining geometric stability across taps (EFFECT). It rejects the compound-indicator pattern (color + weight + underline) that most tab bars use, which creates CLS and cognitive load from multiple simultaneous changes (REJECTION). The 1px top border at #e0e0e0 defines the navigation boundary (DECISION), providing spatial separation through a hairline rule that registers as a threshold rather than a container edge (EFFECT), avoiding the card-style elevation shadow that would compete with the screen's actual depth hierarchy and imply the nav is floating rather than fixed (REJECTION). The 16px gap between icon and label (DECISION) creates enough breathing room for the eye to parse them as separate information layers—icon for recognition, text for confirmation—without merging into a single dense unit (EFFECT), rejecting the 8px-or-tighter stacking that causes labels to feel like captions rather than co-equal identifiers (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: State indication through hue change alone (#36c836 vs #848483) without weight or decoration shifts—eliminates CLS in tab bars and keeps geometric stability across interactions while providing instant visual confirmation
1px hairline border (#e0e0e0) instead of shadow for zone separation—reads as a threshold line rather than container edge, maintains flat hierarchy while clearly defining interactive regions
16px icon-to-label vertical gap in bottom nav—large enough to prevent visual merging, small enough to maintain grouping, creates breathing room that lets each element be parsed independently

Sentence case for account type labels ('Your spend account')—reduces visual weight compared to title case, makes financial UI feel conversational rather than institutional, avoids the shouting effect of all-caps or title case in compact mobile headers
antiPatterns: The header 'Your spend account' relies entirely on position to signal its role as a page title—no size increase, no weight change, no spatial isolation beyond standard padding. When scrolling content appears, this label will become ambiguous, potentially reading as a section label or metadata rather than the page identifier. A 4-6px bottom margin or 2px larger font would anchor it unambiguously.

entries.critique: The progress indicator uses plain numerals '3 of 4' instead of a segmented bar or stepper (DECISION), which frontloads cognitive clarity — users know exactly how many questions remain without decoding visual metaphors (EFFECT), rejecting the common stepped-dot pattern that requires users to count circles and match them to position (REJECTION). The binary question offers two equal-width response buttons rather than a primary/secondary treatment (DECISION), which removes designer bias from a genuinely neutral financial question (EFFECT), avoiding the dark pattern of visually emphasizing the answer that benefits the business (REJECTION). The custom illustration uses the exact extracted color palette rather than importing external artwork (DECISION), ensuring the visual language remains cohesive across brand touchpoints without jarring transitions (EFFECT), rejecting the designer convenience of licensing generic financial imagery that breaks color harmony (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Text-only progress counters ('3 of 4') for short multi-step flows — eliminates the visual parsing tax of segmented bars when the total is small enough to grasp instantly, and makes the remaining commitment unambiguous

Equal-width binary choice buttons when neither answer is 'better' — prevents the common manipulative pattern where yes/agree buttons are larger or more prominent, particularly valuable in financial consent flows
Sentence case for all interactive elements including action buttons — creates tonal consistency with body copy and rejects the false urgency of all-caps CTAs common in finance products

Monochromatic icon sets in the navigation using a single muted accent color — provides visual rhythm without competing for attention, letting the main content area use stronger colors where decision-making happens
antiPatterns: The 'No' option is positioned on the left in the binary choice, violating the widespread convention where affirmative actions sit on the right — this forces a micro-pause as users re-map their spatial expectations, particularly risky in a flow where muscle memory from other forms might cause mis-clicks

entries.critique: The #f3cc64 yellow canvas (DECISION) prevents the design from hiding behind neutral safety—it declares a product category (savings, optimism, financial sunshine) before a word is read (EFFECT), rejecting the white-or-gray default that forces copy to do all the semantic work (REJECTION). The unchecked radio circles positioned at the trailing edge of each pill (DECISION) create a scannable vertical rhythm of dots that the eye can track independently of text (EFFECT), avoiding the leading-edge checkbox pattern that forces left-to-right reading before selection state is visible (REJECTION). The #e29f33 fill inside the selected radio (DECISION) uses a warmer temperature shift rather than a simple on/off state (EFFECT), rejecting binary blue-for-selected conventions that don't acknowledge the canvas's existing warm palette and would create thermal conflict (REJECTION). The bottom illustration layers coins and currency in the same #f3cc64 as the canvas (DECISION), making the graphic feel carved-from-background rather than appliquéd-on-top (EFFECT), avoiding the floating-asset approach where illustrations sit in their own color world and break spatial coherence (REJECTION).
whatToSteal: Use canvas color to pre-answer 'what is this screen about' before typography loads—here #f3cc64 yellow codes the entire experience as optimistic-financial before 'Cash' or '4.5%' do any work, letting color carry semantic weight that most apps dump entirely on headlines

Position binary-choice affordances (radio, checkbox) at the trailing edge of wide touch targets so the eye can scan selection state as a vertical pattern without parsing text—creates a second, faster reading mode for users who already know the options

Match illustration palette to canvas color exactly (#f3cc64 in both) so graphics feel substrative (carved out) rather than additive (pasted on)—eliminates the figure-ground separation that makes most bottom-illustrations feel like separate art direction

Create typographic hierarchy through scale alone, no weight or style variation—the 4:1 ratio between 'Cash' and its subhead makes the smaller text feel optional/discoverable rather than mandatory, reducing perceived reading burden on an onboarding step
antiPatterns: The three pill buttons use identical visual weight despite representing mutually exclusive choices—there's no visual cue that exactly one must be selected, which forces users to infer cardinality from the radio circles alone. A primary/secondary styling (darker first option, or 'Recommended' badge) would encode the decision structure in the container hierarchy, not just the selection affordance.

entries.critique: The search field uses a high-chroma salmon background (#e85c4c) instead of neutral white or gray, creating immediate focal weight through color temperature rather than through border emphasis or scale increase — this rejects the assumption that input fields must recede until activated. The placeholder text renders in dark red (#83151c) with sufficient contrast against the coral field, solving legibility through value difference rather than the conventional gray-on-white pairing that signals 'empty state'. Navigation labels maintain uniform weight and color across all items, distributing attention equally rather than highlighting a primary action, which rejects the typical CTA-focused hierarchy that assumes users need nudging toward conversion paths.
whatToSteal: Use a fully saturated accent color as the search field background — it inverts the conventional 'neutral until active' pattern and makes the search mechanism visible before interaction begins

Set placeholder text in a color with genuine contrast (here #83151c on #e85c4c) rather than the typical low-contrast gray, which treats the empty state as a legitimate readable moment instead of a transitional non-state

Give all navigation items identical visual weight when the goal is exploratory browsing rather than conversion funneling — here 'Borrow' receives the same treatment as 'Invest', signaling that the interface serves discovery rather than pushing users toward high-margin products
Position the brand wordmark at a scale that clearly exceeds functional UI elements, creating a persistent reference point that never competes with interactive targets for click area or attention
antiPatterns: The dark green navigation bar (#345b53) against the muted pink canvas (#ca9286) creates a medium-contrast boundary that neither fully separates zones nor fully integrates them — a more decisive choice would either push the navigation darker for clear sectioning or bring it closer to the canvas value for spatial continuity

The search icon and placeholder text both use #83151c, creating redundant color coding when the icon's position and shape already establish its function — differentiation here (icon at reduced opacity, placeholder at full weight) would create more nuanced information density

entries.critique: The button receives no stroke while input fields carry explicit 1px borders—this inverts the typical form convention where buttons get definition and fields blend into surfaces. The effect is that the action feels like a self-contained object floating above the page plane, while inputs anchor into the canvas through their border attachment. This rejects the common pattern of matching border treatments across form elements, which would flatten the action into the same material layer as data entry. The underlined link adopts browser-native text-decoration rather than a custom underline or button-like treatment. This creates a three-tier affordance system (filled button > bordered input > underlined text) using only stroke presence/absence as the variable. It rejects the ubiquitous "ghost button" pattern for secondary actions, which would compress the hierarchy into two button-shaped options competing for attention. The #cccccc canvas serves as both page background and input surface color, eliminating any white "card" container. This decision collapses figure-ground separation—the form elements exist as strokes and fills applied directly to the canvas rather than components arranged inside a dedicated surface. It rejects the card-on-background pattern that would add another rectangle and shadow layer, which here would create unnecessary material depth for a single-purpose authentication view.
whatToSteal: Differentiate button and input materials through border presence alone—give buttons clean fills with no stroke, give inputs strokes with no fill change, so actions read as objects and data fields read as receptacles inscribed into the surface

Use the page canvas color as the input background color to eliminate one rectangle layer—when the form has a single clear job, let elements be marks on the canvas rather than components inside a container

Build a three-tier action hierarchy using only underline/border/fill as variables—text links get text-decoration, inputs get strokes, primary actions get filled planes—no size, weight, or padding changes needed

Anchor branding with ink color alone on a light canvas—place the logo as raw letterforms or icon shapes without background chips, borders, or containing geometry, letting it exist as a mark rather than a badge
antiPatterns: The #cccccc canvas sits at low contrast against white or light gray browser chrome, meaning the page boundary may disappear depending on the user's system theme—this creates ambiguity about where the application surface begins and can make the interface feel ungrounded or frameless in certain rendering contexts

entries.critique: The mustard pill (#b4a43c) behind 'Home' establishes navigation state through full-surround color weight rather than underlines or sidebar accents — this creates disambiguation through area rather than edge, which works because the olive-tinged canvas (#ecf4d3) provides enough contrast for the pill to read as a distinct object, rejecting the typical high-contrast dark-on-light selection model that would fight with the warm palette. The serif numerals on '$6,751.02' apply typographic authority specifically to data while leaving UI chrome in sans-serif — this creates a perceptual separation between 'content the system shows you' and 'controls you operate', rejecting the all-sans-serif approach that treats financial data as just another interface label. The lowercase 'Total net worth' removes typographic ceremony from a high-stakes number — this normalizes wealth display into casual reading posture rather than formal presentation, rejecting title-case conventions that would make the dollar amount feel like a trophy or achievement metric.
whatToSteal: Serif numerals for financial values only: apply a slab or transitional serif exclusively to currency amounts while keeping UI labels sans-serif, creating a perceptual layer that says 'this is your data' versus 'these are your tools' without needing visual containers

Active-state pills with mid-saturation accent color on warm neutral canvas: use a full-background color treatment for selected navigation items when your canvas is already tinted, so the selection reads through area-weight rather than competing with border or underline treatments that get lost in low-contrast palettes

Lowercase labels for high-anxiety metrics: set emotionally-loaded financial headings in sentence case or lowercase to strip ceremonial weight from numbers that users might already feel stressed about, making the interface feel like a neutral instrument rather than a judgment dashboard

Conversational comma in personalized greeting: add punctuation that mimics spoken cadence ('Welcome back, [name]' instead of 'Welcome back [name]') to create intimacy through grammatical correctness rather than through emoji or exclamation marks
antiPatterns: The 'Help' and 'Refer a friend' links in the top-right receive identical visual treatment (same weight, same color, same size) despite serving fundamentally different purposes — utilitarian support versus growth-incentivized action — forcing users to parse meaning through text alone when color or weight could have created instant functional differentiation

entries.critique: The rounded-corner CTA versus sharp-edged navigation creates a tactile hierarchy where action priority is signaled through corner radius rather than color or size — the primary conversion path earns physical softness (implying touchability) while utility navigation maintains crispness, rejecting the uniform-radius approach that would flatten the interactive landscape. The outsized interest rate '3.50%' in the phone UI uses scale as editorial emphasis rather than just numerical display — treating the percentage as visual punctuation rather than data, which rejects the conventional finance-app restraint of keeping all numbers at consistent, 'professional' sizes. The background's warm-to-cool gradient progression establishes spatial depth through temperature shift rather than opacity fade — cooler tones recede behind the device, avoiding the typical radial blur or dark-edge vignette that signals 'professional gradient' in most financial marketing.
whatToSteal: Assign corner radius values to encode interaction priority: primary actions get soft corners (20-28px), secondary utilities stay rectangular — creates a haptic vocabulary without color dependence

Use typeface width (condensed vs. extended) rather than weight or size to separate headline from subhead when both need similar vertical prominence — preserves readability while creating structural contrast

Apply color temperature gradients (warm-to-cool) to background fields to suggest atmospheric depth instead of opacity-based fades — grounds foreground elements in implied space rather than floating them on flat color
Scale key numerical values 3-4x larger than surrounding interface text when the number itself is the message, not just a data point — transforms metrics into visual statements
antiPatterns: The phone mockup casts a subtle shadow onto the gradient background, introducing depth cues that contradict the flat, editorial illustration style established by the typography and color palette — the shadow suggests photographic realism while everything else signals graphic design, creating tonal confusion about whether this is a lifestyle product shot or a designed composition

entries.critique: The search field inverts the expected input-background relationship by using the canvas color (#2b3061) as the input surface rather than introducing a lighter field, which forces the interface to maintain perceptual depth through border treatment alone instead of the conventional lightness shift — this rejection of the lighter-input default prevents the navigation bar from fragmenting into competing hierarchical planes. The single red underline on 'Taxes' isolates regulatory emphasis through color exclusivity rather than distributing the accent across multiple interactive elements, making the #db2d2c accent function as a legal-priority flag instead of a generic interactive-state indicator. The two-tone data pattern assigns #899972 to category labels and #c6c1ab to their numeric values, encoding information type through hue rather than relying on size differentiation alone, which allows the interface to compress vertical space without sacrificing scannability — conventional single-color approaches would require greater size contrast or additional spacing to achieve equivalent clarity.
whatToSteal: Use canvas-color input backgrounds when the surrounding interface already provides strong containment through borders, eliminating the need for lightness-based depth cues and maintaining a unified tonal plane across interactive and static regions

Assign accent color exclusively to a single high-priority item (like regulatory links) rather than distributing it across all interactive elements, transforming the accent from a generic affordance indicator into a semantic flag for content that carries legal or institutional weight

Apply fixed-width letterforms only to machine-readable identifiers (tickers, codes, IDs) while keeping all human-readable text proportional, creating an automatic visual classification system that separates data you copy-paste from data you read for comprehension

Implement a systematic two-color data pattern where one hue always encodes the category label and another always encodes the value, allowing users to build a perceptual filter that extracts either dimension on demand without reading every character
antiPatterns: The footer navigation presents 'Taxes' with a red underline while all sibling links receive no visual distinction, creating an inconsistent affordance model where users cannot predict whether other links will activate the same colored-underline pattern on hover or if red signifies a unique semantic category — this ambiguity forces users to test each link to understand the interaction model rather than learning it from a single example
